title: Adversarial Co-Thinking: Calibration and Triangulation Across Multiple GenAI Tools in HCI Writing
summary: This paper examines what happens when GenAI tools are fully embedded in the drafting of an academic paper rather than confined to late-stage polishing. To investigate how an intensive multi-tool GenAI workflow differs from conventional academic writing, I drafted this paper from the first sentence in parallel with three GenAI tools - Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini - comparing their outputs against my own intended contribution. Across this process, a recurring pattern took shape that I call adversarial co-thinking: using past peer reviews to calibrate the tools, then setting their outputs against one another to be tested rather than deferred to. I argue that surfacing genuine critique from tools that default to praise is a central practical challenge of working with these tools, and that the skill at stake is evaluative rather than generative. Adversarial co-thinking is a high-skill epistemic practice: it can amplify expertise where it exists, but it can also mask its absence. I further argue that current disclosure frameworks are poorly equipped to capture this shift. The paper offers four propositions for workshop discussion concerning autonomy, supervision, equity of access, and disclosure.

AI.tldr: The author embedded GenAI tools throughout the entire drafting process of an academic paper, developing a method called "adversarial co-thinking" — using peer reviews to calibrate AI outputs and pitting tools against each other to surface genuine critique rather than default praise. The paper argues that the key skill in AI-assisted writing is evaluative judgment, not generation, and that current disclosure frameworks fail to capture this new collaborative dynamic.
AI.motivation: To investigate how fully embedding GenAI tools throughout the entire drafting process (rather than just late-stage polishing) transforms academic writing practice, and to understand the epistemic and practical challenges that arise when authors engage intensively with multiple AI tools in parallel.
AI.method: The author drafted their own paper from the first sentence in parallel with three GenAI tools — Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini — comparing the tools' outputs against their own intended contribution. Past peer reviews were used to calibrate the tools, and the outputs of different tools were set against one another to test rather than defer to them. This reflexive, practice-based methodology treats the writing process itself as the object of inquiry.
AI.result: A recurring pattern emerged, termed "adversarial co-thinking": using peer reviews to calibrate tools and setting their outputs against each other to surface critique. The central practical challenge identified is extracting genuine critique from tools that default to praise. The skill at stake is evaluative (judging output quality) rather than generative (creating output). The practice can amplify existing expertise but can also mask its absence. Current disclosure frameworks are inadequate for capturing this new mode of AI-integrated authorship.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial co-thinking is a high-skill epistemic practice that fundamentally shifts the nature of academic writing. The paper offers four propositions for workshop discussion concerning autonomy, supervision, equity of access, and disclosure, arguing that existing frameworks must evolve to account for deeply integrated GenAI workflows.

title: Exploring Reinforcement Learning for Fluid Transitions Between Clinical Mental Healthcare and Everyday Wellness Support
summary: Mental health struggles wax and wane, yet clinical and wellness interventions typically operate separately, causing frequent breakdowns at care transitions. We explore reinforcement learning (RL) as a means to build digital health systems that deliver clinical and wellness interventions proactively, as part of a coherent care journey. We ask: what complexities does designing such a system involve? We built a contextual bandit that dynamically selects journaling prompts from clinical and wellness repertoires to optimize for an overarching health goal (sustained journaling) and deployed it in a four-week exploratory study (N=38). We found that, first, many benefits of RL-optimized intervention sequences appeared only after interventions ended, raising the question: Should systems that offer coherent clinical-wellness care journeys include stepping-back periods? If so, when and how? Second, participants most engaged with RL-generated interventions deepened their engagement over time, while those most engaged with a constant intervention tended to burn out and drop out later. It raises the question: When should a system blending clinical and wellness interventions reduce intensity to prevent burnout in versus sustain it to maximize treatment gains?

AI.tldr: A contextual bandit RL system dynamically selected clinical and wellness journaling prompts to sustain engagement. In a 4-week study (N=38), benefits of RL-optimized sequences emerged mainly post-intervention, and RL-driven engagement deepened over time versus constant interventions that led to burnout.
AI.motivation: Mental health care suffers from fragmentation between clinical and wellness interventions, particularly at care transitions. The authors explore whether RL can proactively integrate both intervention types into a coherent, continuous care journey, addressing the gap where traditional systems treat these phases separately.
AI.method: The authors built a contextual bandit algorithm that dynamically selects journaling prompts from both clinical (e.g., CBT-based) and wellness (e.g., gratitude) repertoires, optimizing for sustained journaling behavior. This was deployed in a four-week exploratory field study with 38 participants.
AI.result: Two key findings: (1) Many benefits of RL-optimized intervention sequences only manifested after the intervention period ended, suggesting a possible need for "stepping-back" periods. (2) Participants most engaged with RL-generated interventions showed deepening engagement over time, whereas those most engaged with constant (non-adaptive) interventions tended to burn out and drop out later.
AI.conclusion: Designing coherent clinical-wellness RL systems raises critical questions: whether and when to incorporate stepping-back periods for post-intervention benefit emergence, and how to balance intervention intensity to prevent burnout while maximizing therapeutic gains.

title: Personality Anchoring for Social Simulation: Linking Personality, Social Behavior, and Interaction Success with LLM Agents
summary: Social interactions are shaped by the interplay of dispositional traits and situational context, yet systematically investigating how personality configurations between individuals jointly influence social behavior across diverse social contexts remains methodologically challenging. We address this gap by introducing a simulation pipeline adapted from the CHARISMA framework, which employs well-known movie characters and public figures as psychologically grounded agents for multi-LLM social simulation using a method we term personality anchoring. We present a large-scale empirical study examining how dyadic Agreeableness composition influences social interaction outcomes across 1,010 simulated conversations. Our results reveal a monotonic relationship between dyadic Agreeableness composition and shared goal achievement, with Homogeneous-Agreeable pairs achieving success 10 times the rate of Homogeneous-Disagreeable pairs (62% vs. 6%). Behavioral mediation analysis reveals that Agreeableness shapes goal achievement partially through cooperative strategy selection, though it continues to predict outcomes within the same dominant strategy, indicating pathways beyond observable conversational behavior. Robustness analyses confirm high consistency of results across repeated simulations (ICC = 0.89) and stable personality expression across diverse scenarios, validating personality anchoring as a viable operationalization strategy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a simulation pipeline using LLM-based agents anchored to known movie characters/public figures to study how dyadic Agreeableness composition affects social interaction outcomes. Across 1,010 simulated conversations, Homogeneous-Agreeable pairs achieved shared goals at 10× the rate of Homogeneous-Disagreeable pairs (62% vs. 6%), with cooperative strategy partially mediating this effect. Results show high consistency (ICC=0.89) and stable personality expression, validating the "personality anchoring" methodology.
AI.motivation: Systematically investigating how personality configurations between individuals jointly influence social behavior across diverse contexts is methodologically challenging. Traditional methods struggle to isolate dispositional effects from situational confounds at scale. The authors aim to fill this gap by leveraging multi-LLM social simulation with psychologically grounded agents.
AI.method: The authors adapted the CHARISMA framework to build a simulation pipeline using personality anchoring — operationalizing personality traits by anchoring LLM agents to well-known movie characters and public figures with established trait profiles. They conducted a large-scale empirical study with 1,010 simulated dyadic conversations, manipulating Agreeableness composition (Homogeneous-Agreeable, Homogeneous-Disagreeable, and mixed) and measuring shared goal achievement. Behavioral mediation analysis and robustness checks (ICC, cross-scenario consistency) were conducted.
AI.result: A monotonic relationship emerged between dyadic Agreeableness composition and shared goal achievement: Homogeneous-Agreeable pairs achieved 62% success vs. 6% for Homogeneous-Disagreeable pairs. Cooperative strategy selection partially mediated this effect, but Agreeableness continued to predict outcomes within the same dominant strategy, suggesting pathways beyond observable conversational behavior. Robustness analyses confirmed high inter-simulation consistency (ICC=0.89) and stable personality expression across diverse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Personality anchoring is a viable operationalization strategy for multi-agent social simulation. Agreeableness composition robustly shapes interaction outcomes through both behavioral (strategy selection) and extra-behavioral pathways. The pipeline enables systematic, scalable investigation of person-situation interactions that are difficult to study experimentally in humans.

title: MADRAG: Multi-Agent Debate with Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Training-Free Analytic Essay Scoring
summary: We present MADRAG, a training-free framework for analytic essay scoring that combines multi-agent reasoning with retrieval-augmented grounding. Unlike standard LLM-as-judge approaches, which are prone to bias and unstable scoring, MADRAG decomposes evaluation into an interactive process: an Advocate identifies strengths, a Skeptic critiques weaknesses, and a Judge aggregates their arguments into a final score. Crucially, the Judge is augmented with rubric-aligned exemplar retrieval, enabling calibration through comparison with scored examples. Our results show that MADRAG significantly outperforms prompt-based baselines while approaching the performance of supervised systems without requiring task-specific training. Ablation studies demonstrate that retrieval drives calibration gains, while debate improves reasoning on higher-level traits. Our findings highlight the complementary roles of structured interaction and external memory in reliable LLM-based evaluation.

AI.tldr: MADRAG is a training-free framework for analytic essay scoring that uses a multi-agent debate (Advocate, Skeptic, Judge) augmented with retrieval of rubric-aligned exemplars to produce calibrated, reliable scores without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM-as-judge approaches for essay scoring suffer from bias and unstable scoring. The authors aim to address these reliability issues without requiring supervised training, making the approach more practical and accessible.
AI.method: MADRAG employs three interacting agents: an Advocate that identifies essay strengths, a Skeptic that critiques weaknesses, and a Judge that synthesizes arguments into a final score. The Judge is augmented with retrieval of rubric-aligned exemplars from an external memory, enabling calibration through comparison with previously scored examples. The framework is entirely training-free.
AI.result: MADRAG significantly outperforms prompt-based baselines and approaches the performance of supervised systems. Ablation studies show that retrieval drives calibration gains, while the debate mechanism improves reasoning on higher-level scoring traits.
AI.conclusion: Structured multi-agent interaction and external memory (retrieval) play complementary roles in enabling reliable LLM-based evaluation. The combination of debate for nuanced reasoning and exemplar retrieval for calibration offers a practical path toward robust automated essay scoring without task-specific training.

title: Architecturally Significant MLOps Guidelines for ML Model Integration and Deployment: a Gray Literature Review
summary: Context. Despite the growing adoption of Machine Learning Operations (MLOps), teams often approach MLOps projects in an ad hoc manner due to the lack of consolidated architectural guidance. The community would benefit from a reference that synthesizes knowledge to inform the architectural design of MLOps systems, especially regarding the integration and deployment of ML models. Objective. In response, our goal is to provide a comprehensive overview of architecturally significant guidelines for the integration and deployment of ML models in MLOps systems. Method. We conduct a gray literature review of 103 web sources to analyze state-of-practice knowledge on MLOps model integration and deployment. We then apply thematic analysis to synthesize these practices into recommended guidelines. Results. We contribute a collection of 25 architecturally significant MLOps guidelines for model integration and deployment, organized into five categories, and describe their impact on the overall system architecture. Conclusion. Our results serve as an overview of state-of-practice MLOps guidelines to support researchers and practitioners with the integration and deployment of ML models in their MLOps systems.

AI.tldr: A gray literature review of 103 web sources yields 25 architecturally significant guidelines for integrating and deploying ML models in MLOps systems, organized into five categories, addressing the current lack of consolidated architectural guidance.
AI.motivation: MLOps adoption is growing, but teams lack consolidated architectural guidance for designing MLOps systems, particularly for model integration and deployment. The community needs a synthesized reference to inform architectural decisions.
AI.method: The authors conducted a gray literature review of 103 web sources to capture state-of-practice knowledge. They then applied thematic analysis to synthesize the extracted practices into architecturally significant guidelines.
AI.result: A collection of 25 architecturally significant MLOps guidelines for model integration and deployment, organized into five categories, each with described impact on overall system architecture.
AI.conclusion: The synthesized guidelines provide researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive, practice-informed reference for integrating and deploying ML models within MLOps systems.

title: Enhancing Malware Detection with Generative AI: Using Variational Autoencoders to Boost Machine Learning Classifiers' Performance
summary:
The advancement of malware poses obstacles for cybersecurity, necessitating the development of advanced detection techniques. This paper proposes an approach to enhance malware detection through the use of a generative artificial intelligence model. Specifically, variational autoencoders (VAEs) are used with the random forest, XGBoost and sequential model machine learning classifiers. Generated synthetic malware samples are used to address the critical issue of data scarcity for new or less common malware types. This approach can be used to augment datasets to improve classifier robustness.
  The proposed methodology uses VAEs to create high-quality diverse synthetic datasets that closely mimic real-world malware data. The effectiveness of these augmented datasets is evaluated by comparing the performance of the machine learning classifiers when they are trained with the original data and when they are trained with the synthetic data-augmented datasets. The results demonstrate a notable improvement in the accuracy, precision, recall and F1-scores of the classifiers, when they are trained using the augmented datasets. The enhanced performance for detecting various malware classes shows the potential of this approach to facilitate adaptation to evolving malware threats effectively. This work demonstrates the utility of generative AI for cybersecurity. It also provides a foundation for future research aimed at developing more resilient and adaptive malware detection systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) to generate synthetic malware samples for data augmentation, improving the performance of classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost, Sequential Model) in malware detection, particularly for underrepresented malware classes.
AI.motivation: Malware evolution poses significant cybersecurity challenges, and detection systems suffer from data scarcity for new or rare malware types. Traditional classifiers lack robustness when training data is limited, necessitating methods to augment datasets with realistic synthetic samples.
AI.method: The methodology employs Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) to generate high-quality, diverse synthetic malware samples that mimic real-world data. These synthetic samples are used to augment original training datasets. Three classifiers — Random Forest, XGBoost, and a Sequential neural network model — are trained on both original and augmented datasets, and their detection performance is compared using standard metrics.
AI.result: Training classifiers on the VAE-augmented datasets yields notable improvements in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score compared to training on original data alone. The enhanced performance is consistent across multiple malware classes, demonstrating improved generalization.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI, specifically VAEs, proves effective for augmenting malware detection datasets, enabling more robust and adaptive detection systems. The work lays a foundation for future research into resilient, AI-driven cybersecurity defenses.

title: Mind the Gap: Bridging Behavioral Silos with LLMs in Multi-Vertical Recommendations
summary: In multi-vertical e-commerce platforms like DoorDash, relatively newer product verticals such as grocery and retail present a significant opportunity for personalization innovation. A key challenge lies in solving the "cold start" problem for users. This paper introduces a novel framework for enhancing recommendation quality by transferring knowledge from data-rich verticals (e.g., restaurants at DoorDash) to data-sparse ones. We leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform generative inference, synthesizing sparse, high-dimensional features that encapsulate latent user affinities. Specifically, we employ a hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline to derive multi-level taxonomic features from user restaurant order histories and search queries. These generated features, encoding both long-term cross-vertical preferences and short-term intent, are integrated into a production Multi-Task Learning (MTL) ranking model. We demonstrate through extensive offline and online evaluation that this approach significantly improves personalization and engagement in emerging business verticals, effectively bridging the behavioral data gap.

AI.tldr: Presents a framework that leverages LLMs with hierarchical RAG to transfer knowledge from data-rich verticals (restaurants) to data-sparse ones (grocery/retail) on DoorDash, generating high-dimensional taxonomic features that capture latent user preferences and improve cold-start personalization in a production MTL ranking model.
AI.motivation: Newer product verticals (grocery, retail) on multi-vertical e-commerce platforms like DoorDash suffer from the "cold start" problem due to sparse user behavioral data, limiting personalization quality. Transferring knowledge from data-rich verticals like restaurants can bridge this gap.
AI.method: A hierarchical Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline powered by LLMs performs generative inference over user restaurant order histories and search queries. This synthesizes multi-level taxonomic features encoding long-term cross-vertical preferences and short-term intent. These generated features are integrated into a production Multi-Task Learning (MTL) ranking model.
AI.result: Extensive offline and online evaluation demonstrates significant improvements in personalization quality and user engagement in emerging verticals, effectively mitigating the behavioral data sparsity problem.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven generative feature synthesis via hierarchical RAG is an effective approach for cross-vertical knowledge transfer, enabling robust personalization in data-sparse verticals without requiring dense in-domain behavioral data.

title: Improving Cross-Lingual Factual Recall via Consistency-Driven Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained predominantly on English data encode substantial world knowledge, yet often fail to express it reliably in other languages, a phenomenon known as cross-lingual factual inconsistency. To study and address this, we introduce PolyFact, a large-scale parallel multilingual factual QA dataset containing 100K Wikidata-grounded facts across 12 typologically diverse languages. Using PolyFact, we compare light continual pretraining (CPT), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for improving cross-lingual factual recall in Qwen-2.5-7B and OLMo-2-1124-7B. We find that GRPO consistently outperforms SFT, improving both cross-lingual consistency and generalization to unseen languages, while CPT on parallel data yields limited additional gains. Mechanistic analyses further show that GRPO reorganizes multilingual routing by reducing language specialization in MLP layers and attention heads, thereby promoting more shared cross-lingual representations. We release our code, models, and dataset.

AI.tldr: Introduces PolyFact, a 100K multilingual factual QA dataset (12 languages). Compares CPT, SFT, and GRPO for fixing cross-lingual factual inconsistency in LLMs. Finds GRPO best improves consistency and generalization by reorganizing multilingual routing toward shared representations.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained predominantly on English data possess substantial world knowledge but exhibit cross-lingual factual inconsistency — they fail to express that knowledge reliably in other languages. No large-scale parallel multilingual factual QA benchmark existed to systematically study and mitigate this problem.
AI.method: (1) PolyFact dataset: 100K Wikidata-grounded factual QA pairs across 12 typologically diverse languages. (2) Three training strategies compared on Qwen-2.5-7B and OLMo-2-1124-7B: light continual pretraining (CPT) on parallel data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). (3) Mechanistic analysis of how training affects multilingual routing in MLP layers and attention heads.
AI.result: GRPO consistently outperforms SFT on cross-lingual consistency and generalization to unseen languages. CPT on parallel data yields only limited additional gains. Mechanistically, GRPO reduces language specialization in MLP layers and attention heads, promoting more shared cross-lingual representations.
AI.conclusion: GRPO is an effective strategy for mitigating cross-lingual factual inconsistency by reorganizing internal multilingual routing toward language-agnostic representations. The released PolyFact dataset, models, and code support further research in multilingual factual alignment.

title: Elmes*: Automated Construction of Fine-Grained Evaluation Rubrics for Large Language Models in Long-Tail Educational Scenarios
summary: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) for education requires measuring how models teach, not only what they know. Existing benchmarks emphasize domain-general correctness or depend on manually designed rubrics that scale poorly to long-tail pedagogical scenarios. We introduce Elmes*, an end-to-end framework for constructing, refining, and applying fine-grained scenario-specific rubrics. Elmes* combines a declarative multi-agent engine for teacher--student--judge interactions with SceneGen, a self-evolving module that co-optimizes evaluation criteria and test data from expert-defined pedagogical dimensions. Using Elmes*, we build Edu-330, covering 330 scenarios across 11 subjects, 3 grade bands, and 10 task types, with over 1{,}000 second-level indicators. Experiments on Edu-330 and four expert-authored gold-standard scenarios show that educational capability is multidimensional: top-tier LLMs differ mainly in creativity and values integration, knowledge-strong models may fail at Socratic scaffolding, and the education-specialized InnoSpark achieves the best human-evaluated average score. LLM judges preserve human-comparable rankings with much lower scoring variance, but exhibit judge-specific biases such as self-preference. Ablations show that expert-scored few-shot anchoring improves human--LLM alignment, while reasoning enforcement and greedy decoding are model-dependent. Elmes* thus provides scalable diagnostic infrastructure for pedagogically grounded LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: Elmes* is an end-to-end framework for evaluating LLMs' pedagogical capabilities using fine-grained, scenario-specific rubrics co-optimized with test data. It produces Edu-330 (330 scenarios across 11 subjects), revealing that educational capability is multidimensional—top LLMs diverge on creativity, values integration, and Socratic scaffolding—while LLM judges approximate human rankings but exhibit self-preference bias.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks for education focus on domain correctness or rely on manually designed rubrics that don't scale to diverse, long-tail pedagogical scenarios. There is a need for scalable, fine-grained evaluation that measures how models teach—capturing pedagogical dimensions like scaffolding, creativity, and values integration—rather than merely what they know.
AI.method: The authors introduce Elmes*, comprising: (1) a declarative multi-agent engine orchestrating teacher–student–judge interactions, and (2) SceneGen, a self-evolving module that co-optimizes evaluation criteria and test data from expert-defined pedagogical dimensions. Using this framework, they construct Edu-330, a benchmark with 330 scenarios spanning 11 subjects, 3 grade bands, and 10 task types, featuring over 1,000 second-level indicators. They evaluate LLMs on Edu-330 and four expert-authored gold-standard scenarios, comparing LLM judges against human evaluators, and conduct ablations on few-shot anchoring, reasoning enforcement, and greedy decoding.
AI.result: Educational capability is multidimensional: top-tier LLMs differ mainly in creativity and values integration; knowledge-strong models may fail at Socratic scaffolding; the education-specialized model InnoSpark achieves the best human-evaluated average score. LLM judges preserve human-comparable rankings with lower scoring variance but show judge-specific biases such as self-preference. Expert-scored few-shot anchoring improves human–LLM alignment, while reasoning enforcement and greedy decoding effects are model-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Elmes* provides scalable, diagnostic infrastructure for pedagogically grounded LLM evaluation, moving beyond correctness to assess teaching quality across multiple dimensions. The Edu-330 benchmark and the framework's modular design enable systematic identification of model-specific strengths and weaknesses in educational contexts.

title: Detecting and Mitigating Bias by Treating Fairness as a Symmetry Operation
summary: Machine learning systems deployed in high stakes socioeconomic settings routinely display bias. We formalize bias as a symmetry breaking operation: a classifier is fair if its outputs remain invariant under the counterfactual operation of switching a sensitive attribute, with merit features held fixed. We implement loss based regularization as a symmetry restoring mechanism and evaluate the framework on four synthetic datasets with varying levels of noise, correlation, and bias. The framework achieves upwards of 90\% violation reduction, with accuracy costs around 5\%. This framework does not require causal graph knowledge, is computationally lightweight, and generalizes to any sensitive attribute definable as a bit-flip, making it suitable for contexts where local sources of discrimination remain absent from mainstream benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A fairness framework is proposed that treats bias as a symmetry violation: a classifier is fair if its output is invariant to flipping a sensitive attribute while holding merit features fixed. Loss-based regularization restores this symmetry, achieving over 90% bias reduction at roughly 5% accuracy cost, without requiring causal graphs.
AI.motivation: Machine learning classifiers in high-stakes socioeconomic applications routinely exhibit bias. Existing fairness methods often require causal graph knowledge or are tied to specific bias benchmarks, limiting their applicability in settings where local discrimination sources are not captured by mainstream datasets.
AI.method: The authors formalize bias as a symmetry-breaking operation, defining fairness as counterfactual invariance: outputs should be unchanged when a sensitive attribute is flipped while merit features remain fixed. They implement loss-based regularization as a symmetry-restoring mechanism. The framework requires no causal graph knowledge, treats sensitive attributes as bit-flips, and is computationally lightweight.
AI.result: Evaluated on four synthetic datasets with varied noise, correlation, and bias levels, the framework achieves upwards of 90% violation reduction with accuracy costs around 5%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed symmetry-based fairness framework is effective, lightweight, and broadly generalizable, making it well-suited for real-world deployment contexts where local discrimination sources may be absent from standard fairness benchmarks.

title: CANote: Empowering Fact-checking Note Writing Through Scaffolded and Provenance-based Human-AI Collaboration
summary: Crowdsourced fact-checking mechanisms, such as X's Community Notes, play a critical role in mitigating the spread of misinformation. However, drafting high-quality, evidence-based debunking notes imposes a substantial burden on contributors. We present CANote, an AI-assisted debunking note writing system featuring evidence correlation and structured co-drafting. CANote scaffolds the workflow by extracting subclaims from social media posts, providing provenance through explicit links between subclaims and retrieved evidence, and generating neutral, structural drafts to support human reasoning. We evaluated CANote against manual writing (N=52 fact-checkers, N=52 lay users) on simulated X platform, where we found CANote significantly improves note quality. Notably, CANote enables lay users to write notes that have comparable quality to those written by experts. While the task completion time and perceived cognitive load remain comparable to manual drafting, CANote significantly increases user satisfaction. However, this assistance introduces a trade-off, resulting in a reduced sense of user ownership and control over the debunking note.

AI.tldr: CANote is an AI-assisted system for writing Community Notes-style debunking notes that extracts subclaims, links them to evidence (provenance), and generates structured drafts. It improves note quality, enabling lay users to match expert-level output, increases satisfaction, but reduces perceived ownership.
AI.motivation: Writing high-quality, evidence-based debunking notes for crowdsourced fact-checking (e.g., X's Community Notes) imposes a heavy burden on contributors, limiting scalability and quality. There is a need for AI assistance that scaffolds the process without undermining human reasoning.
AI.method: The authors built CANote, which scaffolds three stages: (1) extracting subclaims from social media posts, (2) linking subclaims to retrieved evidence with explicit provenance, and (3) generating neutral, structured drafts. Evaluation: between-subjects study (N=52 fact-checkers, N=52 lay users) on a simulated X platform, comparing CANote-assisted writing vs. manual writing, measuring note quality, task time, cognitive load, satisfaction, and sense of ownership.
AI.result: CANote significantly improves note quality. Lay users with CANote produce notes comparable in quality to experts writing manually. Task completion time and perceived cognitive load remain similar to manual drafting. User satisfaction significantly increases. However, CANote reduces users' sense of ownership and control over the final note.
AI.conclusion: AI-assisted debunking with structured co-drafting and evidence provenance can democratize high-quality fact-checking by elevating lay users to near-expert performance, but designers must address the trade-off between assistance and user agency/ownership.

title: Modeling U.S. Attitudes Toward China via an Event-Steered Multi-Agent Simulator
summary: Understanding the dynamic evolution of opinions, such as U.S. public attitudes toward China, is essential for assessing geopolitical risks. However, existing LLM-based multiagent simulators predominantly rely on static rules and fixed datasets, limiting their ability to capture the dynamic, event-driven nature of macro-level opinion shifts in real-world settings. To address this limitation, we propose an Event-Steered Multi-Agent Simulator (ES-MAS), in which significant events and daily news continuously drive opinion evolution through dynamic interactions among agents. We first construct the China-U.S. Relation Evolution (CURE) dataset, covering 20 quarters from 2021 to 2025, including 258 major events and over 14,000 daily news articles, and providing a comprehensive temporal foundation for modeling opinion dynamics. Building upon the CURE dataset, we propose a Dual-Stream Data Integration Engine (DSDIE) that aligns simulations with historical timelines via macro-level events while enabling personalized information exposure based on individual agent profiles and contextual signals. Furthermore, we design a News-Driven Dynamic Interaction (NDDI) module, which adaptively groups agents with shared news interests into localized interaction contexts, facilitating bottom-up consensus formation while mitigating the risk of isolated information cocoons. Experimental results on the CURE dataset demonstrate that ES-MAS substantially outperforms existing simulators in reproducing real-world historical trends, offering a scalable and effective framework for modeling dynamic opinion evolution.

AI.tldr: ES-MAS is an event-driven multi-agent simulator that models dynamic public opinion evolution (e.g., U.S. attitudes toward China) using real-world events and news, outperforming static rule-based simulators on the newly constructed CURE dataset spanning 2021–2025.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent simulators rely on static rules and fixed datasets, failing to capture the dynamic, event-driven nature of macro-level opinion shifts in real-world geopolitical contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose ES-MAS, comprising: (1) the CURE dataset with 258 major events and 14,000+ daily news articles across 20 quarters (2021–2025); (2) a Dual-Stream Data Integration Engine (DSDIE) that aligns simulations with historical timelines via macro-events and enables personalized information exposure; and (3) a News-Driven Dynamic Interaction (NDDI) module that adaptively groups agents by shared news interests for bottom-up consensus formation while preventing information cocoons.
AI.result: Experimental results on the CURE dataset show that ES-MAS substantially outperforms existing simulators in reproducing real-world historical opinion trends.
AI.conclusion: ES-MAS provides a scalable and effective framework for modeling dynamic opinion evolution driven by real-world events, with strong potential for geopolitical risk assessment applications.

title: Queen-Bee Agents: A BeeSpec-Centered Architecture for Governed Enterprise MCP Orchestration
comment: Technical report. Prototype-level systems evidence; 59 enterprise-style tasks
summary: Enterprise agent systems increasingly need to connect large language models to private tools, internal knowledge, and Model Context Protocol (MCP) interfaces. In this setting, raw task capability is insufficient: organizations also require policy enforcement, tenant-scoped isolation, and execution that remains within explicit operational boundaries. We present Queen-Bee, a governed multi-agent architecture in which a Queen control plane retrieves capabilities, plans task-scoped execution, and compiles a structured BeeSpec that is executed by specialized Bee agents under constrained tool access. We implement a working prototype with tenant-scoped MCP connectors, audit-backed execution-time governance, retrieval-driven weak incubation, and multiple provisioning backends. We evaluate the system on 59 enterprise-style tasks spanning governance-sensitive requests, retrieval-driven provisioning, scoped local execution, and chemistry workflow integration. The retrieval-driven Queen-Bee variant achieves a task success rate of 0.964, zero governance failures, and substantially better scoped execution quality than both a static Queen-Bee baseline and a permissive single-agent baseline. We further show a multi-Bee chemistry workflow with explicit approval gating and a concrete top-3 shortlist grounded in real upstream evidence and screening artifacts. Additional comparisons with hybrid retrieval and LLM-guided provisioning show that richer provisioning backends are viable but do not outperform the lightweight structured retriever on the current small, highly structured capability registry. The results provide prototype-level systems evidence rather than a production deployment study, and suggest that enterprise agent platforms should be evaluated not only by capability, but also by governed provisioning, isolation behavior, scoped execution quality, and artifact-aware workflow coordination.

AI.tldr: Queen-Bee is a governed multi-agent architecture where a Queen control plane compiles structured BeeSpecs for specialized Bee agents with constrained tool access, achieving 96.4% task success and zero governance failures on enterprise tasks through retrieval-driven provisioning.
AI.motivation: Enterprise LLM agent systems need more than raw capability — they require policy enforcement, tenant-scoped isolation, and explicit operational boundaries when connecting to private tools, internal knowledge, and MCP interfaces.
AI.method: Queen-Bee employs a two-tier architecture: a Queen control plane retrieves capabilities, plans task-scoped execution, and compiles a structured BeeSpec; specialized Bee agents then execute under constrained tool access. The system features tenant-scoped MCP connectors, audit-backed execution-time governance, retrieval-driven weak incubation, and multiple provisioning backends.
AI.result: The retrieval-driven variant achieved 0.964 task success rate across 59 enterprise-style tasks with zero governance failures. It outperformed both a static Queen-Bee baseline and a permissive single-agent baseline on scoped execution quality. A multi-Bee chemistry workflow demonstrated explicit approval gating with grounded top-3 shortlists. Hybrid retrieval and LLM-guided provisioning backends proved viable but did not outperform the lightweight structured retriever on the current small capability registry.
AI.conclusion: Enterprise agent platforms should be evaluated not only by capability but also by governed provisioning, isolation behavior, scoped execution quality, and artifact-aware workflow coordination. Results are prototype-level evidence rather than production deployment validation.

title: Subtle Injection for Ground-truth Inference of LLM Training Data
summary:
As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly trained on scraped web corpora without authorisation, content owners require forensic methods to prove that their documents were included in a model's training set. We propose \textbf{SIGIL} (\textbf{S}ubtle \textbf{I}njection for \textbf{G}round-truth \textbf{I}nference of \textbf{L}LM training data), a framework that embeds imperceptible \emph{canary sequences} into protected text and code such that any LLM trained on those documents exhibits statistically detectable behavioural signatures when probed with targeted queries.
  SIGIL defines five canary strategies -- lexical-rare, lexical-phrase, syntactic, semantic, and code-pattern -- and a \emph{Membership Inference Score} (MIS) grounded in the Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework with formal false-positive rate (FPR) control. Simulator parameters are calibrated against the empirical membership inference literature, yielding realistic heterogeneous results across $36{,}000$ trials: overall AUC $= 0.892$, rising from $0.831$ at $0.1\%$ injection to $0.947$ at $10\%$. Detection rates range from $33\%$ to $78\%$ across model-size and injection-rate conditions. Code Pattern canaries achieve the highest AUC ($0.903$, Cohen's $d = 1.84$); Syntactic Structure the lowest ($0.875$, $d = 1.63$). All four experimental factors -- injection rate, model size, canary strategy, and mixture ratio -- have significant independent effects on MIS ($p < 0.001$). SIGIL maintains AUC $> 0.86$ even under $100\%$ paraphrasing ($\text{AUC} = 0.864$), confirming robustness through semantic leakage that survives surface-level rewriting.

AI.tldr: SIGIL embeds imperceptible canary sequences into documents, enabling statistically detectable proof that an LLM was trained on protected content through a Membership Inference Score with formal false-positive rate control.
AI.motivation: As LLMs increasingly train on unauthorized scraped web corpora, content owners lack forensic tools to prove their documents were included in training data. SIGIL addresses this gap by providing a principled, statistically grounded framework for training data attribution.
AI.method: SIGIL proposes five canary injection strategies (lexical-rare, lexical-phrase, syntactic, semantic, code-pattern) embedded imperceptibly into protected text/code. It introduces a Membership Inference Score (MIS) based on the Neyman-Pearson hypothesis testing framework with formal FPR control. Simulator parameters are calibrated against empirical membership inference literature, evaluated across 36,000 trials varying injection rate, model size, canary strategy, and mixture ratio.
AI.result: Overall AUC = 0.892 (range: 0.831 at 0.1% injection to 0.947 at 10%). Detection rates: 33%–78% across conditions. Code-pattern canaries achieved highest AUC (0.903, Cohen's d=1.84); syntactic structure lowest (0.875, d=1.63). All four experimental factors significantly affected MIS (p<0.001). Robustness confirmed under 100% paraphrasing (AUC=0.864).
AI.conclusion: SIGIL provides a practical, robust forensic framework for proving LLM training data membership, with code-pattern canaries being most effective. The method survives paraphrasing attacks, demonstrating real-world viability for content ownership verification.

title: Towards Retrieving Interaction Spaces for Agentic Search
summary: Retrieval for search agents is still inherited from non-agentic information retrieval: a retriever ranks the corpus and the agent reads a small set of returned documents. Recent direct corpus interaction (DCI) work shows that agents can instead interact with the raw corpus through shell tools such as grep and file reads. But unbounded interaction does not scale: every broad shell command is a scan over the whole corpus, and latency degrades sharply as the corpus grows. We argue that the role of retrieval for agentic search is not just to select documents that fit in the LLM context window, but to construct an interaction space: a bounded subset of the corpus the agent can explore with associated tools. Two design consequences follow. The space needs a boundary supplied by retrieval, and the objects within it should be processed for interaction. As a proof of concept, we propose RISE (Retrieving Interaction SpacE): we use BM25 to construct the interaction space; meanwhile, its documents are processed during indexing for shell-style navigation. On BrowseComp-Plus, RISE matches the pure-shell DCI baseline at 78% accuracy with gpt-5.4-mini at roughly one quarter of the per-query cost. At 1M documents, RISE-BM25 reaches 81% on gpt-5.4-mini, whereas DCI on gpt-5.4-nano degrades to 60% with 33 of 100 wall-clock failures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RISE, a retrieval paradigm that constructs a bounded interaction space for agentic search rather than merely ranking documents. Using BM25 to define the boundary and preprocessing documents for shell-style navigation, RISE matches or exceeds pure direct corpus interaction (DCI) baselines at significantly lower cost and better scalability.
AI.motivation: Current retrieval for search agents inherits a static, non-agentic paradigm where documents are ranked and read linearly. Direct Corpus Interaction (DCI) allows richer exploration via shell tools but scales poorly because broad shell commands scan the entire corpus, causing latency to degrade sharply as corpus size grows. There is a need for a middle ground that enables bounded, efficient agentic exploration.
AI.method: RISE (Retrieving Interaction SpacE) constructs a bounded interaction space using two key design principles: (1) retrieval (BM25) supplies a boundary on the subset of the corpus the agent can interact with, and (2) documents within this space are preprocessed during indexing to enable shell-style navigation commands. This contrasts with both traditional retrieve-then-read pipelines and unbounded DCI.
AI.result: On BrowseComp-Plus, RISE achieves 78% accuracy with gpt-5.4-mini, matching the pure-shell DCI baseline at roughly 25% of the per-query cost. At a corpus scale of 1M documents, RISE-BM25 reaches 81% accuracy on gpt-5.4-mini, while DCI on gpt-5.4-nano degrades to only 60% and suffers 33 out of 100 wall-clock failures.
AI.conclusion: The role of retrieval for agentic search should shift from selecting documents to fit the LLM context window toward constructing an interaction space — a bounded, tool-accessible corpus subset. RISE demonstrates that this approach preserves accuracy while dramatically improving scalability and cost-efficiency compared to unbounded DCI.

title: Re-Centering Humans in LLM Personalization
summary: Despite growing interest, most evaluations of large language models' (LLMs') personalization abilities have relied on synthetic data. It remains unclear how well current personalization systems work for real users. In this paper, we study the gap in LLM personalization performance when using synthetic versus human data. We collect human conversations (550 conversations) and judgments across three stages of personalization: extracting user attributes from conversations (5,949 judgments), pairing relevant attributes with new prompts (11,919), and incorporating relevant attributes into a personalized response (1,101). Incorporating human data reveals system limitations at each stage. Models struggle to extract attributes from human conversations, disagree with human judgments on relevant attributes, and generate personalized responses that humans judge no better than generic responses (though that LLM judges widely rate as better). We introduce two lightweight training-based interventions that shift automated personalization evaluation closer to human data in our first two stages. However, in our third stage we find that learned reward models achieve only modest correlation with human ratings, suggesting that human-aligned personalization quality judgments are difficult to model directly. Our collected data provides a foundation for studying how models should extract, select, and incorporate user information in ways that humans find useful.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the gap between synthetic and human data in evaluating LLM personalization, revealing that models underperform on real user data across three stages: attribute extraction, attribute-prompt pairing, and personalized response generation. The authors introduce training interventions to improve alignment in the first two stages but find that modeling human-aligned quality judgments remains difficult.
AI.motivation: Most LLM personalization evaluations rely on synthetic data, leaving unclear how well these systems actually perform for real users. The authors aim to quantify and understand the performance gap between synthetic and human-based personalization across the full pipeline.
AI.method: The authors collect a dataset of 550 human conversations with annotations across three personalization stages: (1) 5,949 judgments for attribute extraction, (2) 11,919 judgments for attribute-prompt relevance pairing, and (3) 1,101 judgments for personalized response quality. They benchmark LLM performance at each stage, comparing synthetic vs. human data, and introduce two lightweight training-based interventions to better align automated evaluation with human judgments in stages 1 and 2. For stage 3, they test learned reward models against human ratings.
AI.result: Models struggle to extract attributes from human conversations, disagree with human judgments on relevant attribute selection, and produce personalized responses that humans rate no better than generic ones — despite LLM judges consistently rating them as superior. The training interventions improve alignment in stages 1 and 2, but learned reward models achieve only modest correlation with human ratings in stage 3.
AI.conclusion: There exists a significant gap between synthetic and human-based evaluation of LLM personalization. While lightweight interventions can improve automated evaluation in earlier pipeline stages, directly modeling human-aligned quality judgments for personalized responses remains an open challenge. The collected dataset serves as a benchmark for future work on human-useful personalization.

title: FAIR-Calib: Frontier-Aware Instability-Reweighted Calibration for Post-Training Quantization of Diffusion Large Language Models
comment: Accepted as a poster at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) refine tokens iteratively but commit them irreversibly, leading to a "stability lag" where early decisions remain fragile even after being written. We reveal that Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) error easily flips these borderline decisions at the write frontier, which are then permanently locked in and amplified. To address this, we propose Frontier-Aware Instability-Reweighted Calibration (FAIR-Calib), a two-stage PTQ framework for dLLMs. Stage I probes a full-precision teacher to estimate a position prior that combines frontier hits and masked-stage reliability. Stage II performs off-policy, layer-wise calibration by minimizing a reweighted hidden-state MSE, effectively prioritizing the protection of fragile frontier states without requiring expensive end-to-end diffusion rollouts. We further theoretically justify our weighted objective as a surrogate for output KL divergence. Empirically, FAIR-Calib consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on LLaDA and Dream (W4A4), significantly reducing frontier decision flips and suppressing post-commit mismatches across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: dLLMs suffer from irreversible token commitment at the "write frontier," where PTQ-induced errors flip fragile early decisions. FAIR-Calib addresses this via a two-stage framework using a teacher-derived position prior and reweighted hidden-state MSE calibration, theoretically justified as a surrogate for output KL divergence, achieving SOTA on LLaDA and Dream at W4A4.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs generate tokens iteratively and commit them irreversibly, creating a "stability lag": early decisions at the write frontier are borderline and fragile. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) error easily flips these decisions, after which they become permanently locked in and amplified downstream. Existing PTQ methods do not account for this frontier-specific vulnerability, motivating a calibration strategy that explicitly protects these fragile states.
AI.method: FAIR-Calib is a two-stage PTQ framework. Stage I probes a full-precision teacher to estimate a position prior that combines two factors: (a) frontier hits — how often a position is at the write frontier, and (b) masked-stage reliability — how stable the token distribution is during early masked diffusion steps. Stage II performs off-policy, layer-wise calibration by minimizing a reweighted hidden-state MSE loss, where the weights are derived from the Stage I prior. This prioritizes protecting fragile frontier states without requiring expensive end-to-end diffusion rollouts. The weighted MSE objective is theoretically justified as a surrogate for minimizing output KL divergence between the quantized and full-precision model.
AI.result: FAIR-Calib consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PTQ baselines on LLaDA and Dream diffusion LLMs at W4A4 precision. The method significantly reduces frontier decision flips and suppresses post-commit mismatch errors across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The "stability lag" and irreversible commitment in dLLMs pose a unique challenge for PTQ that prior methods overlook. FAIR-Calib demonstrates that explicitly identifying and protecting the write frontier via a reweighted calibration objective is both theoretically grounded and empirically effective, establishing a new SOTA for quantizing diffusion language models.

title: DiBS: Diffusion-Informed Branch Selection
summary: Sudoku is a representative constraint satisfaction problem that requires global structural reasoning under strict discrete constraints. The existing works of solving Sudoku mainly focus on two dominant approaches, i.e., traditional heuristic and deep learning solver. However, they suffer from two complementary limitations: learning-based solvers lack hard correctness guarantees, while complete symbolic solvers are still prone to long-tail search. To address these shortcomings, we propose a novel diffusion model-guided approach, termed as DiBS, for the branch selection search process. Specifically, DiBS keeps the symbolic solver complete and uses the diffusion model as a branch-ordering guide. The core method is ranking candidate values under the current partial assignment and lightweight consistency signal. Furthermore, we provide an in-depth theoretical proof to reveal how it works and why it works. Experiments on the challenging Royle 17-clue Sudoku benchmark show that our DiBS substantially reduces search cost relative to strong heuristic baselines, especially in nodes, backtracks, and long-tail percentiles. Besides, these results confirm that learned global guidance is effective on hard instances where branch-order mistakes are most expensive. All codes are available at https://github.com/shanxierdan/DiBS.

AI.tldr: DiBS proposes a diffusion model-guided branch selection method for solving hard Sudoku instances. It combines a complete symbolic solver with a learned diffusion model that ranks candidate values during search, providing hard correctness guarantees while reducing long-tail search costs.
AI.motivation: Existing Sudoku solvers fall into two categories: deep learning solvers lack correctness guarantees, and heuristic symbolic solvers suffer from long-tail search inefficiency on hard instances. The goal is to combine the completeness of symbolic solvers with learned global guidance to improve branch-ordering decisions where mistakes are most costly.
AI.method: DiBS uses a diffusion model as a branch-ordering guide within a complete symbolic search framework. The diffusion model ranks candidate values given the current partial assignment and lightweight consistency signals, directing the search to promising branches first. The paper also provides theoretical analysis explaining the mechanism.
AI.result: On the Royle 17-clue Sudoku benchmark, DiBS substantially reduces search cost compared to strong heuristic baselines, particularly in nodes explored, backtracks, and long-tail percentiles, demonstrating that learned global guidance is most impactful on hard instances.
AI.conclusion: DiBS successfully bridges the gap between learning-based and symbolic solvers, achieving both completeness (hard correctness guarantees) and efficiency (reduced long-tail search). The results confirm that diffusion model-guided branch ordering is an effective strategy for hard constraint satisfaction problems.

title: Moodie: An Early-Stage Design Exploration for Supporting Fear of Missing Out with LLM-based Chatbots
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure, 1 table. Preliminary work submitted to the ACM CUI 2026 Works-in-Progress (WiP) track
summary: The excessive use of social media has led to the challenge known as Fear of Missing Out (FoMO). Existing studies fail to provide accessible, interactive tools that focus on the emotional and cognitive aspects of FoMO. This work presents Moodie, a chatbot designed using Large Language Models to support emotion regulation and reduce FoMO. We conducted a formative study to understand the needs of individuals with FoMO and developed Moodie. Then, we conducted a preliminary evaluative study (N=21) to observe how participants interact with Moodie and a baseline chatbot (GPT-4o) over one week. The results show that while both Moodie and a baseline chatbot reduced FoMO to a similar extent, Moodie resulted in greater engagement and social connection. This finding raises interesting questions about the advantages of purpose-built chatbots compared to general-purpose models for mental health support. Future research will include chat log analysis, prototype refinements, and longitudinal evaluations.

AI.tldr: Moodie is a purpose-built LLM chatbot for emotion regulation targeting FoMO. A comparative study (N=21) showed Moodie matched GPT-4o in reducing FoMO but yielded significantly higher engagement and social connection, suggesting domain-specific design advantages for mental health chatbots.
AI.motivation: Excessive social media use has given rise to Fear of Missing Out (FoMO), yet existing tools lack accessible, interactive solutions addressing the emotional and cognitive dimensions of FoMO. There is a gap in purpose-built conversational agents tailored specifically for FoMO-related psychological support.
AI.method: A two-phase approach: (1) a formative study to identify the needs of individuals experiencing FoMO, informing the design of Moodie, an LLM-based chatbot; (2) a one-week preliminary evaluative study (N=21) comparing Moodie against a GPT-4o baseline, measuring FoMO reduction, engagement, and social connection.
AI.result: Both Moodie and the GPT-4o baseline reduced FoMO to a comparable degree. However, Moodie outperformed the baseline on engagement and social connection metrics, indicating that domain-specific design confers advantages beyond what a general-purpose LLM can offer.
AI.conclusion: Purpose-built chatbots like Moodie may offer unique benefits for mental health support — particularly in fostering engagement and social connection — that general-purpose models do not replicate. Future work will focus on chat log analysis, iterative prototype refinement, and longitudinal evaluations.

title: Learning Multi-Agent Communication Protocol: Study on Information Entropy Efficiency in MARL
summary: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) have emerged as a fundamental paradigm for distributed problem-solving, where autonomous agents collaborate to achieve complex objectives. Within this framework, Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with communication has demonstrated remarkable success in cooperative tasks. However, existing approaches predominantly pursue performance gains through increasingly complex architectures and expanding communication overhead, lacking principled metrics to evaluate the efficiency of information exchange. In this paper, we focus on enabling agents to learn efficient multi-agent communication protocols that balance performance and information compactness. We propose the Information Entropy Efficiency Index (IEI), a novel metric that quantifies the ratio between message entropy and task performance in learned communication protocols. A lower IEI indicates more compact and efficient message representations. By incorporating IEI into training loss functions, we encourage agents to develop communication protocols that achieve high performance with improved communication efficiency. Extensive experiments across diverse MARL algorithms demonstrate that our approach achieves equivalent or superior task performance compared to baseline methods while improving communication efficiency. These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that performance improvements require complex architectures or increased communication overhead and highlight the potential of improving both task success and communication efficiency to enable scalable MAS.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Information Entropy Efficiency Index (IEI), a metric that quantifies the ratio of message entropy to task performance in MARL communication. By incorporating IEI into the training loss, agents learn compact, efficient communication protocols that match or exceed baseline performance without complex architectures or high communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing MARL communication methods chase performance via increasingly complex architectures and excessive communication, lacking principled metrics to evaluate information exchange efficiency. There is a need for a systematic approach to balance task performance with communication compactness.
AI.method: Introduces the Information Entropy Efficiency Index (IEI), defined as the ratio of message entropy to task performance. A lower IEI signifies more compact, efficient communication. IEI is integrated as a regularization term into standard MARL training loss functions, incentivizing agents to learn protocols that maximize performance while minimizing redundant information in messages.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse MARL algorithms show the IEI-driven approach achieves equivalent or superior task performance compared to baselines while significantly improving communication efficiency, challenging the assumption that higher performance demands greater communication complexity.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that principled information-theoretic regularization can simultaneously improve task success and communication efficiency, paving the way for more scalable multi-agent systems without sacrificing performance.

title: AI-Driven Test Case Generation from Natural Language Requirements: A Survey of Techniques and Research Gaps
summary: Software testing is critical for verifying that systems meet specified requirements, yet remains among the most time-consuming and expensive activities in development. Requirements-based test generation allows test cases to be derived early from requirements artifacts, but generating them directly from natural language is challenging due to inherent ambiguity and imprecision. Recent advances in AI, natural language processing (NLP), and large language models (LLMs) have made automating this pipeline increasingly feasible, while introducing new risks including hallucination, reduced traceability, and inconsistent evaluation. This survey addresses four research questions: what AI and NLP techniques have been proposed for generating test cases from natural language requirements; what tools and frameworks support these approaches; how generated test cases are evaluated; and what research gaps remain. Following Kitchenham and Charters' systematic review guidelines, we searched major scholarly databases spanning 2000-2025 and, after applying strict inclusion criteria, identified 21 primary studies. The literature is organized into three evolutionary eras, revealing that no existing approach simultaneously satisfies six key quality dimensions: automation, ambiguity handling, domain applicability, traceability, evaluation thoroughness, and hallucination control. The survey makes three main contributions: a three-era evolutionary synthesis of AI-based test generation; a six-criteria gap analysis showing no current approach fully addresses all quality dimensions; and four actionable research guidelines targeting hallucination, traceability, complexity sensitivity, and compliance.

AI.tldr: A systematic survey of AI/NLP/LLM-based test case generation from natural language requirements (2000–2025) covering 21 studies, revealing that no approach meets all six key quality dimensions simultaneously, and proposing four future research directions.
AI.motivation: Software testing is expensive and time-consuming. Generating test cases from natural language (NL) requirements promises early test derivation, but NL ambiguity and imprecision make automation difficult. Recent AI/LLM advances make this pipeline more feasible, yet introduce risks such as hallucination, poor traceability, and inconsistent evaluation, motivating a systematic survey of the field.
AI.method: Systematic literature review following Kitchenham and Charters' guidelines. The authors searched major scholarly databases spanning 2000–2025, applied strict inclusion criteria, and identified 21 primary studies. They organized the literature into three evolutionary eras and conducted a gap analysis using six quality dimensions.
AI.result: No existing approach simultaneously satisfies all six quality dimensions: automation, ambiguity handling, domain applicability, traceability, evaluation thoroughness, and hallucination control. The literature was synthesized into three evolutionary eras of AI-based test generation.
AI.conclusion: The survey contributes (1) a three-era evolutionary synthesis, (2) a six-criteria gap analysis showing no approach fully addresses all dimensions, and (3) four actionable research guidelines targeting hallucination, traceability, complexity sensitivity, and compliance.

title: MalTree: Tracing Malware Evolution from Embeddings at Scale
summary: Malware detection remains largely reactive: machine learning models trained on known samples degrade as threats evolve. Understanding evolutionary relationships among malware families can inform proactive defense, but traditional reverse engineering can take months to years to uncover such lineage relationships. We propose MalTree, a framework that applies bioinformatics inspired phylogenetic techniques (UPGMA and Neighbor-Joining) at scale to model malware evolution automatically using structural, behavioral, and image-based features. We introduce temporal validation using VirusTotal timestamps to assess whether inferred trees reflect actual evolutionary order. MalTree achieves 87% temporal consistency, indicating that inferred evolutionary relationships closely align with real-world emergence timelines. Our analysis shows that some families mutate over 10 times faster than others, suggesting that detection strategies should be tailored to family-specific evolutionary tempos. Case studies, including the Mirai botnet, confirm that inferred relationships from our phylogenetic tree align with documented threat intelligence. Our framework provides a foundation for shifting malware analysis from sample-by-sample classification toward lineage-aware evolutionary modeling.

AI.tldr: MalTree introduces a phylogenetic framework inspired by bioinformatics (UPGMA, Neighbor-Joining) to automatically model malware evolution at scale using structural, behavioral, and image-based features. It achieves 87% temporal consistency validated via VirusTotal timestamps and reveals family-specific mutation rates, enabling lineage-aware proactive defense.
AI.motivation: Malware detection is predominantly reactive, with ML models degrading as threats evolve. Understanding evolutionary lineage among malware families could enable proactive defense, but traditional reverse engineering is prohibitively slow (months to years). There is a need for automated, scalable methods to infer malware evolutionary relationships.
AI.method: The authors propose MalTree, which applies two phylogenetic algorithms—UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean) and Neighbor-Joining—to malware samples. Features are extracted across three modalities: structural (e.g., PE header metadata), behavioral (dynamic analysis traces), and image-based (binarized byteplot representations). Temporal validation is performed using first-seen timestamps from VirusTotal to compute a temporal consistency metric, measuring how well inferred tree branching order aligns with real-world emergence chronology. Mutation rate estimation quantifies family-specific evolutionary tempos.
AI.result: MalTree achieves 87% temporal consistency, demonstrating that phylogenetically inferred relationships closely match actual malware emergence timelines. Analysis reveals significant heterogeneity in evolutionary rates: some malware families mutate over 10× faster than others. A case study on the Mirai botnet confirms that MalTree's inferred phylogenetic relationships align with documented threat intelligence reports.
AI.conclusion: MalTree demonstrates the feasibility of automated, lineage-aware malware evolution modeling at scale. The framework supports shifting security paradigms from reactive, sample-by-sample classification toward proactive, evolution-informed defense. Family-specific mutation rates suggest that tailored, tempo-aware detection strategies may outperform uniform approaches.

title: DREAM: Dynamic Refinement of Early Assignment Mappings
summary: Generative recommendation advances item retrieval by reformulating it as autoregressive generation of Semantic IDs (SIDs), compact token sequences that encode item semantics. While SIDs offer a strong semantic prior, current SID-based methods assign each item a single static identifier through offline tokenization before sufficient user feedback is observed. For cold-start items, this one-shot commitment produces poorly discriminative codes, generating misaligned paths that remain unrefined because the associated tokens are rarely sampled during training. We identify this early static commitment, not model capacity, as the fundamental cold-start bottleneck in SID-based generative recommendation. To overcome this bottleneck and bridge the disjoint objectives of tokenization and generation, we propose DREAM (Dynamic Refinement of Early Assignment Mappings), a three-stage framework that resolves this flaw through progressive refinement. First, an intent-aware tokenizer rebuilds the SID space through counterfactual contrastive learning, generating a diverse pool of behavior-aligned candidates per cold-start item. Second, the frozen recommendation backbone serves as an evaluator, selecting the most reliable candidate based on multi-context user support without retraining. Third, a dynamic beam mechanism maintains multiple weighted SID hypotheses throughout training and inference, preventing premature collapse to a single assignment. Extensive experiments on three Amazon benchmarks show that DREAM substantially outperforms state-of-the-art generative and sequential baselines on cold-start metrics.

AI.tldr: DREAM addresses the cold-start bottleneck in SID-based generative recommendation caused by premature static SID assignment, using a three-stage framework with intent-aware tokenization, frozen model evaluation, and dynamic beam search to progressively refine Semantic IDs.
AI.motivation: Current SID-based generative recommenders assign each item a single static Semantic ID via offline tokenization before sufficient user feedback is observed. For cold-start items, this one-shot commitment produces poorly discriminative codes that remain unrefined because associated tokens are rarely sampled during training. The authors identify this early static commitment — not model capacity — as the fundamental cold-start bottleneck.
AI.method: DREAM is a three-stage progressive refinement framework: (1) An intent-aware tokenizer rebuilds the SID space using counterfactual contrastive learning to generate a diverse pool of behavior-aligned candidate SIDs per cold-start item; (2) The frozen pretrained recommendation backbone evaluates candidates by measuring multi-context user support, selecting the most reliable SID without retraining; (3) A dynamic beam mechanism maintains multiple weighted SID hypotheses throughout training and inference, preventing premature collapse to a single assignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three Amazon benchmarks demonstrate that DREAM substantially outperforms state-of-the-art generative and sequential baselines on cold-start metrics.
AI.conclusion: The early static commitment of SIDs is the critical cold-start bottleneck in generative recommendation, and progressive refinement via DREAM effectively bridges the disjoint objectives of tokenization and generation, yielding significant improvements for cold-start item retrieval.

title: UnpredictaBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Distributional Randomness in LLMs
summary: We introduce UnpredictaBench, an evaluation that tests the ability of large language models (LLMs) to capture true underlying distributions. As LLMs are increasingly used as substitutes for other entities (e.g., for humans in economic simulations), the tendency of many models to collapse towards a single plausible answer means a failure to capture the unpredictability of real systems. Recent work on improving output diversity is insufficient for this setting: simulation requires samples that are calibrated to a target distribution, not merely varied outputs. UnpredictaBench isolates a simplified but fundamental version of this problem: sampling outcomes from individual target distributions, including canonical statistical distributions, distributions induced by stochastic programs, and natural-language scenarios that describe random processes. We introduce 448 such problems together with KS@N, a general-purpose evaluation metric that quantifies how well a model outputs approximate black-box target distributions via the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistical test. This is the rate at which we fail to reject model samples of size N against ground-truth samples, with larger N indicating greater difficulty. Tested across open and proprietary models, we find a large spread in distributional capabilities. For instance, when models generate samples of size 100 (KS@100, our standard metric), scores range from near 0 to over 20%. No model is able to achieve over 40% at KS@100, showing significant headroom in distributional sampling as a capability. Although adding reasoning can somewhat increase scores, we find no immediate solution for this issue. UnpredictaBench shows that even simple distributional simulation remains challenging, making it a necessary first step toward using LLMs as stand-ins for complex systems.

AI.tldr: UnpredictaBench is a benchmark of 448 problems testing whether LLMs can sample from target distributions (statistical, stochastic-program, and natural-language), evaluated via a Kolmogorov-Smirnov-based metric (KS@N). No model exceeds 40% at KS@100, revealing that even simple distributional simulation remains a major challenge for LLMs.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as substitutes for humans in economic and social simulations, but they tend to collapse toward a single plausible answer rather than capturing the true underlying variability of real systems. Existing work on output diversity is insufficient because simulation requires calibrated sampling from a target distribution, not merely varied outputs.
AI.method: UnpredictaBench comprises 448 problems across three categories: canonical statistical distributions, distributions induced by stochastic programs, and natural-language descriptions of random processes. The evaluation metric, KS@N, uses the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test to measure how often a model's N samples fail to be rejected as matching the ground-truth distribution, with higher N indicating greater difficulty (standard: KS@100).
AI.result: Across open and proprietary models, KS@100 scores range from near 0% to just over 20%, with no model exceeding 40%. Adding reasoning modestly improves scores but does not resolve the fundamental gap, indicating substantial headroom in distributional sampling as a capability.
AI.conclusion: UnpredictaBench demonstrates that even simple distributional simulation is challenging for current LLMs, establishing this as a necessary capability for reliably using LLMs as stand-ins for complex stochastic systems.

title: Multi-Scale Feature Attention Network for Polymer Classification Using Terahertz Spectroscopy
summary: Reliable polymer identification is essential for ensuring the quality and safety of recycled plastics, yet conventional sorting and spectroscopic techniques often struggle to deliver robust discrimination. Terahertz (THz) spectroscopy offers a promising alternative, providing high-resolution and non-destructive measurements. In this work, we leverage THz signals to classify 12 types of polymers, including pure polymers, multilayer films, commercial blends, and biopolymers. To handle the complexity of these spectral signals, we propose the Multi-Scale Feature Attention Network (MSFAN), a novel deep learning architecture tailored for THz data. The framework integrates feature gating for signal recalibration and multi-scale parallel convolutions to capture diverse frequency patterns. These features are further refined through cross-feature attention and attention pooling, enabling the model to intrinsically highlight the most informative THz regions. MSFAN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, reaching a classification accuracy of 85.2%. This study demonstrates the potential of combining THz spectroscopy with deep learning techniques for effective, scalable, and interpretable polymer classification.

AI.tldr: A deep learning model (MSFAN) combined with terahertz spectroscopy achieves 85.2% accuracy in classifying 12 polymer types, outperforming existing methods for reliable recycled plastic identification.
AI.motivation: Conventional sorting and spectroscopic techniques lack robust discrimination for polymer identification in recycled plastics. THz spectroscopy is promising but produces complex signals requiring advanced analysis.
