AI.method: The Multi-Scale Feature Attention Network (MSFAN) integrates feature gating for signal recalibration, multi-scale parallel convolutions for diverse frequency patterns, cross-feature attention, and attention pooling to highlight informative THz regions.
AI.result: MSFAN achieves 85.2% classification accuracy across 12 polymer types (pure polymers, multilayer films, commercial blends, biopolymers), consistently outperforming state-of-the-art models.
AI.conclusion: Combining THz spectroscopy with deep learning, particularly MSFAN, provides an effective, scalable, and interpretable solution for polymer classification in recycling applications.

title: SafeGene: Reusable Adapters for Transferable Safety Alignment
summary: Open-weight LLMs are increasingly fine-tuned into customized assistants, but downstream fine-tuning can weaken safety alignment and make models more vulnerable to malicious prompts, even when the training data is not intentionally harmful. This creates a recurring safety recovery problem as target models are repeatedly updated with new task data or user interactions. We propose SafeGene, a reusable safety-adapter module designed for cross-task reuse within each architecture-compatible model family. Rather than treating safety recovery as a model-specific repair step, SafeGene treats safety capability as an independent, reusable adapter representation decoupled from task-specific updates. This representation is obtained from aligned--degraded model discrepancies, refined into task-transferable safety vectors through data-aware layer selection, and expressed in each downstream task-adapted model via few-shot layer-wise coefficient recalibration. Experiments across multiple model families, downstream tasks, and safety judges show that SafeGene-enhanced models reduce harmful response rates while maintaining downstream performance, outperforming representative safe adaptation methods in safety--utility trade-off.

AI.tldr: SafeGene proposes a reusable safety-adapter module that decouples safety alignment from task-specific fine-tuning. By extracting safety vectors from aligned–degraded model discrepancies and recalibrating them per downstream task via few-shot coefficient tuning, SafeGene enables cross-task safety recovery without sacrificing utility, outperforming existing safe adaptation methods.
AI.motivation: Open-weight LLMs fine-tuned for downstream tasks suffer from weakened safety alignment, even when training data is benign. Repeated task updates create a recurring safety recovery problem. Existing approaches treat safety recovery as model-specific repair, lacking cross-task reusability.
AI.method: SafeGene treats safety capability as a reusable adapter representation: (1) safety vectors are extracted from discrepancies between aligned and degraded model pairs; (2) data-aware layer selection refines these into task-transferable safety vectors; (3) few-shot layer-wise coefficient recalibration adapts the safety adapter to each downstream task-adapted model without full retraining.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple model families, downstream tasks, and safety judges demonstrate that SafeGene-enhanced models achieve lower harmful response rates while preserving downstream task performance, surpassing representative safe adaptation methods in the safety–utility trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Safety capability can be effectively decoupled from task-specific parameters and expressed as a reusable adapter. SafeGene provides an efficient, cross-task safety recovery mechanism that maintains downstream utility, addressing the recurring safety degradation problem in continually fine-tuned LLM ecosystems.

title: A Model of Integrated Information Processing in Human-AI Interaction
summary: For Human-AI Interaction (HAII) research to move forward, theoretical work linking psychological mechanisms to interface design is needed. Such work should extend rather than replace established HCI and automation research, adapting to the increasing autonomy and agency of AI systems. Building on prior frameworks focused on roles and levels in human interaction with automation, a gap remains from a psychological view: a task-centered, process-oriented account that links mechanisms of action regulation to concrete design and evaluation levers for human-AI coupling, expressed in a unified vocabulary for human and machine. Moreover, existing models may describe how a system is designed (e.g., function allocation in automation) but fall short in showing how this design affects human behavior. We present the Integrated Information Processing (IIP) model, a task-centered, cybernetic model that conceptualizes humans, machines, and their joint activity as coupled control loops. The IIP model uses a unified modeling language for human and artificial agents, making psychological models of action regulation accessible for AI system design. As a core feature, we argue that efficacy within a shared task is characterized by three integration qualities, input adequacy, reference consonance, and output operativity, which critically influence benchmarks of human-centeredness such as transparency and controllability. The model maps interface choices (e.g., XAI techniques) to theory-driven expectations of user behavior, guiding interface design and evaluation. To this end, we present (1) a continuity-preserving theoretical discourse that extends HAII to agency in AI; (2) the IIP model with three information-processing qualities; and (3) applications of the IIP model to exemplary use cases demonstrating implications for interface design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Integrated Information Processing (IIP) model, a task-centered, cybernetic framework that conceptualizes humans, AI, and their joint activity as coupled control loops. It identifies three integration qualities—input adequacy, reference consonance, and output operativity—that determine efficacy in shared tasks and links them to interface design choices and human-centered benchmarks like transparency and controllability.
AI.motivation: HAII research lacks a psychological, task-centered, process-oriented theoretical account that links mechanisms of human action regulation to concrete interface design levers. Existing automation frameworks describe system design (e.g., function allocation) but fail to show how design affects human behavior. A unified vocabulary for both human and artificial agents is also missing.
AI.method: The authors develop the IIP model—a cybernetic, control-loop-based framework using a unified modeling language for human and artificial agents. The model posits three integration qualities (input adequacy, reference consonance, output operativity) as critical mediators between interface design and user behavior. The framework is applied to exemplary use cases to demonstrate implications for interface design and evaluation.
AI.result: The IIP model maps interface choices (e.g., XAI techniques) to theory-driven expectations of user behavior, providing concrete design and evaluation levers. It extends HAII discourse to accommodate increasing AI agency while preserving continuity with established HCI and automation research. Three integration qualities are shown to critically influence transparency, controllability, and other human-centered benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The IIP model fills the gap between psychological action regulation theory and HAII interface design by offering a unified, task-centered framework. It provides (1) a continuity-preserving theoretical extension of HAII to AI agency, (2) the three information-processing qualities as evaluative constructs, and (3) practical design guidance demonstrated through use cases.

title: Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment for Byzantine-Resilient LLM-Agent Collaboration
summary: Byzantine collaboration among large-language-model agents requires a finality-control primitive: given delivered stochastic, structured natural-language proposals, the protocol must decide whether the round supports a commit, what kind of commit, or a typed safe abort. Naive aggregation hides this choice behind a single verdict; classical Byzantine fault tolerance hides it behind byte-identity that LLM proposals do not satisfy. We introduce Hierarchical Certified Semantic Commitment (H-CSC), a BFT-inspired protocol that converts embedding-derived finality signals over verdict-conditioned proposal groups into one of three typed outcomes: a semantic_commit (a 2f+1 within-verdict semantic core backs the verdict, emitting a parameter-bound digest over the quantised aggregate), a verdict_commit (strong verdict margin but dispersed semantic rationale, emitting a verdict-level certificate without claiming a semantic aggregate), or an explicit abort with a typed reason. The contribution is typed finality, not raw commit accuracy. On a controlled semantic-poisoning diagnostic (BCS_v1, 120 episodes), H-CSC commits with low angular deviation on BFT-feasible buckets (0.31 to 2.04 degrees) and aborts 100% of beyond-BFT rounds (n<3f+1) as intended. On a real LLM-agent claim-verification benchmark (MVR-50, 50 tasks) under paired static and rushing Byzantine attacks, H-CSC commits 0.90/0.92 with honest-reference-invalid rates of 0.02/0.00, statistically matching a strong certificate-emitting verdict-only baseline. Unlike that baseline, H-CSC also emits an embedding-backed semantic_commit digest on 74%/72% of rounds, supplying typed provenance. A strict-semantic ablation commits only 0.54/0.48, showing the verdict-level fallback is necessary for coverage (+0.36/+0.44) at the same <=0.04 safety floor; a 100-task cross-model check across four LLMs preserves invalid_hmaj within 0.00 to 0.03.

AI.tldr: H-CSC is a BFT-inspired protocol that provides typed finality (semantic_commit, verdict_commit, or typed abort) for LLM-agent collaboration, using embedding-derived signals to make commit/abort decisions with provenance tracking, achieving strong safety and coverage even under Byzantine attacks.
AI.motivation: LLM-agent collaboration requires finality-control that goes beyond naive aggregation (which masks commit choices) and classical BFT (which relies on byte-identity that LLM proposals don't satisfy). The key gap is the need for a primitive that can decide among typed outcomes — commit with semantic core, commit with only verdict support, or reasoned abort — based on stochastic natural-language proposals.
AI.method: H-CSC introduces a hierarchical certified semantic commitment protocol that: (1) groups proposals by verdict, (2) computes embedding-derived finality signals within and across groups, (3) produces three typed outcomes: semantic_commit (2f+1 within-verdict semantic core backing, emitting a parameter-bound digest over quantised aggregate), verdict_commit (strong vote margin but dispersed semantics, emitting verdict-level certificate without aggregate claim), or explicit abort with typed reason. Evaluated on BCS_v1 (controlled semantic-poisoning, 120 episodes) and MVR-50 (real LLM claim-verification, 50 tasks) under static and rushing Byzantine attacks.
AI.result: On BCS_v1: H-CSC commits with low angular deviation (0.31–2.04°) on BFT-feasible buckets and aborts 100% of beyond-BFT rounds (n<3f+1). On MVR-50: commits 0.90/0.92 with honest-reference-invalid rates of 0.02/0.00, matching a strong certificate-emitting verdict-only baseline. Additionally emits semantic_commit digests on 74%/72% of rounds. Strict-semantic ablation commits only 0.54/0.48, showing verdict-level fallback adds +0.36/+0.44 coverage at ≤0.04 safety floor. Cross-model check (100 tasks, 4 LLMs) preserves invalid_hmaj within 0.00–0.03.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is typed finality, not raw commit accuracy. H-CSC successfully provides a three-outcome finality primitive that combines embedding-backed semantic commitment with verdict-level fallback, maintaining safety while achieving high coverage. The verdict-level fallback is necessary for coverage, and the approach generalizes across multiple LLM backends with consistently low invalid rates.

title: NTILC: Neural Tool Invocation via Learned Compression
summary: Agentic tool-calling language models depend on large registries of callable APIs, functions, and local actions. Placing full tool specifications directly in the prompt incurs a cost that scales linearly with the size of the tool registry, rapidly consuming the context budget. As the registry grows, this leads to higher latency and degrades selection accuracy, particularly due to interference from irrelevant tools. We overcome these limitations by introducing NTILC, a neural tool selection and invocation framework that replaces in-context registry look-up with learned latent retrieval. NTILC maps both user intent and tool specifications into a shared embedding space, enabling tool selection via external retrieval rather than in-context lookup. The language model is conditioned only on the selected tool schema, allowing for precise, constrained argument generation. Central to our approach is a signature-aware composite objective, which augments semantic similarity with constraints derived from tool signatures (e.g., argument schema, type compatibility, and return types). By combining Circle Loss with a Functional Margin Loss, the model enforces separation between tools that are semantically similar but incompatible under their execution signatures. We evaluate NTILC on public tool-selection and function-calling datasets and report context token usage, retrieval accuracy, and selection latency metrics. Across these settings, NTILC reduces context window consumption by over 95% and inference latency by up to 74% compared to long-context ICT baselines.

AI.tldr: NTILC replaces in-context tool specifications with neural latent retrieval, mapping user intent and tool schemas into a shared embedding space. A signature-aware composite loss (Circle Loss + Functional Margin Loss) enforces separation between semantically similar but incompatible tools. Results show >95% context reduction and up to 74% latency improvement over in-context tool-calling baselines.
AI.motivation: Current agentic tool-calling LMs embed full tool registry specifications directly in the prompt, incurring linear context cost growth. As registries scale, this causes context budget exhaustion, higher latency, and degraded selection accuracy due to interference from irrelevant tools.
AI.method: A neural tool selection and invocation framework (NTILC) that: (1) maps user intent and tool specifications into a shared embedding space for retrieval-based selection instead of in-context lookup; (2) conditions the LM only on the retrieved tool schema for constrained argument generation; and (3) employs a signature-aware composite objective combining Circle Loss with Functional Margin Loss to separate tools that are semantically similar but incompatible based on argument schema, type compatibility, and return types.
AI.result: Across public tool-selection and function-calling datasets, NTILC reduces context window consumption by over 95% and inference latency by up to 74% compared to long-context in-context tool-calling baselines.
AI.conclusion: NTILC demonstrates that learned latent retrieval with signature-aware training is a highly effective alternative to in-context tool specification, enabling scalable, accurate, and efficient tool selection for agentic language models even with large tool registries.

title: AgileOS: A GPU Operating System Layer for Protected CUDA Services
summary:
Modern GPU applications increasingly interact with storage systems, network devices, vendor libraries, and GPU-resident services rather than executing only isolated compute kernels. This shift creates a need for operating-system-like protection around GPU services, where service metadata, device queues, memory-mapped I/O regions, and library-internal state should not be directly exposed to untrusted application kernels. However, today's CUDA programming model, by default, still gives each application direct ownership of its CUDA context, device pointers, runtime handles, module loading path, and kernel launches, leaving protected GPU services to build their own ad hoc interfaces and isolation mechanisms.
  This paper presents the initial design and prototype scope of AgileOS, a GPU operating-system layer for protected CUDA services. AgileOS virtualizes CUDA at the library boundary: applications link against client-side CUDA Runtime, Driver, and selected library shims, while a trusted runtime worker owns the real CUDA context and mediates supported operations. To protect service state and module interfaces, AgileOS also defines a GPU memory-management model that separates user allocations from protected module/MMIO ranges, using pointer validation and memory access guards via PTX injection. AgileOS is modularized and flexible, supporting a range of protected services and existing libraries such as cuFFT and PyTorch. The prototype includes client-side interceptors, worker-side CUDA handlers, virtualized CUDA object tables, protected AgileOS modules, a GPU memory manager that separates user allocations from protected module/MMIO ranges, selected trusted library adapters, and the PTX-level kernel memory guard.

AI.tldr: AgileOS is a GPU operating-system layer that virtualizes CUDA at the library boundary to provide OS-like protection for GPU services, preventing untrusted application kernels from directly accessing service metadata, device queues, MMIO regions, and library-internal state through client-side shims, a trusted runtime worker, and PTX-level memory access guards.
AI.motivation: Modern GPU applications increasingly interact with storage, networking, vendor libraries, and GPU-resident services rather than running isolated compute kernels. The default CUDA programming model grants each application direct ownership of its CUDA context, device pointers, and kernel launches, forcing protected GPU services to build their own ad hoc isolation mechanisms. There is a need for systematic, OS-like protection around GPU services.
AI.method: AgileOS virtualizes CUDA at the library boundary: (1) applications link against client-side CUDA Runtime, Driver, and library shims; (2) a trusted runtime worker owns the real CUDA context and mediates all supported operations; (3) a GPU memory-management model separates user allocations from protected module/MMIO ranges using pointer validation; (4) PTX injection provides memory access guards; (5) the system includes client-side interceptors, worker-side CUDA handlers, virtualized CUDA object tables, protected AgileOS modules, and trusted library adapters.
AI.result: The prototype demonstrates a modular and flexible design supporting a range of protected services and existing libraries such as cuFFT and PyTorch, with components including client-side interceptors, worker-side handlers, virtualized object tables, memory management with user/module separation, trusted library adapters, and PTX-level kernel memory guards.
AI.conclusion: AgileOS provides an initial design and prototype demonstrating that OS-like protection can be retrofitted onto the CUDA programming model through library-boundary virtualization, enabling protected GPU services without requiring applications to surrender their familiar CUDA interfaces.

title: SSRLive: Live Streaming Recommendation with Dynamic Semantic ID
summary: Live streaming has emerged as one of the fastest-growing forms of online media, enabling instant content broadcasting and real-time engagement between users and streamers. Despite the effectiveness of existing recommendation algorithms in this domain, they often suffer from limited utilization of computational resources, with low FLOPs that hinder further performance enhancement. Generative recommendation techniques, which have gained traction in various industrial tasks, offer a promising avenue for improving live streaming recommendations. However, directly applying generative methods to live streaming is non-trivial due to two major challenges: (1) static semantic IDs (SIDs) cannot reflect the rapidly changing nature of live room content; and (2) generative pipelines generally do not incorporate user--streamer interaction signals (e.g., likes, orders), which are critical for modeling user intent toward both the streamer and showcased products. To address these challenges, we introduce SSRLive: Dynamic Semantic ID-guided Streaming Recommendation for Live platforms. The proposed framework integrates a generative module and a discriminative module in a unified architecture. The generative component employs an encoder-decoder design to produce both static and dynamic SIDs, enabling timely representation of live room content while leveraging multimodal information. The discriminative component refines task-specific representations by combining SIDs with user features, augments them with user-streamer interaction data, and performs multi-task predictions. Online A/B tests in real-world deployment demonstrate tangible benefits: watch time (+3.38%), GMV (+0.72%), follower growth (+3.12%), and interaction volume (+2.92%). These improvements highlight the effectiveness and business value of SSRLive, which is now fully deployed, serving hundreds of millions of active users.

AI.tldr: SSRLive introduces a unified generative-discriminative framework that uses dynamic semantic IDs to capture rapidly changing live room content and incorporates user-streamer interaction signals, achieving significant business metric improvements when fully deployed at scale.
AI.motivation: Existing live streaming recommendation algorithms underutilize computational resources (low FLOPs), limiting performance. While generative recommendation has proven effective in other industrial domains, naive application to live streaming fails because: (1) static semantic IDs cannot capture the fast-evolving content of live rooms, and (2) generative pipelines typically omit user-streamer interaction signals (likes, orders) that are essential for modeling user intent toward both streamers and products.
AI.method: SSRLive proposes a unified architecture with two modules: (1) A generative encoder-decoder component that produces both static and dynamic semantic IDs, leveraging multimodal information to represent live room content in a timely manner. (2) A discriminative component that fuses these SIDs with user features, enriches them with user-streamer interaction data, and performs multi-task predictions (watch time, GMV, follows, interactions).
AI.result: Online A/B testing in production showed improvements across four key metrics: watch time (+3.38%), GMV (+0.72%), follower growth (+3.12%), and interaction volume (+2.92%). The system is fully deployed, serving hundreds of millions of active users.
AI.conclusion: SSRLive successfully bridges generative recommendation with live streaming by introducing dynamic SIDs and interaction-aware modeling, delivering substantial business impact at industrial scale.

title: How Language Models Fail: Token-Level Signatures of Committed and Persistent Reasoning Failures
summary: Failures in language model reasoning emerge through distinct processes that leave identifiable signatures in the reasoning trace. We characterize these failures using token-level uncertainty signals, finding they arise through two empirically distinguishable processes. The first is committed failure, in which a model locks onto an incorrect reasoning path early in its trace. A central diagnostic signature is the commitment point, beyond which considering additional tokens hurt rather than help failure detection. In the second, persistent uncertainty, uncertainty instead accumulates throughout, and the full trace is needed to best distinguish failing from successful completions. These signatures reproduce across 23 model-dataset configurations, with the framework's falsifiable predictions holding in 20 of 23 cases, well above chance across both failure modes. Finally, we demonstrate our failure mode framework has direct implications for self-consistency, identifying when uncertainty signals complement it and when it can be selectively skipped. These results offer a foundation for understanding when LLM reasoning failures become detectable and for adapting detection strategies accordingly.

AI.tldr: Language model reasoning failures follow two distinct, empirically distinguishable patterns — committed failure (early lock-in to an incorrect path, with a diagnostic "commitment point") and persistent uncertainty (accumulating uncertainty throughout the trace). These patterns generalize across 23 model-dataset configurations, and the framework informs when self-consistency methods help versus when they can be skipped.
AI.motivation: To understand how and why LLM reasoning failures occur by identifying detectable, mechanistic signatures in the reasoning trace, moving beyond binary success/failure classification to a process-level characterization of failure modes.
AI.method: The authors analyze token-level uncertainty signals from LLM reasoning traces across 23 model-dataset configurations. They empirically identify two failure processes: (1) committed failure, diagnosed by a "commitment point" after which additional tokens degrade failure detection; and (2) persistent uncertainty, where the full trace is needed for optimal detection. They test falsifiable predictions derived from this framework and evaluate implications for self-consistency methods.
AI.result: The two failure modes reproduced across 23 model-dataset configurations. The framework's falsifiable predictions held in 20 of 23 cases, significantly above chance for both failure modes. The framework also identified when uncertainty signals complement self-consistency and when self-consistency can be selectively skipped.
AI.conclusion: LLM reasoning failures leave identifiable, process-level signatures that enable principled detection strategies. Distinguishing between committed failures and persistent uncertainty has practical implications for optimizing self-consistency and other detection methods, laying a foundation for adaptive failure detection in language model reasoning.

title: MacArena: Benchmarking Computer Use Agents on an Online macOS Environment
comment: Accepted to the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: Computer-use agents (CUAs) operate graphical user interfaces (GUIs) through vision and control primitives, and their capabilities have advanced rapidly, driven in part by standardized online evaluation benchmarks such as OSWorld, which serve both as evaluation tools and as training environments for reinforcement learning. However, macOS remains underserved in this landscape: the only existing benchmark, macOSWorld, covers a narrow slice of first-party applications with simpler tasks, and runs on x86 virtual machines incompatible with Apple Silicon. We introduce MacArena, a benchmark of 421 manually verified tasks spanning 50 applications that combines a curated port of OSWorld tasks, content sourced from macOSWorld, and 49 new macOS-native tasks, all running on Apple's native Virtualization framework on Apple Silicon. We argue that macOS presents distinct GUI challenges beyond what Linux-based benchmarks capture, and our evaluation supports this claim: strong model performance on existing benchmarks can reflect familiarity with task distributions rather than genuine cross-platform GUI competence. Notably, model rankings invert between ported and macOS-native tasks, with a leading model trailing by over 26% on the MacArena subset, suggesting that macOS poses a genuinely harder environment for current GUI agents.

AI.tldr: MacArena is a new benchmark of 421 manually verified macOS GUI tasks spanning 50 applications, running natively on Apple Silicon. It reveals that strong model performance on existing Linux-based benchmarks does not transfer to macOS, with model rankings inverting between ported and macOS-native tasks, suggesting macOS poses genuinely harder challenges for GUI agents.
AI.motivation: macOS is underserved in GUI agent benchmarking: the only existing benchmark (macOSWorld) has limited application coverage, simpler tasks, and relies on x86 VMs incompatible with Apple Silicon. The authors argue that macOS presents distinct GUI challenges not captured by Linux-based benchmarks, and that current benchmark performance may reflect task distribution familiarity rather than genuine cross-platform competence.
AI.method: The authors construct MacArena by (1) curating and porting OSWorld tasks to macOS, (2) incorporating content from macOSWorld, and (3) designing 49 new macOS-native tasks. All tasks run on Apple's native Virtualization framework on Apple Silicon. They evaluate multiple state-of-the-art CUAs on both ported and native task subsets to compare cross-platform performance and ranking stability.
AI.result: Model rankings invert between ported OSWorld tasks and macOS-native tasks. A leading model trails by over 26% on the MacArena-native subset, indicating that strong performance on existing benchmarks does not reflect genuine cross-platform GUI competence. macOS-native tasks prove substantially harder for current agents.
AI.conclusion: macOS presents a genuinely harder environment for current GUI agents, and existing Linux-based benchmarks may overestimate cross-platform generalization. MacArena provides a more rigorous, Apple Silicon–native evaluation platform that exposes limitations masked by familiarity with existing task distributions.

title: Lean4Agent: Formal Modeling and Verification for Agent Workflow and Trajectory
summary: Equipping Large Language Models (LLMs) to execute reliable multi-step workflows has become a central challenge in artificial intelligence. Despite recent advances in LLMs' agentic capabilities, most agent systems still lack formal methods for specifying, verifying, and debugging their workflow and execution trajectories. This challenge mirrors a long-standing problem in mathematics, where the ambiguity of natural languages (NLs) motivates the development of formal languages (FLs). Inspired by this paradigm, we propose **Lean4Agent**, to the best of our knowledge, the first framework that uses Lean4, a dependent-type FL to model and verify agent behavior. **Lean4Agent** launches **FormalAgentLib**, an extensible Lean4 library for formally modeling and verifying agent workflows' semantic consistency under explicit assumptions, and enabling localization of execution-time failures revealed by trajectories. Building on **FormalAgentLib**, we further develop **LeanEvolve**, which applies results in **FormalAgentLib** to revise workflows to enhance its capability. Extensive experiments on a hard problem subset of SWE-Bench-Verified and a subset of ELAIP-Bench across 5 leading LLMs indicate that the verification-passing workflows outperform the failing ones by an average of **11.94%**, and **LeanEvolve** further improves SWE performance by **7.47%** on average. Furthermore, **Lean4Agent** establishes a foundation for a new field of using expressive dependent-type FL to formally model and verify agent behavior.

AI.tldr: Lean4Agent introduces the first framework using a dependent-type formal language (Lean4) to formally specify, verify, and debug LLM agent workflows. It includes FormalAgentLib for modeling semantic consistency and localizing failures, plus LeanEvolve for workflow revision. Experiments show verification-passing workflows outperform failing ones by 11.94%, and LeanEvolve boosts SWE performance by 7.47%.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent systems lack formal methods for specifying, verifying, and debugging multi-step workflows and execution trajectories. This mirrors the historical problem in mathematics where natural language ambiguity motivated formal languages. The authors aim to bring formal methods to agent behavior modeling.
AI.method: The Lean4Agent framework comprises two components: (1) FormalAgentLib, an extensible Lean4 library for formally modeling agent workflows, verifying semantic consistency under explicit assumptions, and localizing execution-time failures from trajectories; (2) LeanEvolve, which leverages FormalAgentLib's verification results to revise and improve workflows. Both are evaluated on SWE-Bench-Verified (hard subset) and ELAIP-Bench across 5 LLMs.
AI.result: Verification-passing workflows outperform failing ones by an average of 11.94%. LeanEvolve further improves SWE performance by an average of 7.47% across 5 leading LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Lean4Agent demonstrates the viability of using expressive dependent-type formal languages to formally model and verify agent behavior, establishing a foundation for a new research field at the intersection of formal methods and LLM agents.

title: Modelling Opinion Dynamics at Scale with Deep MARL
summary: Modelling opinion dynamics typically relies on hand-crafted local interaction rules to study emergent macroscopic phenomena such as consensus and polarisation. In contrast, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) enables agents to learn such behaviours directly by optimising simple rewards. To explore the potential of MARL for opinion dynamics, we introduce a GPU-accelerated consensus and truth-finding game that scales to populations of up to 1000 agents, comparable to many real-world social sub-networks. To prevent unrealistic conventions, we extend other-play to general-sum social interactions. We next validate our model on a subset of the Bluesky network by recovering agent importance structures from graph topology alone via a learned attention layer, finding that highly conforming populations most closely match human data. In large social media networks such high levels of conformity significantly reduce collective accuracy and promote dishonest agents that lie to fit in. By contrast, small, dynamic hunter-gatherer networks are less affected; here, conformity can even improve collective agreement. This suggests a mismatch between evolved human conformity heuristics and modern social media environments as a potential contributor to misinformation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a GPU-accelerated multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for modelling opinion dynamics at scale (up to 1000 agents), showing that high conformity — while adaptive in small hunter-gatherer networks — becomes maladaptive in large social media environments, reducing collective accuracy and enabling dishonest agents, which may contribute to misinformation spread.
AI.motivation: Traditional opinion dynamics models rely on hand-crafted local interaction rules, limiting their flexibility and realism. The authors aim to explore whether MARL can learn emergent social behaviours (consensus, polarisation) from simple rewards, and whether learned behaviours can be validated against real social network data (Bluesky) to understand the evolutionary mismatch between human conformity heuristics and modern online environments.
AI.method: The authors develop a GPU-accelerated consensus and truth-finding game for populations up to 1000 agents using MARL. They extend the "other-play" concept to general-sum social interactions to prevent degenerate conventions. A learned attention layer recovers agent importance structures from graph topology. The model is validated against a subset of the Bluesky social network, comparing conformity levels against human interaction data.
AI.result: Highly conforming populations most closely match human data on Bluesky. In large networks, high conformity significantly reduces collective accuracy and enables dishonest agents who lie to fit in. By contrast, in small, dynamic hunter-gatherer networks, conformity is less detrimental and can even improve collective agreement. This reveals a mismatch between evolved conformity heuristics and modern social media scale.
AI.conclusion: Evolved human conformity heuristics, adaptive in small-scale ancestral environments, become maladaptive in large modern social media networks, fostering conditions that reduce accuracy and promote dishonest behaviour. This evolutionary mismatch may be a contributing factor to the prevalence of misinformation online.

title: AutoPipelineAI: Context-Aware CI/CD Pipeline Generation from Natural Language
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure, 6 tables, 16 references, IMSA Conference 11-12 July 2026, International Conference on Intelligent Methods, Systems, and Applications 2026 #70415,
summary: Modern software development relies on CI/CD pipelines to automate testing, building, and deployment operations. Configuring DevOps pipelines is challenging and time-consuming, as developers must understand platform-specific syntax and manually create configuration files. This complexity can lead to configuration errors and reduced productivity, especially for developers with limited DevOps experience. This paper introduces the AutoPipelineAI system, which generates CI/CD pipeline configurations using natural language descriptions. The proposed solution uses large language models (LLMs) to translate developer intent, analyze repository structures, and create specific pipeline scripts for environments like GitHub Actions and GitLab CI/CD. It integrates repository-aware analysis, automated validation systems, and a feedback mechanism that confirms the accuracy and usability of the created pipelines. We present the system architecture, its implementation, and an assessment framework designed to measure generation precision, configuration validity, and reduction in setup effort compared to manual pipeline creation. AutoPipelineAI illustrates how LLMs can simplify the complexity of DevOps configuration and enhance developer access to continuous delivery methods. Evaluation results provide early evidence that repository-aware, natural-language-driven CI/CD generation is a viable and promising paradigm for reducing the complexity of DevOps configuration and enabling more accessible software delivery automation.

AI.tldr: AutoPipelineAI leverages LLMs to automatically generate CI/CD pipeline configurations (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD) from natural language descriptions, incorporating repository-aware analysis and automated validation to reduce DevOps setup complexity.
AI.motivation: Manual CI/CD pipeline configuration requires platform-specific syntax expertise, is time-consuming, and error-prone, creating a barrier for developers with limited DevOps experience and reducing overall productivity.
AI.method: The system uses large language models (LLMs) to translate natural language developer intent into pipeline scripts, augmented by repository-aware analysis, automated validation systems, and a feedback mechanism to ensure accuracy and usability of generated configurations.
AI.result: Early evaluation results indicate that repository-aware, natural-language-driven CI/CD generation is a viable paradigm, with the assessment framework measuring generation precision, configuration validity, and setup effort reduction compared to manual creation.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven pipeline generation can meaningfully simplify DevOps configuration complexity and enhance developer accessibility to continuous delivery practices, establishing a promising direction for automated software delivery tooling.

title: The Custody Envelope Threshold: Authority-Scaled Admission of External Artifacts in Institutional Infrastructure
comment: 32 pages. Preregistered framework and protocol paper; empirical pilot is a separate planned study. OSF preregistration and replication package: https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/E57FJ
summary: Modern infrastructure depends on externally maintained artifacts such as package-registry dependencies, CI/CD actions, container images, Terraform providers and modules, developer extensions, model artifacts, and AI tool servers. These artifacts are easy to fetch but difficult for institutions to admit, govern, and revoke. This paper proposes the Custody Envelope Threshold, an authority-scaled model of artifact admission. It argues that direct institutional admission is defensible only when object identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity are sufficiently closed relative to the execution authority delegated to the artifact. When this threshold is not met, institutions tend to proxy, policy-mediate, vendor-mediate, internalize, quarantine, or reject the artifact. The framework is operationalized as a four-condition ordinal instrument and connected to reference-monitor reasoning, least privilege, and transaction cost economics. It is applied to package dependencies, GitHub Actions, container images, Terraform providers and modules, developer extensions, and open model artifacts, with Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers treated as held-out evidence. The paper also specifies a validation design, deterministic prediction function, and OSF replication package for testing whether high-scrutiny institutions converge toward stronger custody closure for high-authority artifacts.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Custody Envelope Threshold—an authority-scaled model for artifact admission in modern infrastructure. It posits that direct institutional admission of externally maintained artifacts is only defensible when object identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity are sufficiently closed relative to delegated execution authority. When the threshold is unmet, institutions resort to proxy, policy-mediate, vendor-mediate, internalize, quarantine, or reject strategies. The framework is operationalized as a four-condition ordinal instrument and applied across package dependencies, CI/CD actions, container images, Terraform artifacts, developer extensions, and open model artifacts, with MCP servers as held-out validation evidence.
AI.motivation: Modern infrastructure heavily depends on externally maintained artifacts (package dependencies, GitHub Actions, container images, Terraform providers, model artifacts, etc.) that are easy to fetch but difficult for institutions to admit, govern, and revoke. Current practice lacks a systematic model for determining when and how institutions should exercise custody control over these artifacts, especially as execution authority scales.
AI.method: The authors develop a conceptual framework—the Custody Envelope Threshold—grounded in reference-monitor reasoning, least privilege principles, and transaction cost economics. The framework is operationalized as a four-condition ordinal instrument assessing object identity, ingress path, and revocation capacity relative to delegated execution authority. It is applied analytically to six artifact categories (package dependencies, GitHub Actions, container images, Terraform providers/modules, developer extensions, open model artifacts), with Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers treated as held-out evidence. A validation design and deterministic prediction function are specified, along with an OSF replication package.
AI.result: The framework yields a structured taxonomy of institutional responses: when the custody envelope threshold is met, direct admission is defensible; when unmet, institutions gravitate toward proxy, policy-mediation, vendor-mediation, internalization, quarantine, or rejection. The analysis across artifact categories demonstrates the framework's explanatory and predictive utility, with MCP servers providing independent confirmatory evidence.
AI.conclusion: The Custody Envelope Threshold provides a systematic, authority-scaled model for reasoning about artifact admission in institutional settings. It predicts that high-scrutiny institutions will converge toward stronger custody closure for high-authority artifacts. The framework bridges software supply chain security, reference-monitor theory, and transaction cost economics, and includes a replicable validation design for empirical testing.

title: Meaning in Order, Order in Meaning: Semantic R-precision for Keyphrase Evaluation
summary: Evaluating the quality of automatically generated keyphrases remains a complex challenge. Traditional metrics either rely on exact lexical matching or consider semantic similarity while ignoring prediction ranking, both of which misalign with how humans judge informativeness and relevance. We introduce Semantic R-Precision (SemR-p), a novel evaluation metric that integrates semantic similarity into the rank-aware R-Precision framework. Designed from a human-centric perspective and inspired by Information Retrieval metrics, SemR-p rewards semantically relevant keyphrases that appear early in the output list. We conducted extensive analyses to assess its semantic sensitivity, ranking awareness, and discriminative power across models and datasets. The results suggest that SemR-p offers a complementary lens for evaluating keyphrase predictions, helping to better reflect user-centred notions of relevance alongside traditional lexical and semantic matching metrics.

AI.tldr: SemR-p is a new keyphrase evaluation metric that combines semantic similarity with ranking awareness, rewarding early placement of semantically relevant predictions to better align with human judgments of informativeness.
AI.motivation: Existing keyphrase evaluation metrics are flawed: exact lexical matching is too rigid, while semantic similarity metrics ignore prediction ranking — both fail to capture how humans actually judge keyphrase relevance and informativeness.
AI.method: The authors propose Semantic R-Precision (SemR-p), which extends the rank-aware R-Precision framework by incorporating semantic similarity (via embedding-based similarity scoring). It rewards semantically relevant keyphrases that appear higher in the ranked prediction list. The metric was tested for semantic sensitivity, ranking awareness, and discriminative power across multiple models and datasets.
AI.result: SemR-p demonstrated strong semantic sensitivity, ranking awareness, and discriminative power, offering a complementary perspective to existing lexical and semantic metrics.
AI.conclusion: SemR-p provides a useful, human-centric complement to traditional metrics, better reflecting user-perceived relevance when evaluating automatically generated keyphrases.

title: CAF-Gen: A Multi-Agent System for Enriching Argumentation Structures
comment: Accepted for publication in the proceedings of ICCCI 2026
summary: Formalizing complex reasoning from natural text is one of the central challenges in computational linguistics. It requires systems to understand not just keywords but also the context and complex reasoning embedded in a text. Current Argument Mining (AM) techniques identify basic claims and premises, yet they often struggle to capture the richer structural information required by advanced schemas such as the Carneades Argumentation Framework (CAF), which incorporates features such as premise types, proof standards, and argument schemes. We address this limitation by introducing CAF-Gen, an automated multi-agent framework designed to enrich shallow argument structures into CAF-compliant argument models. By employing an iterative Creator-Reviewer pipeline, a creator agent's output is validated by a critical agent to ensure structural integrity. This multi-agent collaboration is crucial for mitigating the structural instability typical of single-pass generative models. Our experiments demonstrate that the iterative feedback loop improves the quality of the resulting data and achieves strong alignment with the original annotations, while producing structurally richer models. Our findings show that the multi-agent system can overcome the limitations of single-pass generation, providing a robust methodology for the automated modeling of formal argumentation.

AI.tldr: CAF-Gen introduces a multi-agent framework with an iterative Creator-Reviewer pipeline that automatically enriches shallow argument structures into Carneades Argumentation Framework (CAF)-compliant models, overcoming the structural instability of single-pass generative approaches.
AI.motivation: Current Argument Mining techniques capture only basic claims and premises but fail to produce the richer structural information required by advanced argumentation schemas like CAF (which includes premise types, proof standards, and argument schemes). Single-pass generative models suffer from structural instability, limiting automated formal argumentation modeling.
AI.method: CAF-Gen is a multi-agent framework employing an iterative Creator-Reviewer pipeline: a creator agent generates CAF-compliant argument structures from shallow inputs, and a reviewer agent validates the output for structural integrity. The iterative feedback loop between these agents ensures quality and alignment with original annotations.
AI.result: The iterative feedback loop demonstrably improves data quality and achieves strong alignment with original annotations while producing structurally richer argument models. The multi-agent approach successfully overcomes the limitations of single-pass generation.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent collaboration provides a robust methodology for automated modeling of formal argumentation, effectively mitigating structural instability and enabling richer, schema-compliant representations beyond what single-pass generative models can achieve.

title: WAV: Multi-Resolution Block Residual Routing for Deep Decoder-Only Transformers
summary: Residual connections are central to training deep Transformers, but standard PreNorm residual streams aggregate sublayer updates with fixed unit weights. Recent Attention Residuals replace this fixed accumulation with content-dependent depth-wise routing, and Block Attention Residuals make the mechanism efficient by routing over block-level residual summaries. However, a single block summary stores only the low-frequency total residual displacement inside a block, discarding directional structure such as attention-vs-MLP imbalance and early-vs-late block dynamics. We propose WAV v1, a lightweight multi-resolution residual routing method for decoder-only Transformers. Instead of representing each block only by its accumulated residual sum, WAV v1 augments every block with two directional detail bases: a phase basis that contrasts attention and MLP updates, and a split basis that contrasts early and late sublayer updates. These bases are routed together with standard block summaries through the same depth-wise softmax mixer, while negative detail-source initialization and detached RMS matching stabilize training. On character-level TinyStories and Text8 language modeling, WAV v1 shows a clear depth-dependent benefit. Although it is not consistently beneficial at 12 layers, it becomes competitive at 24 layers and outperforms all baselines at 48 layers. At 48 layers, WAV v1 reduces validation loss relative to Block AttnRes from 0.4960 to 0.4738 on TinyStories and from 0.9363 to 0.9305 on Text8, with negligible additional parameters. These results suggest that directional residual details, not only block-level sums, are important for scaling residual routing in deeper Transformers.

AI.tldr: WAV v1 introduces multi-resolution residual routing for decoder-only Transformers, augmenting block-level residual summaries with two directional detail bases (phase and split) that capture attention-vs-MLP imbalance and early-vs-late dynamics. It outperforms Block Attention Residuals at 48 layers on TinyStories and Text8 with negligible parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard PreNorm residuals accumulate sublayer updates with fixed unit weights, and recent Block Attention Residuals only store low-frequency total residual displacement per block, discarding directional structure like attention-MLP imbalance and early-late dynamics. The authors aim to capture these finer-grained directional details for better depth-wise routing in deeper Transformers.
AI.method: WAV v1 augments each block with two directional detail bases: a phase basis (contrasting attention vs. MLP updates) and a split basis (contrasting early vs. late sublayer updates). These bases are routed alongside standard block summaries through a depth-wise softmax mixer. Training is stabilized via negative detail-source initialization and detached RMS matching.
AI.result: At 12 layers, WAV v1 is not consistently beneficial. At 24 layers it becomes competitive, and at 48 layers it outperforms all baselines: validation loss drops from 0.4960 to 0.4738 on TinyStories and from 0.9363 to 0.9305 on Text8 compared to Block AttnRes, with negligible additional parameters.
AI.conclusion: Directional residual details (phase and split bases), not merely block-level sums, are important for scaling residual routing in deeper Transformers. WAV v1 demonstrates a clear depth-dependent benefit, suggesting that multi-resolution routing becomes increasingly valuable as model depth grows.

title: CrowdMath: A Dataset of Crowdsourced Mathematical Research Discussions
summary: Large language models have made substantial progress on mathematical reasoning, but existing benchmarks typically evaluate well-specified problems with final answers, step-by-step solutions, or complete proofs. They do not capture collaborative open-problem solving: a setting in which participants propose partial arguments, identify gaps or errors in prior steps, repair flawed reasoning, and gradually synthesize incremental contributions into a proof. We introduce CrowdMath, a dataset of 164 expert-annotated progress chains from the MIT PRIMES--Art of Problem Solving (AoPS) CrowdMath program (2016-2025), a collaborative research initiative whose discussions have led to peer-reviewed publications. Each chain traces a multi-participant forum discussion from an open-problem statement to a completed proof. Posts are labeled by their functional roles in the evolving solution process, including partial progress, proof completion, erroneous reasoning, and error identification. We define evaluation tasks and benchmark six frontier models. Models achieve 83-88% accuracy on next-post prediction, suggesting that they can follow the local flow of mathematical discussion. However, they struggle to identify the functional significance of individual contributions with the best model achieving only 0.42 macro-F1 on post-role classification. CrowdMath exposes a gap between solving well-specified mathematical problems and understanding collaborative mathematical progress as it unfolds.

AI.tldr: CrowdMath introduces a benchmark dataset of 164 collaborative mathematical proof progressions from the MIT PRIMES-AoPS forum (2016-2025). While LLMs achieve 83-88% accuracy at next-post prediction, they struggle with post-role classification (macro-F1 of 0.42), revealing a gap between solving well-defined math problems and understanding the collaborative process of mathematical discovery.
AI.motivation: Existing mathematical reasoning benchmarks evaluate only well-specified problems with final answers or complete proofs. They fail to capture collaborative open-problem solving, where participants propose partial arguments, identify gaps, repair flawed reasoning, and synthesize incremental contributions into proofs. The authors aim to bridge this evaluation gap.
AI.method: The authors construct CrowdMath, a dataset of 164 expert-annotated progress chains from 9 years of the MIT PRIMES-AoPS CrowdMath collaborative research forum. Each chain traces a multi-participant discussion from open-problem statement to completed proof. Posts are labeled by functional roles (partial progress, proof completion, erroneous reasoning, error identification). They define evaluation tasks — next-post prediction and post-role classification — and benchmark six frontier LLMs.
AI.result: Frontier models achieve 83-88% accuracy on next-post prediction, indicating strong local understanding of discussion flow. However, on post-role classification — identifying the functional significance of individual contributions — the best model reaches only 0.42 macro-F1, substantially underperforming relative to more straightforward mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: CrowdMath exposes a significant gap between LLMs' ability to solve well-specified mathematical problems and their capacity to understand collaborative mathematical progress as it unfolds. The benchmark highlights the need for models that can reason about the roles, errors, and incremental contributions that characterize real mathematical research discourse.

title: Learn to Match: Two-Sided Matching with Temporally Extended Feedback
summary: Two-sided matching markets often involve information that unfolds over time through interviews, repeated interaction, learning, and separation. Existing matching models typically reduce this process to immediate sub-Gaussian feedback about fixed preferences, missing settings where payoff-relevant information is revealed gradually and changes future matching decisions. We introduce a framework with temporally extended feedback, that formulates two-sided matching as a partially observable Markov game with costly pre-match screening, noisy post-match observations, evolving latent profiles, and endogenous continuation or dissolution. We instantiate this framework in Learn2Match, a multi-agent reinforcement-learning benchmark for dynamic matching markets. Learn2Match supports decentralized decision making over whom to interview, whom to match with, and when to dissolve a match, while evaluating policies using regret, social welfare, and an information-friction loss that measures the welfare gap caused by incomplete revelation of latent preferences. We find that independent PPO achieves higher cumulative social welfare and lower cumulative regret than the bandit-style CA-ETC baseline under temporally extended feedback, demonstrating the promise of MARL for dynamic matching markets. However, PPO still incurs higher information-friction loss, revealing that end-to-end MARL does not yet provide the coordinated exploration structure of matching-bandit methods. These results position Learn2Match as a benchmark for developing the next generation of matching-market algorithms: methods that are adaptive like RL agents, statistically disciplined like bandit algorithms, and structurally aware like stable-matching mechanisms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Learn2Match, a multi-agent reinforcement learning benchmark for dynamic two-sided matching markets where preferences are revealed gradually through costly screening, noisy observations, and evolving latent profiles. Experiments show PPO outperforms bandit baselines in cumulative welfare and regret but suffers higher information-friction loss, highlighting the need for MARL methods that combine RL adaptability with bandit-style statistical coordination.
AI.motivation: Existing matching market models assume immediate sub-Gaussian feedback about fixed preferences, ignoring real-world settings (job markets, dating platforms) where payoff-relevant information unfolds gradually through interviews, repeated interactions, learning, and potential dissolution. This gap motivates a framework that captures temporally extended feedback in dynamic matching.
AI.method: The authors formulate two-sided matching as a partially observable Markov game (POMG) with: costly pre-match screening, noisy post-match observations, evolving latent profiles, and endogenous continuation/dissolution decisions. They implement this as Learn2Match, a MARL benchmark supporting decentralized decisions on interviewing, matching, and dissolving. They evaluate using regret, social welfare, and a novel information-friction loss metric. Independent PPO is compared against the CA-ETC bandit baseline.
AI.result: Independent PPO achieves higher cumulative social welfare and lower cumulative regret than the CA-ETC bandit baseline under temporally extended feedback. However, PPO incurs higher information-friction loss (welfare gap from incomplete latent preference revelation), revealing that end-to-end MARL lacks the coordinated exploration structure inherent to matching-bandit methods.
AI.conclusion: Learn2Match serves as a benchmark for next-generation matching-market algorithms that must integrate three desiderata: RL-like adaptivity, bandit-like statistical discipline, and stable-matching structural awareness. Current MARL methods succeed on aggregate metrics but fail on information efficiency, pointing to a clear research direction.

title: Tensor Algebraic Property Skeletons: Amplifying Property-Based Testing for AI Compilers
summary:
Deep learning (DL) compilers such as TVM and ONNX-MLIR lower tensor computation graphs into optimized executables for target backends. Testing these AI compilers has made substantial progress in generating well-formed inputs in the context of fuzzing; however, such generation alone does not catch semantic drifts from algebraic invariants that graph transformations and optimizations are expected to preserve. While tensor algebra has been studied for decades, it has not been transformed into executable property-based tests (PBTs) for DL compilers because doing so requires jointly constructing operators, inputs, and test oracles. The central challenge is no longer generating well-formed inputs for fuzzing DL compilers, but bootstrapping executable PBTs with such inputs and oracles based on tensor algebra.
  We realize this vision in Propilot, an LLM-driven agentic property-based testing framework for DL compilers with GPT 5.5. First, Propilot represents tensor algebra knowledge as reusable property skeletons, each coupled with operator constraints, shape and value rules, and oracle templates. Second, given a target compiler, Propilot instantiates these skeletons into executable PBTs by generating paired tensor computation graphs, concrete tensor inputs, and expected semantic relations as oracles. Next, to prevent generated tests from degenerating into invalid or uninformative PBTs, Propilot validates each PBT candidate before execution for applicability and safety. Validation feedback, execution results, and coverage signals guide subsequent generation.
  We evaluate Propilot on TVM with 212 operators and 20 property skeletons, generating 4,579 PBTs. Compared with direct LLM-based PBT generation, Propilot reduces redundancy by 49% and eliminates invalid tests through explicit property skeletons. This effectiveness translates into finding semantic errors and numerical discrepancies.

AI.tldr: Propilot is an LLM-driven agentic property-based testing framework for deep learning compilers that uses reusable property skeletons from tensor algebra to generate executable tests with oracles, uncovering semantic errors and numerical discrepancies in compilers like TVM.
AI.motivation: Current DL compiler fuzzing focuses on generating well-formed inputs but fails to detect semantic drifts from algebraic invariants that graph transformations should preserve. The key challenge is jointly constructing operators, inputs, and test oracles to create executable property-based tests grounded in tensor algebra.
AI.method: Propilot uses GPT-5.5 to represent tensor algebra as reusable property skeletons (with operator constraints, shape/value rules, and oracle templates), instantiates them into executable PBTs with paired graphs, concrete inputs, and semantic oracles, and validates each candidate for applicability and safety before execution, using validation feedback, execution results, and coverage signals to guide subsequent generation.
AI.result: On TVM with 212 operators and 20 property skeletons, Propilot generated 4,579 PBTs. Compared to direct LLM-based PBT generation, it reduced redundancy by 49% and eliminated invalid tests through explicit property skeletons, successfully finding semantic errors and numerical discrepancies.
AI.conclusion: Propilot demonstrates that bootstrapping executable PBTs using LLM-driven agentic workflows with structured tensor algebra knowledge effectively exposes semantic bugs in DL compilers that input-generation-only fuzzing would miss.

title: What Your Posts Reveal: A Benchmark and Agentic Framework for User-Level Privacy Leakage on Social Media
summary:
Public social media posts can reveal private information through weak cues scattered across text, images, or metadata. Such leakage is often cumulative and cross-post: cues that appear harmless in isolation may jointly expose a user's home, workplace, or routine. However, current research lacks a unified benchmark for user-level multimodal privacy leakage and an evaluation metric that captures exposure severity beyond binary accuracy.
  To address these gaps, we propose SopriBench, a synthetic benchmark guided by leakage patterns abstracted from a private reference corpus of Rednote and Instagram accounts, covering 50 user profiles and 1,569 images with attributes, contextual sensitivity, granularity, leakage type, inference difficulty, and supporting evidence. We further introduce the Privacy Exposure Score (PES), which weights value granularity by contextual sensitivity. Inspired by abductive reasoning, we introduce Argus, a training-free agentic framework for cumulative leakage inference. Argus forms hypotheses from accumulated evidence, verifies supporting evidence, and aggregates cross-post cues into privacy profiles, achieving 0.55 PES, a 25% improvement over the strongest baseline, with the largest gain on cross-post leakage.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SopriBench, a synthetic benchmark for multimodal privacy leakage, introduces the Privacy Exposure Score (PES) metric, and presents Argus, a training-free agentic framework that outperforms baselines by 25% on cumulative cross-post privacy inference.
AI.motivation: Social media posts leak private information through subtle, scattered cues across text, images, and metadata. These cues are cumulatively revealing — individually harmless signals can jointly expose sensitive attributes like home, workplace, or routine. Current research lacks (1) a unified benchmark for user-level multimodal privacy leakage and (2) an evaluation metric capturing exposure severity beyond binary accuracy.
AI.method: The authors introduce three contributions: (1) **SopriBench**, a synthetic benchmark derived from leakage patterns in a private reference corpus of Rednote and Instagram accounts, spanning 50 user profiles and 1,569 images annotated with attributes, contextual sensitivity, granularity, leakage type, inference difficulty, and supporting evidence; (2) **Privacy Exposure Score (PES)**, a metric that weights the granularity of inferred values by their contextual sensitivity; and (3) **Argus**, a training-free agentic framework inspired by abductive reasoning that forms hypotheses from accumulated evidence, verifies supporting evidence, and aggregates cross-post cues into comprehensive privacy profiles.
AI.result: Argus achieves a PES of 0.55, representing a 25% improvement over the strongest baseline. The largest performance gains occur specifically on cross-post leakage scenarios, where cues are distributed across multiple posts.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that cumulative, cross-post privacy leakage is a significant and underexplored threat. The combination of SopriBench, PES, and Argus provides both a standardized evaluation framework and an effective inference method, highlighting the need for multimodal, user-level privacy defenses beyond single-post analysis.

title: Decision-Theoretic Stopping Rules for Document Screening
summary: Deciding when to stop reviewing the results of a search is a common problem with multiple applications. Existing stopping rules developed within Technology-Assisted Review (TAR) aim to achieve a pre-specified recall target and do not take into account the reason for examining the results, potentially leading to sub-optimal recommendations. This paper applies decision theory to the problem and uses it to derive three practical stopping policies based on the Expected Value of Perfect Information. The approach is applied to two professional search tasks: patent examining and systematic reviewing. Experiments on CLEF-IP and medical systematic review datasets show that the proposed approach generally produces more appropriate stopping decisions than existing methods, as demonstrated by higher net utility under the evaluated cost and payoff settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces decision-theoretic stopping rules for search result review based on Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI), outperforming traditional recall-target methods in patent examination and systematic review tasks by yielding higher net utility.
AI.motivation: Existing stopping rules in Technology-Assisted Review (TAR) focus solely on hitting a pre-specified recall target without considering the underlying reason or cost-benefit trade-offs of the review process, potentially leading to sub-optimal stopping decisions.
AI.method: The authors frame the stopping problem within decision theory and derive three practical stopping policies using the Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI). These policies are evaluated on two professional search tasks — patent examination (CLEF-IP dataset) and medical systematic review — against established stopping methods.
AI.result: Experiments on CLEF-IP and medical systematic review datasets demonstrate that the proposed EVPI-based stopping policies generally produce more appropriate stopping decisions than existing methods, as measured by higher net utility under the evaluated cost and payoff configurations.
AI.conclusion: Decision-theoretic stopping rules grounded in EVPI provide a principled, utility-maximizing alternative to recall-target-based stopping heuristics, yielding better practical outcomes across diverse professional search domains.

title: The Piggyback Hypothesis of Generalization: Explaining and Mitigating Emergent Misalignment
summary: The mechanisms behind LLMs' broad over-generalization beyond training examples remain unclear. Emergent misalignment (EM) offers a striking case study: finetuning on narrow tasks induces broad misalignment to semantically-unrelated test domains. In this work, we propose the Piggyback Hypothesis: the chat-template tokens can piggyback the finetuned behaviour onto out-of-domain queries. We validate this hypothesis by showing that subtle perturbations to the prefix (tokens preceding all user queries), or patching the prefix representations with those from the unfinetuned model, can restore alignment without changing the user query. Building on this finding, we propose Token-Regularized Finetuning (TReFT), which regularizes specific token representations during training to mitigate EM. Across different models and multiple EM-inducing datasets, TReFT reduces EM while preserving in-domain learning. On Llama-3.1-8B finetuned on the legal domain, TReFT achieves 33.5% more EM reduction than data interleaving with a retain set of aligned examples. We further show that TReFT extends to other narrow-finetuning settings, including abstention, tool use, and refusal (off-topic generalization is reduced by 54.3% on average), supporting the Piggyback Hypothesis. Broadly, our work highlights that LLMs may learn and generalize in unintended ways and suggests a path toward more constrained finetuning. It also calls for further study of how shared input features can piggyback model behavior across domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the "Piggyback Hypothesis," which posits that chat-template prefix tokens carry fine-tuned behaviors to out-of-domain queries, causing emergent misalignment (EM). The authors validate this by showing prefix perturbations or patching restores alignment, and introduce Token-Regularized Finetuning (TReFT) to mitigate EM while preserving in-domain performance.
AI.motivation: The mechanisms driving LLMs' over-generalization beyond training examples are poorly understood. Emergent misalignment—where narrow finetuning induces broad misalignment to unrelated domains—provides a compelling case study. The authors aim to uncover the mechanistic basis of this phenomenon and develop a mitigation strategy.
AI.method: The authors first propose and validate the Piggyback Hypothesis through two interventions: (1) applying subtle perturbations to the chat-template prefix tokens preceding user queries, and (2) patching prefix representations with those from an unfinetuned model. Building on this insight, they introduce Token-Regularized Finetuning (TReFT), which adds a regularization term during training that constrains specific token representations (particularly prefix tokens) to remain close to their pre-finetuning values, thereby preventing misaligned behavior from "piggybacking" onto out-of-domain queries.
AI.result: TReFT reduces emergent misalignment across different models and multiple EM-inducing datasets while preserving in-domain learning. On Llama-3.1-8B finetuned for the legal domain, TReFT achieves 33.5% more EM reduction than data interleaving with a retain set. The method also generalizes to other narrow-finetuning settings—abstention, tool use, and refusal—reducing off-topic generalization by 54.3% on average.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can learn and generalize in unintended ways, with shared input features (chat-template prefixes) acting as carriers of finetuned behavior across domains. TReFT offers a practical path toward more constrained finetuning, and the Piggyback Hypothesis calls for further investigation into how shared representational components propagate model behaviors across semantically unrelated domains.

title: Are you sure? A Comprehensive and Comprehensible Survey of Uncertainty Quantification in Symbolic Regression
summary: Symbolic regression (SR) is a class of methods that systematically explore the space of mathematical functions to discover models that accurately capture the underlying relationships in a dataset. Despite recent advances in the field, a lack of support for uncertainty quantification (UQ) limits its adoption in real-world decision processes. In regression analysis, UQ provides important information about the model reliability, which can both help to avoid overfitting by accounting for uncertainty in the data, and provide insights for decision-making. This survey is the first to clearly address this issue, with the objective of introducing essential UQ concepts and reviewing the current literature on UQ in SR, which can be broadly organized into three research directions: frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection. Despite its importance, UQ in SR is still underexplored, which motivates further research into reliable UQ methods for SR.

AI.tldr: This survey is the first to address uncertainty quantification (UQ) in symbolic regression (SR), organizing existing methods into frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection approaches while highlighting that UQ remains underexplored in SR despite its importance for model reliability and real-world adoption.
AI.motivation: Despite recent advances in symbolic regression, the lack of support for uncertainty quantification limits its adoption in real-world decision processes. UQ provides critical information about model reliability, helps avoid overfitting by accounting for data uncertainty, and offers insights for decision-making. The field lacks a dedicated survey addressing this gap.
AI.method: This paper is a survey that systematically reviews and organizes the existing literature on uncertainty quantification in symbolic regression into three broad research directions: frequentist approaches, Bayesian approaches, and model selection-based approaches.
AI.result: The survey finds that UQ in symbolic regression is still underexplored, despite its acknowledged importance. Existing methods can be categorized into three directions — frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection — but significant gaps remain in developing reliable UQ methods tailored for SR.
AI.conclusion: The underexplored nature of UQ in SR motivates the need for further research into developing reliable uncertainty quantification methods, which would enhance the practical adoption of symbolic regression in real-world decision-making contexts.

title: Attack Selection in Agentic AI Control Evaluations Meaningfully Decreases Safety
summary: An attacker that strategically chooses when to attack is much harder to catch than one that attacks indiscriminately. AI control is a safety framework for deploying capable but untrusted AI agents under the oversight of a weaker, trusted monitor and a limited human audit budget. Control evaluations stress-test these protocols by pitting a red-team attack policy against the blue-team monitor, but current evaluations typically assume attackers that do not strategically select when to attack. We study this capability, attack selection, in agentic settings by decomposing attack decisions into a start policy, which decides when an attacker should attack, and a stop policy, which decides when an attacker should abort an ongoing attack. Across two agentic settings, BashArena and LinuxArena, both policies substantially lower measured empirical safety without changing the underlying attack capability. At a 1% audit budget, our start policy reduces safety by 20pp on both BashArena and LinuxArena, and our stop policy reduces safety by 20pp on BashArena and 28pp on LinuxArena. These reductions should be interpreted as upper bounds on the effect of attack selection. Existing control evaluations may therefore yield overly optimistic safety estimates against selective attackers. We recommend that future evaluations, system cards, and safety cases elicit attack selection to produce more realistic safety estimates.

AI.tldr: Attackers that strategically choose when to attack are far more dangerous than indiscriminate attackers. The paper introduces attack selection in AI control evaluations, decomposing it into start and stop policies, and shows that both dramatically reduce empirical safety (20-28pp) at a 1% audit budget without changing underlying attack capability. Current evaluations likely overestimate safety against selective attackers.
AI.motivation: Current control evaluations for untrusted AI agents assume attackers that attack indiscriminately, but realistic adversaries would strategically time their attacks to evade detection. This gap means existing safety estimates may be overly optimistic. The paper aims to quantify the impact of strategic attack selection on measured safety.
AI.method: The authors decompose attack selection into two policies: a start policy (deciding when to launch an attack) and a stop policy (deciding when to abort an ongoing attack). They evaluate these policies in two agentic settings — BashArena and LinuxArena — under the AI control framework, pitting a red-team attack policy against a blue-team monitor with limited human audit budgets.
AI.result: At a 1% audit budget, the start policy reduces safety by 20pp on both BashArena and LinuxArena. The stop policy reduces safety by 20pp on BashArena and 28pp on LinuxArena. These reductions represent upper bounds on the effect of attack selection. Both policies substantially lower measured safety without changing the underlying attack capability.
AI.conclusion: Existing control evaluations likely yield overly optimistic safety estimates against selective attackers. The authors recommend that future evaluations, system cards, and safety cases explicitly elicit attack selection strategies to produce more realistic safety estimates.

title: Explain Like I'm 5 or Whatever I Choose: Evaluating the Interactive Potential of Language Model Responses
