summary: Evaluations of large language models (LLMs) in scientific information seeking tasks have become increasingly use-centric, such as conducting live or multi-turn evaluations with real users. These evaluations still assume a single, static chat interface, but as models are integrated into new interfaces, evaluations must shift to incorporate interface-specific criteria. We propose a new evaluation framework based on a formative study with $16$ participants that tests models' ability to generate multiple responses to one query that differ along an interpretable axis of language (language complexity), inspired by direct manipulation interfaces from human-centered design literature. We evaluate GPT-5.1, GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5 + Thinking, and DeepSeek-V3.1 by generating 5 responses at different levels of language complexity for $98$ scientific queries. While models vary complexity across responses, most changes remain inconsistent, with the best performing model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) only shifting reliable complexity measures in the correct direction $46\%$ of the time. Our findings hold with increased sample size and alternative complexity levels.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new evaluation framework for LLMs in scientific information seeking that tests models' ability to generate multiple responses at different levels of language complexity, inspired by direct manipulation interfaces. Evaluations of GPT-5.1, GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5 + Thinking, and DeepSeek-V3.1 reveal that while models can vary complexity, changes are inconsistent — the best model shifts complexity correctly only 46% of the time.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluations for scientific information seeking assume a single, static chat interface, but as models are integrated into new interactive interfaces (e.g., direct manipulation interfaces), evaluations must incorporate interface-specific criteria. The authors aim to bridge this gap by testing models' ability to provide controllable, interpretable variation in responses.
AI.method: The authors conducted a formative study with 16 participants, then designed an evaluation framework that tests LLMs' ability to generate 5 responses per query at different levels of language complexity across 98 scientific queries. They evaluated GPT-5.1, GPT-5 mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5 + Thinking, and DeepSeek-V3.1, measuring whether complexity changes were in the correct direction using reliable complexity measures. Robustness was tested with increased sample sizes and alternative complexity levels.
AI.result: All models can vary language complexity across responses, but changes are largely inconsistent. Claude Sonnet 4.5 performed best, yet only shifted reliable complexity measures in the correct direction 46% of the time. These findings remained stable under increased sample sizes and alternative complexity level configurations.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs lack reliable, fine-grained control over response complexity — a critical capability for next-generation interactive interfaces. The proposed evaluation framework highlights a significant gap between user interface demands and model capabilities, calling for further work on controllable generation in scientific information-seeking contexts.

title: From Privacy to Workflow Integrity: Communication-Graph Metadata in Autonomous Agent Interoperability
summary: Agent-interoperability protocols such as A2A and MCP standardize what agents say to one another, but assume address-based transport over HTTP(S). Such transports protect message content, increasingly with end-to-end encryption. What they leave in the clear is the communication graph: which agent contacts which, when, and how often. In agent systems this graph is more consequential than a privacy framing suggests. Endpoints are often capability-labeled, workflows are structured and chained, and interactions are coupled to real actions, so an observer recovers more than past relationships. It can infer the pending workflow, the task being assembled and the action likely to follow. At machine speed, it can act on that inference before the workflow completes. The threat is therefore one of workflow integrity, not privacy alone: predictive leverage over autonomous action. We give a threat model for the agent communication graph; identify what makes agent metadata distinctively revealing (semanticity, prospectivity, actuation); define transport- and bootstrap-layer privacy properties and weigh candidate transports (SimpleX/SMP, Tor, mixnets) against them; and present an A2A case study in which a metadata-protecting binding is expressible but surfaces the protocol's identity assumptions. We test these on a generative model anchored to a real A2A capture. From passive metadata alone, with no payloads, a classifier recovers a task's class well above chance, from only the workflow's opening; applied together, the properties drive that recovery sharply back toward chance. Beyond what an observer can recover, we measure the leverage of acting on the leak: from a workflow's opening and under a fixed budget, an adversary choosing which workflows to act on realizes in this model most of a clairvoyant attacker's advantage over a metadata-blind one, and the same properties suppress it.

AI.tldr: Agent communication protocols like A2A/MCP protect message content via encryption but expose the communication graph (who contacts whom, when, how often). This metadata leak is uniquely dangerous in agent systems because it reveals workflow structure, pending tasks, and likely future actions — enabling adversaries to predict and disrupt autonomous workflows before they complete. The paper formalizes this as a workflow integrity threat, defines privacy properties to counter it, evaluates candidate transports, and demonstrates experimentally that metadata-protecting properties drive task inference back toward chance while neutralizing adversarial leverage.
AI.motivation: Existing agent-interoperability protocols (A2A, MCP) assume address-based HTTP(S) transport, which — even with end-to-end encryption — leaves the communication graph in the clear. In agent systems, metadata is uniquely revealing because agent endpoints are capability-labeled, interactions are structured into chains, and communication is coupled to real-world actions. An observer can infer not just past relationships but the pending workflow, the task being assembled, and the next likely action — and at machine speed, act on that inference before the workflow completes. This elevates the threat from privacy to workflow integrity: adversarial predictive leverage over autonomous action.
AI.method: The authors (1) construct a formal threat model for the agent communication graph; (2) identify three distinctive properties of agent metadata — semanticity (endpoints carry capability semantics), prospectivity (communication patterns reveal future actions), and actuation (messages couple to real-world effects); (3) define transport-layer and bootstrap-layer privacy properties (e.g., sender-receiver unlinkability, relationship anonymity); (4) evaluate candidate metadata-protecting transports (SimpleX/SMP, Tor, mixnets) against these properties; (5) present an A2A case study exposing identity assumptions that complicate metadata-protecting bindings; and (6) validate experimentally using a generative model anchored to real A2A traces, measuring both task-class recovery from passive metadata and adversarial advantage under a fixed budget.
AI.result: From passive metadata alone (no payloads), a classifier recovers a task's class well above chance using only the workflow's opening. When the defined metadata-protecting properties are applied together, recovery is driven sharply back toward chance. Under a fixed budget, an adversary choosing which workflows to act on from the opening metadata realizes most of a clairvoyant attacker's advantage over a metadata-blind one; the same privacy properties suppress this advantage.
AI.conclusion: Metadata in agent communication graphs constitutes a workflow integrity threat, not merely a privacy concern. The semantic, prospective, and actuated nature of agent metadata gives adversaries predictive leverage over autonomous actions. Transport- and bootstrap-layer privacy properties can effectively neutralize both passive inference and active adversarial advantage, but protocol-level identity assumptions (as surfaced in A2A) pose practical deployment challenges that must be addressed for metadata-protecting agent communication to be viable.

title: Pomona: Continuous Code Quality Improvement via Small, Automated Changes at Bloomberg
summary: In this short experience paper, we present Pomona, a lightweight agentic tool that utilises agent skills for continuous automated code quality improvement. Inspired by the philosophy of Kaizen(TM), Pomona automates a cycle of discovery and incremental repair: a Scanning skill identifies improvement tasks (e.g., linting violations, technical debt markers, and test gaps) and prioritises them in a structured backlog, while a Repair skill generates tiny pull requests (PRs) targeting ~10 lines of diff. This human-in-the-loop design enables frequent, low-risk improvements while maintaining engineer trust and productivity and reducing technical debt. We evaluated Pomona through a one-month deployment in a team and a questionnaire distributed to 10 senior engineers. Our preliminary results are promising: 15 of 17 generated PRs were successfully merged with a median time-to-close of under 2 hours. Furthermore, 8/10 of surveyed engineers expressed a desire to adopt Pomona, praising small diff sizes and Pomona's focus on improving code quality. We conclude by discussing actionable insights for researchers and practitioners on strategies for effective agentic deployment in industry.

AI.tldr: Pomona is a lightweight agentic tool that automates continuous code quality improvement through a Kaizen-inspired cycle: a Scanning skill identifies and prioritizes improvement tasks, while a Repair skill generates small (~10-line) pull requests. In a one-month deployment, 15/17 PRs were merged (median time-to-close under 2 hours), and 8/10 senior engineers wanted to adopt it.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the persistent challenge of technical debt and code quality degradation in software engineering. Traditional approaches often involve large, disruptive refactoring efforts that risk engineer trust and productivity. Inspired by the Kaizen philosophy of continuous, incremental improvement, the work explores whether a lightweight agentic tool can enable frequent, low-risk, automated code quality repairs while maintaining human oversight and engineer buy-in.
AI.method: Pomona employs two agent skills in a human-in-the-loop architecture: (1) a Scanning skill that discovers improvement opportunities — linting violations, technical debt markers, and test gaps — and prioritizes them in a structured backlog; (2) a Repair skill that generates small pull requests with diffs targeting approximately 10 lines. Evaluation was conducted via a one-month real-world deployment within a team and a questionnaire distributed to 10 senior engineers, measuring PR merge rates, time-to-close, and subjective adoption willingness.
AI.result: Of 17 pull requests generated by Pomona, 15 were successfully merged, yielding an 88% acceptance rate. The median time-to-close was under 2 hours, indicating rapid review and integration cycles. In the post-deployment survey, 8 out of 10 senior engineers expressed a desire to adopt Pomona, citing the small diff sizes and the tool's focus on code quality as key positive attributes.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that lightweight, agentic, Kaizen-inspired automation can effectively support continuous code quality improvement in industrial settings. The high PR merge rate, fast turnaround, and strong engineer endorsement suggest that small, incremental, human-in-the-loop repairs foster trust and productivity. The paper ends with actionable insights for deploying agentic tools in industry, emphasizing the value of constrained diff sizes and transparent prioritization.

title: AMD-FCG: An Enhanced Function Call Graph Dataset with Integrated Topological Features for Malware Detection and Classification
summary: As malware illustrates a complex structure and behavior, detection of these has been a significant challenge in the domain of cybersecurity along with related services in daily life. So, it becomes crucial to have a reliable and adaptive solution to address the issue. Among the several detection methods developed over the years, one of the most reliable ones is studying and analyzing the structural and behavioral patterns of malware. These patterns of sophisticated malware can be obtained with the help of Function Call Graphs (FCGs). However, to effectively cover numerous groups of families of malware, it is required to have a sufficiently large dataset for the system to operate on. In order to ensure accuracy and robustness of the system, the dataset should comprise samples of different malwares and a benign application for secure execution of the detection process. This paper introduces AMD-FCG, an enhanced Function Call Graph dataset integrated with topological features of malwares. The framework enhances the detection procedure, streamlining the workflow for cybersecurity professionals and also eliminating the need for dynamic analysis and extensive processing. Therefore, it can be used to develop and deploy more efficient and innovative malware detection systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AMD-FCG, an enhanced Function Call Graph dataset augmented with topological features of malware, designed to streamline static malware detection without requiring dynamic analysis or extensive processing.
AI.motivation: Malware detection is challenging due to complex structures and behaviors. Function Call Graphs (FCGs) are reliable for capturing structural/behavioral patterns, but effective detection requires large, diverse datasets containing both malware and benign samples. Existing workflows often rely on dynamic analysis and heavy processing, which are resource-intensive.
AI.method: The authors propose AMD-FCG, an enhanced Function Call Graph dataset that incorporates topological features of malware. The framework enables static analysis-based detection by leveraging structural properties of FCGs, eliminating the dependency on dynamic execution and extensive computational processing.
AI.result: The AMD-FCG dataset enables a streamlined detection workflow for cybersecurity professionals, supporting the development and deployment of more efficient and innovative malware detection systems.
AI.conclusion: AMD-FCG provides a robust, topology-enriched FCG dataset that simplifies malware detection by removing the need for dynamic analysis, thereby facilitating more efficient, accurate, and scalable malware detection solutions.

title: Beyond Matching: Category-Guided Latent Intent Reasoning for Generative Retrieval in E-Commerce
summary: Generative retrieval offers a new paradigm for e-commerce search by mapping user queries directly to product Semantic Identifiers (SIDs). However, e-commerce queries are often short, noisy, attribute-heavy, and associated with multiple category-consistent products, creating a substantial representation gap between natural-language shopping intent and artificially constructed item SIDs. Explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning can help bridge this gap, but its extra generation cost is difficult to reconcile with the low-latency requirements of online e-commerce systems. To address this challenge, we propose CaLIR (Category-guided Latent Intent Reasoning), a category-guided latent intent reasoning framework for e-commerce generative retrieval. Rather than generating explicit textual rationales, CaLIR learns continuous latent intent states before SID decoding and uses product category hierarchies as a natural scaffold for coarse-to-fine intent reasoning. Specifically, we introduce hierarchical semantic reasoning to align latent states with category-level shopping intent, and query-wise reasoning enhancement to model diverse intent paths under multi-positive queries. CaLIR further combines a query-specific dynamic prefix trie, assembled from pre-indexed category-level tries, with reasoning-aware constrained decoding. Experiments on multilingual e-commerce search datasets show that CaLIR achieves a better balance between retrieval effectiveness and inference efficiency than existing methods, while also demonstrating transferability and robustness across induced hierarchies and different generative backbones.

AI.tldr: CaLIR is a category-guided latent intent reasoning framework for e-commerce generative retrieval that replaces explicit Chain-of-Thought with continuous latent states scaffolded by product category hierarchies, improving retrieval quality without sacrificing inference efficiency.
AI.motivation: E-commerce queries are short, noisy, and attribute-heavy, creating a representation gap between natural-language shopping intent and item SIDs. Explicit CoT reasoning can bridge this gap but incurs prohibitive generation latency for online systems. A method is needed that captures multi-step intent reasoning without extra decoding cost.
AI.method: CaLIR introduces three components: (1) hierarchical semantic reasoning that aligns continuous latent intent states with category-level shopping intent using coarse-to-fine category hierarchies as a scaffold; (2) query-wise reasoning enhancement to model diverse intent paths under multi-positive queries; and (3) a query-specific dynamic prefix trie assembled from pre-indexed category-level tries, combined with reasoning-aware constrained decoding to ensure valid SID generation without explicit CoT text.
AI.result: Experiments on multilingual e-commerce search datasets demonstrate that CaLIR achieves superior retrieval effectiveness while maintaining inference efficiency comparable to non-reasoning baselines, outperforming existing methods in the effectiveness-efficiency trade-off. The framework also shows transferability and robustness across induced hierarchies and different generative backbones.
AI.conclusion: CaLIR demonstrates that latent intent reasoning, guided by category hierarchies, can effectively replace explicit CoT in e-commerce generative retrieval, delivering strong retrieval performance with low inference latency suitable for production online systems.

title: What Do People Actually Want From AI? Mapping Preference Plurality
comment: Accepted at the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '26)
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are often fine-tuned through Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to align with people's preferences and values. However, this method has known limitations: it aggregates conflicting preferences, often relies on unrepresentative samples, and uses only binary comparisons. Analysing 1,500 open-ended responses from the PRISM dataset across 75 countries, we examine what people actually want from AI systems and reveal concrete failures of current methods.
  We find that different people want different things: most values are requested by fewer than a quarter of respondents, with truthfulness the sole exception at 49%. Furthermore, the same words hide divergent meanings: when people describe what they mean by "truthfulness", they reveal distinct, potentially incompatible, epistemological bases, as some ask for sourced claims, some for expert opinions, and some even ask for unpopular views. Certain capabilities, namely how human-like a model behaves, and some features, like AI guardrails, are outright controversial, with some desiring them and others rejecting them. We additionally find that people often use contextual distinctions (what AI should do "by default" versus "if requested") that binary comparisons cannot capture.
  These findings expose fundamental problems in current alignment practices. When 49% request truthfulness but define it differently, this is unlikely to be captured by a single reward model. The persistence of high hallucination rates in well-funded models, despite users' clear demands for accuracy, suggests that current methods fail to identify actual preferences. This paper sheds light on the situated, contested, imperfect signals that are currently being flattened into universal preference models, a practice others have characterised as epistemic violence.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 1,500 open-ended responses across 75 countries reveals that RLHF fundamentally fails to capture diverse, context-dependent, and often contradictory human preferences for AI behavior—most values are minority views, "truthfulness" means incompatible things to different people, and binary comparisons flatten nuanced contextual distinctions.
AI.motivation: Current RLHF practices aggregate human preferences into universal reward models using binary comparisons from unrepresentative samples. The authors argue this approach may systematically misrepresent what diverse populations actually want from AI, potentially committing what others have termed "epistemic violence" by flattening situated, contested signals into monolithic preference models.
AI.method: Qualitative and quantitative content analysis of 1,500 open-ended responses from the PRISM dataset, spanning 75 countries. Responses were coded to identify (1) what values/features people request, (2) how they define key terms like "truthfulness," (3) which capabilities are controversial, and (4) what contextual distinctions people draw that binary comparisons miss.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Most values are minority preferences—only truthfulness exceeds 25% endorsement (at 49%); (2) "Truthfulness" conceals incompatible epistemological demands: some want sourced claims, others expert opinions, others unpopular/heterodox views; (3) Human-likeness and AI guardrails are polarized—some desire them, others reject them; (4) People make nuanced contextual distinctions (e.g., default behavior vs. on-request behavior) that binary preference comparisons cannot capture.
AI.conclusion: Current RLHF alignment methods are structurally inadequate: a single reward model cannot reconcile incompatible definitions of core values, and the persistence of hallucination despite user demand for accuracy suggests systemic failure to identify actual preferences. The paper critiques the practice of flattening diverse, situated, contested signals into universal preference models.

title: Generative Models Erode Human Temporal Learning Through Market Selection
summary: We argue that modern generative models create structural risks for knowledge and cultural production at current, sub-AGI capability levels. We define Human Temporal Learning (HTL) as path-dependent knowledge accumulation through sustained engagement with problems over time. Generative outputs increasingly resemble HTL-intensive work in surface features, so verifying whether a given output reflects genuine human learning grows costly relative to its expected benefit. Once verification loses economic justification, evaluators reward outputs regardless of production mode, and producers who invested years of learning compete on price against outputs that cost almost nothing to generate. We call this pathway value collapse and formalize it through a costly-inspection framework. Cross-domain evidence from academic publishing, legal practice, content platforms, and software security maps onto four stages of verification erosion. Alignment success is orthogonal. Better-aligned models narrow observable gaps between human and AI outputs, making source verification harder and intensifying competitive pressure against HTL-intensive work even when individual AI outputs improve.

AI.tldr: Generative models create "value collapse" for knowledge work: as AI outputs increasingly resemble human-crafted work, verifying genuine human learning becomes economically unjustifiable, leading evaluators to accept all outputs regardless of provenance, which undercuts producers who invested years in skill acquisition. This dynamic operates independently of alignment quality.
AI.motivation: The authors identify a structural risk from generative AI that is orthogonal to alignment: even well-aligned, high-quality models can undermine the economic viability of human knowledge production (termed Human Temporal Learning, HTL) by making verification of output provenance too costly relative to its benefit, thereby collapsing the value of sustained human expertise.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the "value collapse" pathway through a costly-inspection framework and provides cross-domain evidence across four contexts—academic publishing, legal practice, content platforms, and software security—mapping each onto four sequential stages of verification erosion. The argument is primarily theoretical and economic in structure.
AI.result: The analysis demonstrates that value collapse follows a predictable four-stage erosion pattern: (1) AI outputs match surface features of HTL work, (2) verification costs rise relative to benefits, (3) evaluators cease discriminating by source, (4) HTL-intensive producers face price competition from near-zero-cost AI outputs. This occurs regardless of alignment success, as better alignment narrows observable gaps and intensifies the dynamic.
AI.conclusion: Alignment success is orthogonal to value collapse and may paradoxically worsen it by making human and AI outputs less distinguishable. Safeguarding knowledge and cultural production requires interventions beyond alignment, specifically addressing the verification economics that drive the collapse pathway.

title: CARVE-Q: Quantum-Proposed, Classically Certified Interactive Driving Repair
summary: The critical question after a correct driving veto is not only whether a maneuver is unsafe, but whether the blocked interaction admits a lawful, auditable, and responsibility-bounded repair. Prediction and game-theoretic planners can suggest plausible cooperation, yet they do not return a proof that the repair respects hard rules, right-of-way, cost allocation, and ego fallback. We introduce CARVE, Certified Affordable Repair of Vetoed maneuvers via Envelopes, a certificate architecture for prediction-free interactive repair. Given a vetoed maneuver, CARVE constructs a finite repair lattice and emits a structured certificate recording the binding rule, selected joint repair, right-of-way-scaled cooperation envelope, responsibility-weighted cost split, and ego-only fallback. This certificate view reveals the algorithmic bottleneck: multi-owner repair induces a product lattice $M = \prod_j |\mathcal{A}_j|$. We therefore introduce CARVE-Q, a verifier-shielded quantum-AI search layer that applies quantum minimum finding only to this black-box lattice while leaving all safety authority classical. In the conservative verifier-oracle model, exact classical minimum finding requires $Θ(M)$ queries in the worst case, whereas Durr-Hoyer/Grover minimum finding uses $O(\sqrt{M})$ oracle queries with high probability. We prove verifier-shielded certificate soundness, priority non-elicitation, black-box query separation, and finite-precision reversible-oracle constructibility. We then demonstrate state-vector minimum finding on CARVE repair oracles up to 65,536 assignments and validate certificate preservation on Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION replay with 100% right-of-way respect, 100% blame consistency, and zero priority false positives. The result is a trust-bounded quantum-AI pattern for certified autonomy: quantum proposes; CARVE certifies.

AI.tldr: CARVE is a certification architecture for interactive driving repair after a vetoed maneuver. It constructs a repair lattice and emits a structured certificate recording rules, cooperation envelopes, and cost splits. To address the product-lattice bottleneck, CARVE-Q introduces a verifier-shielded quantum search layer using Grover/Durr-Hoyer minimum finding for O(√M) query complexity, while keeping all safety authority classical. The approach is validated on Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION datasets with 100% right-of-way respect and blame consistency.
AI.motivation: After a driving maneuver is vetoed as unsafe, existing prediction and game-theoretic planners can suggest cooperative repairs but cannot provide formal guarantees that repairs respect hard rules, right-of-way, cost allocation, and ego fallback. There is a need for an auditable, responsibility-bounded repair mechanism with provable compliance.
AI.method: CARVE (Certified Affordable Repair of Vetoed maneuvers via Envelopes) is a prediction-free architecture that, given a vetoed maneuver, constructs a finite repair lattice over joint action spaces and emits a structured certificate containing: the binding rule, selected joint repair, right-of-way-scaled cooperation envelope, responsibility-weighted cost split, and ego-only fallback. To handle the product-lattice bottleneck (M = Π_j |A_j|), CARVE-Q applies Durr-Hoyer/Grover quantum minimum finding to the black-box repair lattice, achieving O(√M) oracle queries versus classical Θ(M), while maintaining verifier-shielded classical safety authority. The authors prove certificate soundness, priority non-elicitation, black-box query separation, and finite-precision reversible-oracle constructibility.
AI.result: State-vector quantum minimum finding was demonstrated on CARVE repair oracles up to 65,536 assignments. Validation on Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION replay datasets showed 100% right-of-way respect, 100% blame consistency, and zero priority false positives, confirming certificate preservation.
AI.conclusion: CARVE establishes a trust-bounded quantum-AI pattern for certified autonomy: quantum search proposes candidate repairs, while classical CARVE certification verifies and certifies them, yielding provably compliant interactive repair with formal guarantees.

title: How reliable are LLMs when it comes to playing dice?
summary: We investigate the probabilistic reasoning capabilities of large language models through a controlled benchmarking study on discrete probability problems. We constructed two datasets, respectively a set of standard exercises and a set of counterintuitive exercises, designed to trigger heuristic reasoning, and evaluated 8 state-of-the-art models, each tested with and without Chain-of-Thought prompting. Models achieve an average accuracy of 0.96 on standard problems but only 0.59 on counterintuitive ones. We further provide empirical evidence of token bias: performance drops by over 20% when canonical formulations are replaced by disguised variants. Embedding misleading suggestions in the prompt reduces performance by up to 34%, with no model proving immune. Taken together, the reported findings suggest that current LLMs are not yet genuine probabilistic reasoners, despite their success in advanced mathematical problems.

AI.tldr: A controlled benchmark study reveals that while LLMs achieve 0.96 accuracy on standard probability problems, performance drops to 0.59 on counterintuitive ones. Models are vulnerable to token bias (>20% drop with disguised formulations) and misleading suggestions (up to 34% drop), suggesting LLMs lack genuine probabilistic reasoning despite strong mathematical performance.
AI.motivation: Despite LLMs' impressive success on advanced mathematical benchmarks, it is unclear whether they possess genuine probabilistic reasoning abilities or merely exploit surface-level patterns. The authors aim to systematically probe this distinction, particularly whether models can override misleading heuristics that often trip up human reasoners on counterintuitive probability problems.
AI.method: The authors constructed two datasets: (1) standard discrete probability exercises and (2) counterintuitive problems designed to trigger heuristic (incorrect) reasoning. They evaluated 8 state-of-the-art LLMs under two conditions: with and without Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. Two additional manipulations tested robustness: canonical vs. disguised problem formulations (to measure token bias) and embedding misleading suggestions in prompts.
AI.result: Models averaged 0.96 accuracy on standard problems but only 0.59 on counterintuitive ones. CoT prompting provided limited benefit on counterintuitive problems. Token bias was substantial: disguised formulations caused over 20% performance degradation relative to canonical ones. Misleading suggestions embedded in prompts reduced accuracy by up to 34%, with no model proving immune to this manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not yet genuine probabilistic reasoners. Their high performance on standard problems appears to rely partly on surface-level pattern recognition rather than robust probabilistic reasoning, as evidenced by susceptibility to heuristic triggers, formulation sensitivity, and misleading cues—failure modes inconsistent with true understanding of probability theory.

title: Chiseling Out Efficiency: Structured Skeleton Supervision for Efficient Code Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are capable of generating syntactically correct and functionally complete programs, greatly streamlining software development. However, recent studies reveal that these programs typically execute substantially slower than human-optimized counterparts. Existing approaches to bridging this efficiency gap typically involve either iteratively optimizing code after generation or fine-tuning models on corpora of efficient code. Yet, these methods expose the model to efficiency signals only by mimicking complete, optimized solutions, without explicitly encoding the structural code patterns essential for achieving high runtime performance. Addressing this gap presents two core challenges: (1) extracting and representing latent, efficiency-oriented structural patterns embedded within complex syntax and control flows, and (2) effectively learning these patterns without destabilizing the semantic training of LLMs. To tackle these challenges, we propose EffiSkel, an efficiency skeleton-guided framework that explicitly extracts and learns efficiency skeletons-abstract, reusable structural patterns underpinning efficient code-by leveraging three complementary strategies. These skeletons are integrated into a multi-task learning regime that jointly optimizes code generation and skeleton prediction. Experiments across multiple programming languages and benchmarks demonstrate that EffiSkel significantly enhances both functional correctness and efficiency, resulting on Mercury with DeepSeek-Coder (7B) a +11.11% (vs. EffiCoder) and +3.71% (vs. CodeDPO) higher Efficiency Ratio (ER), and a +0.36 (vs. EffiCoder) and +0.22 (vs. CodeDPO) increase in Average Speedup (AS). These results highlight the effectiveness of explicitly modeling efficiency skeletons in improving the runtime performance of code generated by LLMs.

AI.tldr: EffiSkel proposes an efficiency skeleton-guided framework that explicitly extracts and learns reusable structural patterns (efficiency skeletons) from efficient code via multi-task learning, jointly optimizing code generation and skeleton prediction, yielding significant gains in both functional correctness and runtime efficiency over prior LLM code optimization methods.
AI.motivation: LLMs generate functionally correct but runtime-inefficient code. Existing remedies (iterative post-generation optimization or fine-tuning on efficient corpora) only expose models to efficiency signals through complete solutions, failing to explicitly encode the underlying structural code patterns critical for high performance.
AI.method: EffiSkel uses three complementary strategies to extract efficiency skeletons—abstract, reusable structural patterns underpinning efficient code. These skeletons are integrated into a multi-task learning framework that jointly optimizes code generation and skeleton prediction, allowing the LLM to learn efficiency-oriented structural patterns without destabilizing semantic training.
AI.result: On Mercury with DeepSeek-Coder (7B), EffiSkel achieves +11.11% higher Efficiency Ratio over EffiCoder and +3.71% over CodeDPO, along with Average Speedup improvements of +0.36 over EffiCoder and +0.22 over CodeDPO. Gains are demonstrated across multiple programming languages and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and learning efficiency skeletons significantly enhances the runtime performance of LLM-generated code, outperforming methods that rely solely on mimicking complete optimized solutions.

title: On the Incentive Compatibility of Block Propagation in Bitcoin
summary: Bitcoin is permissionless and does not rely on any central administrator, which gives it strong censorship resistance. At the same time, it is important to incentivize miners to behave in ways that align with the interests of the system as a whole. This paper asks whether miners are individually incentivized to propagate blocks, one of the most fundamental processes in Bitcoin. Miners collectively maintain the blockchain by generating blocks and disseminating them across the network. If miners have an incentive not to propagate some blocks, this would indicate a fundamental flaw in Bitcoin's incentive design. Although prior work has studied how propagation delays affect forks and mining rewards, it has not fully characterized miners' incentives to improve block propagation under different tie-breaking rules. To address this gap, we derive analytical reward expressions for each tie-breaking rule based on a blockchain network model that captures the effect of forks on mining fairness. These expressions explicitly characterize how block propagation delays, hashrate distribution, and tie-breaking rules jointly determine mining rewards. We then use them to analyze miners' incentives to improve block propagation. Our results show, for example, that miners have no mining-reward incentive to relay blocks generated by other miners. By contrast, under the first-seen rule, every non-majority miner is incentivized to receive other miners' blocks more quickly and to propagate its own blocks more quickly. Finally, we compare tie-breaking rules and identify a trade-off between propagation incentives and mining fairness. In particular, the first-seen rule provides the strongest incentives to reduce propagation delays, but it also worsens mining fairness the most.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes whether Bitcoin miners have individual incentives to propagate blocks. It derives analytical reward expressions showing that under prevailing tie-breaking rules, miners lack direct mining-reward incentives to relay others' blocks, though non-majority miners are incentivized to accelerate their own block propagation under the first-seen rule. A trade-off exists between propagation incentives and mining fairness across tie-breaking rules.
AI.motivation: Prior work has not fully characterized miners' incentives to improve block propagation under different tie-breaking rules. If miners lack incentives to propagate some blocks, this would represent a fundamental flaw in Bitcoin's incentive design. This gap motivates a rigorous analytical treatment of how propagation delays, hashrate distribution, and tie-breaking rules jointly determine mining rewards.
AI.method: The authors develop a blockchain network model that captures the effect of forks on mining fairness. They derive closed-form analytical reward expressions for each tie-breaking rule (e.g., first-seen rule), explicitly parameterizing the interaction among block propagation delays, hashrate distribution, and the tie-breaking rule itself. These expressions are then used to analyze miners' marginal incentives regarding propagation behavior.
AI.result: Under typical tie-breaking rules, miners have no mining-reward incentive to relay blocks generated by other miners. Under the first-seen rule, every non-majority miner is incentivized to receive others' blocks faster and to propagate its own blocks more quickly. A trade-off is identified: the first-seen rule provides the strongest propagation incentives but also exacerbates mining unfairness the most.
AI.conclusion: Bitcoin's incentive structure contains a fundamental gap: miners are not individually rewarded for relaying competitors' blocks. Tie-breaking rule design involves an inherent tension between encouraging timely block propagation and maintaining mining fairness, with the first-seen rule representing one extreme of this trade-off.

title: RISE: A Rust Library for Inverted Index Search Engines
summary: Inverted indexes are a crucial data structure for efficient information retrieval in large text corpora. They enable fast full-text search by mapping each term to the documents in which it appears, on top of which efficient algorithms quickly retrieve the documents relevant to a user query. We present RISE, a novel inverted index library implemented in Rust, designed to deliver high performance and efficiency for information retrieval tasks. RISE leverages Rust's safety and performance to provide a robust solution for building and querying inverted indexes, while offering accessible extensibility through its expressive trait system. While developing RISE, we revisited the inverted-index literature, thereby reproducing numerous prior works using this new test bench. We evaluated RISE against existing libraries, demonstrating competitive query performance across various datasets and workloads, with speedups of up to 2x over the current state of the art. Our results indicate that RISE is a promising tool for researchers and practitioners in the field of information retrieval.

AI.tldr: RISE is a high-performance inverted index library built in Rust that achieves up to 2x speedup over state-of-the-art libraries, offering safety, efficiency, and extensibility for information retrieval tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing inverted index libraries fall short in balancing performance, safety, and extensibility. RISE aims to fill this gap by leveraging Rust's safety guarantees and performance characteristics while providing an expressive trait system for easy extensibility, and to serve as a reproducible test bench for prior inverted-index research.
AI.method: The authors implemented RISE, a novel inverted index library in Rust, revisiting and re-implementing numerous prior inverted-index techniques. They evaluated RISE against existing libraries across various datasets and workloads, measuring query performance.
AI.result: RISE demonstrated competitive query performance with speedups of up to 2x over the current state of the art across diverse datasets and workloads.
AI.conclusion: RISE is a promising tool for both researchers and practitioners in information retrieval, combining high performance with safety and extensibility.

title: HKJudge: A Legal Discourse-Annotated Corpus for Interpreting What Courts Find, How They Reason, and What They Rule
summary: Court judgments are central to legal practice and jurisprudence, yet discourse analysis of Hong Kong judgments has received limited attention, owing largely to the absence of expert-annotated corpora. We introduce the Hong Kong Judgment Discourse Dataset (HKJudge), the first sentence-level expert-annotated legal discourse corpus. HKJudge includes criminal judgments across all five levels of HK's court hierarchy, comprising $\sim$290k sentences and $\sim$6.5 million tokens, fully annotated by legal linguistics experts. We design a two-tier discourse schema that captures what facts a court finds, how it reasons, and what it rules. At the sentence level, each sentence is assigned one of 26 rhetorical roles. At the span level, sentences are further annotated with three sentencing elements (charge, imprisonment term, fine). Ten legal linguistics annotators produced the annotations with an inter-annotator agreement of $κ= 0.8$. We formulate two tasks on HKJudge, termed rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction, and provide the first benchmark evaluation of four BERT-based models, two open-source LLMs under zero-shot and fine-tuning settings, and four commercial LLMs on both tasks. Our work demonstrates the value of sentence-level discourse annotation for modeling the structure of HK judgments and provides a rich data foundation for future work on legal judgment prediction. The HKJudge dataset and code are available at https://github.com/xuanxixi/HKJudge.

AI.tldr: HKJudge is the first expert-annotated, sentence-level legal discourse corpus for Hong Kong criminal judgments (~290k sentences, ~6.5M tokens), annotated with 26 rhetorical roles and 3 sentencing elements. It enables benchmarking of BERT-based models, open-source LLMs, and commercial LLMs on rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction.
AI.motivation: Discourse analysis of Hong Kong court judgments has been largely neglected due to the absence of expert-annotated corpora. The authors aim to fill this gap by constructing a high-quality, linguistically annotated dataset to support computational modeling of judgment structure and to stimulate future legal NLP research on HK judgments.
AI.method: The authors construct HKJudge from criminal judgments across all five tiers of the HK court hierarchy. Ten legal linguistics experts annotate the corpus using a two-tier schema: (1) sentence-level: 26 rhetorical roles; (2) span-level: 3 sentencing elements (charge, imprisonment term, fine). Inter-annotator agreement is κ=0.8. Two tasks are defined — rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction — and evaluated using four BERT-based models, two open-source LLMs (zero-shot and fine-tuned), and four commercial LLMs.
AI.result: The annotation process achieves strong inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.8). The paper provides the first benchmark results for rhetorical role classification and legal element extraction on HK judgments, comparing BERT-based, open-source LLM, and commercial LLM performance across both tasks.
AI.conclusion: Expert sentence-level discourse annotation proves valuable for modeling the rhetorical structure of HK judgments. HKJudge establishes a strong data foundation for future legal judgment prediction research and is publicly released along with code to facilitate reproducible, comparative studies.

title: Skip a Layer or Loop It? Learning Program-of-Layers in LLMs
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Substantially extends arXiv:2507.07996. Code: https://github.com/tianyi-lab/PoLar
summary: Large language models (LLMs) perform inference by following a fixed depth and order, non-recurrent execution of all layers. We reveal the wide existence of training-free, flexible, dynamic program-of-layers (PoLar), where pretrained layers can be packed as modules and then skipped or looped to form a customized program for each input. For most inputs, substantially shorter program executions can achieve the same or better accuracy, while incorrect predictions of the original LLM can be corrected by alternative programs with fewer layers. These observations indicate that inference admits multiple valid latent computations beyond the standard forward pass. To efficiently achieve PoLar in practice, we propose a lightweight PoLar prediction network, which learns to generate execution programs that dynamically skip or repeat pretrained layers for each input. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PoLar consistently improves accuracy over standard inference and prior dynamic-depth methods, often while executing fewer layers, and that these gains persist under out-of-distribution evaluation. Our results suggest that fixed-depth execution captures only a narrow subset of an LLM's latent reasoning capacity.

AI.tldr: LLMs can dynamically skip or loop pretrained layers per input ("program-of-layers" or PoLar), achieving better accuracy with fewer executed layers than standard fixed-depth inference. A lightweight predictor network learns to generate these custom execution programs, outperforming fixed inference and prior dynamic-depth methods on math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM inference uses a fixed, sequential execution of all layers for every input. The authors hypothesize that this rigid depth is suboptimal and that many inputs require fewer (or different) layers for correct prediction. They aim to uncover and exploit alternative valid latent computations hidden within pretrained LLMs without any fine-tuning of the model itself.
AI.method: PoLar (Program of Layers): pretrained layers are treated as reusable modules that can be skipped or looped per input, forming a customized execution program. A lightweight PoLar prediction network is trained to generate these programs dynamically. The approach is training-free with respect to the base LLM — only the small predictor is learned, using the frozen pretrained layers.
AI.result: PoLar consistently improves accuracy over standard fixed-depth inference and prior dynamic-depth methods on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, often while executing fewer layers. Gains persist under out-of-distribution evaluation. The paper also demonstrates that incorrect predictions from the original LLM can be corrected by alternative, shorter programs.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-depth execution captures only a narrow subset of an LLM's latent reasoning capacity. The existence of multiple valid computation paths (PoLar) suggests that dynamic, input-dependent layer execution is a promising direction for more efficient and accurate LLM inference.

title: Position: Don't Just "Fix it in Post": A Science of AI Must Study Training Dynamics
comment: Accepted as an oral to the ICML: https://icml.cc/virtual/2026/poster/67142
summary: What would it mean to have a scientific understanding of AI? Models are not static objects: they are snapshots of time-evolving processes shaped by data, objectives, architectures, and optimization dynamics. Yet much of AI research treats models as fixed artifacts, analyzing behaviors after training rather than asking why they emerge. This position paper argues that a science of AI must move beyond post-hoc fixes and study the training dynamics that produce model behavior. Such a science should support progressively stronger forms of understanding: predicting outcomes from early training signals, intervening when trajectories go wrong, and ultimately designing training procedures that more reliably produce desired properties. Scaling laws have made prediction routine for loss; the challenge is extending this success to capabilities, biases, robustness, and safety-relevant behaviors. We articulate requirements for such theories grounded in the history and philosophy of science, examine progress in mechanistic interpretability, fairness, memorization, and simplicity bias, and identify concrete open problems.

AI.tldr: This position paper argues that the scientific study of AI should shift from analyzing models as static artifacts post-training to studying the training dynamics that produce their behaviors. It calls for predictive theories that can forecast model properties from early training signals, enable intervention, and guide the design of more reliable training procedures.
AI.motivation: Current AI research predominantly treats trained models as fixed objects for post-hoc analysis, neglecting the temporal, process-oriented nature of how models develop. This limits scientific understanding to description rather than prediction, intervention, and design. The paper draws on philosophy of science to argue that genuine scientific understanding requires moving beyond cataloging behaviors to explaining why and how they emerge from training dynamics.
AI.method: The authors present a conceptual framework grounded in the history and philosophy of science, articulating three progressive levels of scientific understanding: (1) prediction of outcomes from early training signals, (2) intervention when trajectories deviate, and (3) design of training procedures that reliably yield desired properties. They review existing work in mechanistic interpretability, fairness, memorization, and simplicity bias through this lens, and identify open problems for each level.
AI.result: The paper identifies that scaling laws have successfully routinized prediction for loss, demonstrating the feasibility of predictive theories in AI. However, extending this to capabilities, biases, robustness, and safety-relevant behaviors remains an open challenge. The review across subfields reveals fragmented efforts that could be unified under the proposed dynamics-centered framework.
AI.conclusion: A mature science of AI must treat models as processes unfolding over time, developing theories that can predict behaviors early, correct undesirable trajectories, and engineer training recipes with principled guarantees. This requires bridging empirical scaling work with mechanistic understanding, and the paper outlines concrete open problems toward this goal.

title: SkelDPO: A Skeleton-Guided Direct Preference Optimization Framework for Efficient Code Generation
summary: With the remarkable progress of Code Large Language Models (Code LLMs) in achieving semantic correctness, execution efficiency has become an increasingly important dimension for evaluating their practical utility. However, existing approaches typically treat full programs as a single optimization target during training, without explicitly modeling the structural factors that influence efficiency. As a result, although these models can generate semantically correct code, they fail to learn, at a fine-grained level, the underlying skeleton features that lead to efficient implementations. To address this limitation, we propose SkelDPO (Skeleton-Guided Direct Preference Optimization), a skeleton-guided preference optimization framework that systematically enhances the efficiency of code generation. SkelDPO first identifies efficient and inefficient implementations from the code dataset and, through comparative analysis, locates their efficiency-prone and inefficiency-prone points, forming alignment signals between efficiency and inefficiency skeletons. During training, a joint code and skeleton preference loss is introduced, enabling the model to learn semantic correctness while reinforcing its understanding of efficiency-critical components in code. Results show that SkelDPO consistently surpasses existing methods: compared with SOTA method that relies solely on efficient and inefficient code preference optimization, it improves Pass@1, Beyond@1, and Effi@1 by 3-6%, 3-7%, and 2-5%, with greater improvements observed on complex tasks. Overall, SkelDPO provides a new perspective on skeleton-level efficiency alignment, breaking the limitation of conventional preference optimization that relies solely on correctness or efficiency pairs. All datasets and source code are publicly available at: https://github.com/icpcSkelDPO/SkelDPO.

AI.tldr: SkelDPO introduces skeleton-guided preference optimization for code LLMs, identifying efficiency-prone/inefficiency-prone code skeleton points and using a joint code-skeleton preference loss to improve execution efficiency while maintaining semantic correctness.
AI.motivation: Existing Code LLM optimization approaches treat entire programs as monolithic optimization targets, failing to model fine-grained structural (skeleton) features that drive execution efficiency. Models learn semantic correctness but not the underlying implementation patterns that make code efficient.
AI.method: SkelDPO works in two phases: (1) It identifies efficient and inefficient implementations from a code dataset and locates efficiency-prone/inefficiency-prone points through comparative analysis, forming alignment signals between efficiency and inefficiency skeletons. (2) During training, a joint code and skeleton preference loss is introduced, enabling the model to learn both semantic correctness and efficiency-critical structural components simultaneously.
AI.result: SkelDPO consistently surpasses existing methods, improving Pass@1 by 3-6%, Beyond@1 by 3-7%, and Effi@1 by 2-5% over the SOTA method that uses only efficient/inefficient code preference optimization. Improvements are more pronounced on complex tasks.
AI.conclusion: SkelDPO offers a new perspective on skeleton-level efficiency alignment, breaking the limitation of conventional preference optimization that relies solely on correctness or efficiency pairs. It demonstrates that explicitly modeling code skeletons during preference optimization yields superior efficiency outcomes.

title: FDM: A Framework for Decision-making to build ML-based Malware detection systems
summary: Selecting appropriate machine learning (ML) configurations for malware detection is a complex, multi-criteria problem. Model choice, feature engineering, and update mechanisms must jointly satisfy operational constraints that vary across deployment contexts. This paper proposes the Framework for Decision-making (FDM) to build ML-based malware detection systems. The FDM formalises this selection process using the Weighted Configuration Compatibility Score (WCCS), a multi-criteria scoring function mapping five operational parameters (platform constraint, resource budget, response latency, update frequency, and detection sensitivity) to ranked recommendations across nine configuration dimensions. To validate the framework, four experiments were conducted on three datasets (a private Windows API dataset, the public Malimg image benchmark, and an Android static API dataset). Key results include: (i) XGBoost achieved the best accuracy-to-resource ratio in binary classification (97.46 % test accuracy, <70 MB RAM), outperforming LSTM/BiLSTM which consumed up to 2.8 GB; (ii) in multi-class classification, classical models (XGBoost 79.03 %) outperformed recurrent deep models (BiLSTM 72.27 %), reversing the binary ranking; (iii) class-incremental learning with EfficientNetB0 maintained 99.13 % accuracy with only 0.65 pp degradation across 11 incremental steps; (iv) transfer learning reduced training time by 2.14 times on average for image-based malware data without significant accuracy cost; and (v) autoencoder pre-processing yielded a 14 times training speedup at a cost of only 0.86 pp accuracy. These findings confirm that the optimal ML configuration is context-dependent, validating the FDM's core premise and demonstrating its practical utility for cybersecurity practitioners.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the Framework for Decision-making (FDM), a multi-criteria scoring system (WCCS) that maps five operational parameters to ranked ML configuration recommendations for malware detection. Experiments across three datasets reveal that optimal ML choices are highly context-dependent — XGBoost excels in resource-constrained binary classification, while classical models outperform deep learning in multi-class settings, and techniques like class-incremental learning, transfer learning, and autoencoder pre-processing offer significant efficiency gains with minimal accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Selecting ML configurations (model, features, update mechanisms) for malware detection is a complex multi-criteria problem where operational constraints — platform, resource budget, latency, update frequency, and detection sensitivity — vary widely across deployment contexts. Existing work lacks a systematic, formalized framework for navigating these trade-offs, leaving practitioners to rely on ad-hoc selection.
AI.method: The authors propose the Framework for Decision-making (FDM), formalized through the Weighted Configuration Compatibility Score (WCCS), a multi-criteria scoring function that maps five operational parameters to ranked recommendations across nine configuration dimensions. Validation involved four experiments on three datasets: a private Windows API dataset, the Malimg image benchmark, and an Android static API dataset, comparing models (XGBoost, LSTM, BiLSTM, EfficientNetB0, etc.) across accuracy, resource consumption, training time, and incremental learning performance.
AI.result: (1) XGBoost achieved 97.46% test accuracy with <70 MB RAM in binary classification, vastly outperforming LSTM/BiLSTM (up to 2.8 GB). (2) In multi-class classification, classical models (XGBoost 79.03%) surpassed recurrent deep models (BiLSTM 72.27%), reversing the binary ranking. (3) Class-incremental learning with EfficientNetB0 maintained 99.13% accuracy with only 0.65 pp degradation over 11 steps. (4) Transfer learning reduced training time by 2.14× on average for image-based malware data. (5) Autoencoder pre-processing yielded a 14× training speedup at a cost of only 0.86 pp accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The optimal ML configuration for malware detection is demonstrably context-dependent, validating the FDM's core premise. The framework provides cybersecurity practitioners with a principled, systematic tool for navigating the complex trade-off space of ML-based malware detection system design.

title: HKVM-RAG: Key-Value-Separated Hypergraph Evidence Organization for Multi-Hop RAG
summary: Multi-hop RAG poses a data-engineering problem beyond passage matching: under fixed retrieval budgets, a system must organize retrieved text into evidence units that expose answer chains. Dense retrievers score passages independently, while graph-based memories make associations explicit but often rely on pairwise or entity-centered keys that fragment multi-hop evidence. We present HKVM-RAG, a key-value-separated evidence-organization layer. It assembles answer-path hyperedges from cached passage-level LLM evidence tuples and uses them as retrieval keys, while retaining passage text as answer values. To isolate key-space design, our fixed-substrate protocol holds the tuple cache, candidate passages, reader, and evaluation budget constant across pairwise graph and hypergraph variants. Weighted hypergraph key-value retrieval improves over KG-PPR by +3.426 F1 on 2WikiMultiHopQA and +3.592 F1 on MuSiQue; HotpotQA shows that higher structured support coverage need not yield standalone answer-F1 gains. We therefore study WHG-KV as an evidence-control signal rather than a dense-retrieval replacement. Oracle and train-to-dev analyses identify support selection as repairable, and a dense-aware controller combines frozen ColBERTv2 and HKVM rank/score features using out-of-fold HKVM predictions. It reaches 88.846, 65.073, and 85.810 F1 on the three benchmarks, improving over ColBERTv2 by +11.084, +6.763, and +5.966 F1. Source-level ablations show that matched non-WHG structured signals do not match the WHG-KV gains. These results provide bounded evidence that key-value-separated hypergraph organization can serve as a reusable evidence-control mechanism for multi-hop RAG.

AI.tldr: HKVM-RAG introduces a key-value-separated hypergraph organization layer for multi-hop RAG that uses hyperedges as retrieval keys and passage text as values. Combined with a dense-aware controller, it improves F1 scores by +3.4–3.6 over KG-PPR and +5.9–11.1 over ColBERTv2 across three multi-hop QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Multi-hop RAG suffers from a data-engineering bottleneck: under fixed retrieval budgets, retrieved passages must be organized into evidence units that explicitly expose answer chains. Dense retrievers score passages independently and graph-based methods using pairwise or entity-centered keys fragment multi-hop evidence, motivating a hypergraph-based organization layer that preserves complete reasoning paths.
AI.method: HKVM-RAG proposes a key-value-separated evidence-organization layer: (1) it caches passage-level LLM evidence tuples, (2) assembles answer-path hyperedges from these tuples as retrieval keys, (3) retains passage text as answer values. A fixed-substrate protocol isolates key-space design by holding tuple cache, candidate passages, reader, and evaluation budget constant. A dense-aware controller combines frozen ColBERTv2 and HKVM rank/score features using out-of-fold HKVM predictions. Weighted hypergraph key-value (WHG-KV) retrieval is evaluated against KG-PPR and other structured variants.
AI.result: WHG-KV improves over KG-PPR by +3.426 F1 on 2WikiMultiHopQA and +3.592 F1 on MuSiQue. On HotpotQA, higher structured support coverage does not yield standalone F1 gains. The dense-aware controller achieves 88.846, 65.073, and 85.810 F1 on the three benchmarks, improving over ColBERTv2 by +11.084, +6.763, and +5.966 F1 respectively. Source-level ablations confirm non-WHG structured signals do not match WHG-KV gains.
AI.conclusion: Key-value-separated hypergraph organization provides bounded evidence as a reusable evidence-control mechanism for multi-hop RAG, not as a dense-retrieval replacement but as a complementary control signal that substantially boosts downstream QA performance.

title: Signal-Driven Observation for Long-Horizon Web Agents
summary: Web agents operating over long horizons ingest raw DOM and accessibility trees -- routinely tens of thousands of tokens -- at every action step, causing progressive context degradation that erodes reasoning well before tasks complete. We argue that this coupling of observation frequency to action frequency is an architectural mistake. Drawing on the insight from Recursive Language Models that querying a document outperforms reading it wholesale, we propose Signal-Driven Observation (SDO): a dedicated sub-call reads the full DOM but returns only task-relevant elements and their selectors, and is re-invoked only when a lightweight signal detector fires -- triggered by URL transitions, newly visible interactive elements, action failures, or exogenous browser events. We outline the open problems SDO introduces and call on the community to treat observation compression as a core architectural decision in web agent design.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that web agents degrade by ingesting full DOM/accessibility trees at every step. It proposes Signal-Driven Observation (SDO), where a sub-call extracts only task-relevant elements and refreshes only when triggered by lightweight signals (URL changes, new interactive elements, failures, browser events), framing observation compression as a core architectural problem.
AI.motivation: Current web agents couple observation frequency to action frequency, ingesting tens of thousands of tokens of raw DOM at each step. This causes progressive context degradation, eroding reasoning capability long before tasks complete. The authors argue this architectural coupling is fundamentally mistaken.
AI.method: Signal-Driven Observation (SDO) decouples observation from action via two mechanisms: (1) a dedicated sub-call that reads the full DOM but returns only task-relevant elements and their CSS/XPath selectors (compressed, actionable output), and (2) a lightweight signal detector that triggers re-observation only on salient events — URL transitions, appearance of newly visible interactive elements, action failures, or exogenous browser events.
AI.result: The paper is a position/vision paper and does not present empirical results. It outlines open problems introduced by SDO, including reliable signal design, selector robustness across DOM mutations, and trade-offs between compression fidelity and recall of task-relevant elements.
AI.conclusion: Observation compression should be treated as a first-class architectural concern in web agent design, not an optimization afterthought. The authors call on the community to explore signal-driven, query-based observation regimes inspired by Recursive Language Models.

title: Gaussian Process Latent Factor Regression for Low-Data, High-Dimensional Output Problems
comment: 9 pages content + 22 pages appendix/references. Supporting code at https://github.com/edstevenson/GPLFR
summary: In the sciences, regression tasks often require predicting high-dimensional outputs from few training examples. Multi-output Gaussian processes excel in low-data regimes but typically struggle with high-dimensional outputs. Compress-then-predict pipelines such as PCA-GP (principal component analysis plus Gaussian process regression) handle high dimensionality, but rely on bases optimized for reconstruction rather than prediction. To address this gap, we propose a model that represents each output as a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state drawn from a Gaussian process prior. By analytically marginalizing the decoder weights, we couple compression and prediction in a single objective that scales to high-dimensional outputs. We refer to this model as Gaussian process latent factor regression (GPLFR). We demonstrate GPLFR by building the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets.

AI.tldr: GPLFR is a model that couples dimensionality reduction and Gaussian process regression by representing high-dimensional outputs as linear-Gaussian decodings of a low-dimensional GP latent state, with decoder weights analytically marginalized out, enabling prediction from few examples. The authors demonstrate it via a spatially resolved climate emulator for rocky exoplanets.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional regression with few training samples is common in science. Multi-output GPs handle small data well but fail in high dimensions. Compress-then-predict pipelines like PCA-GP manage dimensionality but use reconstruction-optimized bases, not prediction-optimized ones. The gap is the lack of a unified framework that jointly optimizes compression and prediction for high-dimensional, low-data settings.
AI.method: The model represents each output as a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state drawn from a GP prior. Critically, the decoder weights are analytically marginalized out, yielding a single objective that couples compression (latent representation) and prediction (GP regression). This integrated objective scales to high-dimensional outputs.
AI.result: GPLFR enables the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets, a task with high-dimensional outputs and scarce training data, demonstrating practical utility in a cutting-edge scientific domain.
AI.conclusion: GPLFR bridges the gap between multi-output GP scalability and prediction-optimal dimensionality reduction, offering a principled joint framework for high-dimensional regression from few examples, with strong empirical validation on exoplanet climate emulation.

title: Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT): Fully Realising a GPU-based Symmetric Pseudo-Boolean SAT Solver
summary: We present Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT), a GPU-accelerated solver for pseudo-Boolean satisfiability based on continuous local search (CLS). AFSAT realises the proof-of-concept approach, FastFourierSAT, into a fully-engineered solver supporting any heterogeneous mixture of symmetric constraint types and lengths within a single problem instance. Using the JAX compiler, AFSAT leverages pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and just-in-time (JIT) compilation to perform massively parallel CLS across batches of candidate assignments. We demonstrate substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the proof-of-concept. We achieve this by way of identifying and addressing various limitations that arise from memory latency and floating-point representation, as well as leveraging automatic parallelisation and compact representations. The inherent representational and stability limitations of floating point are partially addressed by a tailored discrete Fourier transform implementation. We achieve near-linear throughput when scaling to multiple accelerators via JAX array sharding.

AI.tldr: AFSAT is a GPU-accelerated pseudo-Boolean satisfiability solver that improves upon the FastFourierSAT proof-of-concept by leveraging JAX for massively parallel continuous local search, achieving better numerical stability, runtime, and memory efficiency through a tailored discrete Fourier transform and near-linear multi-accelerator scaling.
AI.motivation: To transform the theoretical FastFourierSAT proof-of-concept into a fully-engineered, practical solver capable of handling heterogeneous symmetric constraint types and lengths within a single problem instance, while addressing the practical bottlenecks of memory latency, floating-point instability, and single-device scaling limitations that hindered the original approach.
AI.method: The solver employs continuous local search (CLS) over batches of candidate assignments, using JAX for pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and JIT compilation. A tailored discrete Fourier transform implementation mitigates floating-point representation and stability issues. Multi-accelerator scaling is achieved via JAX array sharding, yielding near-linear throughput. Compact representations and automatic parallelisation further improve memory efficiency.
AI.result: Substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the FastFourierSAT proof-of-concept. Near-linear throughput scaling when distributing computation across multiple accelerators via JAX array sharding.
AI.conclusion: AFSAT successfully demonstrates that a fully-engineered GPU-accelerated CLS-based pseudo-Boolean SAT solver is practical, overcoming the representational and stability limitations of floating-point arithmetic through a tailored DFT, and achieving efficient multi-GPU scalability.

title: LLM Agent-Assisted Reverse Engineering with Quantitative Readability Metrics
summary:
Automatic decompilers produce functionally correct but often unreadable C code. This paper addresses one stage of the reverse engineering workflow: improving the readability of decompiled code using LLM agents guided by quantitative metrics.
  We present a three-phase research evolution. Phase 1 (tool-driven steering via Ghidra MCP) suffered from incomplete coverage and inconsistent improvements due to lack of quantitative guidance. Phase 2 (structural similarity validation alone) revealed that agents optimize for metrics in unintended ways, producing structurally equivalent but less readable code. Our contribution is the Quantitative Readability Score (QRS) framework, a composite metric combining a structural similarity gate with three independent readability sub-metrics (Lexical Surprisal, Structural Simplicity, and Idiomatic Quality).
  We demonstrate that QRS-guided refinement enables LLM agents to make targeted readability improvements without sacrificing correctness. We provide a discussion of the broader reverse engineering workflow (binary lifting, decompilation cleanup, and achieving functional equivalence) as context, however, it remains out of scope.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Quantitative Readability Score (QRS) framework, a composite metric combining structural similarity with three readability sub-metrics, to guide LLM agents in improving decompiled C code readability without sacrificing functional correctness.
AI.motivation: Automatic decompilers produce functionally correct but unreadable C code. Existing LLM-based approaches lack quantitative guidance, causing agents to optimize for metrics in unintended ways (e.g., producing structurally equivalent but less readable code). There is a need for principled, measurable readability improvement in the reverse engineering workflow.
AI.method: A three-phase research evolution: Phase 1 used tool-driven steering via Ghidra MCP (suffered from incomplete coverage); Phase 2 used structural similarity validation alone (agents gamed the metric); Phase 3 introduces the QRS framework — a composite metric with a structural similarity gate and three readability sub-metrics: Lexical Surprisal, Structural Simplicity, and Idiomatic Quality. QRS-guided refinement enables targeted, correctness-preserving readability improvements by LLM agents.
AI.result: QRS-guided refinement enables LLM agents to make targeted readability improvements without sacrificing correctness. Phase 1 and 2 failures demonstrated that tool-only or single-metric approaches are insufficient; the composite QRS framework successfully constrains agent behavior toward genuine readability gains.
AI.conclusion: The QRS framework effectively guides LLM agents to improve decompiled code readability while preserving functional correctness. Broader reverse engineering workflow aspects (binary lifting, decompilation cleanup, functional equivalence) are discussed as context but remain out of scope.

title: Blockchain Infrastructure for Intelligent Cyber--Physical--Social Systems:Post-Quantum Security, Interoperability, and Trustworthy Data Economies in the Era of Embodied AI
summary: The deployment of embodied artificial intelligence via world-model-based robotics presents a transformative opportunity for blockchain infrastructure, establishing urgent demand for trustworthy data provenance, cross-organizational governance, and incentive-compatible sharing across decentralized ecosystems. Simultaneously, quantum computing advances recognized by the 2025 Nobel Prize in Physics and the Turing Award threaten the cryptographic primitives securing these data economies, creating an interdependent imperative: long-lived verification for embodied AI depends on crypto-agile architectures capable of withstanding quantum adversaries. This tutorial examines blockchain as the coordination layer bridging this dual transition, from financial substrate to foundational Cyber-Physical-Social Systems infrastructure that simultaneously secures against quantum cryptanalysis and enables scalable, trustworthy data economies. The session opens with an immersive AWS Braket demonstration engaging participants with superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom hardware to assess cryptographic threat timelines and witness ECDSA-to-post-quantum signature transitions. Five integrated modules progress from embodied AI and world-model requirements through quantum hardware reality and evidence-based security migration, to scalable cross-shard architectures via BrokerChain protocols, trustworthy data economies implementing Croissant metadata standards and robotic learning provenance, and industry ecosystem integration for multi-modal cloud deployment. By bridging quantum hardware realities with embodied AI data requirements, this tutorial charts blockchain as unified infrastructure for next-generation decentralized intelligent environments, providing open-source frameworks and roadmaps for architecting quantum-resistant, interoperable, and data-trustworthy systems.

AI.tldr: This tutorial presents blockchain as the unifying coordination layer for two converging transitions: (1) embodied AI requiring trustworthy, decentralized data economies, and (2) the quantum threat to cryptographic primitives. It covers quantum hardware threat assessment, post-quantum cryptographic migration, scalable cross-shard architectures, data provenance standards, and multi-modal cloud deployment.
AI.motivation: Embodied AI via world models demands trustworthy data provenance, cross-organizational governance, and incentive-compatible sharing across decentralized ecosystems. Simultaneously, quantum computing advances threaten the cryptographic foundations securing these data economies. There is an urgent, interdependent imperative: long-lived verification for embodied AI requires crypto-agile architectures capable of withstanding quantum adversaries.
