AI.method: The tutorial integrates five modules: (1) embodied AI and world-model data requirements; (2) quantum hardware reality assessment using AWS Braket (superconducting, trapped-ion, neutral-atom) to evaluate cryptographic threat timelines with ECDSA-to-post-quantum signature transition demos; (3) evidence-based security migration; (4) scalable cross-shard architectures via BrokerChain protocols; and (5) trustworthy data economies using Croissant metadata standards and robotic learning provenance, culminating in industry ecosystem integration for multi-modal cloud deployment.
AI.result: The tutorial delivers open-source frameworks and roadmaps for architecting quantum-resistant, interoperable, and data-trustworthy systems. It bridges quantum hardware realities with embodied AI data requirements, positioning blockchain as foundational Cyber-Physical-Social Systems infrastructure rather than merely a financial substrate.
AI.conclusion: Blockchain can serve as unified infrastructure for next-generation decentralized intelligent environments by simultaneously addressing quantum threats through crypto-agility and enabling scalable, trustworthy data economies for embodied AI. The convergence of these two transitions makes blockchain the essential coordination layer bridging quantum-resistant security with decentralized AI data governance.

title: FLOWREADER: Min-Cost Flow Optimization for Multi-Modal Long Document Q&A
summary: Long, multimodal documents force retrieval-augmented systems to assemble answers from evidence fragmented across text, tables, and slides broken across cells in a long table, spread over multiple slides, or split between a figure and its discussion. Top-$k$ chunk retrieval treats each fragment independently and cannot represent how evidence connects. We introduce FLOWREADER, which reframes evidence assembly as a min-cost flow problem on a multimodal node graph: a single scoring vector $h$ controls source selection (via MMR), sink selection (via a length-aware answerability proxy), and the costs and capacities of every edge. The optimal flow is decomposed into candidate evidence paths, a compact non-redundant subset is selected by entropy-regularized replicator dynamics, and parallel VLM workers under a dual-process gate produce the answer with a single System-2 refinement pass triggered when answer consistency is low or the routed flow is strained. On VisDoMBench, FLOWREADER is best on the two subsets dominated by fragmented evidence PaperTab ($58.40$, $+1.30$ over G^{2}-Reader) and SlideVQA ($72.93$, $+0.62$) and competitive on SPIQA, FetaTab, and SciGraphQA. Macro-averaged across all five subsets, FLOWREADER ($65.47$) is within $0.74$ of the strongest baseline (G^{2}-Reader, $66.21$). Overall, these results show that min-cost flow performs well on fragmented multimodal evidence, where top-$k$ retrieval fails. It also provides a unified way to control scoring, routing, selection, and adaptive compute together.

AI.tldr: FLOWREADER reformulates evidence assembly from fragmented multimodal documents as a min-cost flow problem on a node graph, unifying source selection, routing, and answerability. It achieves SOTA on two VisDoMBench subsets focused on fragmented evidence and provides a unified framework for controlling scoring, selection, and adaptive compute.
AI.motivation: Long multimodal documents fragment evidence across text, tables, figures, and slides. Standard top-k chunk retrieval treats each fragment independently, failing to model how disparate pieces of evidence connect to answer a question. This motivates a holistic evidence assembly approach that captures inter-fragment relationships.
AI.method: FLOWREADER builds a multimodal node graph and solves a min-cost flow problem governed by a single scoring vector h. This controls: (1) source selection via MMR, (2) sink selection via a length-aware answerability proxy, and (3) edge costs/capacities. Optimal flow is decomposed into candidate evidence paths, from which a compact non-redundant subset is selected using entropy-regularized replicator dynamics. Parallel VLM workers then produce answers under a dual-process gate; a System-2 refinement pass is triggered only when answer consistency is low or flow is strained.
AI.result: On VisDoMBench, FLOWREADER achieves best results on the fragmentation-heavy subsets: PaperTab (58.40, +1.30 over G²-Reader) and SlideVQA (72.93, +0.62). It is competitive on SPIQA, FetaTab, and SciGraphQA. Macro-averaged across all five subsets, FLOWREADER (65.47) is within 0.74 of the strongest baseline, G²-Reader (66.21).
AI.conclusion: Min-cost flow is effective for fragmented multimodal evidence where top-k retrieval fails. FLOWREADER provides a unified mechanism for controlling scoring, routing, selection, and adaptive compute, demonstrating that a single optimization framework can handle the complexities of multimodal evidence assembly.

title: Data-Efficient Autoregressive-to-Diffusion Language Models via On-Policy Distillation
summary: We study the transformation of autoregressive models (ARLMs) into diffusion language models (DLMs). Rather than pretraining from scratch, prior work replaces the causal attention in ARLMs with bidirectional attention and then trains the resulting model using a DLM objective. However, these approaches incur two distribution shifts. First, transitioning from a next-token prediction objective to a DLM objective can discard knowledge acquired by the ARLM during training. Second, standard DLMs suffer from a train-inference mismatch, as the training loss is defined on randomly masked sequences rather than the trajectories encountered at inference produced by confidence-based decoding. To address both challenges, we introduce an On-Policy Diffusion Language Model (OPDLM) in which On-Policy Distillation (OPD) is employed for ARLM-to-DLM transformation. Specifically, OPDLM is trained via self-OPD, where the student, an ARLM with bidirectional attention, generates its own trajectories, and the teacher, the original frozen ARLM, distills its knowledge by providing target logits on these trajectories. By training directly in an on-policy manner, OPDLM eliminates the train-inference mismatch in DLMs, while distillation from the original model enhances knowledge retention from the ARLM. Empirical results demonstrate that OPDLM requires 15x to 7,000x fewer training tokens with strong performance across a wide variety of tasks. OPDLM avoids the prohibitive cost of DLM pretraining and positions DLM transformation as a form of ARLM post-training.

AI.tldr: OPDLM transforms autoregressive language models (ARLMs) into diffusion language models (DLMs) via On-Policy Distillation, where the ARLM student generates its own trajectories and the frozen ARLM teacher provides target logits, eliminating train-inference mismatch and preserving pretrained knowledge with 15x–7,000x fewer training tokens.
AI.motivation: Prior ARLM-to-DLM transformation methods suffer from two distribution shifts: (1) switching from next-token prediction to a DLM objective discards ARLM knowledge, and (2) standard DLMs have a train-inference mismatch since training uses random masks while inference uses confidence-based decoding trajectories.
AI.method: OPDLM uses On-Policy Distillation (OPD), specifically self-OPD: the student model (an ARLM with bidirectional attention) generates its own trajectories during training, while the teacher (the original frozen ARLM) distills knowledge by providing target logits on those trajectories. This on-policy training directly aligns training and inference distributions.
AI.result: OPDLM achieves strong performance across diverse tasks while requiring 15x to 7,000x fewer training tokens compared to prior approaches, making DLM transformation a practical form of ARLM post-training rather than costly pretraining from scratch.
AI.conclusion: On-policy distillation effectively bridges ARLMs and DLMs by resolving both the knowledge retention and train-inference mismatch issues, positioning DLM transformation as an efficient post-training paradigm rather than a prohibitively expensive pretraining endeavor.

title: Principles and Practice of Deep Representation Learning: or a Mathematical Theory of Memory
comment: version 2; TeX source and supplementary material at https://ma-lab-berkeley.github.io/deep-representation-learning-book/
summary:
In the current era of deep learning and especially generative models, there is significant investment in training very large generative models. Thus far, such models have been "black boxes" that are difficult to understand in the sense that they have opaque internal mechanisms, leading to difficulties in interpretability, reliability, and control. Naturally, this lack of understanding has led to both hype and fear.
  This book is an attempt to "open the black box" and understand the mechanisms of large deep networks, through the perspective of representation learning, which is a major factor - arguably the single most important one - in the empirical power of deep learning models. A brief outline of this book is as follows. Chapter 1 will summarize the threads that underlie the whole text. Chapters 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 will explain the design principles of modern neural network architectures through optimization and information theory, reducing the process of architecture development (long having been described as a sort of "alchemy") to undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus exercises once the underlying principles are introduced. Chapters 7 and 8 will discuss applications of these principles to solve problems in more paradigmatic ways, obtaining new methods and models which are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design, and yet no less - sometimes even more - powerful than the black-box models they resemble. Chapter 9 will discuss potential future directions for deep learning, the role of representation learning, as well as some open problems.

AI.tldr: The book aims to demystify large deep networks by explaining their internal mechanisms through the lens of representation learning, using optimization and information theory to reduce architectural design from "alchemy" to principled exercises, ultimately enabling models that are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design.
AI.motivation: Large generative deep learning models are treated as opaque "black boxes" with difficult-to-understand internal mechanisms, leading to problems in interpretability, reliability, and control — fueling both unwarranted hype and fear. There is a pressing need to open the black box and systematically understand how these models work.
AI.method: The book approaches the problem through the framework of representation learning, which it identifies as the single most important factor behind deep learning's empirical success. It employs principles from optimization and information theory to explain neural network architecture design, reframing what was historically considered "alchemy" into undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus exercises. The text then applies these principles to develop new methods and models that are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design.
AI.result: The book demonstrates that by applying information-theoretic and optimization principles, one can derive neural network architectures in a principled manner and construct models that match or exceed the performance of black-box counterparts while being inherently interpretable and controllable.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning is the central organizing principle for understanding deep networks. By opening the black box through this lens, the field can move beyond trial-and-error architecture design toward principled, interpretable, and controllable models, with significant implications for future deep learning research and applications.

title: A Study of Parallel Continuous Local Search
summary: We study parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) as a solution approach for Boolean satisfiability problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean (PB) constraints. Here, the $n$-variable PB-satisfiability problem is relaxed to a continuous optimisation problem with a differentiable objective function on an $n$-dimensional hypercube. For satisfiable instances, the global minimisers of this optimisation problem correspond to satisfying assignments of the SAT problem at hand. We present several novel findings via empirical experiments: (i) redundant constraints can inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS shows promise as a sub-solver in hybridised settings, quickly completing partial assignments; and (iii) local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality (i.e., degree of satisfaction), due to saddle-dense objectives where additional solver steps yield diminishing returns. Our findings inform practical uses of CLS for SAT on modern accelerator hardware.

AI.tldr: Parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) is applied to Boolean satisfiability with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints, revealing that redundant constraints hinder convergence, CLS excels as a hybrid sub-solver for partial assignments, and saddle-dense objectives cause rapid convergence to stable solution-quality distributions with diminishing returns.
AI.motivation: Investigate the practical viability of parallel Continuous Local Search as a solution method for SAT problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints, and understand its convergence behavior, the role of redundant constraints, and its potential in hybrid solver architectures—especially targeting modern accelerator hardware.
AI.method: The n-variable PB-satisfiability problem is relaxed into a continuous, differentiable optimization problem over an n-dimensional hypercube. Parallel CLS is then applied to this relaxed problem, and empirical experiments are conducted to evaluate convergence behavior, the effect of redundant constraints, hybrid sub-solver performance, and the nature of the optimization landscape (saddle-dense objectives).
AI.result: Three key findings: (i) redundant constraints inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS is effective as a sub-solver in hybrid settings, rapidly completing partial assignments; (iii) local search quickly reaches a stable distribution of solution quality due to saddle-dense objectives, where further steps produce diminishing returns.
AI.conclusion: CLS is a promising approach for SAT on modern accelerator hardware, particularly within hybrid frameworks where it can efficiently handle partial assignments. Practitioners should avoid redundant constraints and account for the rapid plateauing effect caused by saddle-dense landscapes.

title: Empirical Study on the Characteristics and Evolution of AI-usage in GitHub Repositories: Evidence from Code Comments
summary: Developers increasingly use AI tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, and Claude in everyday software workflows, but prior studies often evaluate LLM outputs in isolation rather than examining how developers adapt them in real projects. We analyze 35,361 GitHub code comments that explicitly reference AI use and their associated code blocks. We first open-code 500 unique comments and code blocks to derive a taxonomy of AI-assisted development activities, then annotate the full dataset using two LLM-based classifiers and aggregate predictions with Dawid-Skene expectation-maximization. We also analyze 12,996 subsequent commit messages to study how AI-assisted code evolves after introduction, and examine temporal trends from December 2022 to March 2026. Our results show that developers primarily use LLMs for code implementation, followed by code enhancement, debugging, documentation, and testing. Subsequent commits frequently involve refactoring and cleanup, feature integration and extension, and bug fixing, indicating sustained human oversight in adapting AI-assisted code. Over time, AI-referencing comments shift from direct code generation toward knowledge and conceptual support and code enhancement. These findings suggest that AI tools are becoming embedded not only as code-generation aids, but also as collaborative support mechanisms whose outputs are refined, extended, and corrected by developers over time.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 35,361 GitHub comments reveals developers use LLMs primarily for implementation, enhancement, debugging, documentation, and testing. AI-assisted code undergoes sustained human refinement via refactoring, feature extension, and bug fixing. Temporal trends show a shift from direct code generation toward conceptual support and enhancement.
AI.motivation: Prior studies evaluate LLM outputs in isolation, neglecting how developers actually adapt AI-generated code within real-world projects. This work addresses that gap by studying AI-referencing code comments and subsequent commit histories at scale.
AI.method: Collected 35,361 GitHub code comments explicitly referencing AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, etc.) and their associated code blocks. Derived a taxonomy of AI-assisted development activities through open-coding of 500 unique comment-code pairs. Annotated the full dataset using two LLM-based classifiers with Dawid-Skene expectation-maximization for label aggregation. Analyzed 12,996 subsequent commit messages to track code evolution. Examined temporal trends from December 2022 to March 2026.
AI.result: Primary AI-assisted activities ranked: code implementation (most common), then code enhancement, debugging, documentation, and testing. Subsequent commits predominantly involve refactoring/cleanup, feature integration/extension, and bug fixing — evidencing sustained human oversight. Temporal analysis reveals a shift from direct code generation toward knowledge/conceptual support and code enhancement over time.
AI.conclusion: AI tools are evolving beyond mere code-generation aids into collaborative support mechanisms whose outputs are systematically refined, extended, and corrected by developers. This suggests a maturation in human-AI collaboration patterns in software engineering.

title: DPAgent-in-the-Middle: Agentic Defense and Repair Against AI-Groomed Deceptive Patterns
summary: Privacy deceptive patterns in web interfaces systematically manipulate users into disclosing personal data, yet existing defenses are fragmented, static, and increasingly vulnerable to manipulation by large language models. Moreover, data voids, areas of information scarcity within the web ecosystem, create fertile ground for adversaries to inject misleading content that can be scraped and learned by AI systems, thereby amplifying both deceptive design and model misbehavior. In this paper, we formalize a new threat model, AI grooming, where attackers exploit data voids to seed benign-looking but malicious samples that corrupt model reasoning and normalize deceptive practices. To address this threat in privacy deceptive patterns, we present DPAgent, an agentic and reasoning-aware framework that orchestrates four specialized agents to mitigate the AI Grooming threat via a proactive defense that combines latent space purification with defensive prompting and operates directly in live web environments to proactively explore, detect, and repair privacy deceptive user interfaces before they reach end users. Extensive evaluations show that DPAgent detects 90.98% of groomed samples, achieves state-of-the-art privacy deceptive pattern detection with a micro F1 of 0.816, explores over 80% of pattern types while visiting only about 10% of the pages required by baselines, and successfully repairs 77% of detected deceptive interfaces. A large-scale study of 485 websites in the wild reveals that up to 98% contain at least one privacy deceptive pattern, over 90% of which can be mitigated by DPAgent. User studies further confirm that DPAgent effectively reduces privacy risks while preserving browsing experience. Our results demonstrate the promise of agent-in-the-middle defenses for securing the web UI supply chain against deceptive design and emerging AI threats rooted in data void exploitation.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes "AI grooming," a threat where attackers exploit data voids to inject samples that corrupt model reasoning, and proposes DPAgent—a multi-agent framework that proactively detects, explores, and repairs privacy deceptive web patterns, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing defenses against privacy deceptive patterns are fragmented, static, and susceptible to manipulation by large language models. Data voids enable adversaries to inject misleading content that gets scraped by AI systems, amplifying deceptive design. The paper identifies a gap in addressing how data void exploitation can be weaponized to corrupt model behavior and normalize deceptive practices at scale.
AI.method: DPAgent is an agentic framework comprising four specialized agents: (1) a latent space purification agent that identifies and cleanses groomed samples in embedding space, (2) a defensive prompting agent that inoculates model reasoning against deceptive inputs, (3) a web exploration agent that proactively crawls and discovers deceptive UIs, and (4) a repair agent that automatically patches detected deceptive interfaces. The system operates directly in live web environments as an "agent-in-the-middle" defense.
AI.result: DPAgent detects 90.98% of groomed samples, achieves micro F1 of 0.816 for privacy deceptive pattern detection, explores over 80% of pattern types while visiting only ~10% of pages needed by baselines, and repairs 77% of deceptive interfaces. A wild study of 485 websites found 98% contain at least one deceptive pattern, with >90% mitigable by DPAgent. User studies confirm reduced privacy risk without harming browsing experience.
AI.conclusion: The agent-in-the-middle defense paradigm shows strong promise for securing the web UI supply chain. DPAgent demonstrates that proactive, multi-agent systems combining latent space purification, defensive prompting, and live web intervention can effectively counter both existing deceptive design patterns and emerging AI-driven threats rooted in data void exploitation.

title: Constrained Dominant Sets for Multimodal Document Question Answering
summary: Long multimodal document question answering is limited by which evidence reaches the reader, rather than by the quantity retrieved. In lengthy documents, findings often recur across figures, captions, and introductory sentences, causing similarity based retrievers in modern multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems to allocate resources to near-duplicates while overlooking complementary evidence. This work introduces a retriever that selects evidence as a Constrained Dominant Set (CDS) on a query-augmented affinity graph, offering three advantages that similarity ranking does not. First, the query is encoded as a hard structural constraint, ensuring that every selected element is directly connected to the question through the cluster anchor. Second, the relevance-redundancy balance is determined automatically by a spectral bound, eliminating the need for manually tuned trade offs required by diversity-aware selectors. Third, the selection process achieves a global equilibrium via replicator dynamics, thereby avoiding the distortions introduced by greedy heuristics. The method is inherently graph-based and does not require training. Using a Qwen3-VL-32B reader, CDS establishes a new state of the art on VisDoMBench ($66.99$ average) and improves over the no-retrieval baseline by $37.1$ points on VisDoMBench and $4.8$ on MMLongBench-Doc.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a graph-based retriever that selects evidence via Constrained Dominant Sets (CDS), balancing relevance and redundancy without manual tuning. It achieves SOTA on VisDoMBench and significant gains on MMLongBench-Doc for long multimodal document QA.
AI.motivation: In long multimodal documents, similarity-based retrievers in RAG systems tend to retrieve near-duplicate evidence (e.g., recurring figures and captions) while missing complementary information. This redundancy wastes the limited evidence budget and harms QA performance.
AI.method: A training-free, graph-based retriever that frames evidence selection as finding a Constrained Dominant Set (CDS) on a query-augmented affinity graph. The query acts as a hard structural constraint (cluster anchor), a spectral bound automatically balances relevance and redundancy, and replicator dynamics achieve a global equilibrium — avoiding greedy selection pitfalls.
AI.result: Using a Qwen3-VL-32B reader, CDS achieves a new state-of-the-art average of 66.99 on VisDoMBench, and improves over the no-retrieval baseline by 37.1 points on VisDoMBench and 4.8 points on MMLongBench-Doc.
AI.conclusion: CDS provides a principled, training-free alternative to similarity-based and diversity-aware retrievers, demonstrating that constrained dominant set selection on a query-augmented graph effectively addresses the redundancy-overlooking problem in multimodal RAG for long documents.

title: Does Topic Sentiment Cause Perceived Ideology? Comparing Human and LLM Annotations in Political News Articles
summary: We ask whether topic sentiment has a causal effect on perceived political ideology, and whether the answer depends on who assigns the ideology label. Using articles from AllSides, paired with shared sentiment annotations from Llama-3.3-70b-versatile, we compare ideology labels from expert human annotators, GPT-4o-mini (baseline and finetuned), and Llama-3.3-70B. We apply Double Machine Learning (DML) and community-level mediation analysis across all four annotation paradigms. Human annotations yield no significant causal effects at the community level. Fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini achieves the highest classification accuracy (F1=72.48) and is the only annotator paradigm that produces significant community-level treatment effects and significant natural direct effects (NDEs) in mediation. We interpret this as evidence of shortcut learning: fine-tuning on ideology-labeled data causes the model to internalise a spurious sentiment--ideology coupling not operative in human judgment for this task. This coupling is structurally invisible to F1-based evaluation, with implications for the use of LLM annotations as silver labels and as proxies for human judgment in downstream causal analyses.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether topic sentiment causally influences perceived political ideology across human and LLM annotators. Using Double Machine Learning and mediation analysis, the authors find only a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini exhibits significant causal effects, which they attribute to shortcut learning rather than genuine human-like judgment. This exposes risks in using LLMs as proxies in causal social science research.
AI.motivation: To determine whether topic sentiment exerts a causal influence on perceived political ideology, and whether the annotator (human vs. LLM, fine-tuned vs. not) moderates this relationship, thereby probing the validity of using LLMs as substitutes for human judgment in downstream causal analyses.
AI.method: Political news articles from AllSides with sentiment annotations from Llama-3.3-70b-versatile are used. Ideology labels come from four paradigms: expert human annotators, GPT-4o-mini (baseline), fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini, and Llama-3.3-70B. Causal effects are estimated via Double Machine Learning (DML), with community-level mediation analysis decomposing total effects into natural direct effects (NDEs). Classification accuracy (F1) is also benchmarked across paradigms.
AI.result: Human annotations yield no significant causal effects. Fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini achieves the highest F1 (72.48) and is the only paradigm producing significant community-level treatment effects and significant NDEs. The authors interpret this as shortcut learning: fine-tuning injects a spurious sentiment–ideology coupling absent in human judgment, yet this coupling remains invisible to F1-based evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning LLMs on ideology-labeled data can induce spurious sentiment–ideology associations that mimic high performance but distort causal structure, undermining their reliability as silver labels or human proxies in causal social science research.

title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
comment: 28 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy110101013/fmscope
summary:
Objective. EEG foundation models (FMs) report strong accuracy on clinical resting-state EEG. However, high accuracy under subject-disjoint cross-validation remains ambiguous: it can reflect a genuine clinical biomarker, or subject-identity features that correlate with the label. We name this the Identity Trap and ask whether it can be diagnosed at the representation level before fine-tuning.
  Approach. We propose FMScope, a frozen-representation protocol packaging five diagnostics: variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. We apply it to three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 layout: subject relation of label x presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker.
  Main results. (i) The Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x a random null in 12/12 pairs, rising in all 12 under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). This dominance is a removable linear axis: erasing it improves label decoding where the label varies within subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp across external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is one subject carrier: removing it drops the subject probe by 9-19 pp on LaBraM and CBraMod. REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only in cells with a literature-established cross-subject marker.
  Significance. The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded instance of shortcut learning: the preferred cue has a measurable physiological component, and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope separates gains reflecting a biological marker from those reflecting subject identity.

AI.tldr: The "Identity Trap" is a confound where EEG foundation models exploit subject-identity features rather than genuine clinical biomarkers under subject-disjoint cross-validation. The authors propose FMScope, a frozen-representation diagnostic protocol, to disentangle these sources of accuracy before fine-tuning. Applied across three FMs and four datasets, they find that subject variance universally dominates, is linearly removable, and is partly carried by aperiodic 1/f activity. Fine-tuning amplifies label-relevant variance only when a true cross-subject EEG marker exists.
AI.motivation: Subject-disjoint cross-validation is the standard for evaluating EEG-based biomarkers, but high accuracy can be driven either by a genuine disease signature or by subject-identity features spuriously correlated with the label (the "Identity Trap"). No diagnostic framework existed to distinguish these two sources at the level of frozen pretrained representations, motivating FMScope.
AI.method: FMScope is a frozen-representation protocol with five diagnostics: (1) variance decomposition to quantify subject vs. label variance; (2) subject-axis erasure via linear removal to test whether subject dominance is separable; (3) aperiodic 1/f ablation to assess a physiological carrier of subject identity; (4) layer-wise label probing to track where label information emerges; and (5) within-subject direction consistency. The protocol is tested on three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets arranged in a 2×2 design: whether the label varies within-subject × whether a literature-established cross-subject EEG marker exists.
AI.result: (i) Subject variance is 13–89× the random null in all 12 frozen FM–dataset pairs and increases under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). Erasing the subject axis improves label decoding where the label varies within-subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp in external cohorts). (ii) Removing aperiodic 1/f drops subject-probe accuracy by 9–19 pp for LaBraM and CBraMod; REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label variance only in datasets with a known cross-subject EEG marker.
AI.conclusion: The Identity Trap is a physically grounded form of shortcut learning: the confound has a measurable physiological component (aperiodic 1/f), and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope can separate performance gains attributable to a genuine biological marker from those reflecting subject identity, offering a principled evaluation framework for EEG FMs.

title: AEGIS: A Backup Reflex for Physical AI
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation tends to fail gradually: one bad step degrades the state, and the policy spirals into a basin from which it cannot recover. The failure is often visible before it happens. We introduce AEGIS (Activation-probe Early-warning, Gated Inference Switching), a selective escalation method that uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect high-risk steps while there is still time to act. When the probe flags a step, control switches to a stronger separate policy, but only for the steps that need it. On LIBERO-Spatial, AEGIS recovers 10.1% of the trajectories the weak policy alone loses, versus 4.6% for budget-matched blind escalation and 5.1% for a random-trigger placebo. These gains are significant under one-sided exact paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment over three pre-registered contrasts: +5.4pp over blind escalation, p=8.5e-6; +5.0pp over random triggering, p=1.0e-4; paired-trajectory bootstrap CIs exclude zero. AEGIS activates the stronger policy on only 38% of steps, so the lever is timing rather than compute. The probe clears its precondition with an early-window AUROC of 0.764, 95% CI [0.70, 0.84], read from the weak-policy path over the first 30% of trajectory steps before any handoff. We pre-register the full analysis plan, including a conditional recovered-task-rate estimand and explicit kill criteria, and confirm the result on 700 common-random-number episodes per arm, with nA-fail=646.

AI.tldr: AEGIS uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect impending failure early, selectively escalating to a stronger policy only when needed, recovering significantly more failed trajectories than budget-matched baselines while activating the strong policy on just 38% of steps.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robot manipulation fails gradually—one bad step cascades into unrecoverable failure, yet the failure is often visible before it occurs. Existing solutions either blindly escalate to stronger policies (wasteful) or fail to intervene early enough. There is a need for selective, early-warning escalation that intervenes only when necessary.
AI.method: AEGIS employs a lightweight probe operating on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect high-risk steps early (within the first 30% of trajectory steps). When the probe flags a step, control switches to a separate, stronger policy only for the flagged steps. The method is evaluated on LIBERO-Spatial using 700 common-random-number episodes per arm (nA-fail=646). Statistical significance is assessed via one-sided exact paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment and paired-trajectory bootstrap confidence intervals, all under a pre-registered analysis plan with conditional recovered-task-rate estimand.
AI.result: AEGIS recovers 10.1% of trajectories the weak policy alone loses, outperforming budget-matched blind escalation (4.6%) and random-trigger placebo (5.1%). Gains: +5.4pp over blind escalation (p=8.5e-6) and +5.0pp over random triggering (p=1.0e-4); bootstrap CIs exclude zero. The probe achieves early-window AUROC of 0.764 (95% CI [0.70, 0.84]). The stronger policy is activated on only 38% of steps, demonstrating that timing rather than compute drives the improvement.
AI.conclusion: Selective, probe-guided escalation (AEGIS) effectively recovers failing long-horizon manipulation trajectories by detecting early-warning signals in weak-policy activations. The approach is statistically robust, compute-efficient, and validated through a rigorous pre-registered experimental design.

title: From Custom Logic to APIs: Understanding and Recommending API Replacement Refactorings
summary: Software refactoring is essential for maintaining code quality. However, API replacement refactoring, which replaces custom logic with API calls, remains underexplored. Existing refactoring tools provide limited support for detecting such opportunities because they rely on predefined templates and have difficulty capturing complex, multi-statement semantic equivalents. To address this limitation, we conduct the first empirical study of API replacement refactorings by mining 166,299 commits across six open-source Java projects and manually analyzing a curated subset of 1,800 commits, from which we identify 366 validated instances to characterize their scope, categories, and recurring patterns. Based on these insights, we propose AKIRA (Adaptive Knowledge Discovery and Retrieval), a hybrid framework that integrates pattern-deterministic heuristics with a refactoring-aware knowledge base to assess the practical feasibility of recommending API replacement refactorings. Our evaluation shows that AKIRA achieves 90% recall and 88% precision on a manually curated dataset. Furthermore, on the external RETIWA dataset, AKIRA significantly improves the state of the art by increasing recall from 21% to 81% and precision from 40% to 78%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining static pattern matching with semantic reasoning to support the automation of recommending complex API replacement refactorings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first empirical study of API replacement refactorings (replacing custom logic with API calls), identifying patterns from 366 validated instances in open-source Java projects. The authors then propose AKIRA, a hybrid framework combining pattern-deterministic heuristics with a refactoring-aware knowledge base, achieving 90% recall/88% precision on curated data and significantly outperforming the state of the art on the RETIWA benchmark (81% recall, 78% precision vs. prior 21%/40%).
AI.motivation: Current refactoring tools poorly support API replacement refactoring because they rely on rigid, predefined templates and struggle to recognize complex, multi-statement semantic equivalents. No prior empirical study has systematically characterized the scope, categories, and patterns of such refactorings, leaving a gap in both understanding and automation.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large-scale empirical study by mining 166,299 commits across six open-source Java projects, manually analyzing 1,800 commits to curate 366 validated API replacement refactoring instances. From these, they characterize patterns and categories. They then develop AKIRA, a hybrid framework that combines (1) pattern-deterministic heuristics for static pattern matching with (2) a refactoring-aware knowledge base enabling semantic reasoning, to assess feasibility and recommend API replacement refactorings.
AI.result: AKIRA achieves 90% recall and 88% precision on a manually curated dataset. On the external RETIWA benchmark, AKIRA dramatically improves recall from 21% to 81% and precision from 40% to 78% compared to prior state-of-the-art. These results validate the hybrid approach of static pattern matching plus semantic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Combining static pattern matching with semantic reasoning via a refactoring-aware knowledge base is highly effective for automating API replacement refactoring recommendations. The empirical study provides foundational insights into the nature and patterns of API replacement refactorings, and AKIRA demonstrates practical feasibility with strong performance gains over existing approaches.

title: HAVE: Host Active Verification Engine for Closing the Contextual Reality Gap in Security Digital Twins
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Security Digital Twins (SDTs) provide continuously updated virtual replicas of infrastructure for threat simulation, yet they rely on theoretical CVSS scores to assign lateral-movement probabilities -- creating the Contextual Reality Gap: risk is overestimated where unacknowledged mitigations neutralize exploits, and drastically underestimated where logic flaws bypass all memory-safety defenses. We present the Host Active Verification Engine (HAVE), an SDT extension that deploys a safety-constrained host agent to measure the empirical probability of compromise $\hat{p}$ via maximum-likelihood estimation over snapshot-isolated Bernoulli trials. A Wilson interval-width confidence weight $α_w$ propagates $\hat{p}$ into Monte Carlo simulations via a Bayesian blending rule formally related to the Beta-Binomial posterior. Evaluation across four vulnerability classes, three security tiers, and two production binaries shows HAVE reduces $P_{\text{reach}}$ by 38.2% in false-positive scenarios and increases it by 132.4% in false-negative scenarios, with a net +124.1% correction; post-HAVE estimates vary by only $1.12\times$ across calibration exponents $κ$, versus $4.6\times$ for CVSS-only baselines.

AI.tldr: The Host Active Verification Engine (HAVE) addresses the "Contextual Reality Gap" in Security Digital Twins (SDTs) where CVSS-based lateral-movement probabilities are unreliable. HAVE deploys a safe host agent that empirically measures compromise probability through maximum-likelihood estimation over snapshot-isolated Bernoulli trials, then propagates this into Monte Carlo simulations via a Bayesian blending rule with Wilson-interval confidence weighting. Evaluation shows major corrections: 38.2% reduction in false-positive scenarios and 132.4% increase in false-negative scenarios, yielding substantially more stable estimates across calibration exponents.
AI.motivation: SDTs use theoretical CVSS scores for threat simulation lateral-movement probabilities, creating a Contextual Reality Gap: risk is overestimated where unacknowledged mitigations exist (false positives) and underestimated where logic flaws bypass memory-safety defenses (false negatives). CVSS cannot capture real environmental mitigations or logic-level vulnerabilities, leading to inaccurate risk assessment in digital twin environments.
AI.method: HAVE deploys a safety-constrained host agent that executes snapshot-isolated Bernoulli trials to empirically measure probability of compromise (p̂) via maximum-likelihood estimation. A Wilson interval-width confidence weight (α_w) is computed and propagated into Monte Carlo simulations using a Bayesian blending rule formally related to the Beta-Binomial posterior. The approach spans four vulnerability classes, three security tiers, and two production binaries for evaluation.
AI.result: HAVE reduces reach probability (P_reach) by 38.2% in false-positive scenarios and increases it by 132.4% in false-negative scenarios, yielding a net +124.1% correction. Post-HAVE estimates vary by only 1.12× across calibration exponents κ, compared to 4.6× for CVSS-only baselines, demonstrating significantly improved stability and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: HAVE effectively closes the Contextual Reality Gap by replacing theoretical CVSS-based probabilities with empirically measured compromise likelihoods, producing more accurate and stable SDT simulations. The Bayesian blending with confidence weighting ensures reliable propagation of empirical measurements into threat simulations.

title: Gated Bidirectional Linear Attention for Generative Retrieval
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Accepted at SIGIR 2026
summary:
In recommender systems, generative retrieval typically uses an encoder-decoder setup: an encoder processes a user interaction history, and an autoregressive decoder then generates recommended items. In large-scale streaming services, active users accumulate very long histories over time. As histories grow, the encoder becomes a major latency bottleneck because softmax attention scales quadratically with sequence length. In our experiments, using bidirectional attention in the encoder substantially improves quality. However, most sub-quadratic attention methods focus on causal attention.
  We propose Gated Bidirectional Linear Attention (GBLA), a linear-time bidirectional attention layer that extends kernelized linear attention with three lightweight components: local causal mixing (Conv1D), sequence-level key gating for soft forgetting, and a gated RMSNorm output. On a large-scale Yandex Music dataset, a hybrid encoder that interleaves self-attention (SA) and GBLA in a 1:2 ratio (one SA block followed by two GBLA blocks) matches bidirectional self-attention quality. On H100 GPUs, GBLA reaches up to an $8.2\times$ single-layer speedup at a history length of 32768, compared to FlashAttention-v3. Finally, we show that the same hybrid design generalizes beyond our proprietary setting, consistently preserving self-attention retrieval quality on public Amazon benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Gated Bidirectional Linear Attention (GBLA), a linear-time bidirectional attention mechanism that replaces quadratic-complexity self-attention in the encoder of generative retrieval systems. GBLA uses kernelized linear attention augmented with local causal mixing, sequence-level key gating, and gated RMSNorm to match bidirectional self-attention quality while achieving up to 8.2× speedup at 32K sequence length.
AI.motivation: In large-scale streaming recommenders, user interaction histories grow very long, making the encoder's softmax attention a latency bottleneck due to quadratic scaling. While bidirectional attention substantially improves retrieval quality, most existing sub-quadratic attention methods target causal (unidirectional) settings, leaving a gap for efficient bidirectional alternatives.
AI.method: GBLA extends kernelized linear attention with three lightweight components: (1) local causal mixing via Conv1D to capture fine-grained local patterns, (2) sequence-level key gating that enables soft forgetting of stale historical interactions, and (3) a gated RMSNorm output for stabilized training. A hybrid encoder interleaves standard self-attention (SA) blocks with GBLA blocks in a 1:2 ratio (one SA followed by two GBLA), balancing quality and efficiency.
AI.result: On the Yandex Music dataset, the 1:2 hybrid encoder matches the quality of full bidirectional self-attention. GBLA achieves up to 8.2× single-layer speedup over FlashAttention-v3 at a history length of 32,768 on H100 GPUs. The hybrid design also generalizes to public Amazon benchmarks, consistently preserving self-attention retrieval quality.
AI.conclusion: GBLA offers a practical, high-quality linear-time alternative to bidirectional self-attention for long-history generative retrieval, validated both on proprietary large-scale data and public benchmarks. The hybrid stacking strategy proves effective for balancing the expressiveness of full attention with the efficiency of linear attention.

title: Modular Monolingual Adaptation using Pretrained Language Models
summary: Building monolingual language models (LMs) for low-resource languages typically relies on adapting pretrained language models (PLMs) by finetuning the whole model on the target language. This approach is widely favored over training from scratch, as it enables effective knowledge transfer. Additionally, prior work has shown that using a language-specific tokenizer can enhance the adaptability. In this work, we hypothesize that full model tuning is often unnecessary and propose a more modular approach. Specifically, we replace the tokens, freeze the corresponding embeddings, and tune the rest of the model. We use Scottish Gaelic, Irish, and Quechua for our experiments, with Quechua being a very low-resource language (8.5k training instances). Evaluation on natural language understanding (NLU) tasks -- mask filling, NER, and POS -- shows that our proposed approach improves performance when adapting models to low-resource languages. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the effectiveness of training strategies, the choice of pretrained embeddings, and models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a modular adaptation approach for low-resource languages: replace tokens, freeze embeddings, and tune only the rest of the model, rather than full finetuning. Experiments on Scottish Gaelic, Irish, and Quechua (8.5k instances) on NLU tasks show performance gains, with analysis of training strategies, embedding choices, and model selection.
AI.motivation: Full model finetuning for adapting PLMs to low-resource languages may be unnecessarily expensive and suboptimal. The authors hypothesize that a modular approach — freezing token embeddings and selectively tuning other components — can be more effective, especially when using language-specific tokenizers.
AI.method: Replace the tokenizer with a language-specific one, freeze the corresponding (new) token embeddings, and tune only the remaining model parameters. Evaluated on NLU tasks: mask filling, NER, and POS tagging. Compared against full finetuning baselines across three languages including the very low-resource Quechua.
AI.result: The proposed modular approach improves performance on NLU tasks compared to full model finetuning when adapting to low-resource languages. Comprehensive analysis covers training strategy effectiveness, pretrained embedding choices, and model selection.
AI.conclusion: Freezing token embeddings while tuning the rest of the model is a more effective adaptation strategy for low-resource languages than full model finetuning, offering both performance gains and potentially greater parameter efficiency.

title: Capturing non-Markovian dynamics in non-equilibrium stochastic systems using flow matching
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, Accepted to 2026 Conference on Physics and AI (PAI26)
summary: Hydrodynamic models of stochastic particle systems represented by coarse-grained stochastic partial differential equations (SPDE), such as the regularized Dean-Kawasaki (DK) equation, do not accurately capture the short-time system dynamics that is dominated by non-Markovian effects, and low particle density regimes where the distributions are highly non-Gaussian. We develop a generative flow matching method that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations that explicitly incorporates non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects. As a demonstration, we use this method to simulate the Kramers first passage time problem for a system of non-interacting Brownian particles. We show the model accurately captures the short-time behavior and provides better predictions of the statistical moments of the number density when compared against the solution of the Markovian baseline, regularized DK equation.

AI.tldr: A generative flow matching method is developed to model flux probability distributions from particle simulations, capturing non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects that coarse-grained SPDEs miss, demonstrated on the Kramers first passage time problem.
AI.motivation: Standard hydrodynamic models like the regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation fail in two regimes: short-time dynamics dominated by non-Markovian effects, and low particle density regimes where distributions are highly non-Gaussian. There is a need for a method that can directly capture these effects from particle simulation data.
AI.method: The authors develop a generative flow matching approach that directly learns the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations. This data-driven framework explicitly incorporates non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects into the stochastic dynamics, bypassing the limitations of conventional coarse-grained SPDEs.
AI.result: The method was validated on the Kramers first passage time problem for non-interacting Brownian particles. It accurately captured short-time behavior and outperformed the regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation in predicting statistical moments of the number density.
AI.conclusion: Generative flow matching on particle fluxes provides a powerful alternative to SPDE-based hydrodynamic models, particularly for regimes where non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects are significant.

title: A Geometric Account of Activation Steering through Angle-Norm Decomposition
summary: Linear activation steering has gained popularity as a simple and empirically effective way to control language model behavior. More recently, spherical steering paradigms have been proposed to address limitations of additive interventions, often motivated by the assumption that hidden-state norm does not carry concept-relevant information. In this work, we revisit this assumption through a controlled empirical study designed to disentangle the roles of angular and radial components. We show that steering methods differ mainly in how they couple two geometric effects: changing a token's angular alignment with a concept direction and changing its hidden-state norm. Across seven language models, we find that concepts are represented primarily in angular structure, supporting the motivation for spherical methods, but that norm remains important for the stability and downstream effects of steering. Our results explain why interventions with similar concept-level effects can behave differently, and suggest that activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable angular and radial components of the intervention, rather than by a single additive coefficient that entangles these two effects.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the geometric roles of angular direction and hidden-state norm in activation steering for LLMs. It finds that concepts are primarily encoded in angular structure (supporting spherical methods), but norm is crucial for steering stability and downstream effects. The authors advocate for decoupling angular and radial components in steering interventions.
AI.motivation: Recent spherical steering methods assume hidden-state norm is concept-irrelevant, but this assumption lacks rigorous empirical validation. The paper aims to disentangle the distinct roles of angular and radial (norm) components in activation steering to explain why similar interventions produce different behaviors.
AI.method: A controlled empirical study across seven language models that systematically isolates and manipulates the angular alignment (direction) and radial magnitude (norm) of hidden states, comparing linear additive steering with spherical steering paradigms.
AI.result: Concepts are primarily represented in angular structure (supporting spherical methods), but hidden-state norm significantly affects steering stability and downstream token-level effects. Additive and spherical methods differ fundamentally in how they couple angular and radial changes.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering should be explicitly parameterized by separate, interpretable angular and radial components rather than a single additive coefficient that entangles both effects, enabling more principled and predictable control of language model behavior.

title: Porting Declarative UI to HarmonyOS: A Heuristic-guided LLM Approach
summary:
As an emerging operating system, HarmonyOS has a significant demand for software migration from platforms such as Android and iOS, where the User Interface (UI) translation accounts for a critical link. However, the latest UI development has shifted to declarative paradigms, e.g., Kotlin Jetpack Compose (KJC) for Android, SwiftUI for iOS, and ArkUI for HarmonyOS, rendering prior translation approaches inapplicable, as they target either backend logic or legacy imperative UIs.
  As such, this paper targets ArkUI and proposes an automatic translation approach, namely ArkTrans, to port UI files from Android and iOS to HarmonyOS.
  ArkTrans overcomes two salient challenges during the translation: (1) Programming Language (PL) unfamiliarity, and (2) severe syntactic chaos. Towards the first challenge, ArkTrans heuristically constructs ArkUI skeletons by extracting metadata from source PL, thereby guiding LLMs' initial translation. As for the second challenge, ArkTrans executes empirically revealed post-fixing rules via pattern matching to repair most of the remaining syntactic errors. To examine the effectiveness of ArkTrans, we construct a 100-sample parallel UI page translation benchmark from KJC/SwiftUI to ArkUI at the file level. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LLMs with direct/one-shot prompting cannot translate a single compilable UI page. In contrast, at most 90.67\% ArkTrans-translated files can be successfully compiled with high visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: ArkTrans is an automatic UI translation tool that ports declarative UI files from Android (Kotlin Jetpack Compose) and iOS (SwiftUI) to HarmonyOS (ArkUI). It addresses programming language unfamiliarity via heuristic skeleton-guided LLM translation and fixes syntactic chaos through pattern-matching post-fixing rules. Evaluated on a 100-sample benchmark, ArkTrans achieves up to 90.67% compilable files compared to 0% with direct/one-shot LLM prompting.
AI.motivation: HarmonyOS requires massive software migration from Android and iOS. With UIs now using declarative paradigms (KJC, SwiftUI, ArkUI), existing translation tools targeting backend logic or imperative UIs are obsolete, creating a gap for declarative UI-to-UI cross-platform translation.
AI.method: ArkTrans uses a two-stage approach: (1) Heuristic skeleton construction — extracts metadata from source PL to build ArkUI skeletons that guide LLM-based initial translation, mitigating PL unfamiliarity. (2) Post-fixing via pattern matching — applies empirically derived repair rules to correct residual syntactic errors. Evaluation uses a 100-sample parallel UI page translation benchmark (KJC/SwiftUI → ArkUI) at the file level.
AI.result: Direct prompting and one-shot prompting with LLMs yield zero compilable UI pages. ArkTrans achieves up to 90.67% successfully compiled files with high visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: ArkTrans effectively enables automatic declarative UI translation from Android/iOS to HarmonyOS by combining heuristic skeleton guidance with rule-based post-fixing, significantly outperforming standalone LLM prompting approaches.

title: The Sound of Malware: A Memory Forensics Approach for Android Malware Analysis via Audio Signals
summary: Android malware analysis is currently facing increasing challenges in achieving robust classification and detecting stealth attacks. Modern threats employ advanced evasion strategies such as code obfuscation, dynamic loading, packing, and even steganographic manipulation of traditional static and dynamic features. These techniques reduce the effectiveness of signature-based systems and degrade the reliability of Machine Learning models that depend on explicit semantic indicators such as permissions, API calls, or control-flow structures. In this work, we propose \approachname, a memory forensics malware detection framework that shifts the analysis perspective from semantic program modeling to signal-based structural representation. Both static bytecode and early-execution memory snapshots are transformed into audio waveforms through direct binary-to-waveform mapping, preserving low-level structural patterns without requiring disassembly or feature engineering. The resulting signals are processed using handcrafted spectral descriptors, Convolutional Neural Networks, and transformer-based embeddings. Experiments on CICMalDroid2020 dataset and VirusTotal malware demonstrate that \approachname achieves up to 98.0\% accuracy, outperforming static sonification and competitive state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a malware detection framework that converts Android bytecode and memory snapshots into audio waveforms, then classifies them using spectral analysis and deep learning, achieving up to 98% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Modern Android malware increasingly employs evasion techniques (obfuscation, packing, dynamic loading, steganography) that undermine traditional signature-based and ML-based detectors relying on explicit semantic features like permissions, API calls, or control-flow structures. A new perspective beyond semantic modeling is needed.
AI.method: The framework transforms both static bytecode and early-execution memory snapshots into audio waveforms via direct binary-to-waveform mapping, preserving low-level structural patterns without disassembly or feature engineering. The resulting signals are analyzed using three approaches: handcrafted spectral descriptors, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and transformer-based embeddings for classification.
AI.result: Experiments on the CICMalDroid2020 dataset and VirusTotal malware samples show the approach achieves up to 98.0% accuracy, outperforming static sonification methods and competitive state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from semantic program modeling to signal-based structural representation via memory forensics and audio sonification provides a robust and effective alternative for Android malware detection, resistant to common evasion strategies.

title: PaperFlow: Profiling, Recommending, and Adapting Across Daily Paper Streams
summary: Scientific paper recommendation is typically evaluated as static ranking over a fixed candidate set, yet real scientific reading unfolds as a daily, longitudinal process in which interests shift and feedback accumulates. We introduce PaperFlow, a framework that organizes it into three coupled stages: Profiling, which constructs and maintains a structured, inspectable scholarly profile from heterogeneous cold-start evidence; Recommending, which ranks each date-specific paper stream through multi-signal aggregation under a fixed display budget; and Adapting, which updates user state from semantically distinct feedback signals and models interest drift across days. We further define a longitudinal user-day benchmark that fixes users, dates, candidate pools, visible inputs, and hidden simulated relevance labels under a shared temporal information boundary. The benchmark contains 24 simulated research users, 50 daily paper streams, 1,200 user-day episodes, 20,727 unique papers, and 497,448 episode-paper records. We additionally specify a blind human-evaluation protocol to validate alignment between automatic metrics and expert judgments. Experiments against five scientific recommendation baselines show that PaperFlow achieves the strongest oracle-based ranking, the highest behavioral alignment with simulated reading selections, and the best blind human-evaluation score.

AI.tldr: PaperFlow is a longitudinal scientific paper recommendation framework with three stages—Profiling, Recommending, and Adapting—that models daily interest drift through multi-signal aggregation and feedback-based state updates, evaluated on a new benchmark of 24 simulated users across 1,200 user-day episodes, outperforming five baselines on ranking, behavioral alignment, and human evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing scientific paper recommendation is evaluated as static ranking over fixed candidate sets, which fails to capture the longitudinal, daily nature of real scientific reading where user interests shift and feedback accumulates over time.
AI.method: PaperFlow decomposes longitudinal recommendation into three coupled stages: (1) Profiling — constructs inspectable scholarly profiles from heterogeneous cold-start evidence; (2) Recommending — ranks daily paper streams via multi-signal aggregation under a fixed display budget; (3) Adapting — updates user state from semantically distinct feedback signals to model interest drift across days. A new longitudinal user-day benchmark is introduced with 24 simulated users, 50 daily streams, 1,200 episodes, and ~500K episode-paper records, plus a blind human-evaluation protocol for validating metric alignment.
AI.result: PaperFlow achieves the strongest oracle-based ranking, highest behavioral alignment with simulated reading selections, and best blind human-evaluation score compared against five scientific recommendation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Longitudinal, multi-stage recommendation with explicit interest drift modeling and heterogeneous feedback adaptation significantly outperforms static ranking approaches, and the proposed benchmark offers a reproducible foundation for future work on dynamic scientific recommendation.

title: When to Think Deeply: Inhibitory Deliberation for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reasoning Large Language Models can improve problem-solving performance through deliberative inference, but invoking slow reasoning for every input is computationally expensive and often unnecessary. We propose IDPR, a framework for response-conditioned inhibitory deliberation. IDPR first generates a concise intuitive answer and then uses an inhibition controller to decide whether that specific response should be released or suppressed in favor of slow reasoning. Unlike input-only routers, the inhibition controller conditions on the fast answer and fast-side evidence, including confidence, logit margin, parseability, and generation cost. We train the controller from paired fast-slow outcomes and select the inhibition threshold on a held-out validation set under an accuracy-first slow-call budget. On a held-out 5,000-example mathematical reasoning test set, IDPR invokes slow reasoning on only 8.20% of examples and improves accuracy from 47.90% to 48.92%. Under the same slow-call budget, random routing decreases accuracy to 46.76%, while the strongest confidence-based baseline reaches 48.22%. IDPR also achieves the highest corrective precision, showing that response-conditioned inhibition better identifies fast answers that benefit from slow reasoning.

AI.tldr: IDPR is a framework that generates a fast intuitive answer first, then uses a response-conditioned inhibition controller to decide whether to suppress that answer and invoke slow reasoning, achieving accuracy gains (47.90%→48.92%) while calling slow reasoning on only 8.20% of examples.
AI.motivation: Slow/deliberative reasoning in LLMs improves problem-solving but is computationally expensive and unnecessary for every input. Existing input-only routers make decisions before seeing the model's fast answer, missing valuable response-side signals about whether the fast answer is likely correct.
AI.method: IDPR first produces a concise intuitive answer, then an inhibition controller conditions on the fast answer and fast-side evidence (confidence, logit margin, parseability, generation cost) to decide whether to release or suppress it. The controller is trained from paired fast-slow outcomes, and the inhibition threshold is selected on a validation set under an accuracy-first slow-call budget.
AI.result: On a 5,000-example math reasoning test set, IDPR invokes slow reasoning on 8.20% of examples and improves accuracy from 47.90% to 48.92%. Under the same budget, random routing drops accuracy to 46.76%, and the best confidence-based baseline reaches only 48.22%. IDPR achieves the highest corrective precision.
AI.conclusion: Response-conditioned inhibitory deliberation is more effective than input-only routing for selective slow reasoning, better identifying which fast answers benefit from correction while maintaining a low computational budget.

title: Explainable Runtime Dependency Tracking for AI-RAN Conflict Monitoring
summary:
Future AI-integrated Radio Access Networks (AI-RAN) will combine open programmability with learning-enabled xApps, rApps, and control functions that act on shared parameters and key performance indicators (KPIs). For conflict monitoring, it is not enough to know which applications are deployed; the system must also know whether the parameter--KPI dependencies assumed by runtime diagnosis remain valid under the current operating regime. This paper studies a lightweight monitoring primitive for that purpose: tracking an interpretable dependency representation from streaming telemetry events.
  We represent active dependencies by a Boolean matrix and use Boolean matrix multiplication to check whether recent parameter-activity and KPI-response events are consistent with the current estimate. We propose a sliding-window inference procedure that reuses the estimate when it remains consistent and recomputes it when recent observations indicate structural change. The tracker is intended as an explainable signal for conflict diagnosis and slow-loop model refresh, not as an autonomous mitigation mechanism. Experiments on controlled Boolean event streams show efficient and accurate tracking under dependency changes and Boolean observation noise.

AI.tldr: A lightweight Boolean matrix-based monitoring primitive for AI-RAN that tracks parameter-KPI dependency consistency using sliding-window inference, enabling conflict diagnosis by detecting structural changes in dependency relationships from streaming telemetry events.
AI.motivation: In AI-RAN, multiple learning-enabled xApps/rApps share parameters and KPIs, creating potential conflicts. Existing conflict monitoring only tracks which applications are deployed, but cannot verify whether assumed parameter-KPI dependencies remain valid under changing operating regimes. A lightweight, interpretable mechanism is needed to continuously validate these dependency assumptions.
AI.method: Active dependencies are represented as a Boolean matrix. Boolean matrix multiplication checks consistency between recent parameter-activity and KPI-response events against the current dependency estimate. A sliding-window inference procedure is proposed: the estimate is reused while observations remain consistent, and recomputed only when recent events indicate structural change. The tracker serves as an explainable signal for conflict diagnosis and slow-loop model refresh, not as an autonomous mitigator.
AI.result: Experiments on controlled Boolean event streams demonstrate efficient and accurate tracking under both dependency changes and Boolean observation noise.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Boolean matrix-based tracking primitive provides a lightweight, interpretable, and effective mechanism for monitoring dependency consistency in AI-RAN, offering a practical building block for conflict diagnosis in dynamic network environments.

title: OpenSkill: Open-World Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures and 8 tables. Code is avalable at https://github.com/OpenLAIR/OpenSkill
summary: Self-evolving agents requires adaptation after deployment, but existing approaches assume a usable learning loop, such as curated skills, successful trajectories, or verifier signals. Real open-world deployments may provide none of these, offering only a task prompt. In this work, we study open-world self-evolution, where an agent must build both its skills and its own verification signals from scratch, using open-world resources but no target-task supervision. We propose OpenSkill, a framework that bootstraps this loop: it acquires grounded knowledge and verification anchors from documentation, repositories, and the web, synthesizes them into transferable skills, and refines those skills against self-built virtual tasks grounded in the anchors rather than in target answers. The open world thus supplies both the knowledge to be learned and a supervision-independent practice environment, with target-task supervision reserved for final evaluation. Across three benchmarks and two target agents, OpenSkill attains the best automated pass rate while satisfying the no-supervision constraint. Analysis shows its skills transfer across models without model-specific adaptation, and its self-built verifier aligns with ground-truth outcomes despite never accessing them.

AI.tldr: OpenSkill is a framework for open-world self-evolution where agents bootstrap both skills and verification signals from scratch using only documentation, repositories, and the web — no target-task supervision. It synthesizes transferable skills and refines them on self-built virtual tasks grounded in verification anchors, achieving top automated pass rates under the no-supervision constraint.
AI.motivation: Existing agent self-evolution approaches assume access to curated skills, successful trajectories, or external verifier signals. In real open-world deployments, none of these may be available — only a task prompt. This paper addresses the harder problem of bootstrapping self-improvement entirely from open-world resources without any target-task supervision.
AI.method: OpenSkill operates in three stages: (1) **Acquisition** — extracts grounded knowledge and verification anchors from documentation, code repositories, and web resources; (2) **Synthesis** — composes acquired knowledge into transferable, reusable skills; (3) **Refinement** — iteratively improves skills by practicing on self-constructed virtual tasks whose correctness is grounded in the verification anchors rather than target-task labels. Target-task supervision is reserved solely for final evaluation.
AI.result: Across three benchmarks and two target agents, OpenSkill achieves the best automated pass rate while respecting the no-supervision constraint. Skills transfer across different models without model-specific adaptation. The self-built verifier aligns with ground-truth outcomes despite never having access to them.
AI.conclusion: Open-world self-evolution without target-task supervision is feasible. OpenSkill demonstrates that agents can bootstrap their own learning loop — building both capabilities and verification mechanisms from open resources — yielding transferable skills and verifiers that generalize across models while matching ground-truth judgment.

title: MetaConfigurator: AI-Assisted RDF Authoring from JSON Data
comment: Submitted as post-proceedings for the deRSE26 conference
summary:
Scientific workflows increasingly generate structured JSON data that is easy to exchange but difficult to interpret consistently across systems due to lacking semantic interoperability. While JSON Schema ensures structural validation, it provides no native support for Linked Data semantics.
  This paper presents an RDF Authoring View extending the open-source JSON Schema editor MetaConfigurator, enabling researchers to transform existing JSON, YAML, or CSV data into RDF using AI-assisted RML mappings, refine triples, execute SPARQL queries, visualize knowledge graphs, and export RDF serializations within a single integrated web interface. This workflow is supported by ontology-aware IRI auto-completion, bidirectional synchronization between JSON-LD text views and RDF triple tables, and AI-assisted SPARQL query generation from natural language hints.
  We demonstrate the workflow using laboratory data from metal-organic framework (MOF) synthesis experiments. Protocol data describing reagents, procedure steps, and quantities is converted from JSON to ontology-based JSON-LD via RML mappings. We then refine the semantic representation, query relationships between experimental conditions and outcomes, and explore the resulting knowledge graph interactively. This integrated environment bridges conventional structured data management with Semantic Web technologies while preserving experimental context and lowering technical barriers through AI assistance.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an RDF Authoring View integrated into the MetaConfigurator JSON Schema editor, enabling researchers to convert structured data (JSON, YAML, CSV) into RDF using AI-assisted RML mappings, with features for triple refinement, SPARQL querying, knowledge graph visualization, and RDF export — all within a single web interface.
AI.motivation: Scientific workflows produce structured JSON data that lacks semantic interoperability across systems. JSON Schema provides structural validation but no native Linked Data semantics, creating a gap between conventional data management and Semantic Web technologies that hinders consistent interpretation of data.
AI.method: The authors extend the open-source MetaConfigurator JSON Schema editor with an RDF Authoring View that features: (1) AI-assisted RML mapping generation for transforming JSON/YAML/CSV to RDF; (2) ontology-aware IRI auto-completion; (3) bidirectional synchronization between JSON-LD text views and RDF triple tables; (4) AI-assisted SPARQL query generation from natural language; (5) integrated knowledge graph visualization and RDF export capabilities.
AI.result: The workflow is demonstrated on MOF synthesis laboratory data, converting protocol data (reagents, procedure steps, quantities) from JSON to ontology-based JSON-LD via RML mappings. The authors show successful semantic refinement, relationship querying between experimental conditions and outcomes, and interactive knowledge graph exploration.
AI.conclusion: The integrated environment successfully bridges conventional structured data management with Semantic Web technologies, preserving experimental context while lowering technical barriers through AI assistance, enabling researchers without deep Semantic Web expertise to produce interoperable Linked Data.

title: Fast Bounded-Independence Functions and Their Duals
comment: Full version of paper to appear in ITC 2026. 34 pages
summary:
We continue the study of {\em fast} functions, computable by linear-size circuits, that share useful properties of random functions. Motivated by cryptographic applications, we generalize and improve on previous results in this area, obtaining the following results:
  - For any constant $t$, we construct a fast $t$-wise independent hash function with algebraic degree $\log_2 t$ (over $\mathbb F_2$), simultaneously optimizing both asymptotic circuit size and degree.
  - We simplify and improve a recent construction (ITCS 2026) of a family of fast codes with fast duals, both meeting the Gilbert-Varshamov bound. Unlike the previous construction, our construction has negligible failure probability, can accommodate general fields and rates, supports a systematic encoding, and admits fast universal encoders.
  - We strengthen the above to support stronger random-like properties, such as optimal combinatorial list-decoding. This is achieved by constructing, for any constant $t$, a family of fast linear functions that map any $t$ linearly independent inputs to uniform and statistically independent outputs. Prior to our work, this was only known for $t=1$.
  We demonstrate the usefulness of the above results to cryptography. This includes the first nontrivial protocols for perfectly secure multiparty computation whose circuit complexity scales linearly with the number of parties, as well as protocols for computing encrypted matrix-vector products with optimal asymptotic circuit complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper advances the study of "fast" functions (computable by linear-size circuits) with pseudorandom properties.
