Key results include: (1) a fast t-wise independent hash family with optimal degree log₂t; (2) improved fast codes with fast duals meeting the Gilbert-Varshamov bound, featuring negligible failure probability and systematic encoding; (3) fast linear functions mapping t linearly independent inputs to uniform, statistically independent outputs (generalizing beyond t=1). Applications include perfectly secure MPC with linear circuit complexity in the number of parties and asymptotically optimal encrypted matrix-vector products.
AI.motivation: The work is motivated by cryptographic applications requiring functions that behave like random functions yet are extremely efficient — computable by linear-size circuits. Prior constructions of fast pseudorandom objects had various limitations (suboptimal degree, non-negligible failure probability, restricted to t=1 for certain properties). The paper aims to overcome these limitations and demonstrate cryptographic utility.
AI.method: The paper generalizes and improves prior constructions using algebraic techniques over F₂. For t-wise independence, it achieves degree log₂t via optimized polynomial-based constructions. For fast codes with fast duals, it simplifies a prior ITCS 2026 construction, eliminating failure probability issues and extending to general fields/rates with systematic encoding. The strongest result — fast linear functions with t-wise output independence — extends from the t=1 case by constructing families that map any t linearly independent inputs to uniform, statistically independent outputs.
AI.result: Three main constructions: (1) fast t-wise independent hash with algebraic degree exactly log₂t, simultaneously optimal in circuit size and degree; (2) fast codes with fast duals meeting Gilbert-Varshamov bound, with negligible failure probability, supporting general fields/rates, systematic encoding, and fast universal encoders; (3) for any constant t, fast linear functions mapping t linearly independent inputs to uniform independent outputs. Cryptographic applications: first nontrivial perfectly secure MPC with circuit complexity O(n) in party count, and encrypted matrix-vector products with optimal asymptotic complexity.
AI.conclusion: The paper substantially advances the theory of fast pseudorandom functions, closing gaps between what is achievable with random functions versus efficiently computable ones. The constructions achieve optimal or near-optimal parameters and enable new cryptographic protocols — notably perfectly secure MPC with linear scaling — that were previously out of reach. The results suggest fast functions can effectively substitute for random functions in a broad range of applications.

title: Bradley-Terry Rankings for Recommender Systems Across Dataset Taxonomies
summary: The ranking of recommendation algorithms is a challenging problem since model performance is sensitive to dataset characteristics such as sparsity, sequential structure, and scale. This drives a demand for a proper methodology for fair comparison between algorithms. Naive aggregation of performance metrics (e.g., averaging NDCG over benchmarks) can yield misleading rankings, undermining practical selection. To address this problem, we introduce a novel, data-driven ranking methodology based on Bradley-Terry (BT) model. We demonstrate that the obtained ranking depends on key dataset statistics. Additionally, we propose a novel metric for evaluating ranking consistency and demonstrate robustness of our ranking to incomplete data. Finally, we introduce a dataset-specific methodology for ranking algorithms on unseen datasets without running the models, relying on extensions of the Bradley-Terry framework, including BT trees and BT models with covariates.

AI.tldr: A novel Bradley-Terry-based ranking methodology for recommendation algorithms that accounts for dataset characteristics, provides robustness to incomplete data, and enables prediction of rankings on unseen datasets without re-running models.
AI.motivation: Ranking recommendation algorithms is difficult because performance is highly sensitive to dataset properties (sparsity, sequential structure, scale). Naive aggregation of metrics like averaged NDCG across benchmarks produces misleading rankings, hindering practical algorithm selection. A fair, data-driven comparison methodology is needed.
AI.method: The authors introduce a data-driven ranking methodology built on the Bradley-Terry (BT) model. They extend this framework with BT trees and BT models with covariates to enable dataset-specific ranking on unseen datasets without executing the models.
AI.result: The resulting ranking is shown to depend on key dataset statistics. A novel metric for evaluating ranking consistency is proposed, and the ranking demonstrates robustness to incomplete data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BT-based methodology provides a principled, fair, and practical solution for comparing and ranking recommendation algorithms across heterogeneous benchmarks and predicting rankings on new datasets.

title: Evidence Graph Consistency in Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Model-Dependent Analysis of Hallucination Detection
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Advanced Machine Learning and Data Science; to appear in the IEEE Xplore proceedings
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) reduces but does not eliminate hallucination in large language models. Existing detection methods rely on flat similarity between generated answers and retrieved passages, ignoring structural relationships among evidence pieces and answer claims. We propose Evidence Graph Consistency (EGC), a framework that constructs a local evidence graph per response and computes five structural consistency measures as hallucination indicators. Evaluated on the full question answering split of RAGTruth across six LLMs (5,767 responses), EGC reveals a consistent model-family split: graph consistency features show the expected diagnostic direction for hallucinations in Llama-2 models but exhibit systematic reversal in GPT-4, GPT-3.5, and Mistral-7B. This reversal suggests qualitatively different hallucination patterns across model families and indicates that embedding-based graph consistency cannot serve as a model-independent hallucination detection signal.

AI.tldr: EGC constructs per-response evidence graphs and measures structural consistency between claims and retrieved passages to detect hallucinations, but finds that graph consistency features reverse diagnostic direction across model families (Llama-2 vs. GPT/Mistral), undermining model-independent detection.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination detection for RAG relies on flat similarity metrics that ignore structural relationships among evidence and claims. The authors hypothesize that structural consistency between the evidence graph and claim graph could serve as a more nuanced hallucination signal.
AI.method: EGC constructs a local evidence graph per response from retrieved passages (nodes as atomic facts, edges as relationships) and computes five structural consistency measures against the answer's claim graph: node overlap, edge overlap, graph edit distance, embedding-based node similarity, and embedding-based edge similarity. Evaluated on 5,767 responses from the RAGTruth QA split across six LLMs (Llama-2-7B/13B/70B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Mistral-7B).
AI.result: For Llama-2 models, graph consistency features correlate negatively with hallucination as expected (hallucinated responses show lower consistency). However, GPT-4, GPT-3.5, and Mistral-7B exhibit a systematic reversal where hallucinated responses show higher graph consistency. This reveals qualitatively different hallucination patterns across model families.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based graph consistency cannot serve as a model-independent hallucination detection signal. The model-family split suggests that stronger models may hallucinate by faithfully reflecting misleading retrieved content rather than fabricating unsupported claims, fundamentally limiting the generalizability of graph-consistency-based detection.

title: Uncertainty-Aware LLM-Guided Policy Shaping for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IEEE CAI). 6 pages, 3 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/USD-AI-ResearchLab/uncertainty-aware-llm-rl
summary: Sparse rewards and heterogeneous task sequences remain persistent challenges in Reinforcement Learning (RL), often resulting in slow convergence, weak generalization, and inefficient exploration. We propose Uncertainty-Aware LLM-Guided Policy Shaping (ULPS), a novel framework that integrates a calibrated Large Language Model (LLM) into the RL training loop to provide structured, uncertainty-modulated behavioral guidance. ULPS employs an A*-based oracle to synthesize optimal symbolic trajectories, which are used to fine-tune a BERT-based language model. During training, this model supplies action suggestions whose influence is conditioned on epistemic uncertainty estimated via Monte Carlo (MC) dropout. An entropy-based blending mechanism adaptively balances LLM guidance and the learned policy (via Proximal Policy Optimization, PPO), allowing the agent to prioritize reliable priors while preserving adaptability. We evaluate ULPS on the MiniGridUnlockPickup benchmark and observe consistent improvements in success rate, reward efficiency, and sample complexity over unguided, uncalibrated, and standard RL baselines. ULPS achieves more than 9% improvement in execution accuracy after fine-tuning, requires fewer environment interactions, and yields higher reward AUC. Our results demonstrate that integrating symbolic A* trajectories, pretrained language priors, and uncertainty-aware control offers a principled and effective approach to multi-task reinforcement learning in sparse-reward domains, with potential extensibility to partially observable and multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: ULPS integrates a fine-tuned BERT model (trained on A*-generated optimal trajectories) into PPO-based RL, using MC-dropout uncertainty estimates and entropy-based blending to modulate LLM guidance, achieving improved sample efficiency and success rates on sparse-reward multi-task benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Sparse rewards and heterogeneous task sequences cause slow convergence, poor generalization, and inefficient exploration in RL. Existing methods lack principled ways to incorporate structured prior knowledge with calibrated uncertainty, motivating a framework that adaptively balances external guidance with learned policies.
AI.method: ULPS has three components: (1) An A* oracle synthesizes optimal symbolic trajectories used to fine-tune a BERT-based language model. (2) During RL training, the fine-tuned model provides action suggestions, whose influence is modulated by epistemic uncertainty estimated via MC dropout. (3) An entropy-based blending mechanism adaptively weights LLM guidance against the PPO-learned policy, allowing the agent to rely on reliable priors while maintaining adaptability.
AI.result: Evaluated on MiniGridUnlockPickup, ULPS achieves over 9% improvement in execution accuracy post fine-tuning, requires fewer environment interactions, yields higher reward AUC, and consistently outperforms unguided, uncalibrated, and standard RL baselines in success rate and sample complexity.
AI.conclusion: Combining symbolic A* trajectories, pretrained language priors, and uncertainty-aware control provides a principled, effective approach for multi-task RL in sparse-reward domains, with potential extensibility to partially observable and multi-agent settings.

title: AdMem: Advanced Memory for Task-solving Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise as tool-using agents but remain limited in long-horizon tasks that require remembering, organizing, and reusing knowledge. Prior memory approaches aim to resolve the situation, but mainly focus on storing factual information. Recent work on procedural memory improves task reuse, yet often reduces to replaying past successes without addressing failure cases or online scalability. We introduce a unified and automatic memory framework that integrates semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level design combining short-term and long-term stores. A multi-agent architecture with actor, memory, and critic agents enables automatic memory generation, reward annotation, and adaptive retrieval. Long-term memory is managed through reward-based evaluation, merging, and pruning, ensuring scalability and continual improvement. Experiments across various environments show that our approach improves robustness and success on long multi-turn tasks compared to existing baselines. This work highlights the importance of comprehensive, adaptive memory for advancing LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: A unified memory framework integrating semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level (short-term/long-term) design, managed by a multi-agent architecture (actor, memory, critic) with reward-based evaluation, merging, and pruning, improves LLM agent robustness and success on long-horizon, multi-turn tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring knowledge retention, organization, and reuse. Existing memory methods focus on factual storage, while procedural memory approaches merely replay past successes without learning from failures or scaling online. A comprehensive, adaptive memory framework is needed.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified, automatic memory framework with three memory types — semantic, episodic, and procedural — organized in a bi-level architecture (short-term and long-term stores). A multi-agent system comprising actor, memory, and critic agents handles automatic memory generation, reward annotation, and adaptive retrieval. Long-term memory is maintained via reward-based evaluation, merging, and pruning to ensure scalability and continual improvement.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse environments demonstrate improved robustness and task success rates on long multi-turn tasks compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Comprehensive, adaptive memory integrating semantic, episodic, and procedural components is critical for advancing LLM-based autonomous agents, particularly for complex, long-horizon tasks.

title: SWE-Explore: Benchmarking How Coding Agents Explore Repositories
summary: Repository-level coding benchmarks such as SWE-bench have driven a rapid surge in the capabilities of coding agents. Yet they usually treat coding tasks as a holistic, binary prediction problem (e.g., resolved or unresolved), neglecting fine-grained agent capabilities such as repository understanding, context retrieval, code localization, and bug diagnosis. In this paper, we introduce SWE-Explore, a benchmark that isolates the evaluation of repository exploration, a critical capability of coding agents. Given a repository and an issue, SWE-Explore asks an explorer to return a ranked list of relevant code regions under a fixed line budget. SWE-Explore covers 848 issues across 10 programming languages and 203 open-source repositories. For each instance, we derive line-level ground truth from independent agent trajectories that successfully solved the same issue, distilling the specific code regions their solution paths actually consulted. We evaluate exploration along coverage, ranking, and context-efficiency dimensions, showing that these metrics strongly track downstream repair behavior. Across a broad set of retrieval methods, general coding agents, and specialized localizers, we find that agentic explorers form a clear tier above classical retrieval. While file-level localization is already strong for modern methods, line-level coverage and efficient ranking remain the key axes differentiating state-of-the-art explorers.

AI.tldr: SWE-Explore is a benchmark that isolates and evaluates repository exploration capabilities of coding agents across 848 issues, 10 languages, and 203 repositories, using line-level ground truth from successful agent trajectories to measure coverage, ranking, and context-efficiency — finding that agentic explorers outperform classical retrieval, with line-level coverage and ranking as key differentiators.
AI.motivation: Existing repository-level coding benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench) treat coding tasks as binary (resolved/unresolved), obscuring fine-grained agent capabilities like repository understanding, context retrieval, code localization, and bug diagnosis. There is a need to isolate and rigorously evaluate repository exploration as a distinct capability.
AI.method: The authors introduce SWE-Explore, a benchmark where, given a repository and an issue, an explorer must return a ranked list of relevant code regions under a fixed line budget. Line-level ground truth is derived from independent agent trajectories that successfully solved the same issue, by extracting the specific code regions those solution paths actually consulted. Evaluation uses three dimensions: coverage, ranking, and context-efficiency metrics.
AI.result: Agentic explorers form a clear tier above classical retrieval methods. File-level localization is already strong for modern methods, but line-level coverage and efficient ranking remain the key axes differentiating state-of-the-art explorers. The exploration metrics strongly track downstream repair behavior.
AI.conclusion: Repository exploration is a critical, measurable capability for coding agents. SWE-Explore enables fine-grained evaluation beyond binary outcomes, revealing that while file-level localization is largely solved, precise line-level coverage and ranking efficiency are the frontiers where current methods must improve.

title: MalSkillBench: A Runtime-Verified Benchmark of Malicious Agent Skills
summary:
AI coding agents such as Claude Code and Gemini CLI increasingly extend themselves with third-party skills: markdown packages bundling natural-language instructions, executable scripts, and tool permissions. Because a skill is at once code and agent-facing instruction, it introduces a supply chain dependency whose risk is neither pure code nor pure prompt. Detection tools have never been measured against verified ground truth spanning this hybrid space, leaving their effectiveness unknown and wild-only evaluations biased.
  We present MalSkillBench, the first runtime-verified benchmark of malicious agent skills: 3,944 malicious skills labeled along a three-dimensional taxonomy of 108 cells. Of these, 3,214 come from a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline admitting only samples whose malicious behavior fires inside a Docker sandbox under system-call monitoring and an LLM judge; we add 703 in-the-wild and 4,000 matched benign skills. Our measurements are consistent: code injection reaches 94.5% verification yield but prompt injection only 75.8%, the same fragility that later makes it hard to detect; the wild sample is narrow, dominated by one cryptocurrency-theft campaign (86.6% one behavior, 81% from two accounts) with a small but architecturally new tail attacking the agent control plane; the strongest skill-specific detector reaches 98.4% recall on code injection yet collapses on prompt-injection and agent-control attacks, and wild-only scoring swings the ranking by up to 66 recall points; supply-chain scanners and prompt-injection defenses each see only half of a skill, and no combination recovers the code-instruction relationship. Detecting malicious skills therefore requires reasoning jointly over task intent, code, and instructions. We release the dataset, pipeline, baselines, and results.

AI.tldr: MalSkillBench is a benchmark of 3,944 verified malicious AI agent skills with a 108-cell taxonomy, revealing that code injection verifies easily but prompt injection is fragile, wild samples are narrow, and no existing detector handles both code and instruction threats jointly.
AI.motivation: AI coding agents increasingly rely on third-party skills that blend code, natural-language instructions, and tool permissions, creating a hybrid supply-chain attack surface that existing detectors have never been evaluated against with verified ground truth, leaving their real-world effectiveness unknown.
AI.method: The authors built MalSkillBench with a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline that produces malicious skills only if malicious behavior is confirmed inside a Docker sandbox under system-call monitoring and an LLM judge. Skills are labeled along a 108-cell three-dimensional taxonomy. The benchmark includes 3,214 pipeline-generated samples, 703 wild samples, and 4,000 matched benign skills. Multiple detectors (skill-specific, supply-chain scanners, prompt-injection defenses) are benchmarked.
AI.result: Code injection achieves 94.5% verification yield vs. 75.8% for prompt injection. The strongest skill-specific detector hits 98.4% recall on code injection but collapses on prompt-injection and agent-control attacks. Wild-only evaluation skews rankings by up to 66 recall points. Supply-chain scanners and prompt-injection defenses each capture only half the threat; no combination recovers the code-instruction relationship.
AI.conclusion: Detecting malicious agent skills requires joint reasoning over task intent, code, and instructions. The dataset, pipeline, baselines, and results are publicly released.

title: TA-RAG: Tone-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Peer-Support Health Communication
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) successfully grounds large language model (LLM) outputs in trusted documents, but factual grounding alone is insufficient for sensitive peer-support health communication. In domains such as HIV peer support, responses must also be accessible, stigma-free, empathetic, and tailored to the recipient. This paper presents TA-RAG, a lightweight, prompt-based tone-aware RAG framework that embeds explicit tone control into a RAG pipeline without requiring model fine-tuning. We operationalise tone across four core components: stigma-free rewriting, readability adjustment, recipient adaptation, and empathy rephrasing. We evaluate TA-RAG through component-level tests using questions derived from HIV Online Learning Australia (HOLA), UNAIDS terminology guidance, readability metrics, peer-support standards from National Association of People with HIV Australia (NAPWHA), and a public empathy dataset. Results show that the TA-RAG's components improve their targeted communication quality while preserving key content. These findings emphasise that prompt-based tone control is a potential direction for making RAG outputs suitable for sensitive peer-support health communication.

AI.tldr: TA-RAG is a lightweight, prompt-based tone-aware retrieval-augmented generation framework that adds explicit tone control (stigma-free rewriting, readability adjustment, recipient adaptation, empathy rephrasing) to RAG pipelines, enabling LLMs to produce responses suitable for sensitive HIV peer-support communication without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG ensures factual grounding but fails to address the nuanced communication requirements of sensitive peer-support health domains like HIV, where responses must also be accessible, stigma-free, empathetic, and recipient-tailored. Factual accuracy alone is insufficient for effective, safe peer-support interactions.
AI.method: The authors propose TA-RAG, a prompt-based framework that injects tone control into a RAG pipeline through four operationalized components: (1) stigma-free rewriting, (2) readability adjustment, (3) recipient adaptation, and (4) empathy rephrasing. Each component is implemented via carefully designed prompts without any model fine-tuning. Evaluation uses component-level tests drawing on HOLA-derived questions, UNAIDS terminology guidance, readability metrics, NAPWHA peer-support standards, and a public empathy dataset.
AI.result: TA-RAG's individual components demonstrably improve their respective targeted communication qualities — reducing stigma, enhancing readability, adapting to recipients, and increasing empathy — while preserving the core factual content from the retrieval step.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-based tone control represents a viable and promising direction for adapting RAG outputs to meet the multidimensional communication demands of sensitive peer-support health contexts, without the overhead of fine-tuning.

title: PromptPrint: Behavioral Biometrics Through Natural Language Prompting in LLMs
summary: Authorship attribution research has traditionally focused on long-form, expressive texts; however, interactions with large language models (LLMs) are typically brief and task-driven prompts. This raises a fundamental question: do such prompts contain a stable, author-identifiable, and distinctive signal? We introduce PromptPrint, a systematic study of prompt-based identity, the hypothesis that a user's habitual vocabulary, syntax, and discourse patterns form a learnable behavioral biometric. Using 20,680 real prompts from 1,034 users, we establish three key findings. First, lexical representations significantly outperform semantic encoders, supporting the "lexical stability hypothesis": identity is primarily encoded in surface-level word choice rather than abstract intent. Second, stylometric features exhibit a "uniqueness-consistency paradox": users are highly distinctive across the population, yet behaviorally inconsistent across contexts. Third, adversarial analysis reveals a clear vulnerability spectrum: identity signals are robust to minor lexical perturbations but degrade substantially under semantic paraphrasing. Overall, our results demonstrate strong identification performance at scale, establishing prompt-based identity as a viable behavioral biometric. This work introduces a new perspective on user modeling in LLM interactions, with important implications for security and privacy. Data and code will be released upon the acceptance of our work.

AI.tldr: PromptPrint analyzes whether short, task-driven LLM prompts contain stable, identifiable authorial signals. Using 20,680 prompts from 1,034 users, it finds that lexical (surface-level) features outperform semantic features for authorship attribution, users are highly distinctive yet behaviorally inconsistent, and identity signals resist minor lexical perturbations but degrade under semantic paraphrasing, establishing prompt-based identity as a viable behavioral biometric.
AI.motivation: Traditional authorship attribution focuses on long, expressive texts, but LLM interactions consist of brief, task-driven prompts. The paper investigates whether such prompts harbor a stable, identifying signal — a "prompt-based identity" — that could serve as a behavioral biometric, with implications for security and privacy.
AI.method: The study uses a dataset of 20,680 real-world prompts from 1,034 users. It systematically compares lexical representations against semantic encoders for authorship attribution; analyzes stylometric features for distinctiveness (inter-user) and consistency (intra-user); and conducts adversarial analysis by perturbing prompts lexically and via semantic paraphrasing to test signal robustness.
AI.result: Three key findings: (1) Lexical representations significantly outperform semantic encoders, supporting the "lexical stability hypothesis" — identity resides in surface-level word choice rather than abstract intent. (2) A "uniqueness-consistency paradox": users are highly distinctive across the population but behaviorally inconsistent across contexts. (3) Adversarial analysis shows a vulnerability spectrum — identity signals are robust to minor lexical perturbations but degrade substantially under semantic paraphrasing. Strong identification performance is demonstrated at scale.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-based identity is a viable behavioral biometric. The work introduces a new perspective on user modeling in LLM interactions, with significant implications for security (e.g., impersonation detection) and privacy (e.g., de-anonymization risks).

title: Spatiotemporal Imputation with Graph-Informed Flow Matching
summary: Missing data is a common challenge in spatiotemporal systems, arising in applications such as air quality monitoring and urban traffic management. Traditional machine learning approaches, like recurrent and graph neural networks, rely on iterative propagation, which tends to accumulate errors over time and space. Recent diffusion-based methods mitigate error propagation but require iterative sampling and often depend on problem-agnostic Gaussian priors, limiting both efficiency and effectiveness. To address these limitations, we propose GiFlow, a Graph-Informed Flow Matching framework for spatiotemporal imputation. GiFlow replaces the typical Gaussian prior with a graph-informed prior constructed via spatiotemporal filtering of observable signals, which better aligns the source distribution to the target and thereby simplifies the generation trajectory. The flow field is parameterized by a hybrid vector field model that integrates spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation, enabling joint modeling of spatial and temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed GiFlow outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches in spatiotemporal imputation. The code is available at https://github.com/zepengzhang/GiFlow.

AI.tldr: GiFlow proposes a graph-informed flow matching framework for spatiotemporal imputation that replaces Gaussian priors with graph-informed priors derived from observable signals, and uses a hybrid vector field integrating spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation, outperforming SOTA on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Missing data is pervasive in spatiotemporal systems (e.g., air quality, traffic). Traditional ML methods (RNNs, GNNs) suffer from error accumulation via iterative propagation, while diffusion-based methods mitigate this but rely on slow iterative sampling with problem-agnostic Gaussian priors, harming both efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.method: GiFlow replaces the Gaussian prior with a graph-informed prior built by spatiotemporally filtering observable signals, simplifying the generation trajectory. The flow field uses a hybrid vector field model combining spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation for joint dependency modeling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show GiFlow outperforms state-of-the-art spatiotemporal imputation approaches.
AI.conclusion: GiFlow effectively addresses error accumulation and inefficient sampling in spatiotemporal imputation by aligning the prior distribution to the target via graph-informed priors and jointly modeling spatial-temporal dependencies, achieving superior performance. Code is publicly available.

title: Evidence-Based Intelligent Diagnostic and Therapeutic Visualization System with Large Language Models: Multi-Turn Interaction and Multimodal Treatment Plan Generation
comment: 29 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, including supporting information
summary: Aim: Existing AI-assisted traditional Chinese medicine diagnostic tools suffer from opaque reasoning processes, passive interaction, and limited treatment plan presentation. This study proposes a knowledge-enhanced visual diagnostic system to improve the transparency and interpretability of syndrome differentiation and treatment. Methods: The system is built upon a Neo4j knowledge graph comprising 241 syndromes, 1,263 symptoms, and 2,485 relations. It incorporates a four-stage symptom matching pipeline (exact, semantic, fuzzy, and large language model verification), an information gain-driven proactive questioning strategy optimized with genetic algorithms, and a multimodal treatment presentation integrating artificial intelligence-generated illustrations, three-dimensional meridian-acupoint models, and evidence-based literature. Results: Knowledge graph constraints reduced non-standard outputs by 32%. Case studies validated the effectiveness of the interactive workflow across patient self-assessment, clinician-assisted diagnosis, and traditional Chinese medicine education. Automated paired-comparison evaluation across 30 cases further demonstrated significant improvements in diagnostic trust (Cohen's d = 1.82, p < 0.001), reduced cognitive load (improvements in four of five dimensions), and higher credibility of evidence-based references (4.21 vs. 2.95). Conclusions: The proposed system enhances the transparency of traditional Chinese medicine diagnostic reasoning and the interpretability of treatment plans through knowledge graph-driven visualization and multimodal interaction, offering a practical solution for trustworthy artificial intelligence-assisted traditional Chinese medicine applications.

AI.tldr: A knowledge graph-enhanced visual diagnostic system for TCM that improves reasoning transparency and treatment interpretability through structured symptom matching, proactive questioning, and multimodal treatment presentation.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-assisted TCM diagnostic tools suffer from opaque reasoning, passive interaction, and limited treatment plan presentation. There is a need for trustworthy, interpretable AI in traditional Chinese medicine.
AI.method: Built on a Neo4j knowledge graph (241 syndromes, 1,263 symptoms, 2,485 relations) with: (1) a four-stage symptom matching pipeline (exact, semantic, fuzzy, LLM verification); (2) an information gain-driven proactive questioning strategy optimized via genetic algorithms; (3) multimodal treatment presentation using AI-generated illustrations, 3D meridian-acupoint models, and evidence-based literature.
AI.result: Knowledge graph constraints reduced non-standard outputs by 32%. Case studies validated effectiveness across self-assessment, clinician-assisted diagnosis, and TCM education. Automated paired-comparison (30 cases) showed significant improvements: diagnostic trust (Cohen's d=1.82, p<0.001), reduced cognitive load in 4/5 dimensions, and higher evidence-based reference credibility (4.21 vs 2.95).
AI.conclusion: The system enhances TCM diagnostic reasoning transparency and treatment plan interpretability through knowledge graph-driven visualization and multimodal interaction, offering a practical trustworthy AI-assisted TCM solution.

title: QBugLM: An Agentic Benchmarking Framework for LLM-based Quantum Software Debugging
comment: This paper was accepted at IEEE QSW 2026
summary: Quantum software bugs often yield silent, incorrect outputs rather than explicit errors, making them particularly difficult to detect and repair with conventional techniques. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on classical software engineering tasks, their ability to debug quantum code remains largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose QBugLM, a multi-agent framework that automates the quantum software debugging pipeline, from taxonomy-driven bug injection to LLM-based detection and repair, and finally to simulation-based validation, for framework-agnostic OpenQASM 3.0 programs. We further conduct a comprehensive case study using QBugLM to benchmark two LLMs, Claude 4.6 Sonnet and Qwen3 Coder Next, across different prompting strategies, bug categories, and quantum programs. Our results show that iterative feedback is critical, as a single retry raises Pass@1 from below 25% to above 80%. Moreover, simpler structured prompting can even outperform Chain-of-Thought and ReAct for reasoning-capable models under fixed-resource constraints. Our work takes initial steps toward benchmarking LLM capabilities for debugging quantum programs and offers practical insights to support future efforts in automated quantum software repair.

AI.tldr: QBugLM is a multi-agent framework for automated quantum software debugging (detection and repair) targeting OpenQASM 3.0 programs. A case study benchmarking Claude 4.6 Sonnet and Qwen3 Coder Next reveals that iterative feedback dramatically improves repair success (Pass@1 from <25% to >80%), and simpler prompting strategies can outperform Chain-of-Thought/ReAct under fixed-resource constraints.
AI.motivation: Quantum software bugs produce silent, incorrect outputs that are hard to detect and repair with conventional techniques. While LLMs excel at classical software engineering, their effectiveness on quantum code debugging remains unexplored, creating a gap the authors aim to address.
AI.method: The authors propose QBugLM, a multi-agent framework with four stages: (1) taxonomy-driven bug injection into framework-agnostic OpenQASM 3.0 programs, (2) LLM-based bug detection, (3) LLM-based repair, and (4) simulation-based validation. A comprehensive case study benchmarks Claude 4.6 Sonnet and Qwen3 Coder Next across different prompting strategies (including Chain-of-Thought and ReAct), bug categories, and quantum programs.
AI.result: Iterative feedback proves critical: a single retry lifts Pass@1 from below 25% to above 80%. Simpler structured prompting can outperform both Chain-of-Thought and ReAct for reasoning-capable models under fixed-resource constraints.
AI.conclusion: This work takes initial steps toward benchmarking LLM capabilities for quantum program debugging and provides practical insights for future automated quantum software repair efforts.

title: Your UnEmbedding Matrix is Secretly a Feature Lens for Text Embeddings
summary: Large language models exhibit impressive zero-shot capabilities across a wide range of downstream tasks. However, they struggle to function as off-the-shelf embedding models, leading to suboptimal performance on massive text embedding benchmarks. In this paper, we identify a potential cause underlying this deficiency. Our motivation stems from an unexpected observation: text embeddings tend to align with frequent but uninformative tokens when projected onto the vocabulary space. We argue that this excessive expression of high-frequency tokens suppresses the model's ability to capture nuanced semantics. To address this, we introduce EmbedFilter, a simple linear transformation designed to refine text embeddings derived from LLMs directly. Specifically, we uncover that the unembedding matrix within LLMs encodes a latent space that is actively writing these frequent tokens into embedding space. By filtering out this subspace, EmbedFilter suppress the influence of high-frequency tokens, thereby enhancing semantic representations. As a compelling byproduct, this enables an inherent dimensionality reduction, lowering index storage and speedup retrieval while fully preserving the refined embedding quality. Our experiments across multiple LLM backbones demonstrate that LLMs equipped with EmbedFilter achieve superior zero-shot downstream performance even with significantly reduced embedding dimensions. We hope our findings provide deeper insights into the mechanisms of LLM-based representations and inspire more principled designs to improve text embeddings training. Our code is available at https://github.com/CentreChen/EmbFilter.

AI.tldr: LLMs produce suboptimal text embeddings because high-frequency token signals dominate the embedding space. The authors propose EmbedFilter, a linear transformation that subtracts the subspace encoded by the LLM's unembedding matrix, suppressing frequent-token interference and enabling dimensionality reduction without quality loss.
AI.motivation: An unexpected observation: when LLM text embeddings are projected onto the vocabulary space, they disproportionately align with frequent but semantically uninformative tokens. This "excessive expression" of high-frequency tokens suppresses the model's capacity to capture nuanced semantics, explaining why off-the-shelf LLMs underperform on text embedding benchmarks.
AI.method: EmbedFilter applies a simple linear transformation to LLM-derived embeddings. The key insight is that the unembedding matrix (the final linear layer mapping hidden states to vocabulary logits) encodes a latent subspace responsible for writing frequent-token signals into the embedding. By identifying and filtering out this subspace, EmbedFilter suppresses high-frequency token influence. This operation inherently yields reduced-dimensional embeddings, lowering storage and accelerating retrieval.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple LLM backbones show that EmbedFilter-equipped models achieve superior zero-shot downstream performance on massive text embedding benchmarks, even with significantly reduced embedding dimensions. The method preserves (or improves) embedding quality while enabling index compression and faster retrieval.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal a mechanistic explanation for LLM embedding deficiencies and demonstrate that a principled, training-free linear filter can substantially improve embedding quality. The authors hope this inspires more principled designs for text embedding training.

title: A Four-Condition Diagnostic Protocol for Evidence Utilization in Long-Context and Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
summary: Final-answer accuracy, retrieval recall, and citation overlap do not by themselves identify whether a long-context or retrieval-augmented language model used the evidence it was given. A model can answer from parametric memory, fail despite receiving the right passages, or cite evidence without converting it into the requested answer. This paper proposes a matched four-condition evidence-availability protocol--no evidence, full context, retrieved evidence, and oracle-evidence reference--for diagnosing evidence utilization under fixed examples, prompts, score fields, retrieval settings, and validity checks. ONCU is used as a protocol-bound estimator of recovered oracle-reference evidence advantage and is computed only for denominator-valid groups; denominator-free answer, evidence, retrieval, and failure-audit metrics are reported separately. The empirical study evaluates five local open-weight models from the Qwen, Gemma, Llama, and Mistral families across Controlled-ONCU-safe16K, HotpotQA-ONCU, and 2WikiMultiHopQA-ONCU, with 18,000 ONCU-compatible predictions. The main finding is a task-dependent bottleneck split: controlled synthetic settings primarily expose full-context utilization failures, whereas the tested realistic multi-hop settings primarily expose retrieval-chain coverage failures in denominator-free answer and evidence metrics, with ONCU supporting the same direction on oracle-improving groups. The contribution is a diagnostic protocol for separating no-evidence answerability, oracle-evidence recoverability, full-context utilization, and retrieval-conditioned utilization, rather than a single-score leaderboard for long-context or retrieval-augmented systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ONCU, a matched four-condition diagnostic protocol that separates no-evidence answerability, oracle-evidence recoverability, full-context utilization, and retrieval-conditioned utilization to reveal distinct bottleneck patterns (full-context failures in synthetic settings vs. retrieval-coverage failures in realistic multi-hop QA) across five open-weight LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics like final-answer accuracy, retrieval recall, and citation overlap fail to diagnose *how* models use evidence. A model may answer correctly from parametric memory without touching provided context, fail despite receiving relevant passages, or cite evidence without translating it into a correct answer. There is no principled protocol to disentangle these distinct failure modes.
AI.method: A matched four-condition protocol is designed: (1) no-evidence, (2) full-context, (3) retrieved-evidence, and (4) oracle-evidence reference. The ONCU estimator computes recovered oracle-reference evidence advantage, restricted to denominator-valid groups where the oracle condition succeeds. Denominator-free metrics (answer, evidence, retrieval, and failure-audit) complement ONCU to avoid selection bias. The protocol fixes examples, prompts, scoring, retrieval settings, and validity checks across conditions. Five open-weight models (Qwen, Gemma, Llama, Mistral families) are evaluated on Controlled-ONCU-safe16K, HotpotQA-ONCU, and 2WikiMultiHopQA-ONCU with 18,000 predictions.
AI.result: A task-dependent bottleneck split emerges: controlled synthetic settings predominantly surface full-context utilization failures (models fail to use evidence even when it is present), whereas realistic multi-hop settings predominantly surface retrieval-chain coverage failures. ONCU corroborates these findings on oracle-improving groups, while denominator-free answer and evidence metrics independently support the same direction.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is a diagnostic protocol, not a leaderboard. By separating four evidence conditions and computing ONCU only on denominator-valid subsets, the framework enables precise attribution of model failures to specific evidence-utilization bottlenecks, revealing that optimization priorities must differ between synthetic and realistic evaluation regimes.

title: Towards Serverless Semi-Decentralized Federated Learning with Heterogeneous Optimizers
comment: Under review at IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking
summary: We investigate cluster formation, involving the number and composition of clusters, in decentralized federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous machine learning (ML) optimizers. While clustering in centralized FL has enabled scalability and resource savings, its value and development in fully decentralized environments have yet to be explored. Optimizing cluster formation in such environments is challenging, especially due to the complex coupling between network graph structures, local data heterogeneity, and different local ML model optimizers. To address these challenges, we propose serverless semi-decentralized FL (SSD-FL), a methodology requiring no persistent server infrastructure. In SSD-FL, cluster formation occurs via a lightweight, one-time device-to-device (D2D) initialization phase, after which actual ML model training (alongside consensus and convergence processes) is fully serverless. Functionally, SSD-FL segments global rounds into intra-cluster and inter-cluster regimes, ensuring global convergence and consensus through novel "effective loss functions" that integrate device-specific ML optimizers with network graph-based regularization. Next, SSD-FL leverages the consensus gap via the Cheeger inequality to develop an iterative clustering algorithm evaluated against our derived convergence and consensus bounds, which incorporate a unique scoring metric to quantify data and optimizer heterogeneity across devices. Finally, experimental evaluation against three categories of decentralized FL methodologies validate that SSD-FL improves both convergence speeds and communication efficiency across various network graphs, datasets, and local optimizer regimes.

AI.tldr: SSD-FL is a serverless semi-decentralized federated learning framework that uses a one-time D2D initialization for cluster formation and a novel iterative clustering algorithm based on the Cheeger inequality and consensus gap, with theoretical convergence bounds accounting for heterogeneous local optimizers and data distributions.
AI.motivation: Clustering has been explored in centralized FL for scalability, but its potential in fully decentralized environments remains unexamined. The key challenge is the complex coupling among network topology, local data heterogeneity, and heterogeneous ML optimizers across devices, making optimal cluster formation difficult.
AI.method: SSD-FL employs: (1) a lightweight, one-time D2D initialization phase for cluster formation without persistent servers; (2) segmentation of global rounds into intra-cluster and inter-cluster regimes with novel "effective loss functions" that integrate device-specific ML optimizers with graph-based regularization; and (3) an iterative clustering algorithm leveraging the consensus gap via Cheeger inequality, evaluated against derived convergence/consensus bounds with a scoring metric that quantifies data and optimizer heterogeneity.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation against three categories of decentralized FL methods shows SSD-FL improves both convergence speed and communication efficiency across diverse network graphs, datasets, and local optimizer configurations.
AI.conclusion: SSD-FL demonstrates that principled cluster formation in fully decentralized FL is feasible and beneficial, achieving superior convergence and communication efficiency without requiring persistent server infrastructure, even under heterogeneous data and optimizer settings.

title: Workflow-to-Skill: Skill Creation via Routing-Workflow-Semantics-Attachments Decomposition
summary: Large language model agents increasingly rely on Skills to encode procedural knowledge, yet high-quality Skills remain costly to hand-write. This paper studies automatic Skill construction from heterogeneous interaction evidence, including demonstrations, agent trajectories, tool traces, and execution logs. We argue that trace-to-skill construction is not simple summarization tasks, because traces are fragmented, redundant, and may miss rare but safety-critical behaviors. To address this, we introduce RWSA, a workflow-oriented intermediate representation that decomposes Skills into Workflow structure, execution Semantics, and runtime Attachments, capturing task decomposition, control flow, verification, safety, rollback, and state management. Building on RWSA, we propose W2S, a framework that segments traces, induces local Skill drafts, aligns shared structures, reconciles branches, and compresses redundancy while preserving evidence and confidence annotations. Experiments on 70 Skills show that W2S improves behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over summarization- and prompting-based baselines, highlighting the need to treat traces as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RWSA, a workflow-oriented intermediate representation for Skills, and W2S, a framework that automatically constructs executable Skills from heterogeneous interaction traces, improving behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over baselines.
AI.motivation: High-quality Skills for LLM agents are costly to hand-write. Existing trace-to-skill approaches treat the problem as summarization, but traces are fragmented, redundant, and often miss rare safety-critical behaviors. A method is needed that treats traces as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text.
AI.method: The authors propose RWSA (Workflow, execution Semantics, runtime Attachments), a structured intermediate representation capturing task decomposition, control flow, verification, safety, rollback, and state management. Building on RWSA, they introduce W2S, a framework that segments traces, induces local Skill drafts, aligns shared structures, reconciles branches, and compresses redundancy while preserving evidence and confidence annotations.
AI.result: Experiments on 70 Skills demonstrate that W2S improves behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over summarization-based and prompting-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating traces as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text is essential for faithful Skill construction. The RWSA representation and W2S framework effectively capture procedural knowledge from heterogeneous interaction evidence, enabling more reliable LLM agent Skills.

title: Is US Defense Acquisition Ready to Acquire AI-Enabled Capabilities? Assessing the DoD Software Acquisition Pathway Through a Scenario-Based Policy Analysis
comment: Submitted to ACM Digital Government: Research and Practice Journal on April 2026
summary:
As AI systems transition from experimental prototypes to mission-critical tools, their dependence on dynamic data, evolving models, and governance raises questions about whether existing acquisition pathways can keep pace. The U.S. Department of Defense has modernized its acquisition processes through the Adaptive Acquisition Framework, with the Software Acquisition Pathway (SWP) serving as the primary mechanism for acquiring software-intensive capabilities. This paper evaluates whether SWP is sufficient to address the unique demands of AI acquisition.
  In this work, we perform a scenario-based evaluation that traces a notional AI-enabled program through key SWP planning activities to assess how policy translates into program artifacts and decisions. We use Policy Scenario Analysis to examine whether the SWP-centered governance stack provides sufficient actionable support for AI acquisition. The governance stack provides a viable foundation for iterative delivery and AI testing. However, we identify a recurring actionability problem in the core guidance. AI-specific controls for data provenance, lifecycle management, and human oversight remain distributed across supplemental documents rather than embedded in the program-facing mechanisms through which SWP is executed. This disconnect leaves program offices reliant on inconsistent local interpretation. We conclude by recommending an AI-supporting sub-path and targeted artifact refinements to better bridge this policy-to-artifact gap.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether the DoD's Software Acquisition Pathway (SWP) is sufficient for AI acquisition. Using scenario-based Policy Scenario Analysis, it finds that while SWP provides a viable iterative foundation, AI-specific controls for data provenance, lifecycle management, and human oversight are scattered across supplemental documents rather than embedded in core program-facing mechanisms. The authors recommend an AI-supporting sub-path and targeted artifact refinements to close this policy-to-artifact gap.
AI.motivation: As AI becomes mission-critical within the DoD, its unique demands—dynamic data dependencies, evolving models, and governance requirements—may outpace existing acquisition pathways. The SWP was designed for software-intensive systems, but it remains unclear whether it adequately addresses AI-specific challenges, necessitating a structured evaluation of its sufficiency.
AI.method: The authors employ Policy Scenario Analysis, tracing a notional AI-enabled program through key SWP planning activities. This approach maps how existing policy translates into concrete program artifacts and acquisition decisions, revealing the alignment and disconnects between high-level governance and operational execution.
AI.result: The SWP governance stack provides a viable foundation for iterative delivery and AI testing. However, a recurring actionability problem exists: AI-specific controls (data provenance, lifecycle management, human oversight) are distributed across supplemental documents rather than embedded in the core program-facing mechanisms of SWP. This forces program offices to rely on inconsistent local interpretation.
AI.conclusion: The paper recommends developing an AI-supporting sub-path within the acquisition framework and refining specific program artifacts to embed AI controls directly into SWP execution mechanisms, thereby bridging the policy-to-artifact gap and reducing reliance on ad hoc local interpretation.

title: Synthetic APTs: the Collapse of TTP-Based Attribution
summary: Cyber Threat Intelligence CTI attribution relies on identifying the Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures TTPs that distinguish one threat actor from another. This approach presupposes that each adversary leaves a recognizable operational fingerprint. This work investigates whether AI driven adversary emulation challenges that presupposition. We deploy agents from our Cybersecurity SuperIntelligence CSI framework, configured as five Advanced Persistent Threat APT groups, APT28, APT29, APT41, APT44, and Lazarus Group, against AI driven Defender agents across two cyber ranges provided by CYBER RANGES, equipped with defensive software Wazuh, Velociraptor, Elasticsearch and active AI driven defenders: an enterprise network and a military infrastructure. Across 20 experiments using two defender models, a binary pattern emerges: all 10 Enterprise range experiments resulted in compromise 2 to 12 hosts per experiment, while all 10 Military range experiments were successfully defended or resulted in stalemates, regardless of APT profile or defender model. In 8 of 10 Enterprise experiments, attackers independently weaponized the defender's own Velociraptor endpoint management platform as a command and control channel, a convergent behavior not encoded in any threat intelligence profile. We argue that in the AI era, wherein agents can be deployed provided the right models are available and subject to the right scaffolding and agentic configuration, the entry barrier for operating like a nation state APT collapses: beyond nation states, individuals can now act like commonly identified threat actors, and with it, fundamentally undermine TTP based attribution.

AI.tldr: AI-driven adversary emulation across cyber ranges reveals that attacker agents, regardless of APT profile, independently converged on weaponizing the defender's own Velociraptor platform as C2, challenging the foundational assumption of TTP-based CTI attribution by demonstrating that AI agents can replicate nation-state APT behaviors without being those actors.
AI.motivation: Current CTI attribution assumes each threat actor leaves a unique, recognizable TTP fingerprint. This work questions whether AI-driven adversary emulation undermines that assumption, as AI agents may independently converge on similar TTPs regardless of which APT group they are configured to emulate.
AI.method: The authors deployed CSI framework agents configured as five APT groups (APT28, APT29, APT41, APT44, Lazarus Group) against AI-driven defender agents across two CYBER RANGES environments: an enterprise network and a military infrastructure. Defenders used Wazuh, Velociraptor, Elasticsearch, and active AI-driven defenders. Twenty experiments were conducted using two defender models.
AI.result: A stark binary pattern emerged: all 10 Enterprise range experiments resulted in compromise (2–12 hosts each), while all 10 Military range experiments were successfully defended or stalemated. Critically, in 8 of 10 Enterprise experiments, attackers independently weaponized the defender's Velociraptor endpoint management platform as a C2 channel — a convergent behavior absent from any threat intelligence profile.
AI.conclusion: In the AI era, the barrier to operating like a nation-state APT collapses: individuals can now emulate known threat actors, fundamentally undermining TTP-based attribution. Attacker behavior may reflect convergent AI-driven optimization rather than operator identity, rendering operational fingerprinting unreliable.

title: When Better Codebooks Are Not Enough: Predictive Performance and Behavioral Reliability in LLM Political Event Coding
summary:
High accuracy does not necessarily make an LLM a faithful coder. This issue matters because many social-science studies rely on expert-written codebooks to turn text into structured data. We study this problem in political event coding, a challenging source-target relation classification task beyond ordinary sentence-level classification, where models must determine what one actor did to another using detailed coding rules.
  We test whether expert codebooks become more effective when operationalized into LLM-friendly forms with clearer definitions, examples, retrieved context, and rules for difficult cases. We then evaluate behavioral reliability under controlled changes to label names, codebook order, and label-definition mappings. Clearer codebooks substantially improve classification performance, especially for fine-grained event classification. However, these predictive gains do not fully translate into behavioral reliability. Models may produce valid labels and recover definitions while still failing behavioral reliability tests under controlled codebook changes.
  These findings suggest that codebook-guided LLM systems should be evaluated not only by accuracy, but also by whether they preserve the coding logic that makes coded outputs meaningful for social-science research.

AI.tldr: Clearer codebooks boost LLM accuracy in political event coding, but improved accuracy does not guarantee behavioral reliability — models fail consistency tests under controlled perturbations, raising validity concerns for social-science applications.
AI.motivation: Social-science research relies on expert-written codebooks to convert text into structured data. While LLMs can automate this coding, high accuracy alone may mask a lack of faithfulness to the underlying coding logic. This paper investigates whether accuracy gains from operationalizing codebooks for LLMs correspond to genuine behavioral reliability, using political event coding as a challenging testbed.
AI.method: The authors transform expert codebooks into LLM-friendly formats by adding clearer definitions, examples, retrieved context, and rules for difficult cases. They then evaluate both classification performance and behavioral reliability under controlled experimental perturbations: changing label names, reordering codebook entries, and altering label-definition mappings. Political event coding (source-target relation classification) serves as the task.
AI.result: Clearer codebooks substantially improve classification accuracy, especially for fine-grained events. However, these predictive gains do not fully translate into behavioral reliability: models may produce valid labels and recover definitions yet still fail consistency tests when label names, codebook order, or label-definition mappings are altered.
AI.conclusion: Codebook-guided LLM systems must be evaluated on both accuracy and adherence to coding logic. Relying solely on predictive performance risks deploying models that produce correct-looking but unfaithful outputs, undermining the validity of coded data in social-science research.

title: The Geography of Algorithmic Judgment: LLM Intermediaries, Place Identity, and Racial Steering in Housing Search
comment: 13 pages with supplemental tables and figures, AIES '26 Submission
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly assuming an intermediary role in housing search through the integration of listing platforms within conversational interfaces, mediating access to information, search, and recommendations within urban settings. We expand on prior work on racial steering in LLMs by conducting a behavioral audit of seven open-weight and closed-source LLMs across four U.S. cities, testing location recommendations across three iterative prompting conditions that progressively add lifestyle preference context and reflect fair housing paired-testing methodologies. We find that steering is an emergent behavior of the model's interpretive license rather than primarily a static property. Steering results from the interaction of a user's identity, preference articulation, and the spatial logic that a model has internalized about learned representations of place, preference, and opportunity in a given city, and how different types of users relate to it. While steering was present, it was not uniform in direction or magnitude across evaluated conditions. Preference-conditioned testing often increased or reconfigured the number of models that exhibited steering behaviors relative to baseline conditions, suggesting that LLMs may interpret what the same housing preference means differently depending on the racial identity of the user. Our findings also demonstrate that the city is not a neutral testing unit for LLM evaluation in place-based sectors, and results from one local market cannot be assumed to generalize to another. Local and domain expertise will be required in the housing sector to ensure that legal and institutional commitments to fair housing are not undermined while adopting AI tools that mediate spatial access.

AI.tldr: A behavioral audit of seven LLMs across four U.S. cities reveals that racial steering in housing recommendations is an emergent, context-dependent behavior shaped by the interaction of user identity, stated preferences, and the model's internalized spatial logic — not a static property. Steering varies by city and intensifies or reconfigures when lifestyle preferences are added, underscoring the need for localized domain expertise to uphold fair housing commitments.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly mediating housing search through conversational interfaces, raising concerns about racial steering in algorithmic recommendations. Prior work has examined static steering, but the authors argue that steering is more accurately understood as an emergent, interactive phenomenon that depends on how user identity, articulated preferences, and the model's learned spatial representations combine — a dynamic not captured by baseline-only audits.
AI.method: The authors conducted a behavioral audit of seven LLMs (both open-weight and closed-source) across four U.S. cities. They employed an iterative prompting design with three conditions: a baseline query, and two additional conditions that progressively introduced lifestyle preference context. This design mirrors fair housing paired-testing methodologies, systematically varying user racial identity and preference articulation to observe how steering emerges, shifts, or intensifies across conditions and geographies.
AI.result: Steering was present across models but was non-uniform in direction and magnitude. Preference-conditioned testing often increased or reconfigured which models exhibited steering relative to baseline, indicating LLMs interpret identical housing preferences differently based on the user's racial identity. Critically, results varied by city, demonstrating that local housing markets are not interchangeable testing units and that findings from one city do not generalize to others.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based housing steering is an emergent, relational phenomenon rather than a fixed model property. This demands evaluation frameworks that account for interaction between identity, preference, and place. The authors conclude that local and domain expertise is essential in the housing sector to prevent AI tools from undermining fair housing law, and that audits must be conducted at the level of specific local markets rather than assuming cross-city generalizability.

title: Declarative Skills for AI Agents in Knowledge-Grounded Tool-Use Workflows
summary: We study orchestration mechanisms for tool-using AI agents in realistic customer-service workflows over an unstructured knowledge base. We argue that declarative agents -- AI agents equipped with natural-language skill files appended to the system prompt -- are an effective orchestration paradigm. Concretely, we compare (i) a DeclarativeAgent that reads three domain-specific skill files at inference time and decides its own control flow, (ii) an ImperativeAgent based on a programmatic state machine with explicit phases, and (iii) an unscaffolded baseline agent modeled after the $τ$-Knowledge benchmark agent. Our ImperativeAgent is motivated by externalised-control inference as in Recursive Language Models and graph-based orchestration frameworks. We formalise the three agents as policy classes within a decentralised partially-observable Markov decision process and analyse their information-theoretic and structural properties; we then test the predicted differences empirically on five language models and two retrieval regimes. Our results show that retrieval quality is a dominant bottleneck for AI agents: when evidence is incomplete or skewed, all agents degrade substantially, and skill files cannot recover lost performance. Under high-quality retrieval, however, declarative skills consistently improve accuracy on procedural tasks and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative state machine's brittleness does not reliably improve task success or compliance.

AI.tldr: This paper compares three orchestration paradigms for tool-using AI agents in customer-service workflows: a DeclarativeAgent using natural-language skill files, an ImperativeAgent based on a programmatic state machine, and an unscaffolded baseline. Under high-quality retrieval, declarative skills improve procedural accuracy and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative approach proves brittle. Retrieval quality emerges as the dominant bottleneck across all agents.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to identify effective orchestration mechanisms for AI agents operating over unstructured knowledge bases in realistic customer-service settings. They challenge the assumption that programmatic, state-machine-based control flows (imperative orchestration) are superior, instead hypothesizing that declarative, natural-language skill specifications appended to the system prompt can yield better flexibility and robustness.
AI.method: The authors formalize three agent policy classes within a decentralized partially-observable Markov decision process (Dec-POMDP) framework and analyze their information-theoretic and structural properties. They then empirically compare: (1) a DeclarativeAgent that reads domain-specific skill files at inference time and autonomously determines control flow; (2) an ImperativeAgent using a programmatic state machine with explicit phases (inspired by Recursive Language Models and graph-based orchestration); and (3) an unscaffolded baseline modeled after the τ-Knowledge benchmark agent. Experiments span five language models and two retrieval regimes.
AI.result: Retrieval quality is the dominant performance bottleneck: when evidence is incomplete or skewed, all agents degrade substantially, and skill files cannot compensate. Under high-quality retrieval, declarative skills consistently improve accuracy on procedural tasks and reduce orchestration errors. The imperative state machine, however, exhibits brittleness and does not reliably improve task success or compliance.
AI.conclusion: Declarative orchestration via natural-language skill files is an effective paradigm for tool-using AI agents, outperforming imperative state machines in flexibility and error reduction when retrieval quality is high. Programmatic control flow introduces brittleness without reliable gains. The authors conclude that retrieval infrastructure, not orchestration strategy, is the primary limiting factor in agentic customer-service systems.

title: Socratic-SWE: Self-Evolving Coding Agents via Trace-Derived Agent Skills
summary: LLM-driven software engineering agents have become a central testbed for real-world language-model capability, yet their training remains limited by the availability of high-quality SWE tasks. Existing synthetic data methods typically create tasks through fixed mutation or bug-injection procedures, making the resulting distributions largely independent of the agent's own weaknesses and training progress. We introduce Socratic-SWE, a closed-loop self-evolution framework that reuses the agent's historical solving traces as a source of training signal. Rather than treating traces only as evidence for reward computation, Socratic-SWE distills them into structured agent skills that summarize recurring failures and effective repair patterns. These skills then guide the generation of targeted repair tasks in real repositories. Candidate tasks are checked through execution-based validation and scored with a solver-gradient alignment reward, so that the retained tasks are both verifiable and useful for improving the Solver. The updated Solver produces new traces, enabling the task curriculum to adapt over successive rounds. Across SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Lite, SWE-bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0, Socratic-SWE consistently improves over self-evolving baselines under the same compute budget, reaching 50.40% on SWE-bench Verified after three iterations. These results suggest that solving traces can serve as a scalable substrate for self-evolving SWE agents.

AI.tldr: Socratic-SWE is a closed-loop self-evolution framework for LLM-driven software engineering agents that distills the agent's own solving traces into structured skills, then uses those skills to generate targeted repair tasks, enabling the agent to iteratively improve by learning from its own failures and successes.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic data methods for training SWE agents rely on fixed mutation or bug-injection procedures, producing task distributions that are decoupled from the agent's specific weaknesses and training dynamics. There is a need for a method that creates training tasks adaptively, informed by the agent's own solving behavior.
AI.method: Socratic-SWE operates in a closed loop: (1) The Solver agent produces solving traces on SWE tasks; (2) These traces are distilled into structured "agent skills" capturing recurring failure modes and effective repair patterns; (3) Skills guide generation of targeted repair tasks in real repositories; (4) Candidate tasks undergo execution-based validation and are scored via a solver-gradient alignment reward, retaining only verifiable and useful tasks; (5) The Solver trains on these tasks, produces new traces, and the curriculum adapts over successive rounds.
AI.result: Socratic-SWE consistently outperforms self-evolving baselines under the same compute budget across multiple benchmarks. On SWE-bench Verified, it reaches 50.40% after three iterations. Improvements are also demonstrated on SWE-bench Lite, SWE-bench Pro, and Terminal-Bench 2.0.
AI.conclusion: Solving traces can serve as a scalable substrate for self-evolving SWE agents. The closed-loop distillation of agent behavior into structured skills enables adaptive curriculum generation, leading to substantial and consistent improvements over fixed synthetic data approaches.

title: Rethinking IoT Intrusion Detection: Augmenting Routing Metrics with Radio Features
comment: 4 Pages, 8 figures, Accepted to Swedish National Computer Networking Workshop (SNCNW) 2026
summary: Machine learning-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) for RPL-based IoT networks often rely solely on routing layer features, which provide only a partial view of network behaviour. In this work, we investigate whether incorporating Transmit (TX) and Receive (RX) radio features alongside the standard RPL feature set can improve detection performance in an LSTM-based IDS. We evaluate the proposed approach across three different attack types, namely DIS-Flooding, Local Repair, and Worst Parent under varying network sizes. The results show that incorporating TX and RX improves the IDS's overall detection performance by up to ~4% in F1-score compared with using routing-layer features alone, with the most notable gain observed for the Worst Parent attack.

AI.tldr: Adding TX/RX radio features to standard RPL routing features improves LSTM-based IDS detection performance for IoT networks by up to ~4% F1-score, with the largest gain for Worst Parent attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing ML-based IDS for RPL-based IoT networks rely only on routing-layer features, providing an incomplete view of network behavior. This work investigates whether radio-layer features (TX/RX) can complement routing features to enhance detection.
AI.method: An LSTM-based IDS is trained and evaluated using both standard RPL routing features alone and combined with TX/RX radio features. Evaluation covers three attack types (DIS-Flooding, Local Repair, Worst Parent) across varying network sizes.
AI.result: Incorporating TX and RX features improves overall detection performance by up to ~4% in F1-score over routing features alone, with the most notable gain on the Worst Parent attack.
AI.conclusion: Radio-layer features provide complementary information that enhances LSTM-based IDS detection in RPL-based IoT networks, particularly for attacks like Worst Parent that manifest more clearly at the radio level.

title: RECAP: Regression Evaluation for Continual Adaptation of Prompts
summary: Production agentic systems routinely face evolving constraints and must comply from the very next interaction. Scenarios like a tool-call notification changing a compliance threshold or a policy update adding disclosure requirements fit this criteria, having close to no room for errors in production. This proactive adaptation setting is common in deployment, but absent from current benchmarks, which assume either static constraint sets or reactive protocols with evaluation feedback. We introduce RECAP, a benchmark that measures continual-learning phenomena (forgetting, regression, forward transfer) at the constraint level under a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol: prompt optimization methods receive only the constraint specification and must generalize before seeing any test data. Evaluating six methods across four LLMs and three schedules with evolving constraints, we find that these methods show no significant improvement in performance, even after incurring a higher latency. These methods, designed for offline or reactive settings, are inadequate for the proactive paradigm. Our work emphasizes the growing need for designing proactive prompt adaptation methods, where the models must remain robust to evolving needs in deployment.

AI.tldr: RECAP is a benchmark for evaluating prompt optimization methods under proactive, evolving constraints — without test-time feedback. Current methods designed for static or reactive settings fail to improve performance, highlighting the need for proactive adaptation approaches.
AI.motivation: Production agentic systems must adapt to evolving constraints (e.g., policy updates, compliance thresholds) immediately from the next interaction, with zero room for errors. Existing benchmarks assume static constraints or reactive protocols with evaluation feedback, creating a gap between benchmark evaluation and real-world deployment requirements.
AI.method: RECAP measures continual-learning phenomena — forgetting, regression, and forward transfer — at the constraint level using a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol. Prompt optimization methods receive only the constraint specification and must generalize before seeing any test data. The benchmark evaluates six methods across four LLMs and three constraint evolution schedules.
AI.result: All six evaluated methods showed no significant performance improvement under the proactive paradigm, despite incurring higher latency. These methods, designed for offline or reactive settings, proved inadequate when constraints evolve without test-time feedback.
AI.conclusion: There is a pressing need to design prompt adaptation methods specifically for proactive settings, where models must remain robust to evolving deployment needs without relying on evaluation feedback.

title: Quantum-Inspired Trace-Augmented Evidence Selection for Reasoning over Structured Hypothesis Spaces
summary: Large language models (LLMs) now solve a wide range of expert-level exams at or above human level, yet remain brittle on specialised, evidence-intensive domains such as law. On these tasks, errors arise not only from gaps in world knowledge but also from subtle distinctions between pieces of evidence and inconsistent use of supporting evidence.
