The most common aggregator over sampled chain-of-thought (CoT) traces, majority vote, returns the most popular answer regardless of whether its evidence is actually strongest. We propose to treat the selection of CoT reasoning fragments into a set of evidence as an explicit combinatorial optimisation problem, allowing well-supported but minority hypotheses to override noisy majorities, and to evaluate the approach on legal-reasoning benchmarks that are particularly sensitive to evidence quality. We introduce EP-HUBO (Evidence Pool Higher-Order Binary Optimisation), which generates multiple CoT traces with a small local model, parses fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, solves a higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation per pool with quality-derived weights (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness), and delegates a single adjudication call per question to a frontier model. We evaluate EP-HUBO on two evidence-intensive legal benchmarks using both simulated annealing on classical hardware and the Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine from Quantum Computing Inc. HUBO-style optimisation gives a principled way to aggregate reasoning fragments while preserving minority-but-correct hypotheses, and is most valuable in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not already absorbed the benchmark material.

AI.tldr: EP-HUBO treats CoT evidence aggregation as a combinatorial optimization problem using higher-order binary optimization, allowing minority-but-well-supported hypotheses to override noisy majorities, evaluated on legal reasoning benchmarks with both classical and quantum hardware.
AI.motivation: LLMs fail on evidence-intensive domains like law due to subtle evidentiary distinctions and inconsistent evidence use. Majority voting over CoT traces ignores evidence quality, favoring popularity over correctness.
AI.method: EP-HUBO generates multiple CoT traces via a small local model, parses fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, solves higher-order unconstrained binary optimization with quality-derived weights (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness), and delegates final adjudication to a frontier model. Evaluated using simulated annealing and Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine.
AI.result: HUBO-style optimization provides principled evidence aggregation that preserves minority-but-correct hypotheses, proving especially valuable in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not memorized benchmark material.
AI.conclusion: Explicit combinatorial optimization over CoT evidence fragments offers a principled alternative to majority voting for evidence-intensive tasks, with practical value in non-memorized domains.

title: Agentic Very Much! Adoption of Coding Agent in New GitHub Projects
summary: In previous work, we investigated the adoption of coding agents in GitHub projects, finding that it was very significant. This study follows this line of work, but analyses new projects, that were created after the previous study. In this new sample, we find that the adoption of coding agents is more than twice as high. We also find that the adoption is significantly more intensive, as the proportion of AI-assisted commits is sensibly higher, despite strong signs that we do not detect all of it.

AI.tldr: A follow-up study reveals that adoption of AI coding agents in new GitHub projects has more than doubled, with AI-assisted commits being significantly more intensive than previously observed, despite likely underdetection.
AI.motivation: To track the evolving adoption trajectory of AI coding agents in open-source GitHub projects by examining a fresh cohort of projects created after the initial study period, thereby capturing temporal trends in adoption rate and intensity.
AI.method: The authors replicate their prior methodology on a new sample of GitHub projects created after the original study timeframe, analyzing both the prevalence of coding agent adoption and the proportion of AI-assisted commits within those projects.
AI.result: Adoption of coding agents in the new project sample is more than twice as high as in the previous study. Additionally, the intensity of adoption — measured by the proportion of AI-assisted commits — is sensibly higher, despite evidence that the detection method misses a substantial portion of AI-generated contributions.
AI.conclusion: AI coding agent adoption in open-source development is accelerating rapidly, both in breadth (more projects using them) and depth (higher share of AI-generated code per project). The true adoption figures are likely even higher than reported due to detection limitations.

title: Authorized and Verifiable Searchable Encryption Based on Public Key Equality Test for Cloud Storage
summary: Cloud storage revolutionizes data management but raises conflicts between functionality and privacy. Public Key Encryption with Equality Test (PKEET), an advanced cryptographic technique, can enable multi-user searchable encryption (SE) through cross-key ciphertext comparison without shared keys. However, existing PKEET-based SE schemes lack ciphertext-file-level authorization, public verifiability, or SE-level support. This paper first proposes a novel PKEET scheme, AVPKEET (Authorized and Verifiable PKEET). It enables non-transferable and non-replayable authorization of ciphertext files, while supporting public verifiability, all without the need for trusted third parties. Then we propose an AVPKEET-based SE scheme, denoted as AVSE (Authorized and Verifiable SE), featuring one-time non-transferable tokens bound to users and nonces, batch operations, and fine-grained access control (ALL, PARTIAL, SINGLE). We prove OW-CCA2 security, token unforgeability, and verification soundness under standard assumptions. Experiment results demonstrate that AVSE achieves the most compact token size (168 bytes) while uniquely providing both ciphertext-file-level authorization and public verification, with acceptable overhead for cloud storage deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AVPKEET, a novel Public Key Encryption with Equality Test scheme that supports ciphertext-file-level authorization and public verifiability without trusted third parties, and builds AVSE, a searchable encryption system with compact one-time tokens, batch operations, and fine-grained access control.
AI.motivation: Existing PKEET-based searchable encryption schemes lack three critical features simultaneously: ciphertext-file-level authorization, public verifiability, and SE-level support, creating a gap between functionality and privacy in cloud storage.
AI.method: The authors first construct AVPKEET with non-transferable, non-replayable ciphertext authorization and public verifiability. Then they build AVSE atop AVPKEET, incorporating one-time tokens bound to users and nonces, batch operations, and three-tier fine-grained access control (ALL, PARTIAL, SINGLE). Security is proven under OW-CCA2, token unforgeability, and verification soundness in the standard model.
AI.result: AVSE achieves the most compact token size (168 bytes) among comparable schemes while uniquely providing both ciphertext-file-level authorization and public verification, with acceptable overhead for cloud storage deployment.
AI.conclusion: The AVPKEET/AVSE framework fills the gap in PKEET-based SE by simultaneously delivering fine-grained authorization, public verifiability, and practical efficiency, making it suitable for real-world cloud storage environments.

title: ShallowBench: Benchmarking Generative Drug Design Models on Shallow-Pocket Targets
summary: While generative AI models have demonstrated remarkable success in structure-based drug design, they predominantly rely on deep binding pockets and struggle to sample effective ligands for challenging low-pocketability targets, such as the historically "undruggable" oncology targets KRAS and MYC. To address this gap, we introduce ShallowBench, a strictly curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets extracted from CrossDocked2020. By computing the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume and the underlying protein atom voxel volume, we successfully isolated targets with low concavity while ensuring sufficient surface area for binding. Evaluating various state-of-the-art generative models reveals weaker predicted binding affinity on these low-concavity interfaces. ShallowBench therefore provides a rigorous benchmark for generative biology models and highlights the necessity of new architectural innovations or loss functions capable of navigating these challenging targets.

AI.tldr: ShallowBench introduces a curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket protein targets to evaluate generative AI models on historically "undruggable" low-concavity targets (e.g., KRAS, MYC), revealing that current SBDD models struggle with weak binding affinity on these challenging interfaces.
AI.motivation: Generative AI models for structure-based drug design (SBDD) excel with deep binding pockets but fail on low-pocketability targets like KRAS and MYC — historically "undruggable" oncology proteins. No existing benchmark systematically evaluates model performance on shallow-pocket targets.
AI.method: The authors constructed ShallowBench from CrossDocked2020 by computing the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume and the underlying protein atom voxel volume, isolating 5,780 targets with low concavity but sufficient surface area for binding. State-of-the-art generative models were then evaluated on this benchmark for predicted binding affinity.
AI.result: All evaluated state-of-the-art generative models exhibited weaker predicted binding affinity on low-concavity interfaces compared to deep-pocket targets, confirming the inadequacy of current architectures for shallow-pocket drug design.
AI.conclusion: ShallowBench provides a rigorous, standardized benchmark that exposes the limitations of current generative SBDD models on shallow-pocket targets and underscores the need for new architectural innovations or loss functions tailored to these challenging interfaces.

title: Accounting for Context: Shaping Moral Credences for Value Alignment
summary: Ensuring that agent behaviours are aligned with human moral values inevitably raises the problem of how to account for the plurality of moral perspectives that societies -- and even individuals -- typically adopt. Work on moral uncertainty proposes mechanisms to fairly and democratically aggregate evaluations of actions across different moral theories. However, this paper argues that one needs to account for contextual factors when aggregating moral evaluations. For example, consequentialist perspectives assume an ability to accurately determine how an agent's actions change the world; an assumption that often does not hold in real world settings. We, therefore, formalise agent decision making under moral uncertainty, while also accounting for these kinds of contextual factors. We thereby show that a seemingly commonsensical property -- the weak Pareto principle -- is violated. We argue that this apparent problem is, in fact, a variation of Simpson's paradox, and hence reveals the limitations of aggregation mechanisms that ignore the impact of contextual factors.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that aggregating moral theories under uncertainty must account for contextual factors (e.g., unreliable causal knowledge in consequentialism). It formalizes decision-making under moral uncertainty with context and shows the weak Pareto principle is violated, revealing this as a Simpson's paradox variant that exposes limitations of context-ignoring aggregation mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Existing moral uncertainty frameworks aggregate evaluations across moral theories to handle pluralism, but they typically ignore contextual factors that affect the reliability or applicability of each theory. The paper aims to demonstrate that neglecting such context leads to aggregation failures.
AI.method: Formalization of agent decision-making under moral uncertainty that incorporates contextual factors; demonstration that the weak Pareto principle is violated under this formalization; identification of the violation as an instance of Simpson's paradox.
AI.result: When contextual factors are accounted for, the weak Pareto principle — a seemingly commonsensical aggregation property — is violated. This reveals a structural limitation in moral aggregation mechanisms that ignore context.
AI.conclusion: The violation of the weak Pareto principle, understood as a variation of Simpson's paradox, underscores the necessity of incorporating contextual factors into moral uncertainty frameworks for aligning agent behavior with human values.

title: Defending Jailbreak Attacks on Large Language Models via Manifold Trajectory Kinetics
comment: Accepted to USENIX Security '26 Cycle 2. Code is available at https://github.com/Rookie143/mtk
summary: Jailbreak prompts can bypass alignment guardrails in large language models (LLMs) and elicit unsafe outputs, making reliable deployment-time detection critical. Prior detection approaches largely rely on a fixed metric space, e.g., raw inputs, gradients, or hidden features, in which benign and jailbreak prompts are linearly separable. We show this assumption breaks under (i) pseudo-malicious prompts that are benign by intent but contain safety-related keywords, and (ii) adaptive attacks that explicitly optimize against the deployed detector. To overcome this limitation, we shift our focus from identifying a universal metric space to analyzing the more robust neighborhood structure of the underlying data manifold. We present Manifold Trajectory Kinetics (MTK), which treats an LLM as a kinetic system transforming inputs into outputs and detects jailbreaks by tracking how a prompt's neighborhood structure evolves across layers. Benign prompts remain close to benign neighborhoods throughout inference, whereas jailbreak prompts exhibit a characteristic trajectory that begins near malicious seeds and later strategically shifts toward benign neighborhoods to evade refusal.Across four LLMs and ten jailbreak attacks, MTK achieves strong robustness to both failure modes: on pseudo-malicious prompts, it attains a jailbreak true positive rate of 95% at a false positive rate of 5% on benign prompts and 2% on pseudo-malicious prompts, and under adaptive attacks, it maintains a true positive rate of 85%. We further demonstrate the superior performance of MTK for jailbreak detection in vision-language models. Our code is available at https://github.com/Rookie143/mtk.

AI.tldr: Manifold Trajectory Kinetics (MTK) detects LLM jailbreaks by tracking how a prompt's neighborhood structure evolves across network layers, overcoming failures of fixed-metric-space detectors against pseudo-malicious prompts and adaptive attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing jailbreak detectors assume benign and malicious prompts are linearly separable in a fixed metric space (raw inputs, gradients, or hidden features). This assumption fails under two practical scenarios: (1) pseudo-malicious prompts that are benign but contain safety-related keywords, and (2) adaptive attacks that explicitly optimize against the deployed detector. A more robust detection approach is needed.
AI.method: The authors propose Manifold Trajectory Kinetics (MTK), which models the LLM as a kinetic system and analyzes how a prompt's neighborhood structure on the data manifold evolves across transformer layers. Benign prompts stay near benign neighborhoods throughout inference, while jailbreak prompts exhibit a distinctive trajectory: starting near malicious seeds and later shifting toward benign neighborhoods to evade refusal. Detection is based on this characteristic trajectory pattern rather than a fixed metric space.
AI.result: Across 4 LLMs and 10 jailbreak attacks, MTK achieves strong robustness: on pseudo-malicious prompts, it attains 95% jailbreak true positive rate (TPR) at 5% false positive rate (FPR) on benign prompts and only 2% FPR on pseudo-malicious prompts. Under adaptive attacks, it maintains 85% TPR. MTK also demonstrates superior performance for jailbreak detection in vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from fixed metric spaces to neighborhood manifold structure provides significantly more robust jailbreak detection. MTK's trajectory-based approach effectively handles both pseudo-malicious prompts and adaptive attacks, representing a meaningful advance in deployment-time LLM safety.

title: Korean Culture into LLM Alignment: Toward Cultural Coherence
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop on Culture X AI
summary: Cultural-aspect work on large language models is dominated by a negative target: which outputs to suppress. We argue that a constructive counterpart is also needed, a working definition of what a culturally coherent response is rather than only what it must avoid, and instantiate it for Korean. We design an alignment-data pipeline around a prompt-based LLM seed generator that expands a Korean harm taxonomy, with a Korean-culturally-adapted safe-response policy at its centre: a per-category guideline grounded in Korean legal frameworks, social norms, and interpretive conventions, against which three frontier models each produce a candidate response. DPO fine-tuning on the resulting triplets improves the Korean cultural safe rate across six open-weight LLMs while causing no large degradation on Korean general-capability benchmarks, and qualitative outputs show fine-tuned models naming Korean statutes and institutional procedures and, where appropriate, supplying constructive Korean-context information alongside refusal.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that cultural alignment of LLMs needs not just suppression of harmful outputs but a constructive definition of culturally coherent responses. The authors instantiate this for Korean by building an alignment-data pipeline with a Korean-adapted safe-response policy grounded in legal and social norms, then use DPO fine-tuning to improve cultural safety without degrading general capability.
AI.motivation: Current cultural alignment work on LLMs focuses predominantly on a negative target — identifying and suppressing harmful outputs. The authors argue this is insufficient and that a constructive counterpart is needed: a positive, working definition of what constitutes a culturally coherent response, not merely what to avoid.
AI.method: The authors design an alignment-data pipeline centered on (1) a prompt-based LLM seed generator that expands a Korean harm taxonomy; (2) a Korean-culturally-adapted safe-response policy with per-category guidelines grounded in Korean legal frameworks, social norms, and interpretive conventions; (3) three frontier models each producing a candidate response, yielding triplets; and (4) DPO fine-tuning on these triplets across six open-weight LLMs.
AI.result: DPO fine-tuning improves the Korean cultural safe rate across all six open-weight LLMs, with no large degradation on Korean general-capability benchmarks. Qualitatively, fine-tuned models appropriately name Korean statutes and institutional procedures and supply constructive Korean-context information alongside refusals when warranted.
AI.conclusion: A constructive approach to cultural alignment — defining what a culturally coherent response should include, not just what to avoid — is both feasible and effective. Grounding safe-response policies in jurisdiction-specific legal and social norms yields models that are not only safer but also more helpful within a given cultural context.

title: MSAIC-Net: A Multi-Scale Attention and Imbalance-Aware Contrastive Network for ECG-Based Myocardial Substrate Abnormality Detection
summary: Myocardial substrate abnormalities, such as myocardial scar and myocardial infarction (MI), are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes. Electrocardiography (ECG) provides a low-cost and widely available tool for detecting these abnormalities, but ECG-based detection remains challenging due to heterogeneous lead-dependent manifestations, high-dimensional multi-lead signals, class imbalance, and the limited interpretability of deep learning models. We propose a multi-scale attention-enhanced convolutional network (MSAIC-Net) for ECG-based myocardial substrate abnormality detection. MSAIC-Net employs parallel atrous convolutional branches to extract ECG features across multiple temporal receptive fields. %, enabling the model to capture both local and longer-range temporal patterns. Channel attention is then used to adaptively reweight informative lead-wise and feature-channel representations. To address class imbalance and improve feature separability, we introduce a novel imbalance-aware supervised contrastive learning strategy that encourages samples from the same class to form compact representations while increasing separation between abnormal and normal samples. Lead-wise permutation importance is further incorporated to quantify the contribution of each ECG lead and improve model interpretability. The proposed method was evaluated on two complementary datasets: a low-data institutional cohort from the University of Virginia (UVA) Health System for myocardial scar classification and the large-scale public PTB-XL dataset from PhysioNet for MI identification. Experimental results show that MSAIC-Net outperforms baseline models, with particularly pronounced improvements in the low-data UVA cohort. Overall, the proposed framework provides an effective and interpretable approach for ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities.

AI.tldr: MSAIC-Net is a multi-scale attention-enhanced convolutional network that combines parallel atrous convolutions, channel attention, contrastive learning for class imbalance, and lead-wise permutation importance to improve ECG-based detection of myocardial scar and infarction, outperforming baselines especially in low-data settings.
AI.motivation: ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities (scar, MI) is challenging due to heterogeneous lead-dependent manifestations, high-dimensional multi-lead signals, severe class imbalance, and poor interpretability of existing deep learning models. There is a need for an effective, interpretable method that works well even with limited labeled data.
AI.method: MSAIC-Net architecture comprises: (1) parallel atrous (dilated) convolutional branches capturing multi-scale temporal features across different receptive fields; (2) channel attention mechanism for adaptive reweighting of informative leads and feature channels; (3) a novel imbalance-aware supervised contrastive loss that pulls same-class representations together and pushes opposite-class representations apart; and (4) lead-wise permutation importance for post-hoc interpretability, quantifying each lead's contribution. Evaluated on UVA institutional cohort (scar) and PTB-XL (MI).
AI.result: MSAIC-Net outperformed baseline models on both datasets. Improvements were particularly pronounced on the low-data UVA myocardial scar cohort, demonstrating the method's robustness under data scarcity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MSAIC-Net framework provides an effective, interpretable approach for ECG-based myocardial substrate abnormality detection, with strong performance in both large-scale and low-data clinical scenarios.

title: Exploring Agentic Tool-Calling Decisions via Uncertainty-Aligned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents often make suboptimal tool-use decisions, including unsupported tool invocation and hallucinated direct responses, which may accumulate errors throughout multi-step interactions. Existing approaches mainly improve these behaviors through inference-time correction or coarse-grained reward signals based on decision outcomes and structured checklists, leaving the uncertainty characteristics of agent decisions underexplored. We observe that decision-oriented reinforcement learning tends to weaken the uncertainty separation between correct and incorrect actions, resulting in overconfident mistakes and weaker exploration signals. Therefore, we propose TRUST, which incorporates uncertainty quantification into reward design as a repulsive force for maintaining uncertainty separation, and labels lightweight key-turn annotations for unified post-training of multi-turn trajectories. Experimental results across diverse tool-use benchmarks show that TRUST consistently enhances both decision quality and agent performance while maintaining more reliable uncertainty estimates during optimization.

AI.tldr: TRUST introduces uncertainty quantification into reward design for LLM-based tool-use agents, using uncertainty separation as a repulsive force to prevent overconfident mistakes while leveraging lightweight key-turn annotations for multi-turn post-training, yielding improved decision quality and reliable uncertainty estimates.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents exhibit suboptimal tool-use decisions (unsupported invocations, hallucinated responses), and existing coarse-grained reward signals or inference-time corrections fail to address the underlying issue: decision-oriented RL weakens uncertainty separation between correct and incorrect actions, causing overconfident errors and poor exploration.
AI.method: TRUST incorporates uncertainty quantification directly into reward design as a repulsive force to maintain separation between uncertainty estimates of correct vs. incorrect actions. It also employs lightweight key-turn annotations to enable unified post-training across multi-turn trajectories, rather than relying solely on outcome-based or checklist-based rewards.
AI.result: Across diverse tool-use benchmarks, TRUST consistently improves both decision quality and overall agent performance while preserving more reliable uncertainty estimates during optimization, compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Integrating uncertainty quantification into RL reward design effectively mitigates overconfident mistakes in LLM agents, and lightweight key-turn supervision enables scalable multi-turn post-training with better-calibrated uncertainty.

title: Empirical Evaluation of Large Language Models for Migration of Code Fragments to Post-Quantum Cryptography
summary: The transition to post-quantum cryptography (PQC) requires not only replacing vulnerable cryptographic primitives, but also refactoring the surrounding software logic. While existing PQC migration frameworks provide organizational guidance, practical code-level remediation remains largely manual and error-prone. This paper evaluates whether large language models (LLMs) can be trained to assist in the migration of pre-quantum cryptographic code fragments to post-quantum counterparts while preserving functional correctness. To this end, we introduce a reproducible experimental framework built around a synthetic dataset of 800 paired Python code fragments covering six cryptographic families and combined multi-primitive cases. Each pair is validated through category-specific functional tests, enabling both dataset quality control and objective evaluation of model-generated migrations. Four models are assessed: GPT-4.1 in a zero-shot setting, and fine-tuned versions of GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4.1-mini, and CodeLlama-7B-Instruct. The results show that domain-specific fine-tuning is essential for reliable cryptographic migration. The fine-tuned GPT-4.1-mini model achieves the best overall performance, with a mean static similarity of 0.9072 and a dynamic functional correctness rate of 92.5%, substantially outperforming the zero-shot baseline. A complementary validation on six open-source repositories further shows that the approach can produce useful migrations in localized cryptographic modules, while also revealing limitations in larger projects with complex dependencies and cross-module interactions. These findings suggest that fine-tuned LLMs can serve as practical components in future crypto-agile migration pipelines, provided they are coupled with automated verification and dependency-aware validation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether fine-tuned LLMs can automate the migration of pre-quantum cryptographic code to post-quantum equivalents. Using a synthetic dataset of 800 paired Python fragments and functional validation tests, the authors demonstrate that fine-tuned GPT-4.1-mini achieves 92.5% functional correctness, substantially outperforming zero-shot baselines. Validation on six open-source repositories confirms practical utility in localized modules while highlighting challenges with complex dependencies.
AI.motivation: Existing PQC migration frameworks provide organizational guidance but lack automated code-level remediation, leaving manual refactoring as the dominant — and error-prone — approach. The authors aim to assess whether LLMs can fill this gap by reliably transforming pre-quantum cryptographic code into functionally correct post-quantum counterparts.
AI.method: The authors construct a synthetic dataset of 800 paired Python code fragments spanning six cryptographic families (including combined multi-primitive cases). Each pair is validated via category-specific functional tests. Four models are evaluated: zero-shot GPT-4.1, and fine-tuned GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4.1-mini, and CodeLlama-7B-Instruct. Evaluation uses static similarity metrics and dynamic functional correctness rates. Complementary validation is performed on six real-world open-source repositories.
AI.result: Domain-specific fine-tuning proves essential: the fine-tuned GPT-4.1-mini achieves a mean static similarity of 0.9072 and 92.5% dynamic functional correctness, significantly exceeding the zero-shot baseline. Real-world repository validation confirms useful migrations in localized cryptographic modules but reveals limitations in larger projects with complex dependencies and cross-module interactions.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuned LLMs can serve as practical components in crypto-agile migration pipelines, but must be coupled with automated verification and dependency-aware validation to handle real-world complexity. The approach shows strong promise for localized cryptographic code migration while acknowledging current scalability limitations.

title: Quantifying Media Representation Dynamics Across 25 Years of News Reporting on Policing-related Deaths
summary: We perform the largest known computational analysis of Canadian news narratives about police-involved deaths, spanning 4,000 articles from the last quarter-century. We develop a novel computational model, PerspectiveGap, grounded in prior sociological work on media representation of policing. We find that reporting on police-involved deaths on average features perspectives from state bureaucrats at a rate nearly three times as much as perspectives from other members of the public, including relatives, community members, eyewitnesses, lawyers representing the family, or civil liberties groups. A considerable fraction of articles contain no points of view from civilian actors, though civilian representation has increased in recent years. Qualitatively, we find that state bureaucrats' accounts of these deaths tend to be clinical and procedural, while civilian discourse carries considerably more emotional valence. The PerspectiveGap framework developed here can be contextualized to other jurisdictions, offering a scalable approach for analyzing how media systems construct narratives around policing and accountability.

AI.tldr: A large-scale computational analysis of 4,000 Canadian news articles over 25 years reveals that state bureaucrat perspectives dominate reporting on police-involved deaths at nearly three times the rate of civilian voices. State accounts are clinical and procedural, while civilian discourse carries emotional weight. The authors introduce PerspectiveGap, a scalable computational framework for analyzing media narrative construction around policing and accountability.
AI.motivation: Prior sociological research has identified imbalances in media representation of policing, but lacks large-scale, longitudinal, computational approaches to systematically quantify whose perspectives are amplified or marginalized in news coverage of police-involved deaths.
AI.method: The authors developed PerspectiveGap, a novel computational model grounded in sociological theory, and applied it to a corpus of 4,000 Canadian news articles spanning approximately 25 years (the last quarter-century), quantitatively comparing the prevalence and nature of state bureaucrat versus civilian perspectives.
AI.result: State bureaucrat perspectives appear at nearly 3× the rate of civilian perspectives (including relatives, community members, eyewitnesses, family lawyers, and civil liberties groups). A substantial fraction of articles contain no civilian viewpoints at all, though civilian representation has improved in recent years. Qualitatively, state accounts are clinical/procedural, while civilian discourse exhibits markedly higher emotional valence.
AI.conclusion: Canadian news media systematically privilege state bureaucrat voices over civilian ones in coverage of police-involved deaths. The PerspectiveGap framework is portable to other jurisdictions and offers a scalable method for auditing media narrative construction around policing and accountability.

title: Flatland: The Adventures of Gradient Descent with Large Step Sizes
comment: Accepted for the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: The training of neural networks often entails objective functions that are not globally $L$-smooth. For these functions, it is both theoretically and practically difficult to reply to the question: what is the largest possible step size that ensures the convergence of gradient descent (GD)? We address this longstanding open question in deep learning by providing a unifying definition of "large" step sizes that requires only local Lipschitz (or even Hölder) continuity of the gradient. We design first-order adaptive methods that provably yield large step sizes and show that they operate at the edge of stability (EoS) right from the start of the training. In particular, the loss decreases nonmonotonically and the product between the step size and sharpness, i.e., the largest eigenvalue of the Hessian, stays above the EoS threshold of 2 throughout training. Using our method, we are also able to minimize the sharpness all the way down to its global minimum. Contrary to expectation, we find that encountering globally-flat regions too early in the training may both slow down convergence and jeopardize the generalization ability of the network. Exploiting a self-stabilization argument, we allow GD to enter slightly sharper valleys and turn unsuccessful training runs into very successful ones.

AI.tldr: This paper defines "large" step sizes for gradient descent under only local Lipschitz/Hölder gradient continuity, designs adaptive methods that provably achieve such step sizes while operating at the edge of stability (EoS) from training onset, and reveals that encountering globally-flat regions too early harms convergence and generalization, whereas slightly sharper valleys can be beneficial via self-stabilization.
AI.motivation: Address the longstanding open problem of determining the maximum convergent step size for gradient descent when the objective is not globally L-smooth—a common scenario in neural network training where theoretical guarantees are scarce and practical tuning is heuristic.
AI.method: The authors (1) provide a unifying definition of "large" step sizes requiring only local Lipschitz or Hölder continuity of the gradient, (2) design first-order adaptive methods that provably achieve these large step sizes, (3) demonstrate that these methods operate at the edge of stability (Hessian's largest eigenvalue times step size > 2) from the start, and (4) employ a self-stabilization mechanism that allows GD to enter slightly sharper valleys, converting failing runs into successful ones.
AI.result: The proposed methods yield large step sizes while maintaining convergence; loss decreases nonmonotonically with the step-size–sharpness product staying above 2 throughout training; sharpness can be minimized to its global minimum. Surprisingly, encountering globally-flat regions too early slows convergence and harms generalization, while slightly sharper valleys improve outcomes via self-stabilization.
AI.conclusion: Non-global L-smoothness can be navigated with locally-adaptive large step sizes operating at the edge of stability. Flatness is not universally desirable—early exposure to globally-flat regions is detrimental, and self-stabilization through sharper valleys can salvage otherwise failing training runs.

title: Teaching the Way, Not the Answer: Privileged Tutoring Distillation for Multimodal Policy Optimization
summary: Recent post-training methods, particularly Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), have significantly enhanced the reasoning ability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). However, the sparse nature of verifiable rewards provides little token-level supervision for failed rollouts, often leading to inefficient exploration in complex multimodal reasoning tasks. Although policy distillation can offer dense guidance, external teacher based methods introduce substantial computational overhead, while answer conditioned tuning methods may expose answer-level information and induce shortcut-like generation behavior. To address these limitations, we propose PTD-PO, a Privileged Tutoring Distillation Policy Optimization framework for RLVR that provides dense guidance without exposing the answer to the student policy. Specifically, PTD-PO constructs structured privileged hints from spatial attention guidance and intermediate textual reasoning steps, and uses them through in-context learning to produce step-wise token-distribution supervision. The student is still optimized under the original answer-free context, and its failed rollouts are aligned with the hint-augmented reference model at the token-distribution level. To further stabilize distillation under the distribution shift between guided and unguided contexts, we introduce a Top-K Jensen-Shannon divergence objective that focuses alignment on informative token probabilities while reducing memory overhead. Experiments on LVLMs ranging from 2B to 8B parameters show that PTD-PO consistently outperforms RLVR and distillation baselines, mitigates entropy collapse, and improves complex multimodal reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: PTD-PO introduces a privileged tutoring distillation framework for RLVR that provides dense, token-level supervision on failed rollouts via structured hints (spatial attention + intermediate reasoning) without exposing the answer, using a Top-K Jensen-Shannon divergence objective to stabilize distillation under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: RLVR improves LVLM reasoning but suffers from sparse verifiable rewards, offering little token-level supervision for failed rollouts and causing inefficient exploration. Existing solutions like teacher-based distillation are costly, while answer-conditioned tuning risks shortcut learning. The goal is dense guidance without exposing answer information.
AI.method: PTD-PO: (1) Constructs privileged hints from spatial attention guidance and intermediate textual reasoning steps. (2) Uses in-context learning with these hints to generate a reference model that provides step-wise token-distribution supervision. (3) Aligns the student's failed rollouts (under answer-free context) with the hint-augmented reference at token-distribution level. (4) Employs a Top-K Jensen-Shannon divergence objective focusing alignment on informative token probabilities to stabilize distillation under distribution shift.
AI.result: Experiments on LVLMs (2B–8B parameters) show PTD-PO consistently outperforms RLVR and distillation baselines, mitigates entropy collapse, and improves complex multimodal reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: PTD-PO effectively addresses the sparse supervision problem in RLVR by providing dense, answer-free guidance through privileged tutoring distillation, achieving superior multimodal reasoning without the drawbacks of teacher-based or answer-conditioned methods.

title: On the Shoulders of Giants: Empowering Automated Smart Contract Auditing via the GiAnt Corpus
summary: High-quality smart contract auditing datasets are crucial for evaluating security tools and advancing smart contract security research. Two major limitations of existing datasets are the manual-induced scalability bottleneck and the deficiency in data granularity and diversity. To address these limitations, we propose GiANT, an automated framework designed to curate smart contract auditing datasets by distilling vulnerability insights from real-world auditing reports. GiANT employs a divide-and-conquer strategy coupled with the Chain-of-Thought technique to extract structured vulnerability information from Code4rena reports, followed by an LLM-as-a-judge mechanism to perform rigorous quality assurance. To evaluate GiANT's effectiveness, we run it on 388 real-world audit reports and generate the GiAnt Corpus comprising 7,711 vulnerability findings across five severity levels. Manual assessment of the dataset demonstrates exceptional reliability in information extraction, achieving a mean quality score of $4.76\pm0.37$ (out of 5) with inter-rater agreement $κ$ of 0.88. We further validate the practicality of our dataset by benchmarking 4 state-of-the-art LLMs on vulnerability detection, code summarization, mitigation recommendation, and automated gas optimization tasks, to establish performance baselines, thereby providing a valuable data foundation for future research in automated smart contract auditing.

AI.tldr: GiANT automates the curation of high-quality smart contract auditing datasets from Code4rena reports using a divide-and-conquer + Chain-of-Thought approach with LLM-as-judge quality assurance, generating a 7,711-finding corpus (quality score 4.76/5, κ=0.88) and benchmarking four SOTA LLMs on vulnerability detection and related tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing smart contract auditing datasets suffer from two critical limitations: (1) a manual-induced scalability bottleneck that hinders large-scale dataset creation, and (2) insufficient data granularity and diversity, limiting their utility for evaluating and advancing security tools and research.
AI.method: GiANT is an automated framework that distills vulnerability insights from real-world Code4rena audit reports. It applies a divide-and-conquer strategy combined with Chain-of-Thought prompting to extract structured vulnerability information (e.g., severity, location, description). An LLM-as-a-judge mechanism then performs rigorous quality assurance on the extracted data to ensure reliability.
AI.result: Running on 388 audit reports, GiANT produced the GiAnt Corpus with 7,711 vulnerability findings across five severity levels. Manual assessment yielded a mean quality score of 4.76 ± 0.37 (out of 5) with a κ inter-rater agreement of 0.88, indicating exceptional extraction reliability. Benchmarking four SOTA LLMs established performance baselines for vulnerability detection, code summarization, mitigation recommendation, and automated gas optimization.
AI.conclusion: GiANT successfully automates scalable, high-quality auditing dataset curation, overcoming manual bottlenecks and granularity limitations. The resulting GiAnt Corpus provides a valuable, reliable foundation for future research in automated smart contract auditing, as evidenced by strong manual validation and comprehensive LLM benchmarking.

title: Progress-SQL: Improving Reinforcement Learning for Text-to-SQL via Progressive Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning has recently shown promise in improving large language models for Text-to-SQL generation, yet existing methods typically optimize one-shot rewards defined over a single SQL state. Such rewards provide limited guidance for iterative SQL correction and are insufficient to capture the improvement of multi-turn SQL refinement. In this paper, we propose Progress-SQL, a multi-turn reinforcement learning framework with progressive rewards for Text-to-SQL. Our approach introduces an Oracle-guided Diagnostic Tree (ODT), which abstracts SQL queries into clause-level structural profiles and produces diagnostic feedback for next-turn refinement. To provide dense and robust reward signals, we combine ODT-based structural alignment with lexical alignment and define a progressive reward that measures the improvement from the initial SQL to the final SQL. We further incorporate a progression latency reward that favors earlier correctness and an execution status reward that encourages recovery from the invalid SQL. Experiments on BIRD, Spider, and Spider robustness variants demonstrate that our method consistently improves Text-to-SQL performance across both primary and robustness evaluations.

AI.tldr: Proposes Progress-SQL, a multi-turn RL framework for Text-to-SQL that uses Oracle-guided Diagnostic Trees and progressive rewards (structural alignment, lexical alignment, progression latency, execution status) to iteratively refine SQL queries, outperforming existing methods on BIRD, Spider, and robustness benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for Text-to-SQL rely on one-shot rewards defined over a single SQL state, which inadequately guide iterative SQL correction and fail to capture improvement across multi-turn refinement. Dense, progressive reward signals are needed.
AI.method: (1) Oracle-guided Diagnostic Tree (ODT) abstracts SQL into clause-level structural profiles and produces diagnostic feedback; (2) Progressive reward combining ODT-based structural alignment with lexical alignment measures improvement from initial to final SQL; (3) Progression latency reward favors earlier correctness; (4) Execution status reward encourages recovery from invalid SQL. All integrated into a multi-turn RL framework.
AI.result: The method consistently improves Text-to-SQL performance on BIRD, Spider, and Spider robustness variants across both primary and robustness evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Multi-turn RL with progressive, dense reward signals derived from structural and lexical alignment significantly enhances Text-to-SQL generation and iterative refinement capabilities.

title: Synthics: Synthetic Physics-like Datasets for Machine Learning
summary: Representative data is fundamental in machine learning, as limited data hinders generalisation. Collecting sufficient real-world samples is often infeasible. Synthetic data generation offers a practical solution, but only if the generated data faithfully reflects the structure of real observations. In this paper, a method for generating synthetic regression datasets that structurally resemble physics equations from a given equation corpus is presented. The approach uses a Bayesian Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar to capture the underlying algebraic structure of the corpus, from which novel equations are sampled. To ensure the generated inputs lie within a physically meaningful domain, the applicability domain is characterised for each equation through non-intrusive probing, also recovering inter-variable constraints. Input sampling further mimics realistic experimental conditions by drawing from random sub-ranges of the valid domain with mixed uniform and truncated normal distributions. The generated data is statistically validated against the Feynman equation corpus using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests. The generated equations match the corpus on all of the eight studied structural features, compared to only two for an unsmoothed purely probabilistic grammar, demonstrating that the Bayesian prior is essential for structural fidelity given the size of the corpus. In a downstream hyperparameter-tuning task, a gradient-boosted regressor tuned on the synthetic data picks, on average, the 6th-best configuration out of 20 on real data, matching the result of tuning on real data itself and substantially outperforming random expression trees (10th) and noise (19th).

AI.tldr: A Bayesian Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar is used to generate synthetic regression datasets that structurally mimic the Feynman physics equation corpus. The method learns algebraic structure, constrains inputs to physically valid domains, and produces data that statistically matches real equations on all eight structural features studied. Models tuned on the synthetic data perform nearly as well as those tuned on real data.
AI.motivation: Limited real-world data hinders generalization in machine learning. While synthetic data generation offers a solution, it requires the generated data to faithfully reflect the structure of real observations. The paper addresses the challenge of generating synthetic regression datasets that preserve the algebraic and structural properties of physics equations.
AI.method: The method employs a Bayesian Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) to learn the algebraic structure from a corpus of physics equations (Feynman equation corpus). Novel equations are sampled from this grammar. Applicability domains are characterized via non-intrusive probing to recover inter-variable constraints and ensure physically meaningful inputs. Input sampling uses mixed uniform and truncated normal distributions drawn from random sub-ranges of the valid domain. Validation is performed using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests against the original corpus.
AI.result: The generated equations match the Feynman corpus on all eight studied structural features, whereas an unsmoothed purely probabilistic grammar matches only two. In a downstream hyperparameter-tuning task, a gradient-boosted regressor tuned on synthetic data picks the 6th-best configuration out of 20 on average, matching real-data tuning performance and substantially outperforming random expression trees (10th) and noise baselines (19th).
AI.conclusion: The Bayesian prior is essential for structural fidelity given the limited corpus size. The proposed method produces synthetic regression data that reliably substitutes for real data in hyperparameter tuning, demonstrating practical utility for domains where real data collection is infeasible.

title: The Sim-to-Real Gap of Foundation Model Agents: A Unified MDP Perspective
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted by KDD 2026 Blue Sky Ideas Track
summary: Foundation model agents are increasingly deployed for real-world decision-making, but suffer from the sim-to-real gap. While robotics and classical control have mature frameworks to address this gap, the foundation model community is treating agent robustness as an entirely novel phenomenon. Our paper proposes formalizing the foundation model agent evaluation and training gap as a classical sim-to-real problem structured entirely around the four elements of a Markov Decision Process, including Observation, Action, Transition, and Reward. In this paper, we set a comprehensive research agenda that translates classical discrepancies into the foundation model domain and advocates for adopting established solutions like domain randomization. We provide concrete examples, such as a multilingual tool calling to demonstrate how severe observation space gaps lead to operationally invalid actions despite correct semantic intent. Ultimately, this agenda aims to drive a paradigm shift, yielding a unified vocabulary and standardized stress test benchmarks to foster a new generation of highly trustworthy agents for reliable real-world applications.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that the foundation model agent community should frame evaluation and training gaps as a classical sim-to-real problem structured around the four MDP elements (Observation, Action, Transition, Reward), rather than treating agent robustness as a novel phenomenon. It calls for adopting established solutions like domain randomization and proposes a research agenda with standardized benchmarks for trustworthy real-world agents.
AI.motivation: Foundation model agents are increasingly deployed in real-world settings but suffer from a sim-to-real gap. While robotics and classical control have mature frameworks for this, the foundation model community treats agent robustness as entirely new, leading to fragmented approaches. The authors aim to unify the discourse by mapping the problem onto the well-understood MDP framework.
AI.method: The paper proposes a conceptual framework that formalizes the foundation model agent gap as a sim-to-real problem structured around the four MDP components: Observation (sensory/perceptual discrepancies), Action (execution discrepancies), Transition (environment dynamics mismatches), and Reward (objective misalignment). It advocates translating classical solutions like domain randomization into the foundation model domain and provides concrete illustrative examples (e.g., multilingual tool calling demonstrating observation-space gaps).
AI.result: The paper is primarily a position/agenda paper rather than an empirical study. Its key contribution is the conceptual mapping itself, illustrated through a concrete example where multilingual tool-calling reveals how observation-space gaps (language variations) produce operationally invalid actions despite correct semantic intent. The result is a structured research agenda calling for unified vocabulary and standardized stress-test benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Reframing foundation model agent robustness as a classical sim-to-real problem enables the community to leverage decades of established solutions from robotics and control theory. This paradigm shift promises a unified vocabulary and standardized benchmarks, ultimately fostering a new generation of highly trustworthy agents suitable for reliable real-world deployment.

title: Lost in Migration: Exposing Android Framework Vulnerabilities in Parallel Java-Kotlin Implementations
summary: Android has adopted Kotlin alongside Java across apps and core system components. During this shift, we observe parallel implementations in the Android Open Source Project (AOSP) where the same component is implemented in both Java and Kotlin. In principle, their functional purposes are identical. In practice, subtle semantic divergences can appear. Such divergences are not vulnerabilities by themselves, but they provide useful clues that may reveal flaws in surrounding enforcement logic. To the best of our knowledge, this paper presents the first systematic study of Java-Kotlin parallel implementations in the Android framework and examines their security implications. We design and build ParaDroid, an analysis framework that identifies parallel methods at scale and compares their behaviors. ParaDroid normalizes code into a bytecode-level intermediate representation, reconstructs class-to-source mappings, and uses large language models to reason about method semantics and identify behavioral divergences. Evaluated on AOSP Android 14-16, ParaDroid identified 329 parallel method pairs and 37 vulnerable divergences. We responsibly disclosed the exploitable issues to the Android Security Team. Three vulnerabilities and two bugs have been confirmed, and two CVE IDs have been assigned. Our results demonstrate that parallel Java-Kotlin code paths provide a practical surface for discovering security flaws in modern Android.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ParaDroid, a framework for systematically identifying Java-Kotlin parallel implementations in the Android Open Source Project and detecting behavioral divergences. Analyzing AOSP Android 14-16, the authors found 329 parallel method pairs and 37 vulnerable divergences, with 3 vulnerabilities and 2 bugs confirmed by the Android Security Team, plus 2 CVE assignments.
AI.motivation: Android's gradual adoption of Kotlin alongside Java has led to parallel implementations of the same components in both languages within AOSP. While functionally identical in principle, subtle semantic divergences can arise, acting as clues that expose flaws in surrounding security enforcement logic. No prior work had systematically studied these parallel Java-Kotlin implementations or their security implications.
AI.method: The authors built ParaDroid, which: (1) normalizes both Java and Kotlin code into a bytecode-level intermediate representation; (2) reconstructs class-to-source mappings to identify parallel method pairs at scale; and (3) employs large language models to reason about method semantics and detect behavioral divergences between paired implementations.
AI.result: Evaluation on AOSP Android 14-16 identified 329 parallel method pairs and 37 vulnerable divergences. Responsible disclosure to the Android Security Team resulted in confirmation of 3 vulnerabilities and 2 bugs, with 2 CVE IDs assigned.
AI.conclusion: Parallel Java-Kotlin code paths in Android constitute a practical attack surface for discovering security flaws. ParaDroid demonstrates that systematic, LLM-assisted comparison of such parallel implementations can effectively uncover previously unknown vulnerabilities in modern Android.

title: The Dark Regulome: Disentangling Predictability from Regulation in Genomic Foundation Models
summary: High-grade gliomas integrate into neural circuits through functional synapses with neurons, raising the question of which noncoding elements shape synaptogenic gene expression in tumor cells. The regulatory program written across the dark genome, what we call the $\textit{dark regulome}$, is the natural substrate to probe, and sequence foundation models offer a zero-shot route through in-silico mutagenesis (ISM); yet likelihood-based scoring is tautologically coupled to local sequence predictability, leaving the regulatory interpretation underdetermined. Across three architecturally distinct foundation models (Caduceus-Ph, HyenaDNA, Enformer) and 30,448 dark genome elements at 92 glioma-relevant loci, we introduce a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic that separates predictability-driven from regulation-driven RIS variance. A sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon survives every control we apply, but the LM-derived element-class hierarchy does not: a six-feature linear baseline matches Caduceus top-decile membership at AUC $= 0.985$. Cross-architecture decomposition cleanly separates a sequence-predictability layer (the two language models co-rank long well-predicted transposable elements) from a regulatory-output layer (Enformer alone retains residual cCRE-discriminative signal), with literally zero overlap between the two top-100 lists. Conservation, brain cis-eQTL, and STRING-PPI cross-checks then anchor what biology survives: top-100 elements across all three models are $3.3\times$ enriched per model for matching brain eQTLs ($p_\mathrm{emp} < 5\times 10^{-3}$), while a tempting transposable-element regulatory layer and a striking NRXN1+NLGN1 protein-pair convergence both fail proper permutation tests once those tests are constructed. We deliver the diagnostic as a general methodological tool for any ISM-based regulatory study.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a diagnostic framework to disentangle whether in-silico mutagenesis scores from sequence foundation models reflect true regulatory function or merely local sequence predictability. Applied to the dark genome in glioma, the authors show that most model-derived regulatory hierarchies collapse when controlled for sequence predictability, but a subset of elements with genuine regulatory signal — enriched for brain eQTLs — survives rigorous permutation testing.
AI.motivation: High-grade gliomas form functional synapses with neurons, implicating noncoding regulatory elements in driving synaptogenic gene expression. The "dark regulome" (noncoding genome) is the natural place to search, and sequence foundation models enable zero-shot in-silico mutagenesis (ISM) to score regulatory potential. However, ISM scores are confounded by local sequence predictability — highly predictable sequences score highly regardless of regulatory function — making it impossible to distinguish regulation-driven signal from predictability-driven artifacts using standard likelihood-based scoring.
AI.method: The authors develop a residualization-and-permutation diagnostic applied across three architecturally distinct foundation models (Caduceus-Ph, HyenaDNA, Enformer) to 30,448 dark genome elements at 92 glioma-relevant loci. They residualize ISM scores against sequence-predictability metrics and use permutation tests to separate the two variance components. They further validate surviving elements using conservation, brain cis-eQTL enrichment, and STRING-PPI analyses, with careful empirical permutation controls for each cross-check.
AI.result: A sharp 10kb proximal-regulatory horizon is robust across all controls. However, the language-model-derived element-class hierarchy collapses: a six-feature linear baseline predicts Caduceus top-decile membership at AUC=0.985. Cross-architecture decomposition reveals two cleanly separated layers — Caduceus-Ph and HyenaDNA co-rank long, well-predicted transposable elements (sequence-predictability layer), while only Enformer retains residual cCRE-discriminative signal (regulatory-output layer) with zero overlap between the two top-100 lists. Top-100 elements across all three models show 3.3× enrichment for matching brain eQTLs (p_emp < 5×10⁻³), but a proposed transposable-element regulatory layer and an NRXN1+NLGN1 protein-pair convergence both fail proper permutation tests.
AI.conclusion: Without rigorous controls, ISM-based rankings from sequence foundation models conflate predictability with regulatory function. The residualization-and-permutation diagnostic provides a general-purpose tool to disentangle these confounds, and only a small subset of dark genome elements — validated by orthogonal evidence like brain eQTL enrichment — survive as genuine regulatory candidates. The diagnostic is offered as a methodological standard for any ISM-based regulatory study going forward.

title: TorchKM: A GPU-Oriented Library for Kernel Learning and Model Selection
summary: TorchKM is an open-source library for kernel machines, including support vector machines, kernel logistic regression, and kernel quantile regression, with GPU acceleration. The library features a scikit-learn-style API and is designed to exploit GPU-friendly linear algebra, accelerating the full training and model-selection pipeline through intelligent reuse of matrix operations. Benchmarks show competitive predictive performance together with substantial speedups over standard baselines. Code and documentation are available at https://github.com/YikaiZhang95/torchkm, and the package can be easily installed via PyPI.

AI.tldr: TorchKM is a GPU-accelerated, scikit-learn-compatible library for kernel machines (SVMs, kernel logistic/quantile regression) that achieves significant speedups through efficient linear algebra and intelligent matrix reuse.
AI.motivation: Standard kernel machine implementations lack GPU acceleration and fail to exploit reusable matrix operations across the full training and model-selection pipeline, leading to computational bottlenecks.
AI.method: The library provides a scikit-learn-style API and leverages GPU-friendly linear algebra with intelligent reuse of kernel matrix computations to accelerate both model training and hyperparameter selection end-to-end.
AI.result: Benchmarks demonstrate predictive performance competitive with standard baselines while delivering substantial speedups due to GPU acceleration and operation reuse strategies.
AI.conclusion: TorchKM offers a practical, high-performance, and easily accessible (PyPI-installable) open-source solution for accelerating kernel machine workflows on modern GPU hardware.

title: StainFlow: Entity-Stain Tracking and Evidence Linking for Process Rewards in GUI Agents
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a promising approach for improving GUI Agents in long-horizon, stochastic digital environments, but trajectory-level success feedback is too sparse to provide reliable credit assignment for intermediate exploration steps. To mitigate this issue, recent studies introduce Process Reward Models (PRMs), which provide finer-grained training feedback through global milestone verification or local step-level evaluation. However, these methods still suffer from two level-specific limitations: global milestone decomposition is subjective and singular, making it difficult to accommodate the multiple valid execution paths in real GUI tasks, while fixed local judging windows may miss long-range key evidence or dilute the decision signal with irrelevant frames. Inspired by stain-tracing mechanisms in network flow analysis, we propose StainFlow, an entity-stain-flow process reward model for GUI Agents. To reduce the subjectivity of global partitioning, we introduce the Global Entity Stain Tracking module, which extracts visually verifiable task entities and tracks how their stain concentrations and states evolve along the trajectory, allowing task phases to be objectively separated by changes in the entity evidence flow. To improve the accuracy of local verification, we introduce the Local Stain Evidence Linking module. Centered on the triggering entities of each candidate key node, it retrieves relevant steps based on their stain concentrations and state changes, and dynamically constructs high-density evidence windows for verifying true key nodes. Extensive experiments on AndroidWorld and OGRBench show that StainFlow relatively improves online RL success by 3.2% and trajectory completion judgment accuracy by 1.8%.

AI.tldr: StainFlow introduces an entity-stain-flow process reward model for GUI agents, using stain-tracing mechanisms from network flow analysis to objectively partition task phases via entity state evolution and dynamically construct evidence windows for accurate step verification, achieving 3.2% improvement in online RL success and 1.8% improvement in trajectory completion judgment.
AI.motivation: Existing Process Reward Models (PRMs) for GUI agent RL suffer from two limitations: (1) global milestone decomposition is subjective and cannot accommodate multiple valid execution paths, and (2) local judging windows with fixed lengths either miss long-range key evidence or dilute decision signals with irrelevant frames. Sparse trajectory-level feedback remains insufficient for reliable credit assignment.
AI.method: StainFlow adapts stain-tracing from network flow analysis into two modules: (1) Global Entity Stain Tracking — extracts visually verifiable task entities, tracks their stain concentrations and state evolution along trajectories, and objectively separates task phases based on entity evidence flow changes; (2) Local Stain Evidence Linking — anchors on triggering entities of candidate key nodes, retrieves relevant steps by stain concentrations and state changes, and dynamically constructs high-density evidence windows for verifying true key nodes.
AI.result: On AndroidWorld and OGRBench, StainFlow achieved a 3.2% relative improvement in online RL success rate and a 1.8% relative improvement in trajectory completion judgment accuracy compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: StainFlow effectively addresses the subjectivity of global milestone decomposition and the rigidity of local evidence windows in PRMs for GUI agents by leveraging entity-stain-flow tracking, leading to demonstrable gains in both policy learning and trajectory evaluation.

title: Verifiable and Confidential DNN Inference on Low-End Edge Devices
summary:
Deploying deep neural network (DNN) inference on low-end edge devices raises two key challenges: protecting model confidentiality against a potentially compromised edge system and enabling verifiable inference without incurring prohibitive overhead. Existing approaches either house partial models and inference software within trusted execution environments (TEEs), resulting in high cost and an application-dependent trusted computing base (TCB), or execute in untrusted environments, providing little security.
  In this work, we present VECODI, a framework for verifiable and confidential DNN inference on constrained edge devices. At its core, VECODI introduces SHANGRI-LA, a new execution abstraction on TrustZone-M TEEs that establishes a third runtime environment with privileges strictly between the Secure and Non-Secure Worlds. VECODI leverages SHANGRI-LA to execute untrusted inference code in the Non-Secure World while using minimal application-agnostic Secure-World support to protect model confidentiality and enable verifiability (with respect to proper execution of inference code and model parameters) of inference results. We realize VECODI on a real-world NUCLEO-L552ZE-Q development board and open-source its prototype. Our results show VECODI's small TCB, memory footprint, and runtime overhead, making it a practical option for secure inference in low-end edge devices.

AI.tldr: VECODI is a framework that enables confidential and verifiable DNN inference on constrained edge devices by introducing SHANGRI-LA, a new TrustZone-M execution abstraction that creates a third privilege level between Secure and Non-Secure worlds, achieving small TCB, low memory footprint, and minimal runtime overhead.
AI.motivation: Deploying DNN inference on low-end edge devices faces two conflicting challenges: protecting model confidentiality from compromised edge systems and enabling verifiable inference. Existing solutions either place partial models in TEEs (high cost, large TCB) or run entirely in untrusted environments (weak security). There is a need for a practical approach that balances security and efficiency on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: VECODI introduces SHANGRI-LA, a novel execution abstraction on ARM TrustZone-M TEEs that establishes a third runtime environment with privileges strictly intermediate between the Secure and Non-Secure Worlds. It executes untrusted inference code in the Non-Secure World while using minimal, application-agnostic Secure-World support to protect model confidentiality and provide verifiability — ensuring both correct execution of inference code and integrity of model parameters. The framework is implemented and evaluated on a real NUCLEO-L552ZE-Q development board.
AI.result: VECODI demonstrates a small trusted computing base (TCB), low memory footprint, and minimal runtime overhead on real hardware (NUCLEO-L552ZE-Q), confirming its practicality for secure inference on low-end edge devices. The prototype is open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: VECODI, powered by the SHANGRI-LA abstraction, provides a practical solution for verifiable and confidential DNN inference on constrained edge devices, overcoming the cost-security trade-offs of prior TEE-based and untrusted-environment approaches.

title: Translate-R1: Cost-Aware Translation Tool Use via Reinforcement Learning
comment: 14 pages main text plus appendix, 7 figures, 11 tables
summary:
The performance gap across languages in LLMs is well documented, and closing it natively requires pretraining or fine-tuning on corpora that, for most languages, do not exist. Translation offers an alternative: converting an input into the model's dominant language unlocks its full capabilities at once. Applying translation to every input, however, is wasteful for languages the model already handles, while leaving the choice to the model fails in the opposite way, as LLMs are overconfident and skip the tool even when they cannot understand the input. Prior work resolves this with language-specific rules, domain heuristics, language identifiers, or external routers, each requiring manual engineering. We instead learn a single policy that decides when to translate from reward alone, developing language- and domain-adaptive introspection that assesses its own comprehension and invokes translation only when it cannot solve a task natively.
  Using data built by our answer-preserving translation pipeline, we continue RL on the post-trained Qwen3-4B across 22 languages in 3 resource tiers (High, Low, XLow) and 5 domains, and introduce confidence-gated GSPO for cost-sensitive tool use. The gated policy lifts reward over the baseline by +4.6 on High, +23.5 on Low, and +17.5 on XLow. Against an unconstrained policy that almost always translates, it preserves full reward at 63% of the cost and is Pareto-optimal across 87% of the cost-sensitivity range. Additionally, to simulate behavior on a completely unseen language, we create 2 synthetic languages, where our gated policy improves +18.7 over the overconfident baseline that underutilizes the tool even on these incomprehensible inputs. The policy transfers zero-shot to 9 held-out languages, and we analyze how tool use emerges over training, per language and per domain.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes learning a single, reward-driven policy for adaptive translation in LLMs, enabling the model to introspectively decide when to translate an input rather than relying on manually engineered rules. Using confidence-gated GSPO on Qwen3-4B across 22 languages and 5 domains, the policy significantly boosts reward in low-resource languages while maintaining cost efficiency, achieving Pareto-optimal trade-offs and zero-shot transfer to unseen languages.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit a well-documented performance gap across languages. Closing this gap natively requires scarce multilingual corpora. Translation unlocks capabilities but applying it universally is wasteful for languages the model already handles, while letting the model decide fails because LLMs are overconfident and skip translation even when needed. Existing solutions rely on brittle, manually engineered rules (language identifiers, domain heuristics, external routers). The authors aim to replace this with a learned, adaptive policy driven purely by reward.
AI.method: The authors develop an answer-preserving translation pipeline to build training data across 22 languages (tiered as High, Low, XLow) and 5 domains. They continue RL on post-trained Qwen3-4B using a proposed algorithm called confidence-gated GSPO (Group Sampling Policy Optimization), which introduces cost-sensitive tool use by penalizing unnecessary translation calls. The policy learns language- and domain-adaptive introspection: the model assesses its own comprehension and invokes translation only when it cannot solve a task natively. The approach is also tested on 2 synthetic languages to simulate completely unseen languages, and zero-shot transfer is evaluated on 9 held-out languages.
AI.result: The gated policy lifts reward over baseline by +4.6 (High), +23.5 (Low), and +17.5 (XLow). Against an unconstrained policy that nearly always translates, it preserves full reward at 63% of the cost and is Pareto-optimal across 87% of the cost-sensitivity range. On synthetic languages, it improves +18.7 over the overconfident baseline. The policy transfers zero-shot to 9 held-out languages. Analysis shows how tool use emerges over training per language and per domain.
AI.conclusion: A single learned policy for adaptive translation, guided by reward and confidence-gated optimization, effectively balances performance and cost across diverse languages and domains. The approach eliminates the need for manual engineering of translation rules, generalizes to unseen languages, and demonstrates that LLMs can develop introspective capabilities for tool-use decisions through reinforcement learning.

title: Hierarchical Semantic-Constrained Heterogeneous Graph for Audio-Visual Event Localization
summary: Open-vocabulary audio-visual event localization (OV-AVEL) jointly models audio-visual cues to recognize and temporally localize events, including categories unseen during training. Existing methods primarily learn joint audio-visual representations in Euclidean space, but still face two significant challenges. First, the lack of supervision signals for unseen categories makes it difficult to maintain audio-visual consistency across multiple temporal scales. Second, the lack of hierarchical constraints between segment- and video-level semantics prevents the model from establishing semantic consistency across different levels. To address these challenges, we propose a hierarchical semantic constrained heterogeneous graph (HSCHG) for audio-visual event localization framework. We first construct a heterogeneous hierarchical graph in Euclidean space, which includes audio and visual segment nodes and their corresponding video-level nodes. We use multi-directional temporal edges to capture complete temporal information within each modality. Simultaneously, we employ a dual-threshold filtering gated fusion strategy, introducing cross-modal information only when the alignment confidence is high. Furthermore, we introduce bidirectional semantic constraints between segment- and video-level representations to achieve semantic consistency across different levels. Based on this, we map the multi-level audio-visual representations and text prototypes uniformly into hyperbolic space. We use a hierarchical entailment regularization loss to characterize the hierarchical relationships between videos and segments. Extensive experimental results show that our method outperforms existing methods on the OV-AVEL benchmark. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: HSCHG proposes a hierarchical semantic constrained heterogeneous graph framework for open-vocabulary audio-visual event localization, mapping multi-level representations into hyperbolic space with entailment regularization to address cross-scale consistency and hierarchical semantic constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing OV-AVEL methods operating in Euclidean space face two key challenges: (1) lack of supervision for unseen categories makes cross-scale audio-visual consistency difficult, and (2) absence of hierarchical constraints between segment-level and video-level semantics prevents consistent multi-level semantic modeling.
AI.method: The method constructs a heterogeneous hierarchical graph with audio/visual segment and video nodes connected via multi-directional temporal edges. A dual-threshold filtering gated fusion strategy introduces cross-modal information only under high alignment confidence. Bidirectional semantic constraints enforce segment-video consistency. All multi-level representations and text prototypes are then mapped into hyperbolic space with a hierarchical entailment regularization loss to model video-segment hierarchical relationships.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the OV-AVEL benchmark demonstrate that HSCHG outperforms all existing methods. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each proposed component.
AI.conclusion: The HSCHG framework effectively addresses open-vocabulary audio-visual event localization by combining heterogeneous graph modeling in Euclidean space with hyperbolic space mapping and hierarchical entailment regularization, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

title: Hearing the Unspoken: Language Model Priors for Acoustic Adversarial Attacks
summary: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems operating in real-time settings must process acoustic input under strict temporal constraints, where transcription decisions are inherently made on incomplete information. This causal constraint serves as an information bottleneck on attackers, significantly limiting attack performance.
