However, in some languages, LLM usage was simultaneously more expensive (higher token consumption) and less accurate.
AI.conclusion: High-quality machine translation (synthetic data) may be adequate for large-scale multilingual reasoning evaluations when official translations are unavailable. The cost-accuracy disparity in certain languages highlights an efficiency concern for multilingual LLM deployment.

title: From Sampled Outcomes to Capability Distributions: Rethinking Supervision for LLM Routing
summary: Existing LLM routing methods typically treat a model's single response to a query as its capability label for training routers. However, because LLM generation is inherently stochastic, such single-shot supervision provides only a noisy observation of a query-model pair's behavior rather than a reliable capability estimate. We show that this assumption introduces systematic noise into routing supervision, making learned routing policies less reliable. To address this issue, we propose DARS (Distribution-Aware Routing Supervision), a framework that constructs routing supervision from a distributional view of model behavior. Instead of relying on a single generated response, DARS considers uncertainty from both the input side and the output side, capturing how semantically equivalent query formulations and stochastic generations affect model performance. Based on these distribution-aware observations, DARS builds more reliable supervision signals for routing. Experiments across diverse tasks show that single-shot labels can be misleading for model selection, while distribution-aware supervision provides more stable labels and improves learned routing behavior. Our results suggest that reliable LLM routing should move beyond single-response observations and be grounded in query-level model capability distributions.

AI.tldr: Traditional LLM routing uses a single model response as a noisy capability label. DARS proposes distribution-aware supervision that accounts for variability in both query formulations and stochastic generations, yielding more stable and reliable routing policies.
AI.motivation: LLM generation is inherently stochastic, so using a single response as a capability label introduces systematic noise into routing supervision. The authors argue that single-shot observations fail to capture the true behavior of a query-model pair, leading to unreliable learned routing policies.
AI.method: DARS constructs routing supervision from a distributional perspective, incorporating uncertainty from two sources: (1) the input side—semantically equivalent query reformulations, and (2) the output side—stochastic variations across multiple model generations. These distribution-aware observations are then used to build more reliable supervision signals for training routers.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that single-shot labels are often misleading for model selection, while distribution-aware supervision produces more stable labels and improves learned routing behavior.
AI.conclusion: Reliable LLM routing should move beyond single-response observations and instead be grounded in query-level model capability distributions, suggesting a fundamental shift in how routing supervision is constructed.

title: SigmaScale: LLM Compression with SVD-based Low-Rank Decomposition and Learned Scaling Matrices
summary: We present SigmaScale, a method for learning auxiliary scaling matrices $S$ to aid truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) based Large Language Model (LLM) compression. Instead of deriving scaling matrices analytically, SigmaScale optimizes two sets of vectors that define diagonal row and column scaling transformations under an activation-aware compression loss. We show that learned scaling lowers the effective intrinsic rank of weight matrices, as reflected by reductions in effective-rank entropy, and that this reduction is strongly correlated with compression loss. Experiments on Llama 3.1 8B Instruct and Qwen3-8B show that SigmaScale is competitive with closely related state-of-the-art SVD-based compression methods across perplexity and zero-shot benchmarks. By using learned activation-aware transformations, SigmaScale explores a more flexible route to low-rank LLM compression by adapting to the structure of individual model weights. The advantage observed in specific tasks makes our approach a valid option for applications requiring a reduced LLM-inference computing cost.

AI.tldr: SigmaScale learns auxiliary diagonal scaling matrices for SVD-based LLM compression by optimizing row and column scaling vectors under an activation-aware loss, reducing effective rank entropy and achieving competitive perplexity and zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: Existing SVD-based LLM compression methods often derive scaling matrices analytically; this work explores whether learned, activation-aware scaling transformations can more flexibly adapt to individual weight matrix structures to improve low-rank compression quality.
AI.method: SigmaScale optimizes two sets of vectors that define diagonal row and column scaling transformations ($S$) applied to weight matrices. The optimization minimizes an activation-aware compression loss. The learned scaling reduces the effective intrinsic rank, quantified via effective-rank entropy, which is shown to strongly correlate with compression loss.
AI.result: On Llama 3.1 8B Instruct and Qwen3-8B, SigmaScale achieves competitive results against state-of-the-art SVD-based compression methods across perplexity and zero-shot benchmarks. Effective-rank entropy reduction correlates strongly with compression loss.
AI.conclusion: Learned activation-aware scaling transformations offer a flexible and valid approach to low-rank LLM compression, with particular advantages in specific downstream tasks, making SigmaScale suitable for reducing LLM inference compute costs.

title: Uniform Stability and Generalization Error of GD and SGD on Fixed-Point Parameters
summary: We analyze generalization error, uniform stability, and uniform argument stability of gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) over discrete parameter spaces, where each update involves deterministic or stochastic rounding. We show that deterministic rounding degrades the generalization error of GD on convex, Lipschitz, and smooth loss functions, increasing the rate from $O(T/n)$ to $O(T/\sqrt{n})$, and establish matching lower bounds. We further prove that uniform stability of GD becomes $Ω(T)$, showing that stability-based generalization bounds are vacuous in this setting. In contrast, for the same losses, stochastic gradient descent with deterministic rounding admits nontrivial uniform stability guarantees, which differ qualitatively from the real-valued case and exhibit distinct dependencies on the number of iterations and the dimension: we prove tight bounds $O(T/n)$ for one dimension and $O(T^2/n)$ for higher dimensions. We also show that stochastic rounding can introduce generalization error that increases with the dimension; such a phenomenon is absent in standard real-valued optimization and in the deterministic rounding case. Finally, we provide upper bounds on uniform argument stability for stochastic rounding schemes and show that these bounds are tight when the loss can be represented as a sum of coordinate-wise functions.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes generalization error and stability of gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) in discrete parameter spaces with deterministic or stochastic rounding. Key findings: (1) deterministic rounding degrades GD generalization from O(T/n) to O(T/√n) with matching lower bounds; (2) GD uniform stability becomes Ω(T), rendering stability-based bounds vacuous; (3) SGD with deterministic rounding admits nontrivial stability guarantees — O(T/n) for 1D, O(T²/n) for higher dimensions; (4) stochastic rounding introduces dimension-dependent generalization error absent in real-valued optimization; (5) tight uniform argument stability bounds are provided for stochastic rounding under coordinate-wise separable losses.
AI.motivation: In real-valued optimization, stability-based generalization bounds for GD/SGD are well-understood. However, when parameters are constrained to discrete spaces (e.g., due to quantization, low-precision training, or hardware constraints), updates must be rounded. The impact of rounding on generalization and algorithmic stability is not well characterized. This paper aims to fill this gap by systematically analyzing how deterministic and stochastic rounding affect generalization error and stability in discrete optimization.
AI.method: The authors analyze GD and SGD over discrete parameter spaces where each update applies either deterministic rounding (e.g., nearest neighbor) or stochastic rounding (randomized rounding with expectation matching the true value). They derive upper and lower bounds on generalization error, uniform stability, and uniform argument stability using tools from learning theory and optimization. The analysis considers convex, Lipschitz, and smooth loss functions, and carefully tracks dependencies on iteration count T, sample size n, and dimension d.
AI.result: (1) Deterministic rounding degrades GD generalization from O(T/n) to O(T/√n), with matching lower bounds. (2) GD uniform stability under deterministic rounding is Ω(T), making stability-based generalization bounds vacuous. (3) SGD with deterministic rounding achieves O(T/n) uniform stability in 1D and O(T²/n) in higher dimensions — both tight. (4) Stochastic rounding introduces dimension-dependent generalization error not seen in real-valued or deterministic rounding settings. (5) Tight upper bounds on uniform argument stability for stochastic rounding are established when the loss decomposes coordinate-wise.
AI.conclusion: Rounding in discrete optimization fundamentally alters generalization and stability properties compared to real-valued optimization. Deterministic rounding is particularly harmful for GD, while SGD with deterministic rounding retains nontrivial guarantees. Stochastic rounding introduces novel dimension-dependent effects. These results provide theoretical guidance for algorithm design in discrete parameter settings such as low-precision training and quantized neural networks.

title: Style or Content? Evaluating Style Classifiers with Controlled Content Overlap
summary: Style classifiers can use content cues that correlate with style labels in naturally collected data, yet we lack a systematic way to measure this reliance. We study this problem with a controlled content overlap setup built on parallel Bible translations. Specifically, we define the overlap parameter $α$ as the normalized residual of mutual information between content identity and style label, so that it measures how much content is shared across style classes: from no shared content ($α=0$) to fully shared content ($α=1$). Cross-overlap evaluation of RoBERTa-based classifiers shows that low-overlap models degrade when content cues are removed, while high-overlap models transfer more robustly. A cross-style content retrieval probe further shows that content becomes less recoverable as $α$ increases, with training dynamics showing this removal occurs gradually. Together, these results suggest that controlled overlap provides a simple diagnostic for separating style learning from content shortcuts.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a controlled content overlap framework using parallel Bible translations to systematically measure the extent to which style classifiers rely on content cues rather than genuine stylistic features, revealing that low-overlap models degrade without content shortcuts while high-overlap models transfer robustly.
AI.motivation: Style classifiers trained on naturally occurring data often exploit content cues that spuriously correlate with style labels, but no systematic method exists to quantify this reliance. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing a principled diagnostic for disentangling style learning from content-based shortcuts.
AI.method: Using parallel Bible translations as a controlled setting, the authors define a normalized overlap parameter α based on the residual mutual information between content identity and style label (0 = no shared content, 1 = fully shared content). They train RoBERTa-based classifiers under varying α conditions and evaluate cross-overlap transfer performance. Additionally, they employ a cross-style content retrieval probe to measure content recoverability across different α levels and analyze training dynamics.
AI.result: Classifiers trained under low-α (low content overlap) conditions degrade significantly when content cues are removed during cross-overlap evaluation. In contrast, high-α models transfer more robustly. The retrieval probe confirms that content becomes progressively less recoverable as α increases, and training dynamics show this disentanglement occurs gradually rather than abruptly.
AI.conclusion: The controlled overlap parameter α serves as a simple yet effective diagnostic tool for assessing whether a style classifier has learned genuine stylistic features versus exploiting content shortcuts, with implications for building more robust and transferable style classification systems.

title: GenPO++: Generative Policy Optimization with Jacobian-free Likelihood Ratios
summary: Generative policies provide expressive and multimodal action distributions, making them attractive for reinforcement learning (RL) in complex continuous-control tasks. Among them, flow-based policies are especially appealing because they generate actions through deterministic transport maps. However, applying such generative policies to likelihood-based on-policy learning remains limited by the difficulty of evaluating the probability of executed actions. Existing flow RL methods either replace the true action-density ratio with approximate surrogates, which can introduce biased updates, or recover exact likelihoods through dummy-action augmentation, which enlarges the policy space and increases computation. In this work, we propose GenPO++, a reversible generative policy optimization framework that uses history states as auxiliary memory in a high-order reversible ODE solver, yielding exact inversion without changing the original action dimension. The resulting generative policy map has a log-determinant determined only by fixed solver coefficients, enabling exact and Jacobian-free likelihood-ratio computation. This design preserves the expressiveness of generative flow policies while avoiding both action ratio bias and dummy-action overhead. We evaluate GenPO++ on large-scale simulated control, fine-tuning, and real-world robotic manipulation tasks, where it achieves competitive or superior performance over state-of-the-art on-policy RL methods, while improving training stability and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: GenPO++ proposes a reversible generative policy optimization framework for on-policy RL that uses history states as auxiliary memory in a high-order reversible ODE solver, enabling exact, Jacobian-free likelihood-ratio computation without dummy actions or biased surrogates.
AI.motivation: Existing flow-based RL policies suffer from either biased updates due to approximate action-density ratios, or increased computation and policy space enlargement from dummy-action augmentation. A method for exact likelihood evaluation of generative flow policies without these drawbacks is needed.
AI.method: GenPO++ frames the policy as a high-order reversible ODE solver where history states serve as auxiliary memory, guaranteeing exact inversion without altering the original action dimension. The log-determinant depends solely on fixed solver coefficients, enabling exact and Jacobian-free likelihood-ratio computation.
AI.result: GenPO++ achieves competitive or superior performance over state-of-the-art on-policy RL methods on large-scale simulated control, fine-tuning, and real-world robotic manipulation tasks, with improved training stability and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: GenPO++ preserves the expressiveness of generative flow policies while eliminating both action-ratio bias and dummy-action overhead, making it a practical and effective approach for likelihood-based on-policy RL in continuous control.

title: Learning Perspectivist Social Meaning via Demographic-Conditioned Fusion Embeddings
summary: Social meaning in language is inherently perspectival, varying across annotator backgrounds, demographics, and ideological positions. However, most NLP systems collapse this variation into a single ground-truth label, ignoring the diversity of interpretations. In this work, we model social dimensions along a perspectivist spectrum, capturing how interpretations vary across demographic groups on a dataset consisting of 28k human annotations. We benchmark multiple modeling paradigms, including zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuned approaches, and propose fusion embeddings that integrate textual and demographic representations. Our fusion models yield consistent and statistically significant improvements over text-only baselines across all fusion strategies (+5.9-6.5% relative macro PR-AUC), with shuffle ablations confirming that demographic profiles carry genuine predictive signal rather than spurious correlations.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the perspectival nature of social meaning in language by modeling how interpretations vary across annotator demographics, using 28k annotations. The authors benchmark multiple modeling paradigms and propose fusion embeddings combining text and demographic representations, achieving significant improvements (+5.9–6.5% relative macro PR-AUC) over text-only baselines.
AI.motivation: Most NLP systems treat social meaning annotation as having a single ground-truth label, collapsing the inherent diversity of interpretations that arise from annotators' varying backgrounds, demographics, and ideological positions. The work is motivated by the need to capture this perspectival variation rather than erase it.
AI.method: The authors model social dimensions on a perspectivist spectrum using a dataset of 28k human annotations. They benchmark zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuned paradigms, and propose fusion embeddings that integrate textual features with demographic representations. Shuffle ablations are used to verify that demographic signals are genuinely predictive rather than spurious.
AI.result: Fusion models that combine text and demographic embeddings consistently and statistically significantly outperform text-only baselines, with relative improvements of +5.9–6.5% in macro PR-AUC across all fusion strategies. Shuffle ablation experiments confirm that demographic profiles carry genuine predictive signal.
AI.conclusion: Modeling social meaning in language requires a perspectivist approach that incorporates annotator demographics. Fusion embeddings that integrate textual and demographic representations provide a robust method for capturing interpretational diversity, outperforming text-only models and demonstrating that demographic information contributes meaningful predictive power.

title: Accelerating Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimisation via Predictive-Gradient Catalysts
summary: This paper presents a general acceleration mechanism for multi-objective Bayesian optimisation (MOBO) that leverages Gaussian process predictive gradients as auxiliary signals. Rather than replacing existing Pareto-compliant acquisition functions, the proposed approach augments them with local stationarity information derived from surrogate-derived gradients, enabling faster convergence toward the global Pareto set under limited evaluation budgets. Two catalyst instantiations are investigated: an adaptive Multiple-Gradient Descent Algorithm-Based Catalyst (MGDA) and a predefined-weight variant that enables focused exploration when budgets are tight. Experiments on the DTLZ benchmark suite (using 2 objectives and 10 decision variables) show that predictive gradient catalysis can deliver significant acceleration compared to other acquisition functions (EHVI, AugTch, tMPoI, SAF) when surrogates are accurate, particularly for stationary problems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a gradient-based catalyst mechanism that augments existing Pareto-compliant acquisition functions in multi-objective Bayesian optimisation, using GP predictive gradients to accelerate convergence toward the global Pareto set. Two variants (adaptive MGDA-based and predefined-weight) are tested on DTLZ benchmarks, showing significant speedups when surrogates are accurate.
AI.motivation: Standard multi-objective Bayesian optimisation (MOBO) relies on acquisition functions that explore the Pareto frontier but can be sample-inefficient under limited evaluation budgets. The authors aim to accelerate convergence by exploiting local stationarity information from GP predictive gradients without replacing existing acquisition functions.
AI.method: The method introduces a "predictive gradient catalysis" mechanism that computes Gaussian process predictive gradients to extract local stationarity (descent) information. This auxiliary signal augments — rather than replaces — standard Pareto-compliant acquisition functions (e.g., EHVI, AugTch, tMPoI, SAF). Two catalyst instantiations are proposed: (1) an adaptive variant using the Multiple-Gradient Descent Algorithm (MGDA) that dynamically balances multiple objective gradients to find a common descent direction, and (2) a predefined-weight variant that biases exploration toward user-specified objective trade-offs under tight budgets.
AI.result: Experiments on the DTLZ benchmark suite (2 objectives, 10 decision variables) demonstrate that predictive gradient catalysis yields significant acceleration over baseline acquisition functions (EHVI, AugTch, tMPoI, SAF) when GP surrogates are accurate, particularly on stationary problems. The MGDA-based adaptive catalyst performs well in general settings, while the predefined-weight variant proves useful for focused exploration under severely constrained evaluation budgets.
AI.conclusion: Predictive gradient catalysis is an effective, plug-and-play acceleration strategy for MOBO that preserves the Pareto-compliance of existing acquisition functions while exploiting local surrogate gradient information for faster convergence. Its effectiveness is contingent on surrogate accuracy and problem stationarity.

title: Explicit Evidence Grounding via Structured Inline Citation Generation
summary: As AI systems become more widely adopted, the demand for factual and faithful generation grows. Properly attributing information through citations becomes, therefore, crucial. This work introduces FullCite, a framework that, in contrast to most previous works, generates structured inline citations linking each claim to both its source document and supporting evidence. FullCite proposes three strategies to inline citation generation: prompt-based generation, constrained decoding over a citation grammar, and posthoc span alignment. Using three question answering benchmarks, namely, ASQA, BioASQ, and ExpertQA, we assess citation quality and faithfulness along three dimensions: document-level correctness, evidence span identification, and claim-citation faithfulness. Our evaluation shows that while LLMs are generally effective at identifying relevant documents, they struggle to identify the precise supporting spans within them. This gap suggests that achieving faithful attributed QA will require research to place greater emphasis on precise evidence span identification.

AI.tldr: FullCite is a framework for generating structured inline citations that link each claim to both a source document and a specific evidence span. It proposes three strategies (prompt-based, constrained decoding, posthoc alignment) and evaluates on ASQA, BioASQ, and ExpertQA, finding that while LLMs identify relevant documents well, they struggle with precise evidence span identification — highlighting a key gap for faithful attributed QA.
AI.motivation: As AI systems see wider adoption, there is a growing demand for factual and faithful generation. Proper attribution via citations is essential for trustworthiness. However, most prior work focuses only on document-level attribution, neglecting the finer-grained task of identifying the specific supporting evidence spans within those documents.
AI.method: FullCite introduces three strategies for inline citation generation: (1) prompt-based generation, where the LLM is prompted to produce citations directly; (2) constrained decoding over a citation grammar, which enforces valid citation structure during generation; and (3) posthoc span alignment, where claims are retroactively aligned to evidence spans after generation. Evaluation is conducted across three QA benchmarks: ASQA (long-form QA), BioASQ (biomedical QA), and ExpertQA (domain-expert QA), assessing quality along three dimensions: document-level correctness, evidence span identification, and claim-citation faithfulness.
AI.result: LLMs demonstrate general effectiveness at document-level citation (identifying the correct source document), but they consistently struggle with precise evidence span identification — i.e., pinpointing the exact supporting text within those documents. This performance gap is observed across all three benchmarks and all three generation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Achieving truly faithful attributed question answering will require the research community to place greater emphasis on the challenging sub-task of precise evidence span identification, rather than focusing solely on document-level attribution.

title: Heterogeneous Effects of Green Finance on Urban Decarbonization: Evidence from 285 Cities in China
summary: While green finance has become a key instrument for low-carbon city transitions, its actual decarbonization effects and transmission mechanisms remain unclear. This study employs econometric models and machine learning-based analysis to examine whether and how green finance reduces city-level carbon intensity. Results show that green finance significantly lowers carbon intensity, with green bonds and green investment having the strongest impacts and evident spatial spillovers. The effects vary by development level, being most pronounced in Fourth- and Fifth-tier cities. Mediation analysis reveals that green finance operates mainly through energy structure optimization, followed by industrial upgrading, foreign direct investment, and technological innovation. SHAP analysis confirms substantial differences across financial instruments, with green bonds, funds, and credit contributing most to decarbonization. Moreover, the marginal impact is stronger in cities with low technological capacity, high industrial dependency, and coal-based energy mixes. These findings provide theoretical support and policy guidance for building a multi-level, regionally differentiated green finance system to promote inclusive low-carbon transitions. Keywords: Green Finance; Carbon Intensity; Decarbonization Effect; Machine Learning; City

AI.tldr: Green finance significantly reduces city-level carbon intensity, primarily through energy structure optimization. Effects vary by financial instrument, city tier, and local conditions, with strongest impacts in less-developed cities and those reliant on coal or heavy industry.
AI.motivation: Despite green finance being widely promoted as a tool for low-carbon transitions, empirical evidence on its actual decarbonization effects and the specific transmission mechanisms at the city level remains unclear, limiting effective policy design.
AI.method: The study combines econometric models (mediation analysis, spatial spillover analysis) with machine learning-based SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis to examine the relationship between green finance instruments and city-level carbon intensity across different urban contexts.
AI.result: Green finance significantly lowers carbon intensity. Green bonds and green investment show the strongest effects with evident spatial spillovers. Impacts are most pronounced in Fourth- and Fifth-tier cities. Mediation reveals energy structure optimization as the primary channel, followed by industrial upgrading, FDI, and technological innovation. SHAP analysis confirms green bonds, funds, and credit contribute most to decarbonization. Marginal effects are stronger in cities with low technological capacity, high industrial dependency, and coal-based energy mixes.
AI.conclusion: A multi-level, regionally differentiated green finance system is needed to promote inclusive low-carbon transitions, with targeted instruments tailored to cities' varying developmental contexts, technological capacities, and energy structures.

title: UrduMMLU: A Massive Multitask Benchmark for Urdu Language Understanding
comment: 27 pages, 18 figures, 17 tables, Submitted to ARR May 2026
summary: Meaningful multilingual evaluation must test models in the target language and educational context. Urdu, spoken by more than 230 million people, lacks a broad MMLU-style benchmark built from native educational sources. We introduce UrduMMLU, a benchmark of 26,431 Urdu MCQs across 26 subjects and five domains, collected from native Urdu MCQ banks and public examination PDFs. Unlike translation-based resources, UrduMMLU covers both standard academic subjects and Urdu- and region-specific content. We label the exam-derived portion through dual human annotation with strict consensus filtering. We evaluate 30 LLMs under English and Urdu prompts, yielding 60 zero-shot evaluations, and further evaluate four open-source LLMs under multiple few-shot settings across both prompt languages. Gemini-3.5-Flash performs best, reaching 90.20% and 90.34% accuracy, while no other model exceeds 85%. The strongest open-source model trails by 7.79 and 8.92 points, and many models lose 25 to 40 points on Urdu-centered Humanities subjects compared with STEM. Few-shot prompting yields only modest gains. UrduMMLU shows that Urdu knowledge remains uneven in current LLMs, especially for regionally grounded content.

AI.tldr: UrduMMLU is a 26,431-question native Urdu benchmark spanning 26 subjects and five domains, built from local exam sources. Evaluating 30 LLMs reveals a large performance gap between proprietary and open-source models, with major weaknesses in Urdu-centered humanities and regional content, and only modest gains from few-shot prompting.
AI.motivation: Existing multilingual benchmarks lack native Urdu content built from local educational materials. With 230+ million speakers, Urdu has no MMLU-style benchmark sourced from its own curriculum, limiting meaningful evaluation of LLMs in this linguistic and cultural context.
AI.method: The authors collected 26,431 Urdu multiple-choice questions from native MCQ banks and public examination PDFs across 26 subjects in five domains (including both standard academics and Urdu-specific content). Exam-derived questions underwent dual human annotation with strict consensus filtering for quality assurance. Thirty LLMs were evaluated in zero-shot settings under both English and Urdu prompts (60 total evaluations), and four open-source LLMs were further tested under multiple few-shot configurations in both languages.
AI.result: Gemini-3.5-Flash achieved top accuracy of 90.20% (English prompt) and 90.34% (Urdu prompt). No other model exceeded 85%. The strongest open-source model trailed by 7.79–8.92 points. Many models lost 25–40 points on Urdu-centered Humanities subjects compared to STEM. Few-shot prompting produced only modest improvements across models.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit uneven Urdu knowledge, with significant performance degradation on regionally grounded content. UrduMMLU provides a rigorous, native-language benchmark that exposes critical gaps in multilingual model capabilities beyond translation-based evaluations.

title: Accelerating Reproducible Research in Synthetic EHR Generation
summary: The generation of high-fidelity synthetic Electronic Health Records (EHR) is crucial for advancing medical research while preserving patient privacy. However, head-to-head comparison of existing generative models is hindered by disjointed codebases, incompatible data loaders, conflicting library dependencies, and inconsistent evaluation protocols. To address these gaps, we introduce a lightweight, end-to-end benchmarking framework for reproducible synthetic EHR evaluation, organized as a unified pipeline spanning data ingestion, standardized model training, and architecture-agnostic evaluation. Our current implementation targets the generation of longitudinal ICD diagnosis codes -- the most commonly studied modality in this literature -- and is built on the community-maintained PyHealth library. We reimplement and unify strong baselines (MedGAN, CorGAN, PromptEHR, HALO) under full ICD-9 vocabulary granularity, and add a lightweight GPT-2 baseline from the general-purpose sequence-modeling literature. We contribute a rigorous, architecture-agnostic privacy-utility evaluation suite that applies identically to GAN- and transformer-based generators, and report bootstrapped confidence intervals across all metrics. We further analyze the poor long-tailed performance of existing models and discuss the extensibility of our framework beyond diagnosis codes. By lowering the engineering barrier to running, extending, and evaluating under a single pipeline, we introduce a starting point for community-driven reproducibility and benchmarking synthetic EHR models.

AI.tldr: A unified benchmarking framework for reproducible evaluation of synthetic EHR generation models (focused on longitudinal ICD codes), reimplementing MedGAN, CorGAN, PromptEHR, and HALO under consistent protocols with rigorous privacy-utility metrics and confidence intervals.
AI.motivation: Head-to-head comparison of synthetic EHR generative models is currently hindered by fragmented codebases, incompatible data loaders, conflicting dependencies, and inconsistent evaluation protocols, creating a reproducibility crisis in the field.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline built on PyHealth that standardizes data ingestion, model training, and architecture-agnostic evaluation. The authors reimplement four strong baselines (MedGAN, CorGAN, PromptEHR, HALO) plus a GPT-2 baseline under full ICD-9 vocabulary, and provide a unified privacy-utility evaluation suite with bootstrapped confidence intervals.
AI.result: The framework enables consistent, apples-to-apples comparison across GAN- and transformer-based generators. Analysis reveals poor long-tailed performance across existing models on rare ICD codes.
AI.conclusion: By lowering engineering barriers through a single unified pipeline, the framework provides a foundation for community-driven reproducibility and standardized benchmarking in synthetic EHR generation, with extensibility beyond diagnosis codes.

title: Geometry of Semantic Space: Comparative Study of Discrete and Continuous Models
summary: This work examines the semantic geometry underlying NLP models. We compare supervised vector embeddings, such as CamemBERT, with lexical co-occurrence graphs that encode semantic relations more directly. While transformer-based embeddings achieve strong performance, their induced geometries often display unsatisfactory distributions. In contrast, graph-based models reveal a clearer and more human-readable organization of meaning. We have implemented a methodology that allows us to perform a comparative analysis either based on the structure of the graphs or based on the topology of the embeddings induced by these two approaches. The results of the comparison -- applied to the French "Great National Debate" corpus a collection of citizen contributions to the public debate -- show a similar local topology but a very different overall structure and topology. Theses findings suggest complementary perspectives between deep supervised models and graph-based models, considering a new pathway to guide neural architectures toward more stable and interpretable convergence with graphs structures.

AI.tldr: Compares CamemBERT embeddings with lexical co-occurrence graphs on French debate corpus; finds similar local topology but divergent global structure, suggesting graphs could guide neural networks toward more interpretable representations.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based embeddings achieve strong NLP performance but produce semantically unsatisfactory, poorly distributed latent spaces. The authors seek to understand this geometric deficiency by contrasting supervised embeddings with more directly interpretable graph-based semantic models.
AI.method: Comparative analysis methodology evaluating both structural and topological properties: (1) supervised vector embeddings from CamemBERT, and (2) lexical co-occurrence graphs built from the French "Grand Débat National" citizen contributions corpus. Comparison performed at both graph-structure and embedding-topology levels.
AI.result: Local topology is similar between the two approaches, but overall structure and global topology diverge significantly. Graph-based models exhibit clearer, more human-readable semantic organization.
AI.conclusion: Supervised deep models and graph-based models offer complementary perspectives on semantic geometry. Incorporating graph structures may provide a pathway to guide neural architectures toward more stable and interpretable convergence.

title: RASFT: Rollout-Adaptive Supervised Fine-Tuning for Reasoning
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is a prevailing method for adapting large language models to reasoning tasks by imitating offline expert demonstrations, often treating a single expert trajectory as the target behavior. However, reasoning is not simple path imitation: rigidly following one demonstrated solution may overfit to surface forms and suppress the model's own reasoning distribution. We propose Rollout-Adaptive Supervised Fine-Tuning (RASFT), a policy-aware SFT framework that calibrates expert supervision according to problem-level solvability estimated from verified on-policy rollouts. For each problem, RASFT strengthens expert guidance when the current policy struggles, while relaxing rigid imitation and incorporating correct self-generated trajectories when the model already exhibits reliable reasoning behavior. To preserve useful reasoning priors, RASFT further introduces a clipped inverse ratio between the frozen reference model and the current policy to constrain excessive policy drift. Experiments across multiple models on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks and two code reasoning benchmarks show that RASFT achieves better overall performance than SFT, SFT variants, and representative RL methods. The code is available at https://github.com/zjd1sq/RASFT.

AI.tldr: RASFT is a policy-aware SFT framework that adaptively calibrates expert supervision based on on-policy rollout solvability. It strengthens expert guidance when the model struggles, relaxes imitation when the model succeeds (incorporating self-generated correct trajectories), and uses a clipped inverse ratio to constrain policy drift.
AI.motivation: Standard SFT treats a single expert trajectory as the target, forcing rigid path imitation that can overfit to surface forms and suppress the model's own reasoning distribution. Reasoning tasks require flexibility beyond mimicking one demonstrated solution.
AI.method: RASFT evaluates each problem via on-policy rollouts to estimate solvability. For unsolved problems, it strengthens expert guidance; for solved problems, it relaxes rigid imitation and incorporates correct self-generated trajectories. A clipped inverse ratio between frozen reference and current policy constrains excessive policy drift, preserving useful reasoning priors.
AI.result: RASFT outperforms standard SFT, SFT variants, and representative RL methods across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks and two code reasoning benchmarks using multiple model families.
AI.conclusion: Adaptively calibrating supervision strength based on the model's current capability (as measured by on-policy rollouts) yields better reasoning performance than uniform expert imitation, demonstrating that policy-aware SFT effectively balances learning from demonstrations with preserving the model's own reasoning distribution.

title: From Correctness to Utility: Gain-Based Prefix Evaluation for LLM Reasoning
summary: Reasoning prefixes shape the future trajectory of LLM problem solving, yet existing process reward models usually evaluate them through local step correctness. We argue that correctness is a useful but indirect proxy for the effect we ultimately care about: whether a prefix increases the probability of successful completion. We define this effect as prefix gain, the solve-rate improvement induced by conditioning lightweight student model group on a prefix, and use it to train a Prefix Utility Model (PUM) with a simple pairwise ranking objective. PUM learns outcome-grounded prefix utility and can score both complete trajectories and partial reasoning prefixes. Across Best-of-$N$ selection, beam search, and reinforcement learning on mathematical reasoning, PUM provides a strong prefix-level supervision signal, especially when candidate pools are large, search budgets increase, or rule-based rewards are sparse. We release all data, models, and code at https://zhiqix.github.io/pum-project-page.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Prefix Utility Model (PUM), which evaluates reasoning prefixes based on their actual impact on task completion probability ("prefix gain") rather than local step correctness. Trained with a pairwise ranking objective using lightweight student models, PUM outperforms existing process reward models in Best-of-N selection, beam search, and RL, especially in large-scale or sparse-reward settings.
AI.motivation: Existing process reward models (PRMs) evaluate reasoning steps based on local correctness, but correctness is only an indirect proxy. The true goal is whether a reasoning prefix increases the probability of successful completion. The authors argue for directly modeling this "prefix gain" — the solve-rate improvement when conditioning on a prefix.
AI.method: 1. Define "prefix gain" as the solve-rate improvement induced by conditioning a lightweight student model group on a given prefix. 2. Train a Prefix Utility Model (PUM) using a pairwise ranking objective: given two prefixes, PUM learns to rank which one yields higher solve-rate. 3. PUM scores both complete trajectories and partial prefixes. 4. Apply PUM as a supervision signal in Best-of-N selection, beam search, and reinforcement learning for mathematical reasoning.
AI.result: PUM provides strong prefix-level supervision across all tested settings. It is particularly effective when candidate pools are large, search budgets increase, or rule-based rewards are sparse. PUM outperforms traditional PRMs that rely on local step correctness.
AI.conclusion: Directly modeling outcome-grounded prefix utility via pairwise ranking yields a more effective supervision signal than local correctness-based process reward models. PUM demonstrates robust improvements across multiple reasoning paradigms, especially under challenging conditions like large search spaces and sparse rewards.

title: A Geometric View for Understanding Concept Learning and Neuron Interpretation in Sparse Autoencoders
summary: We propose a unified mathematical framework for a geometric understanding of concept learning and neuron interpretation in sparse autoencoders (SAEs). While SAEs improve interpretability of neural networks by learning sparse feature representations, a principled definition of ''concept'' and ''learning'' remains unclear. We formalize concepts as sets of data points and cast concept learning as a set-alignment problem between human-defined and model-induced concepts. This formulation distinguishes three increasingly strong notions of learning -- detection, separation, and approximation -- and yields geometric conditions, error bounds, and capacity constraints for when concepts can be represented by individual neurons or multi-neuron units. It also provides a set-theoretic account for common SAE phenomena, including feature splitting, feature absorption, feature families, and hierarchical concepts. Finally, we connect concept learning and neuron interpretation through formal concept analysis, showing that the two directions need not agree and that their many-to-many structure can be organized by concept lattices. Experiments on synthetic data with ReLU and Top-$K$ SAEs illustrate the theory and reveal the effects of SAE size and sparsity on concept learning.

AI.tldr: A mathematical framework casting concept learning in sparse autoencoders as a set-alignment problem, formalizing three tiers of learning (detection, separation, approximation) with geometric conditions and error bounds, and connecting neuron interpretation to formal concept analysis via concept lattices.
AI.motivation: SAEs improve neural network interpretability but lack principled definitions of "concept" and "learning." Existing work treats these informally, making it difficult to rigorously analyze when and how well SAE neurons represent meaningful concepts.
AI.method: Concepts are formalized as sets of data points. Concept learning is formulated as a set-alignment problem between human-defined concept sets and model-induced concept sets. Three learning notions (detection, separation, approximation) are distinguished with associated geometric conditions, error bounds, and capacity constraints. Formal concept analysis is used to connect concept learning with neuron interpretation via concept lattices. Synthetic data experiments with ReLU and Top-K SAEs validate the theory.
AI.result: The framework yields: (1) geometric conditions under which concepts can be represented by individual or multi-neuron units; (2) a set-theoretic account of feature splitting, absorption, families, and hierarchical concepts; (3) proof that concept learning and neuron interpretation need not agree, organized by concept lattices; (4) experimental evidence showing how SAE size and sparsity affect concept learning on synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: The unified mathematical framework provides rigorous foundations for understanding concept learning in SAEs, revealing that learning and interpretation are distinct, many-to-many relationships. The work offers theoretical tools (geometric conditions, capacity bounds, concept lattices) for analyzing and designing SAEs with better interpretability guarantees.

title: Adversarial Creation and Detection of AI-Generated Social Bot Content
summary: The convergence of large language models and social bots allows malicious actors to manipulate the information ecosystem by generating human-like content at scale. Existing models for detecting AI-generated content often fail in the wild, primarily due to the lack of ground-truth data. We address this gap through an adversarial methodology that models the impersonation of real social media users by malicious actors. Using this methodology, we curate a multilingual, cross-platform dataset of paired human and AI-generated messages. Training on such adversarial data yields accurate detection of AI-generated text. Our approach significantly outperforms existing models for content-based bot detection in real-world, out-of-distribution data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an adversarial methodology to model malicious impersonation of real social media users, curates a paired human/AI-generated multilingual dataset, and trains a classifier that significantly outperforms existing bot detection models on out-of-distribution real-world data.
AI.motivation: Existing AI-generated content detection models fail in real-world settings due to the lack of ground-truth data. Malicious actors can exploit LLMs to impersonate real users and manipulate the information ecosystem at scale.
AI.method: An adversarial methodology that explicitly models impersonation of real social media users by malicious actors. This is used to curate a multilingual, cross-platform dataset of paired human and AI-generated messages, which is then used to train a content-based detection classifier.
AI.result: Training on this adversarially constructed data yields accurate detection of AI-generated text, significantly outperforming existing content-based bot detection models when evaluated on real-world, out-of-distribution data.
AI.conclusion: Adversarially modeling impersonation behavior provides a robust training signal that generalizes beyond in-distribution benchmarks, offering a practical path toward reliable AI-generated content detection in the wild.

title: CF-JEPA: Mask-free forward prediction with asymmetric encoder utilization for time-series representation learning
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) for time-series representation learning is dominated by two paradigms: contrastive methods, which face challenges in constructing positive or negative pairs, and masking-based methods, which disrupt the temporal continuity of time-series signals. Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) offers a promising alternative by predicting in representation space rather than reconstructing raw inputs. However, existing time-series JEPA variants still rely on masking and therefore inherit its continuity problem. Crop-based Forward JEPA (CF-JEPA) is proposed as an innovative mask-free framework that replaces masking with multi-horizon forward prediction: random crops serve as context views, and short-, mid-, and long-horizon future representations are predicted in the forward temporal direction, directly leveraging the inherent temporal ordering of time-series data as a learning signal. A strong asymmetry is also identified between the online encoder and the exponential moving average (EMA) target encoder, both produced from a single training run: the online encoder develops higher-rank discriminative features, while the EMA target encoder develops smoother, lower-rank temporal features. Exploiting this asymmetry, classification is routed to the online encoder and forecasting or anomaly detection to the EMA target encoder, achieving a 27% reduction in multivariate forecasting mean squared error (MSE) at no additional training cost. Across 126 University of California, Riverside (UCR) and 26 University of East Anglia (UEA) classification datasets, eight electricity transformer temperature forecasting benchmarks, and Key Performance Indicator /Yahoo anomaly detection, CF-JEPA achieves the highest average accuracy and rank on UCR and UEA among self-supervised baselines and ranks second on univariate forecasting and k-nearest neighbors-scored anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: CF-JEPA introduces a mask-free self-supervised learning framework for time-series that uses multi-horizon forward prediction on random crops, exploiting asymmetry between online and EMA target encoders to achieve state-of-the-art results across classification, forecasting, and anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Current time-series SSL is dominated by contrastive methods (struggle with positive/negative pair construction) and masking-based methods (disrupt temporal continuity). While JEPA avoids raw-input reconstruction, existing time-series JEPA variants still rely on masking. The authors aim to eliminate masking entirely by exploiting the natural temporal ordering of time-series as a learning signal.
AI.method: CF-JEPA uses random crops as context views and predicts short-, mid-, and long-horizon future representations in the forward temporal direction within a JEPA framework. The method also identifies and exploits an inherent asymmetry: the online encoder learns high-rank discriminative features, while the EMA target encoder learns smoother, lower-rank temporal features. Classification is routed to the online encoder; forecasting and anomaly detection are routed to the EMA target encoder.
AI.result: CF-JEPA achieves the highest average accuracy and rank on 126 UCR and 26 UEA classification datasets among SSL baselines, ranks second on univariate forecasting and KNN-scored anomaly detection, and yields a 27% reduction in multivariate forecasting MSE with no additional training cost.
AI.conclusion: Removing masking and instead leveraging forward temporal prediction with crop-based views, combined with asymmetric encoder routing, provides a simple yet highly effective SSL paradigm for time-series that generalizes across diverse downstream tasks.

title: When Large Language Models Fail in Healthcare: Evaluating Sensitivity to Prompt Variations
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used in healthcare for tasks such as clinical question answering, diagnosis support, and report summarization. Despite their promise, these models remain highly sensitive to subtle prompt perturbations, both lexical and syntactic, posing serious risks in safety-critical clinical applications. In this study, we conduct a systematic sensitivity analysis to evaluate the robustness of both general-purpose (e.g., GPT-3.5, Llama3) and medical-specific LLMs (e.g., ClinicalBERT, BioLlama3, BioBERT) using the MedMCQA benchmark. We categorize perturbations into natural and adversarial types and examine their effect on model consistency, accuracy, and reliability in clinical reasoning tasks. Our findings reveal that medical LLMs are not intrinsically safe. Even minor variations in phrasing can alter clinical advice, and targeted adversarial prompts can provoke harmful outputs. In high-stakes settings like healthcare, such unpredictability is unacceptable-models that change diagnoses due to reworded inputs or hallucinate medications when slightly rephrased cannot be reliably trusted by clinicians. While models tend to show resilience to simple lexical substitutions or paraphrasing, they often break down under syntactic reordering or misleading contextual cues. This fragility is evident across both general-purpose and domain-specific LLMs. Notably, adversarial manipulations can lead to clinically dangerous outputs, such as recommending incorrect dosages or omitting critical findings.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates the robustness of both general-purpose and medical-specific LLMs against prompt perturbations using the MedMCQA benchmark, finding that even minor phrasing changes can alter clinical advice and adversarial prompts can provoke harmful outputs, rendering current models insufficiently reliable for safety-critical healthcare applications.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in healthcare for clinical QA, diagnosis support, and report summarization, yet they remain highly sensitive to subtle lexical and syntactic prompt variations. This fragility poses unacceptable risks in safety-critical clinical settings where consistency and reliability are paramount.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic sensitivity analysis using the MedMCQA benchmark, testing both general-purpose LLMs (GPT-3.5, Llama3) and medical-specific LLMs (ClinicalBERT, BioLlama3, BioBERT). Perturbations are categorized into natural types (lexical substitutions, paraphrasing) and adversarial types (syntactic reordering, misleading contextual cues), with effects measured on model consistency, accuracy, and reliability in clinical reasoning.
AI.result: Medical LLMs are not intrinsically safe: minor phrasing variations can alter clinical advice, and targeted adversarial prompts can provoke harmful outputs including incorrect dosage recommendations and omission of critical findings. Models show some resilience to simple lexical substitutions but break down under syntactic reordering or misleading contextual cues. This fragility is consistent across both general-purpose and domain-specific LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs — including domain-specific medical models — lack the robustness required for trustworthy clinical deployment. Their unpredictability under prompt perturbation means they cannot be reliably trusted by clinicians in high-stakes healthcare settings.

title: Hierarchical Forecast Reconciliation for Urban Rail Transit Demand Prediction under Operational Disruptions
summary: Accurate and coherent passenger demand forecasting is essential for Urban Rail Transit (URT) operations. Passenger demand has a hierarchical structure in which origin-destination (OD) flows aggregate to station-level inflows and outflows through conservation constraints. In practice, station-level and OD-level forecasts are often generated independently, producing incoherent predictions that violate these constraints and introduce inconsistencies into operational decision-making. Such issues become more severe during disruptions, when forecasting reliability is most critical. This paper presents the first hierarchical forecast reconciliation framework for joint station-level and OD-level URT demand prediction. A neural Fully Connected Reconciler (FCR) learns a non-linear mapping from incoherent base forecasts to coherent hierarchical predictions while guaranteeing exact structural consistency by construction. The method is benchmarked against OLS, WLS, and Minimum Trace (MinT) variants using Rejsekort smart-card data from the Copenhagen S-train network under one-step, multi-step, and disruption forecasting scenarios. Results show that reconciliation consistently improves OD forecasting accuracy while ensuring hierarchical coherence. Under normal conditions, FCR performs competitively with MinT-based methods. An oracle analysis indicates that perfect station-level forecasts could reduce OD prediction error by up to 34 percent, highlighting the value of improved base forecasts. Under severe disruptions, FCR outperforms classical methods, reducing OD forecasting error by up to 17.45 percent in multi-step destination-side delay scenarios. These findings establish hierarchical reconciliation as an effective mechanism for improving forecast robustness, with the largest benefits occurring under the most challenging operating conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a neural network-based hierarchical forecast reconciliation framework for urban rail transit demand, ensuring coherence between station-level and OD-level predictions. Tested on Copenhagen S-train data, it shows that reconciliation consistently improves OD accuracy, with the largest gains (up to 17.45% error reduction) occurring under disruption scenarios.
AI.motivation: Station-level and OD-level demand forecasts in URT are typically produced independently, leading to incoherent predictions that violate conservation constraints. This inconsistency undermines operational decision-making, especially during disruptions when reliability is most critical. No prior work addresses joint hierarchical reconciliation for URT demand.
AI.method: The authors propose a neural Fully Connected Reconciler (FCR) that learns a non-linear mapping from incoherent base forecasts to coherent hierarchical predictions, guaranteeing structural consistency by construction. The method is benchmarked against OLS, WLS, and MinT reconciliation variants using Rejsekort smart-card data from Copenhagen's S-train network across one-step, multi-step, and disruption forecasting scenarios. An oracle analysis quantifies the upper bound of improvement from perfect station-level forecasts.
AI.result: Reconciliation consistently improves OD forecasting accuracy while ensuring hierarchical coherence. Under normal conditions, FCR performs competitively with MinT-based methods. An oracle analysis shows perfect station-level forecasts could reduce OD error by up to 34%. Under severe disruptions, FCR outperforms classical methods, reducing OD forecasting error by up to 17.45% in multi-step destination-side delay scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical reconciliation is an effective mechanism for improving forecast robustness in URT demand prediction. The largest benefits occur under the most challenging operating conditions (disruptions). The FCR approach provides a principled way to enforce structural consistency while leveraging non-linear relationships in the data.

title: KIT's Submission to Cross-Lingual Voice Cloning in IWSLT 2026
summary: Cross-lingual voice cloning aims to generate speech in a target language while preserving speaker identity from a source-language reference. This task is central to speech translation and is the focus of the IWSLT 2026 Cross-Lingual Voice Cloning track. A key challenge is maintaining intelligibility and naturalness in the presence of accent variation and domain-specific vocabulary. We build on a multilingual text-to-speech model, FishAudio-S2-Pro, and introduce language tag prompting to improve language control and reduce accent leakage. We further apply reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning for task adaptation and observe improvements in intelligibility. Finally, we propose a reference-conditioned lexical matching method that improves pronunciation of domain-specific terms when lexical overlap is present. Results show that language prompting provides the largest gains, while lexical matching yields consistent improvements on matched subsets.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses cross-lingual voice cloning by enhancing a multilingual TTS model (FishAudio-S2-Pro) with language tag prompting, RL fine-tuning, and reference-conditioned lexical matching to improve language control, intelligibility, and domain-specific pronunciation.
AI.motivation: Cross-lingual voice cloning faces challenges in maintaining intelligibility and naturalness when accent variation and domain-specific vocabulary are present. The IWSLT 2026 track motivates systematic improvements in language control and accent reduction.
AI.method: The authors build on FishAudio-S2-Pro, a multilingual TTS model, and introduce three techniques: (1) language tag prompting to improve language control and reduce accent leakage, (2) reinforcement learning fine-tuning for task adaptation and intelligibility gains, and (3) a reference-conditioned lexical matching method that leverages overlapping domain-specific terms between reference and target to improve pronunciation accuracy.
AI.result: Language prompting yields the largest overall performance gains. RL fine-tuning further improves intelligibility. The lexical matching method produces consistent improvements on subsets where lexical overlap between reference and target is present.
AI.conclusion: The combination of language tag prompting, RL fine-tuning, and reference-conditioned lexical matching offers a practical and effective framework for cross-lingual voice cloning, with language prompting emerging as the most impactful component.

title: Constructing VAE Latent Spaces with Prescribed Topology
summary: Variational autoencoders (VAEs) learn low-dimensional latent representations of high-dimensional data. When the data lies on a manifold with non-Euclidean topology, the standard Gaussian prior introduces a topological mismatch that degrades reconstruction quality and prevents faithful representation. We present a constructive mathematical framework that resolves this mismatch for all manifolds that admit a product covering space. These are manifolds expressible as products of elementary factors (circles, intervals, or lines) or as quotients of such products by a finite symmetry group. The class includes cylinders, tori, Möbius strips, Klein bottles, and real projective spaces. Factorized distributions over the elementary factors yield product topologies with closed-form, decoupled KL divergences, so that each latent factor can be shaped independently while keeping training tractable. We catalogue reparametrizable encoder-prior pairs for periodic, bounded, and unbounded supports, and provide coordinate transformations that allow standard neural networks to output non-Euclidean parameters with smooth gradients. For quotient manifolds, the decoder receives group-invariant features of the covering-space coordinates, so that identified points produce identical outputs. Anchor constraints fix the coordinate system relative to the data or create soft topological holes. Experiments on synthetic manifolds and real-image datasets (rotated and cyclically shifted MNIST) confirm that a topology-matched prior aligns KL regularization with the data manifold. The resulting topology-aware models outperform the Gaussian baseline at all practically relevant regularization strengths. The code is available at https://github.com/JvHulst/VAE-Topology.

AI.tldr: VAEs with standard Gaussian priors suffer from topological mismatch when data lies on non-Euclidean manifolds. This paper provides a framework for constructing topology-matched priors using product covering spaces, enabling factorized KL divergences, reparametrizable encoder-prior pairs, and group-invariant decoders for quotient manifolds. Experiments show topology-aware VAEs outperform Gaussian baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard VAEs assume a Gaussian latent prior, which imposes a Euclidean topology on the latent space. When data intrinsically lives on a manifold with non-Euclidean topology (e.g., cylinders, tori, Möbius strips), this creates a topological mismatch that degrades reconstruction quality and prevents learning faithful representations. The goal is to resolve this mismatch in a principled, tractable way.
AI.method: The authors develop a mathematical framework for manifolds admitting a product covering space — those expressible as products of elementary factors (circles S¹, intervals I, lines ℝ) or quotients thereof by finite symmetry groups. Key technical components: (1) Factorized distributions over elementary factors yield product topologies with closed-form, decoupled KL divergences, allowing independent shaping of each latent factor. (2) A catalogue of reparametrizable encoder-prior pairs for periodic, bounded, and unbounded supports, with coordinate transformations enabling standard neural networks to output non-Euclidean parameters with smooth gradients. (3) For quotient manifolds (e.g., Möbius strip, Klein bottle, ℝℙⁿ), the decoder receives group-invariant features of covering-space coordinates so that identified points map to identical outputs. (4) Anchor constraints fix the coordinate system relative to data or create soft topological holes.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic manifolds and real-image datasets (rotated MNIST, cyclically shifted MNIST) demonstrate that topology-matched priors align KL regularization with the data manifold. Topology-aware models outperform the Gaussian baseline across all practically relevant regularization strengths, confirming that resolving the topological mismatch yields better reconstructions and more faithful latent representations.
AI.conclusion: Matching the prior's topology to the data manifold is crucial for VAE performance. The proposed framework handles a broad class of non-Euclidean manifolds via product covering spaces, provides tractable training through decoupled KL divergences and smooth reparametrization, and generalizes to quotient manifolds via group-invariant decoders. The approach is practically validated on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

title: Phun-Bench: Evaluating LLMs on Phonological Understanding in Chinese
summary: Language is a vehicle for thought, intricately tied to sounds, symbols, and meaning. However, most large language model (LLM) research focuses on meaning (semantics) and symbols (spelling) while largely overlooking sounds. Existing benchmarks on LLMs' phonological abilities are either solvable through rote memorization or intertwined with other abilities, making them inadequate to measure LLMs' genuine ability in phonological understanding. Here, we present Phun-Bench, a purpose-built Chinese benchmark with diverse tasks and settings across three dimensions (Homophony, Rhyme, and Phonetic Similarity), designed to systematically evaluate LLMs' phonological understanding. Our results show that while LLMs excel at recalling correct pronunciations, they generally struggle to leverage phonological knowledge in the flexible and intuitive way that human speakers do. Moreover, through detailed analyses, we propose a hypothesis regarding the underlying mechanism of LLMs' phonological understanding and "perception", highlighting an underexplored frontier for future research.

AI.tldr: Phun-Bench is a Chinese benchmark evaluating LLMs' phonological understanding across homophony, rhyme, and phonetic similarity. Results show LLMs recall pronunciations well but struggle with flexible, human-like phonological reasoning, revealing an underexplored research frontier.
AI.motivation: Most LLM research focuses on semantics and orthography while neglecting phonology. Existing phonological benchmarks are either solvable via memorization or confounded with other linguistic abilities, preventing accurate assessment of genuine phonological understanding.
AI.method: The authors introduce Phun-Bench, a purpose-built Chinese benchmark spanning three phonological dimensions — Homophony, Rhyme, and Phonetic Similarity — with diverse tasks and settings designed to isolate and systematically evaluate phonological capabilities in LLMs.
AI.result: LLMs demonstrate strong performance in recalling correct pronunciations but generally fail to flexibly and intuitively apply phonological knowledge in ways characteristic of human speakers. Detailed analyses lead to a proposed hypothesis about the underlying mechanism of LLM phonological "perception."
AI.conclusion: Phonological understanding in LLMs is an underexplored frontier; current models exhibit a gap between pronunciation recall and deeper phonological reasoning, and the proposed mechanistic hypothesis offers a direction for future investigation.

title: Bias in Filter Feature Selection Evaluation: A Meta-Analysis of Datasets, Baselines, and Experimental Design Choices
summary:
Background: Since 1990 many feature selection methods have been proposed across heterogeneous applications. To validate the usefulness of a new method, it needs to be compared against at least one baseline method from the existing literature on a feature selection task using at least one dataset. Recent developments in tabular Deep Learning (DL) and data valuation in Machine Learning (ML) suggest that the evaluation of new methods, algorithms, and models may be consciously or unconsciously biased. We hypothesise that a similar trend exists in feature selection (FS), particularly in filter feature selection (FFS). The aim of this study is therefore to examine FFS studies to identify factors that influence the evaluation and that might consist entry point for biases in order to recommend stronger principles for FFS evaluation.
  Methods: We analyse a sample of 28 high profile FFS studies published between 1994 and 2025. The analysis provides reflections on how to examine FFS studies, highlights lessons learned throughout the process, and gives five evidence-based recommendations for future FFS evaluation.
  Results: Multivariate Linear Regression analysis achieved a score of $R^2=0.33$. It means that 33% of the variance in the performance of new methods against chosen baselines (win rate) is explained by the number of datasets (#Datasets), the number of baselines (#Baselines), and the number of new methods (#NewMethods).
  Discussion: $R^2=0.33$ is considered medium explanation; which is promising given that this is the first such study. The medium explanation result is due to the fact that win rate is influenced by additional factors such as the maturity of the feature selection domain, the type of datasets and baselines, and the simplicity of the regression model used to explain the relationship.

AI.tldr: This study investigates potential biases in filter feature selection (FFS) evaluation by analyzing 28 high-profile studies (1994–2025). A multivariate linear regression shows that 33% of the variance in new methods' win rates is explained by the number of datasets, baselines, and new methods used, suggesting that evaluation design choices may consciously or unconsciously bias performance comparisons.
AI.motivation: Recent developments in tabular deep learning and data valuation suggest evaluation of new ML methods may be biased. The authors hypothesize a similar trend in feature selection, particularly filter methods, and aim to identify factors influencing evaluation that could serve as entry points for bias, ultimately recommending stronger evaluation principles.
AI.method: The authors analyzed a sample of 28 high-profile FFS studies published between 1994 and 2025. They performed a multivariate linear regression to model the relationship between win rate (performance of new methods against baselines) and three predictor variables: number of datasets (#Datasets), number of baselines (#Baselines), and number of new methods (#NewMethods).
AI.result: The regression model achieved R² = 0.33, indicating that 33% of the variance in win rate is explained by the three predictors. This is considered a medium effect size, noteworthy for a first study of its kind.
AI.conclusion: The medium explanatory power (R² = 0.33) suggests win rate is also influenced by additional factors such as domain maturity, dataset and baseline types, and regression model simplicity. The authors provide five evidence-based recommendations for more robust FFS evaluation to mitigate bias.

title: SV-Detect: AI-generated Text Detection with Steering Vectors
summary: Detecting machine-generated text is especially difficult under distribution shift, such as transfer across domains, source models, and editing attacks. We propose a fake-text detector based on steering vectors extracted from the hidden representations of a frozen language model. At each layer, we construct a direction that separates human-written from machine-generated text, and represent each input by its layer-wise alignment with these directions. A lightweight classifier trained on these projection features yields the final detection score. Our method achieves strong performance both in-distribution and under distribution shift, including across domains, source models, and machine-editing transformations such as polishing and rewriting. Interpretation analyses show that the learned directions align with recognizable stylistic cues while capturing substantial additional signal beyond surface features. These results position fake-text detection as a representation-space probing problem and show that steering vectors provide a simple and effective solution.

AI.tldr: A fake-text detector using steering vectors from frozen language model hidden states achieves robust performance under distribution shift, framing detection as a representation-space probing problem.
AI.motivation: Machine-generated text detection degrades significantly under distribution shift (domain transfer, different source models, editing attacks). Existing detectors often rely on surface-level features that fail to generalize, motivating a method that leverages deeper representational signals.
AI.method: Steering vectors are extracted from hidden representations of a frozen LM at each layer — directions separating human vs. machine text. Each input is encoded by its layer-wise projection alignment with these directions. A lightweight classifier trained on these projection features produces the final detection score.
AI.result: Strong in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance across domains, source models, and editing transformations (polishing, rewriting). Steering vectors capture stylistic cues and additional signal beyond surface features.
AI.conclusion: Fake-text detection can be effectively framed as a representation-space probing problem, with steering vectors offering a simple, interpretable, and robust solution that goes beyond shallow surface patterns.

title: SlimSearcher: Training Efficiency-Aware Web Agents via Adaptive Reward Gating
summary: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in complex information-seeking tasks, yet this power comes at a steep computational cost. Driven by accuracy-focused training paradigms, current models adopt brute-force strategies characterized by blind tool dependency and performative reasoning-generating long, redundant trajectories that are far from necessary for resolving these tasks, leading to wasteful tool calls and excessive token consumption. To overcome this efficiency trap, we propose SlimSearcher, a principled framework that pushes the Pareto frontier between accuracy and computational cost across both Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). In the SFT stage, SlimSearcher employs Pareto-efficient filtration to distill trajectories that are both successful and economical, guiding the model toward inherently efficiency-aware search behaviors. During RL, we introduce Adaptive Reward Gating, a dynamic reward-shaping mechanism that evaluates relative tool and token efficiency within a sampled cohort. By cascading these adaptive efficiency metrics with a strict correctness gate, our approach effectively avoids the brevity bias associated with absolute penalties and mitigates reward hacking. Extensive experiments on long-horizon benchmarks, including GAIA, BrowseComp, and XBenchDeepSearch, demonstrate that SlimSearcher reduces average tool-call rounds by 17%-58% while maintaining or improving accuracy.

AI.tldr: SlimSearcher proposes a two-stage framework (SFT + RL) that jointly optimizes accuracy and computational efficiency for deep research agents, using Pareto-efficient trajectory filtration and Adaptive Reward Gating to reduce tool calls by 17-58% without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current deep research agents, driven by accuracy-focused training, exhibit brute-force behaviors—blind tool dependency and performative reasoning—resulting in excessively long trajectories, wasteful tool calls, and high token consumption. The authors identify an "efficiency trap" where models lack incentives to be computationally economical, motivating a principled Pareto optimization approach.
AI.method: SlimSearcher operates in two stages: (1) SFT stage: Pareto-efficient filtration selects trajectories that are both successful (high accuracy) and economical (low tool/token usage), fine-tuning the model toward efficiency-aware search. (2) RL stage: Adaptive Reward Gating dynamically evaluates relative tool and token efficiency within sampled cohorts, combining these with a strict correctness gate to shape rewards. This avoids absolute-penalty brevity bias and mitigates reward hacking.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on long-horizon benchmarks (GAIA, BrowseComp, XBenchDeepSearch) show SlimSearcher reduces average tool-call rounds by 17%-58% while maintaining or improving accuracy, pushing the Pareto frontier between accuracy and computational cost.
AI.conclusion: SlimSearcher demonstrates that explicit efficiency optimization via Pareto-driven data curation and cohort-relative reward shaping can effectively break the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in deep research agents, producing models that are both capable and computationally frugal.

title: LLM-Guided Evolution for Medical Decision Pipelines
summary:
Adapting large language models (LLMs) to clinical workflows often requires costly fine-tuning or manual prompt and pipeline engineering.
