We study LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolution as an inference-time alternative for discovering medical decision strategies and provide an implementation repository at https://github.com/univanxx/llm_guided_evo_medical. We formulate urgency triage, interactive consultation, and medical image classification as evolutionary searches over executable artifacts optimized by task-specific fitness functions.
  Across all three settings, evolution improves over manually designed baselines under practical constraints. In triage, evolved programs increase Semigran accuracy from $77.3\%$ to $87.1\%$ and emergency recall from $0.60$ to $0.97$, while improving safety-weighted held-out MIMIC-ESI performance. In interactive consultation, evolved policies improve the accuracy--cost frontier across Llama-3, Qwen-3.5, and Gemma-4 and transfer to held-out iCRAFTMD. In PneumoniaMNIST, prompt-only evolution improves frozen MedGemma VLMs while preserving strict JSON outputs. Qualitative analysis shows that the gains come from interpretable program-level mechanisms, calibrated triage boundaries, targeted evidence acquisition, selective commitment, and finding-oriented visual decision rules, rather than superficial prompt rewording alone.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolutionary search at inference time as a cost-effective alternative to fine-tuning for adapting LLMs to clinical tasks. Across urgency triage, interactive consultation, and medical image classification, evolved programs significantly outperform manually designed baselines, with gains driven by interpretable, program-level mechanisms rather than superficial prompt rewording.
AI.motivation: Adapting LLMs to clinical workflows typically requires expensive fine-tuning or labor-intensive manual prompt and pipeline engineering. The authors seek an inference-time alternative that discovers effective medical decision strategies automatically, without modifying model weights.
AI.method: The method frames clinical tasks (urgency triage, interactive consultation, medical image classification) as evolutionary searches over executable artifacts using LLM-guided MAP-Elites. Task-specific fitness functions drive the optimization. In triage, evolved programs are evaluated on Semigran accuracy and emergency recall; in consultation, policies are optimized along an accuracy--cost frontier; in image classification, prompt evolution improves frozen MedGemma VLMs while enforcing structured JSON outputs.
AI.result: Evolved triage programs improve Semigran accuracy from 77.3% to 87.1% and emergency recall from 0.60 to 0.97, with better safety-weighted MIMIC-ESI performance. Interactive consultation policies improve the accuracy--cost frontier across Llama-3, Qwen-3.5, and Gemma-4 and transfer to held-out iCRAFTMD. PneumoniaMNIST prompt evolution improves frozen MedGemma VLMs. Gains stem from interpretable mechanisms: calibrated triage boundaries, targeted evidence acquisition, selective commitment, and finding-oriented visual decision rules.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided MAP-Elites evolution is a viable, cost-effective inference-time method for discovering clinical decision strategies that outperform manual designs, yield interpretable improvements, and generalize across tasks and models without fine-tuning.

title: On the Geometry of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) is increasingly used to improve large language model reasoning, but its training dynamics remain poorly understood. We characterize the trajectory of OPD updates in parameter space and compare it with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). A suite of parameter-space diagnostics consistently places OPD in a relaxed off-principal regime: compared with SFT, its updates affect fewer weights and avoid principal directions more strongly, while compared with RLVR, they remain less tightly constrained. Beyond this static localization, OPD exhibits subspace locking: its cumulative updates rapidly enter a narrow low-dimensional channel. Constraining training to the update subspace formed early in training preserves OPD performance but substantially degrades SFT, indicating that the locked subspace is functionally sufficient for OPD. Control experiments further show that sparsifying the update tokens and shifting rollout generation off-policy preserve the rank dynamics, whereas mixing the OPD objective with RLVR changes them. Overall, these results suggest that OPD is not merely an intermediate point between SFT and RLVR, but induces its own update geometry in parameter space.

AI.tldr: On-policy distillation (OPD) exhibits a distinct parameter-space geometry: updates are localized, avoid principal directions, and rapidly converge into a narrow low-dimensional "locked" subspace that is functionally sufficient for performance, unlike SFT or RLVR.
AI.motivation: OPD is widely used to improve LLM reasoning, yet its training dynamics are poorly understood. The authors aim to characterize OPD's parameter-space trajectory and distinguish it from related paradigms (SFT and RLVR).
AI.method: The authors apply a suite of parameter-space diagnostics — including update localization metrics, principal component alignment analysis, and subspace dimensionality tracking — to compare OPD, SFT, and RLVR. They introduce the concept of "subspace locking," where cumulative OPD updates collapse into a low-dimensional channel early in training. They validate functional sufficiency via constraining updates to the early-formed subspace and conduct control experiments manipulating token sparsity, rollout generation policy, and objective mixing.
AI.result: OPD updates are sparser and more off-principal than SFT, yet less constrained than RLVR. OPD exhibits subspace locking: cumulative updates rapidly enter a narrow low-dimensional subspace. Constraining training to this early subspace preserves OPD performance but degrades SFT. Sparsifying update tokens and using off-policy rollouts preserve these rank dynamics, while mixing OPD with RLVR alters them.
AI.conclusion: OPD is not merely an interpolation between SFT and RLVR; it induces its own distinct update geometry in parameter space, characterized by relaxed off-principal localization and subspace locking.

title: M$^3$Exam: Benchmarking Multimodal Memory for Realistic User-Agent Interactions
summary: Language agents are increasingly deployed over accumulating multimodal information, yet existing benchmarks assume a human-human form with sparse visuals and straightforward content, evaluating neither reasoning over authentic multimodal file interaction nor the interpretation of concealed user information. We therefore introduce M$^3$Exam, a query-centric multimodal conversational memory benchmark built on realistic user-agent interaction, with multi-dimensional evaluation spanning cross-modal grounding and implicit information inference. Benchmarking MLLMs and memory systems reveals persistent gaps in cross-modal grounding, cross session reasoning, and the efficiency cost of accumulating multimodal context. We further propose M$^3$Proctor, a multimodal memory method that detects query modality bias and consumes raw visual sources only on demand, improving accuracy by 13% while cutting index-construction time and retrieved tokens by over 70%.

AI.tldr: M$^3$Exam is a multimodal conversational memory benchmark simulating realistic user-agent interactions, revealing gaps in cross-modal grounding. The proposed M$^3$Proctor method mitigates query modality bias via on-demand visual retrieval, boosting accuracy by 13% while reducing token usage and index time by >70%.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for language agents assume human-human interaction with sparse, straightforward visuals, failing to evaluate authentic multimodal file interaction or the inference of implicit/concealed user information. There is a need to benchmark agents over accumulating multimodal conversational memory under realistic conditions.
AI.method: The authors introduce M$^3$Exam, a query-centric multimodal conversational memory benchmark built on realistic user-agent interactions with multi-dimensional evaluation (cross-modal grounding, implicit information inference). They also propose M$^3$Proctor, a multimodal memory method that detects query modality bias and consumes raw visual sources only on demand, rather than indiscriminately encoding all visuals.
AI.result: Benchmarking MLLMs and memory systems on M$^3$Exam reveals persistent gaps in cross-modal grounding, cross-session reasoning, and efficiency costs from accumulating multimodal context. M$^3$Proctor improves accuracy by 13% while cutting index-construction time and retrieved tokens by over 70%.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal conversational memory remains a challenging task for current MLLMs, with significant deficiencies in cross-modal grounding and cross-session reasoning. The proposed M$^3$Proctor demonstrates that query-aware, on-demand visual retrieval offers a practical path toward both stronger accuracy and greater efficiency.

title: Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning for Stochastic Constrained Decision-Making
summary: Stochastic constrained decision-making requires optimizing performance objectives while enforcing statistical requirements such as safety or fairness. However, standard primal--dual methods struggle to update multipliers robustly under stochastic mini-batch feedback, as the noise of mini-batch gradients and constraint estimates can be directly accumulated into the multiplier memory. To address this issue, we propose Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning (RCML), which reformulates multiplier updating as projected-pressure feedback. The central idea is to decompose the projected multiplier into an effective pressure signal for primal descent and a pressure-memory residual for finite-gain multiplier tracking. To handle heterogeneous and noisy observations, we further augment this residual-integral backbone with modular stochastic stabilization components. For the convex-affine backbone, we establish finite-gain convergence, derive a stochastic residual bound under mini-batch feedback, and show that the residual feedback law admits a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points of nonconvex problems. Experiments across optimization, allocation, and fair-ranking tasks show that RCML improves feasibility control and multiplier stability while maintaining competitive objective performance. Code is available here.

AI.tldr: RCML proposes a projected-pressure feedback framework that decomposes multiplier updates into a pressure signal and a residual memory component, with modular stabilization for noisy mini-batch feedback, improving constraint satisfaction and multiplier stability in stochastic constrained optimization.
AI.motivation: Standard primal-dual methods for stochastic constrained decision-making suffer from noise accumulation in multiplier updates under mini-batch feedback, where gradient and constraint estimate noise directly corrupts the multiplier memory, degrading feasibility control and stability.
AI.method: RCML reformulates multiplier updating as projected-pressure feedback, decomposing the projected multiplier into (1) an effective pressure signal driving primal descent and (2) a pressure-memory residual enabling finite-gain multiplier tracking. Modular stochastic stabilization components (e.g., momentum, variance reduction) are added to handle heterogeneous, noisy observations. The convex-affine backbone yields finite-gain convergence guarantees and stochastic residual bounds; a local KKT-residual interpretation is provided near regular KKT points for nonconvex problems.
AI.result: For the convex-affine backbone, finite-gain convergence is established and a stochastic residual bound under mini-batch feedback is derived. The residual feedback law admits a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points of nonconvex problems. Experiments across optimization, allocation, and fair-ranking tasks demonstrate improved feasibility control and multiplier stability with competitive objective performance.
AI.conclusion: RCML effectively addresses noise accumulation in multiplier updates for stochastic constrained optimization, offering a principled residual-controlled framework with modular stabilization that outperforms standard primal-dual methods in feasibility and stability while maintaining objective quality.

title: The Masked Advantage: Uncovering Local-Language Access to Cultural Knowledge in LLMs
summary: Large language models are increasingly used to answer culturally grounded questions across languages, yet it remains unclear whether local cultural knowledge is better accessed through English or the local language. Existing evaluations face two key limitations: many rely on parallel template-based questions that may not reflect how cultural knowledge naturally appears, and raw accuracy conflates general language proficiency with language-conditioned knowledge access. We address these issues with a controlled framework built on real-world cultural questions collected from regional benchmarks and local sources. By crossing question type (culture-agnostic vs. culture-specific) with query language (English vs. local language), and estimating ability with a shared 1PL item response theory model, we separate proficiency from localized knowledge access. Across 13 locales and roughly 80 models, we find a consistent English advantage on culture-agnostic questions, indicating stronger English proficiency. However, after accounting for this proficiency gap, local languages show a positive knowledge-access advantage in nearly all locale-model settings. This advantage is often masked in raw accuracy but becomes more visible for frontier, regionally aligned, or language-adapted models. Our results suggest that weaker local-language performance does not necessarily imply weaker cultural knowledge; rather, local cultural knowledge may be more accessible through the local language but hidden by limited language proficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates whether local cultural knowledge in LLMs is better accessed through English or the local language. Using a controlled framework with real-world questions and IRT modeling across 13 locales and ~80 models, the authors find that while English dominates on culture-agnostic questions (reflecting higher proficiency), local languages yield a positive knowledge-access advantage for culture-specific questions once the proficiency gap is accounted for. This advantage is often masked in raw accuracy but emerges in frontier and regionally aligned models.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of cross-lingual cultural knowledge suffer from two limitations: (1) reliance on parallel template-based questions that misrepresent how cultural knowledge naturally manifests, and (2) raw accuracy metrics that conflate general language proficiency with genuine cultural knowledge access. The authors aim to disentangle these factors.
AI.method: The authors construct a controlled evaluation framework using real-world cultural questions from regional benchmarks and local sources. They cross question type (culture-agnostic vs. culture-specific) with query language (English vs. local language) and apply a shared 1PL item response theory (IRT) model to separately estimate language proficiency and localized knowledge access, thereby isolating the two constructs.
AI.result: A consistent English advantage emerges on culture-agnostic questions, reflecting stronger English proficiency. However, after controlling for this proficiency gap, local languages demonstrate a positive knowledge-access advantage in nearly all locale-model settings. This advantage is often hidden in raw accuracy but becomes apparent for frontier models, regionally aligned models, and language-adapted models.
AI.conclusion: Weaker local-language performance does not necessarily imply weaker cultural knowledge. Instead, local cultural knowledge may be more accessible through the local language but is obscured by limited language proficiency — a finding with important implications for how multilingual LLMs should be evaluated and deployed for culturally grounded tasks.

title: The discovery of the effects of women employment participation on the fertility of developing countries: A panel data approach
summary: The fertility trend in developing countries has experienced a significant decline in the last few decades; at the same time, the role of women in the workplace has improved. To have a better insight of the causality of the rate of women participation in the labor market on the total fertility rate in developing world, this paper divides the dataset of 115 developing countries in the period of 1991-2018 into four continents group (Africa, North/South America, Asia/Pacific, Europe) and then applies a data-driven panel data econometric procedure to mitigate omitted bias. The results suggest that the fertility behaviors of women in the North/South America continents are influenced by their career choice; meanwhile in society of other regions, other factors might be more important to women when thinking of having children. In conclusion, policymakers can reference to the paper and formulate policies to have more incentives in making reproductive decisions and further research in the field needs to consider family policies and patrilocality of developing countries as important data.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the causal relationship between female labor force participation and fertility rates across 115 developing countries (1991–2018), grouped by continent. Using panel data econometrics to reduce omitted variable bias, it finds that women's career choices influence fertility only in the Americas; elsewhere, other factors dominate.
AI.motivation: To better understand the causal direction and heterogeneity of how women's labor market participation affects total fertility rates in the developing world, given the simultaneous trends of declining fertility and rising female workforce involvement.
AI.method: A data-driven panel data econometric procedure applied to a dataset of 115 developing countries over 1991–2018, stratified into four continental groups (Africa, Americas, Asia/Pacific, Europe) to mitigate omitted variable bias.
AI.result: Female labor market participation significantly influences fertility behavior only in North and South America. In Africa, Asia/Pacific, and Europe, other factors appear more consequential in women's reproductive decisions.
AI.conclusion: Policymakers should tailor fertility-related incentives to regional context. Future research must incorporate family policy variables and patrilocality patterns specific to developing countries to improve explanatory power.

title: Sycophantic Praise: Evaluating Excessive Praise in Language Models
summary: Sycophancy in language models is typically studied as excessive agreement or validation, while explicit praise and flattery have received comparatively little attention. We argue that sycophantic praise is a distinct alignment problem that cannot be reliably measured using current methods. We introduce a parameterized framework that measures whether praise is excessive relative to contribution quality and expected user ability. We show that our framework substantially outperforms generic LLM judges in agreement with human annotations, and that sycophantic praise occurs far more frequently in social and interpretive domains than in objective reasoning settings. Together, these findings position praise calibration as a distinct alignment challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper distinguishes sycophantic praise (excessive flattery) from general sycophancy, introduces a parameterized framework to measure praise relative to contribution quality and user ability, demonstrates it aligns better with human annotations than generic LLM judges, and finds praise sycophancy is more prevalent in social/interpretive domains than in objective reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing sycophancy research focuses on excessive agreement or validation, overlooking explicit praise and flattery as a distinct alignment problem. Current methods cannot reliably measure sycophantic praise, creating a gap in understanding this form of misalignment.
AI.method: The authors propose a parameterized framework that quantifies whether praise is excessive by comparing it against two dimensions: (1) the actual quality of the user's contribution, and (2) the expected ability of the user. This framework is then benchmarked against generic LLM judges and human annotations.
AI.result: The proposed framework substantially outperforms generic LLM judges in agreement with human annotations. Additionally, sycophantic praise occurs far more frequently in social and interpretive domains than in objective reasoning settings.
AI.conclusion: Praise calibration constitutes a distinct alignment challenge separate from general sycophancy, requiring specialized measurement approaches and domain-specific mitigation strategies, particularly for social and interpretive contexts.

title: OffQ: Taming Structured Outliers in LLM Quantization by Offsetting
summary: Low-bit quantization has been widely adopted to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) by significantly reducing computational cost and memory usage. However, activation outliers pose a major challenge to effective quantization, often leading to notable performance degradation. In this paper, we introduce OffQ, a method designed to mitigate activation outliers in low-bit quantization through a novel offsetting mechanism. Specifically, OffQ first identifies a low-dimensional outlier subspace in the activations using a proposed top-1 PCA, and then concentrates high-magnitude activations into 1 channel via rotation. OffQ then absorbs this concentrated outlier channel by converting its magnitude into a shared offset, thereby reducing the standard deviation of the activations. This offsetting strategy enables effective W4A4KV4 quantization of LLMs using deployment-friendly uniform-grid and uniform-precision quantization. Extensive experiments across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that OffQ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, consistently improving model accuracy while preserving low-bit efficiency.

AI.tldr: OffQ proposes a method to mitigate activation outliers in low-bit LLM quantization by identifying an outlier subspace via top-1 PCA, rotating to concentrate outliers into a single channel, then absorbing that channel as a shared offset to reduce activation variance, enabling effective W4A4KV4 quantization.
AI.motivation: Low-bit quantization accelerates LLM inference but suffers from activation outliers that cause significant performance degradation. Existing uniform-precision quantization methods struggle with these outliers, motivating a novel offsetting mechanism to enable deployment-friendly W4A4KV4 quantization without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: OffQ operates in three stages: (1) Top-1 PCA identifies a low-dimensional outlier subspace within activations; (2) a rotation matrix is applied to concentrate high-magnitude activations into a single outlier channel; (3) the magnitude of this concentrated channel is converted into a shared offset and absorbed, reducing activation standard deviation. This enables uniform-grid, uniform-precision W4A4KV4 quantization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks show that OffQ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art quantization baselines, improving model accuracy while maintaining low-bit efficiency.
AI.conclusion: OffQ provides an effective, deployment-friendly solution to the activation outlier problem in low-bit LLM quantization, demonstrating that an offsetting strategy combined with rotation-based outlier concentration can achieve superior W4A4KV4 performance across diverse models.

title: Supervision versus Demonstration-Based In-Context Learning for Multiword Expression Classification
summary: Turkish idiomatic light verb constructions (LVCs) are challenging for multiword expression processing because they often share the same surface form as fully literal verb-object combinations while functioning as a single, partially idiomatic predicate. We frame Turkish LVC detection as a binary classification task (literal meaning vs. idiomatic meaning) and evaluate on a manually created controlled set (N=147) with matched negatives: out-of-domain random sentences and in-domain literal controls (NLVC), alongside LVC positives. We compare a supervised Turkish encoder baseline (BERTurk with a classifier head) to three instruction-tuned LLMs from different families under zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting, and analyze how demonstrations shift error profiles. In zero-shot, LLMs perform well on negatives but show very low LVC recall. One-shot prompting sharply improves LVC detection but can induce strong, model-specific biases, leading models to overpredict or underpredict LVCs. A richer few-shot prompt improves calibration and yields robust overall performance for GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen 2.5-14B. Overall, the results highlight substantial prompt sensitivity in Turkish metalinguistic classification: the supervised baseline remains competitive, while prompted LLMs can match or exceed it on LVCs with carefully constructed demonstrations.

AI.tldr: Turkish light verb constructions (LVCs) pose a classification challenge due to surface-form ambiguity with literal verb-object phrases. The study frames LVC detection as binary classification using a controlled dataset with matched negatives. A supervised BERTurk baseline is compared against three instruction-tuned LLMs under zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting. Results reveal that zero-shot LLMs suffer from low LVC recall; one-shot sharply improves detection but introduces model-specific biases; few-shot prompting yields robust, calibrated performance, with GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen 2.5-14B matching or exceeding the supervised baseline.
AI.motivation: Turkish idiomatic LVCs are difficult to detect automatically because they share surface forms with fully literal verb-object combinations, yet function as single idiomatic predicates. Existing multiword expression processing struggles with this ambiguity, motivating a systematic evaluation of both supervised and prompted LLM-based approaches on a carefully controlled Turkish dataset.
AI.method: The task is framed as binary classification (literal vs. idiomatic). A controlled dataset of 147 items is created with LVC positives and matched negatives: out-of-domain random sentences and in-domain literal controls (NLVC). A supervised baseline (BERTurk + classifier head) is compared against three instruction-tuned LLMs from different families under zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting conditions. Error profiles are analyzed to assess how demonstrations shift model behavior.
AI.result: In zero-shot, LLMs perform well on negative examples but show very low LVC recall. One-shot prompting dramatically improves LVC detection but introduces strong, model-specific biases (overprediction or underprediction). Few-shot prompting with richer demonstrations improves calibration. GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen 2.5-14B achieve robust overall performance in the few-shot setting, matching or exceeding the supervised BERTurk baseline.
AI.conclusion: Prompted LLMs exhibit substantial sensitivity in Turkish metalinguistic classification tasks. While the supervised baseline remains competitive, carefully constructed few-shot demonstrations enable LLMs to match or surpass it on LVC detection. The findings underscore the importance of prompt design and demonstration selection for idiomaticity classification in morphologically rich languages like Turkish.

title: Beyond Linear and Overcomplete Regimes: A Mean-Field Analysis of Bottleneck Autoencoders
summary: Autoencoders (AEs) learn low-dimensional representations by mapping data into a latent space while minimizing reconstruction error. Despite their empirical success, theoretical understanding remains limited and largely restricted to linear models or settings without a bottleneck. In this work, we study nonlinear AEs with a fixed finite-dimensional bottleneck in the mean-field (MF) regime. We derive explicit MF learning dynamics for both encoder and decoder, providing a tractable characterization of training in the nonlinear setting. We show that, over finite time horizons, the empirical risk of finite-width networks trained with stochastic gradient descent closely tracks the MF risk trajectory with high probability. At optimality, we further establish that the finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum, demonstrating that finite networks are sufficiently expressive to approximate the infinite-width solution.

AI.tldr: Nonlinear autoencoders with finite-dimensional bottlenecks are studied in the mean-field regime, yielding explicit learning dynamics for encoder and decoder. Finite-width networks trained via SGD provably track the mean-field risk trajectory and converge to the mean-field optimum.
AI.motivation: Theoretical understanding of autoencoders is largely restricted to linear models or cases without bottlenecks, leaving nonlinear AEs with bottlenecks poorly understood. This work aims to bridge this gap by providing a rigorous mean-field analysis.
AI.method: The authors analyze nonlinear autoencoders in the mean-field (MF) regime with fixed finite-dimensional bottlenecks. They derive explicit coupled ordinary differential equations (ODEs) governing the MF learning dynamics of both encoder and decoder parameters, then prove finite-width convergence guarantees: (1) over finite time horizons, the empirical risk of finite-width networks trained with SGD tracks the MF risk with high probability, and (2) at optimality, the finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum.
AI.result: The derived MF dynamics provide a tractable characterization of nonlinear AE training. Finite-width networks with SGD are shown to closely track the MF risk trajectory over finite time with high probability, and the finite-width risk at optimality converges to the MF optimum, confirming sufficient expressivity of finite networks.
AI.conclusion: The mean-field framework yields a theoretically grounded understanding of nonlinear autoencoders with bottlenecks, establishing that MF dynamics accurately capture the behavior of practical finite-width networks and that the infinite-width solution is approximable by finite networks.

title: Learning Explicit Behavioral Models with Adaptive Questions and World-Model Probes
summary: Interactive agents trained only against task return can achieve high scores while failing to represent the mechanisms that make their actions succeed. This makes brittle behavior difficult to diagnose and limits adaptation when environment dynamics change. Existing LLM reflection and policy-code repair can revise behavior from failed trajectories, but questions and world-understanding tests are usually used only after training. We introduce an Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM), a trainable behavioral model that couples task performance with evidence-grounded question answering and executable mechanism prediction. An ESBM represents behavior through typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options and mechanism memory; the mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards and terminal consequences under action interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and active world-model probes convert score failures, QA errors and transition-prediction errors into constraints for local ESBM edits. Candidate models are selected by a multi-criterion rule that jointly evaluates task score, answerability and active world-model consistency. Under the tested Atari-style protocols, ESBM learns high-scoring policies while producing explicit answers and executable mechanism predictions, indicating that adaptive questions can serve as both training pressure and reusable benchmarks for mechanistic policy learning in this setting.

AI.tldr: ESBM is a trainable behavioral model that jointly optimizes task performance, question answering, and executable mechanism prediction. It uses adaptive questions and active world-model probes after each rollout to convert failures into local model edits, selected via multi-criterion evaluation (task score, answerability, world-model consistency). On Atari-style protocols, it achieves high scores while producing explicit, verifiable mechanistic explanations.
AI.motivation: Standard RL agents trained purely on task return can achieve high scores without understanding the causal mechanisms behind their actions, leading to brittle, opaque behavior that is hard to diagnose and fails under distribution shift. Existing LLM-based reflection and code repair operate post-hoc on failed trajectories, but explicit world-understanding tests are typically applied only after training. The authors argue for integrating mechanistic understanding directly into the training loop.
AI.method: The Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM) comprises typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options, and a mechanism memory. The mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards, and terminal consequences conditioned on action interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and active world-model probes generate constraints from score failures, QA errors, and transition-prediction errors. These constraints drive local ESBM edits, and candidate models are selected using a multi-criterion rule balancing task score, answerability (QA performance), and active world-model consistency.
AI.result: Under Atari-style protocols, ESBM learns high-scoring policies while simultaneously producing explicit answers and executable mechanism predictions. The adaptive questioning process serves dual purposes: as training pressure for mechanistic policy learning and as a reusable benchmark for evaluating mechanistic understanding.
AI.conclusion: Integrating adaptive questioning and active world-model probing into the training loop via a symbolic behavioral model enables agents to achieve strong task performance alongside explicit, verifiable mechanistic understanding. This approach addresses brittleness and opacity in learned policies and suggests that mechanistic QA can be both a training signal and an evaluation benchmark.

title: Agentopia: Long-Term Life Simulation and Learning in Agent Societies
summary: Humans learn from social life. Simulating this process with LLM-powered agents represents a promising research direction, raising a natural question: whether LLMs can learn from such simulated social experience to better understand and replicate human behavior. However, prior agent society simulations typically operate at the scale of days, limiting the depth of social interactions and long-term growth. In this paper, we study long-term life simulation and LLM learning in agent societies, with two goals: (1) investigating social behaviors that emerge from life-long simulation, and (2) developing anthropomorphic capabilities in LLMs, particularly intelligence in social life, through years of simulated social experience. Specifically, we present Agentopia, a comprehensive framework for long-term life simulation in multi-agent societies, where 100 agents autonomously pursue personal growth, develop social relationships, and fulfill their needs and goals over 10 simulated years. We define life reward to mirror human well-being, and leverage this reward to train LLMs via rejection sampling. Extensive experiments show that agents exhibit rich emergent social behaviors. Furthermore, life reward training effectively enhances the underlying LLM, which leads to improved agent well-being in simulation, and generalizes to downstream role-playing benchmarks with +15.6% improvement.

AI.tldr: Agentopia is a framework for simulating 100 agents over 10 years, using a "life reward" metric to train LLMs via rejection sampling. Agents exhibit emergent social behaviors, and the training improves both in-simulation well-being and downstream role-playing benchmarks (+15.6%).
AI.motivation: Prior agent society simulations operate at the scale of days, limiting the depth of social interactions and long-term growth. The authors aim to investigate (1) emergent social behaviors from lifelong simulation and (2) whether LLMs can develop anthropomorphic capabilities through years of simulated social experience.
AI.method: Agentopia: a multi-agent framework with 100 agents autonomously pursuing personal growth, social relationships, needs, and goals over 10 simulated years. A "life reward" metric is defined to mirror human well-being. This reward is used to train the underlying LLMs via rejection sampling, selecting trajectories that maximize life reward.
AI.result: Agents exhibit rich emergent social behaviors over the 10-year simulation. Life reward training effectively enhances the underlying LLM, leading to improved agent well-being within the simulation. The training generalizes to downstream role-playing benchmarks, yielding a +15.6% improvement.
AI.conclusion: Long-term social simulation with life reward-driven LLM training produces emergent social behaviors and measurably improves the LLM's anthropomorphic capabilities, demonstrating that simulated social experience can serve as effective training signal for enhancing social intelligence in language models.

title: A machine-learning-assisted progressive digit-randomness screening framework for detecting non-random patterns in raw numerical research data
summary: Raw numerical datasets remain less systematically examined in integrity screening than images, plagiarism, or summary-statistic inconsistencies. We developed the Fabrication-risk Digit Randomness Screening model (FDRS), a statistical and machine-learning framework for detecting non-random digit-pattern irregularities in numerical research data. FDRS integrates single- and joint-decimal-digit tests, Cramer's V, entropy metrics, Kullback-Leibler divergence, digit-preference indices, progressive subsampling, and semi-supervised risk scoring. It was evaluated using an instrument-derived enzymatic absorbance dataset (RawData, n=253) and a blinded manually simulated irregular dataset (ErrData, n=255). RawData showed no significant deviation in single third-decimal-digit analysis, whereas ErrData showed a significant deviation. In joint third-fourth decimal digit analysis, ErrData showed higher Cramer's V, lower normalized entropy, higher KL divergence, and a more persistent progressive-subsampling deviation signal. In internal validation, Elastic-net Logistic Regression achieved the highest AUC (0.98395) and lowest Brier score (0.048439), while Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (0.926667) and balanced accuracy (0.935). RawData received a low ensemble risk score of 0.124627 and was classified as Grade 0; ErrData received a score of 0.740760 and was classified as Grade 3. External real-world benchmarks supported graded risk stratification: three datasets without identified public post-publication concerns were classified as Grade 0 or 1, whereas two datasets from publicly questioned or institutionally handled articles were classified as Grade 2 or 3. FDRS can prioritize raw numerical datasets for further review by integrating interpretable statistical and machine-learning features. It is an auxiliary digit-structure screening tool, not standalone evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

AI.tldr: FDRS is a hybrid statistical/ML framework that detects digit-pattern anomalies in raw numerical datasets using decimal-digit tests, entropy, KL divergence, and semi-supervised risk scoring; validated on real and simulated data, it achieved high discriminative performance (AUC up to 0.984) and successfully stratified external benchmark datasets by integrity concern level.
AI.motivation: Raw numerical datasets are under-scrutinized in research integrity screening compared to images, text plagiarism, or summary statistics. Existing tools rarely target digit-level irregularities in the raw data itself, leaving a gap for detecting potential fabrication through unnatural digit patterns.
AI.method: The FDRS framework combines: (1) single and joint decimal-digit frequency tests, (2) Cramer's V for digit association, (3) normalized entropy and Kullback-Leibler divergence against uniform expectations, (4) digit-preference indices, (5) progressive subsampling to assess deviation persistence, and (6) semi-supervised ensemble risk scoring. Internal validation used Elastic-net Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and other classifiers evaluated via AUC, Brier score, accuracy, and balanced accuracy. External validation applied the model to five real-world datasets with known integrity statuses.
AI.result: RawData (genuine) showed no significant third-decimal-digit deviations; ErrData (simulated irregular) showed significant deviations, higher Cramer's V, lower normalized entropy, higher KL divergence, and persistent progressive-subsampling signals. Elastic-net Logistic Regression achieved the best AUC (0.98395) and Brier score (0.048439); Random Forest achieved best accuracy (0.9267) and balanced accuracy (0.935). RawData scored 0.1246 (Grade 0); ErrData scored 0.7408 (Grade 3). External benchmarks: three clean datasets rated Grade 0–1; two flagged datasets rated Grade 2–3.
AI.conclusion: FDRS serves as an effective auxiliary screening tool for prioritizing raw numerical datasets for further integrity review. It is explicitly not standalone evidence of fabrication but provides interpretable, multi-feature digit-structure risk stratification.

title: $α$-PFN: Fast Entropy Search via In-Context Learning
summary: Information-theoretic acquisition functions such as Entropy Search (ES) offer a principled exploration-exploitation framework for Bayesian optimization (BO). However, their practical implementation relies on complicated and slow approximations, i.e., a Monte Carlo estimation of the information gain. This complexity can introduce numerical errors and requires specialized, hand-crafted implementations. We propose a two-stage amortization strategy that learns to approximate entropy search-based acquisition functions using Prior-data Fitted Networks (PFNs) in a single forward pass. A first PFN is trained to be conditioned on information about the optima; second, the $α$-PFN is trained to predict the expected information gain by training on information gains measured with the first PFN. The $α$-PFN offers a flexible learned approximation, which replaces the complex heuristic approximations with a single forward pass per candidate, enabling rapid and extensible acquisition evaluation. Empirically, our approach is competitive with state-of-the-art entropy search implementations on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, while accelerating the different entropy search variants across all our experiments, with speed ups over 50x. Source code: https://github.com/automl/AlphaPFN.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes α-PFN, a two-stage amortization strategy using Prior-data Fitted Networks (PFNs) to learn entropy search-based acquisition functions for Bayesian optimization in a single forward pass, replacing complex Monte Carlo approximations. It achieves competitive performance with over 50x speedup compared to state-of-the-art entropy search implementations.
AI.motivation: Traditional Entropy Search (ES) acquisition functions in Bayesian optimization rely on complicated, slow Monte Carlo approximations to estimate information gain, which introduces numerical errors and requires hand-crafted, specialized implementations. The authors aim to replace these complex heuristics with a fast, learned alternative.
AI.method: A two-stage amortization approach using PFNs: (1) A first PFN is trained conditioned on information about the optima (i.e., distribution over the optimizer). (2) A second network, the α-PFN, is trained to predict expected information gain by learning from information gains measured using the first PFN. This enables single forward pass evaluation of acquisition functions per candidate point.
AI.result: The α-PFN approach is competitive with state-of-the-art entropy search methods on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks. Critically, it accelerates various entropy search variants by over 50x across all experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed learned approximation via α-PFN offers a flexible, rapid, and extensible alternative to traditional entropy search acquisition, maintaining competitive optimization performance while dramatically reducing computational overhead.

title: Severity-Aware Curriculum Learning with Multi-Model Response Selection for Medical Text Generation
summary: Telehealth systems have become increasingly important for delivering accessible and timely medical information. Existing large language models often struggle to provide consistent and contextually appropriate medical responses across varying levels of case severity. This limitation highlights the need for models that can effectively adapt to the progressive complexity in medical queries. To address this challenge, we introduce a severity-aware multi-model framework that integrates curriculum training strategy with relevance-based response selection. The proposed framework employs a three-stage curriculum learning strategy, where each model is trained sequentially on mild, moderate, and critical cases to progressively acquire domain knowledge. The approach utilizes five large language models, each independently trained under the same curriculum scheme. During inference, all models generate candidate responses, and the most appropriate response is selected as the final output. The framework is trained and evaluated on the MAQA dataset, which provides annotated medical question-answer pairs. Experimental results evaluated using BERTScore demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to both baseline and fine-tuned models, attaining 86.71% in the baseline setting and 90.30% after fine-tuning. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining curriculum learning with multi-model response selection in improving response quality and relevance in medical text generation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a severity-aware multi-model framework for medical question answering that uses a three-stage curriculum learning strategy (mild → moderate → critical cases) to progressively train five LLMs, then selects the best response via relevance-based selection. Achieves 86.71% BERTScore at baseline and 90.30% after fine-tuning on the MAQA dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs struggle to provide consistent, contextually appropriate medical responses across varying levels of case severity, highlighting the need for models that can adapt to progressive complexity in medical queries.
AI.method: A severity-aware multi-model framework that combines: (1) a three-stage curriculum learning strategy where models are sequentially trained on mild, moderate, and critical cases; (2) five independently trained LLMs under the same curriculum scheme; (3) a relevance-based response selection mechanism during inference that picks the most appropriate candidate among all model outputs.
AI.result: On the MAQA dataset, evaluated with BERTScore, the framework achieves 86.71% in the baseline setting and 90.30% after fine-tuning, outperforming both baseline and fine-tuned comparison models.
AI.conclusion: Combining curriculum learning with multi-model response selection is effective for improving response quality and relevance in medical text generation, demonstrating the value of severity-aware progressive training for domain-specific LLM adaptation.

title: Explaining Unsupervised Disease Staging in Huntington's Disease: Insights into Model Representations and Clusters
comment: Accepted for oral presentation and as a full-length paper at the International Conference on AI in Healthcare 2026 (26-28 August 2026, Imperial College London) and will be published by Springer in the Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS) series
summary: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects motor, cognitive, and behavioral functions, where accurate characterization of disease progression remains essential to improve patient outcome and quality of life. Unsupervised machine learning (ML) approaches have demonstrated the ability to uncover disease progression trajectories and meaningful latent stages from longitudinal data; however, their limited interpretability restricts clinical trust and translation. We extend a previously proposed ML-based disease staging framework by applying an explainability analysis to the extracted feature representations and discovered disease stages. Applied to the Enroll-HD dataset, we first project the learned representations into a lower-dimensional space to intuitively assess whether the resulting clusters align with the progression of established clinical measures. We then use saliency maps to identify the clinical features that most strongly contribute to the learned embeddings over time. Finally, we train a surrogate classifier and apply SHAP to quantify feature importance for cluster assignments and to analyze which clinical variables drive transitions between disease stages. The explainability analysis indicates that the learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful disease structure, aligning with established motor and functional severity scores and exhibiting progressive deterioration across clusters. Within this analysis, SHAP reveals a stratification of disease stages, ranging from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency, consistent with known clinical progression patterns, while also highlighting intra-stage variability.

AI.tldr: This paper applies explainability techniques (saliency maps, SHAP) to an unsupervised ML-based disease staging framework for Huntington's disease, demonstrating that learned embeddings align with clinical measures and reveal interpretable disease progression stages ranging from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised ML can discover disease progression trajectories from longitudinal data, but limited interpretability hinders clinical trust and translation. There is a need to validate that ML-derived representations and stages are clinically meaningful.
AI.method: The authors extend a prior ML-based disease staging framework with a three-part explainability analysis on the Enroll-HD dataset: (1) low-dimensional projection of learned representations to assess alignment with established clinical measures; (2) saliency maps to identify features most influential on learned embeddings over time; (3) a surrogate classifier trained on cluster assignments with SHAP to quantify feature importance for stage assignment and transitions.
AI.result: Learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful disease structure, align with motor and functional severity scores, and show progressive deterioration across clusters. SHAP reveals a stratification from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency, consistent with known clinical progression patterns, while also highlighting intra-stage variability.
AI.conclusion: Explainability analysis confirms that unsupervised ML-derived disease stages are clinically coherent and interpretable, supporting potential clinical translation of such frameworks for tracking Huntington's disease progression.

title: REMEDI: A Benchmark for Retention and Unlearning Evaluation in Multi-label Clinical Disease Inference
summary: Language models trained for clinical disease inference are trained on patient data, which may include sensitive and private information, and data owners may request the removal of their data from a trained model due to privacy or copyright concerns. However, exactly unlearning patient-specific data is intractable, and retraining with minor data removal is resource-intensive. While there exists several machine unlearning methods that can be used, their utility is generally restricted to non-medical domains. Moreover, the existing benchmarks for evaluating such unlearning methods primarily utilize synthetically curated datasets, which are not truly representative of real-world systems. Hence, the effectiveness of these unlearning methods in the medical domain is largely unclear. To this end, we introduce REMEDI, an extensive benchmark for machine unlearning tailored to multi-label and multiclass clinical disease inference, where label correlations, longitudinal structure, and safety constraints make unlearning particularly challenging. Unlike the existing benchmarks, REMEDI considers: (1) a relevant application domain (medical), (2) comprehensive unlearning setups involving diverse sets of forget instances, (3) challenging unlearning scenarios including multi-label and multi-class classification tasks, and (4) evaluation metrics involving performance both in terms of utility and extent of unlearning achieved. REMEDI is developed using the MIMIC-III clinical database that contains comprehensive clinical data of patients. Experiments with existing unlearning methods indicate that there exists a trade-off between utility and unlearning performance. They are also largely unsuited to multi-label classification tasks. To facilitate reproducibility, we make our benchmark publicly available.

AI.tldr: REMEDI is a benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning methods in clinical disease inference using real MIMIC-III data, addressing multi-label/multi-class classification with longitudinal structure, where existing methods show a utility-unlearning trade-off and struggle with multi-label tasks.
AI.motivation: Patient data removal requests due to privacy concerns are intractable via exact unlearning and retraining is resource-intensive. Existing machine unlearning methods are limited to non-medical domains and evaluated on synthetic benchmarks that fail to represent real-world clinical settings, leaving their medical-domain effectiveness unclear.
AI.method: The authors introduce REMEDI, a benchmark built on the MIMIC-III clinical database, designed for multi-label and multiclass clinical disease inference. It encompasses diverse forget instance sets, challenging unlearning scenarios, and evaluation metrics measuring both model utility and unlearning extent, while accounting for label correlations, longitudinal structure, and safety constraints.
AI.result: Experiments with existing unlearning methods reveal a trade-off between utility and unlearning performance, and these methods are largely unsuited to multi-label classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: REMEDI provides a realistic, publicly available benchmark that highlights the limitations of current machine unlearning approaches in clinical settings, particularly for multi-label disease inference, and underscores the need for domain-specific unlearning methods.

title: Decision-Aware Evaluation of Physics-Informed Surrogates
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/Dyniel/pinn-gym
summary:
Physics-informed machine learning is often assessed by curve error, although engineering use depends on downstream decisions: ranking candidates, avoiding infeasible designs and limiting regret. We introduce pinn-gym, an open benchmark for material-conditioned lattice design that couples a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle with five printable polymer cards, dimensionless force-response targets and a protocol spanning curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval and mass regret.
  Across per-material, pooled and cross-material settings, low nRMSE is frequently insufficient to identify useful design selections. Physics-informed losses alter trade-offs rather than monotonically improving all metrics, and dimensionless conditioning improves comparability without making transfer symmetric. The benchmark is not a certified material model; within the released oracle, candidate generator and material cards, pinn-gym provides a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems rather than curve predictors alone.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces pinn-gym, an open benchmark for evaluating physics-informed machine learning (PIML) surrogates in material-conditioned lattice design. It goes beyond curve error metrics (nRMSE) to assess downstream decision-making: ranking, feasibility, and regret. Key findings show low nRMSE does not guarantee good design selection, physics-informed losses create trade-offs rather than uniform improvements, and dimensionless conditioning aids comparability without enabling symmetric transfer.
AI.motivation: PIML models are typically evaluated solely on curve prediction error (e.g., nRMSE), but practical engineering use depends on downstream decisions — ranking candidate designs, avoiding infeasible ones, and minimizing regret. There is a need for a reproducible benchmark that evaluates PIML surrogates as decision-support systems rather than mere curve predictors.
AI.method: The authors built pinn-gym, an open benchmark combining: (1) a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle, (2) five printable polymer material cards, (3) dimensionless force-response targets, and (4) an evaluation protocol covering curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval, and mass regret. Experiments span per-material, pooled, and cross-material settings.
AI.result: Low nRMSE is frequently insufficient to identify useful design selections. Physics-informed losses alter trade-offs among metrics rather than monotonically improving all of them. Dimensionless conditioning improves comparability across materials but does not make transfer symmetric.
AI.conclusion: pinn-gym provides a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems. The authors caution it is not a certified material model, but within its released oracle, generator, and material cards, it enables more holistic assessment beyond curve prediction accuracy alone.

title: Geodesics of Dynamic Graphs for Regime Change Detection
summary: Traditional change point detection in dynamic networks assumes abrupt transitions between stationary states, overlooking scenarios of continuous evolution which arise in most real-world applications, such as social networks or physical systems. We address this gap by formally defining regimes as periods of coherent dynamics in temporal graphs, which we characterize as trajectories along geodesics in a suitably defined graph space. This original perspective allows us to define regime changes as significant drifts in dynamics, either toward new trajectories or with pace changes. We leverage graph regression methods to measure the cumulative distance of sequences of observed graphs from the estimated geodesics between their endpoints, in the relevant graph space, which we can combine with change point detection algorithms. We present experiments on dynamic networks, with changing trajectories and varying speeds, in which we outperform state of the art change point detection models. Then, we analyse mobility data during the Covid-19 pandemic, and show that our assumptions on regular network evolution lead to change points that are more aligned to external events compared to the outcomes of baseline methods. Our work is the first to model and detect changes between evolving regimes in graph space, providing a realistic and powerful tool for analyzing complex temporal graph data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel framework for change point detection in dynamic networks that models regimes as continuous trajectories along geodesics in graph space, rather than assuming abrupt transitions between stationary states. It detects regime changes as significant drifts in these trajectories—either directional shifts or pace changes—by measuring cumulative deviations from estimated geodesics, and demonstrates superior performance on synthetic data and COVID-19 mobility networks.
AI.motivation: Traditional change point detection methods for dynamic networks assume abrupt transitions between stationary states. This fails to capture real-world scenarios like social networks or physical systems where networks evolve continuously over time. There is a gap in formally modeling and detecting changes between continuously evolving regimes.
AI.method: The authors formally define regimes as periods of coherent dynamics characterized as trajectories along geodesics in a suitably defined graph space. They leverage graph regression methods to measure the cumulative distance between sequences of observed graphs and the estimated geodesics connecting their endpoints. These cumulative distance measures are then combined with change point detection algorithms to identify regime changes—either shifts toward new trajectories or changes in pace along the same trajectory.
AI.result: The method outperforms state-of-the-art change point detection models on synthetic dynamic networks with changing trajectories and varying speeds. On real-world COVID-19 mobility data, the detected change points align more closely with external events (e.g., lockdowns, policy changes) compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: This work is the first to model and detect changes between evolving regimes in graph space, offering a more realistic and powerful tool for analyzing complex temporal graph data where network evolution is continuous rather than piecewise stationary.

title: RETROSPECT: RETROsynthesis via Sequential Prediction, and Chemically Transformed-ranking
comment: Accepted at the AI for Science workshop (ICML 2026)
summary: Single-step retrosynthesis needs both accurate first-ranked suggestions and candidate lists that are rich enough for downstream selection. We study this as a proposal-selection decomposition. Our system, RETROSPECT, combines a single Transformer proposal model, which we call the ChemAlign Transformer, with a LambdaMART reranker over structural, reaction-template, upstream-score, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. The generator is trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, exponential moving average weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss. On the full USPTO-50K test set of 5,007 reactions, the generator reaches 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy with 99.86% top-1 validity. On the merged candidate-pool benchmark used for reranking, which contains 5,007 test products and about 111 candidates per product, a LambdaMART model trained on the structural feature set reaches 59.4% top-1 with 0.7171 mean reciprocal rank. Feature ablations show that upstream proposal score and template-frequency statistics provide most of the reranking signal, while DFT and reaction-center DFT features provide smaller and less consistent gains. These results support a modular view of retrosynthesis: stronger single-model proposal and learned candidate selection are complementary, and the proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems such as RetroChimera (Maziarz et al., 2024)

AI.tldr: RETROSPECT decomposes single-step retrosynthesis into proposal generation (a Transformer with ChemAlign) and candidate selection (LambdaMART reranker). The generator achieves 55% top-1 exact match on USPTO-50K; the reranker boosts this to 59.4% top-1. Proposal scores and template-frequency features dominate reranking gains, while DFT features add marginal benefit. The proposal model is designed as a drop-in component for ensemble systems.
AI.motivation: Single-step retrosynthesis requires both high top-1 accuracy and rich candidate lists for downstream selection. The authors frame this as a proposal-selection decomposition, aiming to improve both stages independently and in combination, with the proposal model serving as a modular component for larger ensemble systems like RetroChimera.
AI.method: The RETROSPECT system consists of: (1) a ChemAlign Transformer — a single proposal model trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, exponential moving average weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss; (2) a LambdaMART reranker operating on structural, reaction-template, upstream-score, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. Evaluation uses the full USPTO-50K test set (5,007 reactions) and a merged candidate-pool benchmark (~111 candidates per product).
AI.result: The generator achieves 55.00% top-1, 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy, with 99.86% top-1 validity on USPTO-50K. The LambdaMART reranker on the structural feature set reaches 59.4% top-1 with 0.7171 MRR on the merged benchmark. Feature ablation reveals upstream proposal score and template-frequency statistics provide most reranking signal; DFT and reaction-center DFT features yield smaller, less consistent gains.
AI.conclusion: Stronger single-model proposals and learned candidate selection are complementary. The proposal model is effective as a standalone component and can be integrated as a drop-in module within ensemble retrosynthesis frameworks, supporting a modular architecture for the task.

title: Structure-Preserving Correction Learning for Sparse Bayesian Inference in Brain Source Imaging
summary: Classical sparse Type-II Bayesian methods for M/EEG brain imaging support joint estimation of source and noise hyperparameters, but rely on fixed iterative update rules. Although these updates are principled and interpretable, their dynamics cannot be adapted from data. We propose to learn the update mechanism itself while preserving the underlying Bayesian structure by unfolding a classical joint hyperparameter-learning solver into a trainable neural architecture whose layers mirror the original iterations. The resulting framework is initialized to recover the classical solver exactly before training and is enriched through progressively more expressive correction-learning mechanisms, ranging from learnable biases to adaptive MLP and attention-based contextual refinements. In this way, training does not replace Bayesian inference with a black-box predictor, but instead learns structured correction terms while retaining the interpretability and model-based character of the original update dynamics. Structured correction learning therefore aims to improve empirical reconstruction performance without replacing the original model-based inference mechanism. Experimental results show that the learned correction variants improve reconstruction performance and convergence behavior over the baseline unfolded solver while preserving its algorithmic transparency.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes "structured correction learning" — a method that unfolds classical sparse Bayesian source localization algorithms into trainable neural networks. Instead of replacing Bayesian inference with a black-box predictor, it learns additive correction terms (biases, MLPs, attention) to the original update rules, preserving interpretability while improving reconstruction and convergence speed for M/EEG brain imaging.
AI.motivation: Classical Type-II Bayesian methods for M/EEG source imaging use principled but fixed iterative update rules for joint hyperparameter estimation. These rules cannot adapt their dynamics from data, potentially limiting empirical reconstruction performance. The authors aim to improve performance while retaining the interpretability and model-based structure of the original solver.
AI.method: The authors unfold a classical joint hyperparameter-learning solver into a neural architecture whose layers mirror the original iterations. The unfolded network is initialized to exactly replicate the classical solver, then progressively enriched with structured correction-learning mechanisms: learnable biases, adaptive MLP corrections, and attention-based contextual refinements. These corrections are additive residual terms applied to the original update rules, preserving the underlying Bayesian structure.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that the learned correction variants improve both reconstruction performance and convergence behavior compared to the baseline unfolded solver.
AI.conclusion: Structured correction learning successfully enhances empirical reconstruction without sacrificing algorithmic transparency. The framework demonstrates that learning residual corrections to principled Bayesian updates is an effective middle ground between pure model-based inference and black-box deep learning.

title: DEFINED: A Data-Efficient Computational Framework for Fine-Grained Creativity Assessment in Debate Scenarios
summary: Human creativity has emerged as a critical competency in the era of large language models. Assessing creativity in complex, open-ended environments is a grand challenge in data mining, currently hindered by a reliance on standardized simple tasks and the scarcity of fine-grained expert data. As an ecologically valid assessment context, debate reflects multiple dimensions of creativity, encompassing both divergent thinking and convergent thinking. Moreover, debate is a data-rich domain, with a large volume of publicly accessible materials. Current mainstream automated scoring methods are poorly suited to complex settings such as debate, and therefore still rely on costly human evaluation. To this end, this paper proposes DEFINED, a data-efficient computational framework for fine-grained creativity assessment in debate scenarios. DEFINED operationalizes debate creativity through a hierarchical eight-dimensional metric system, implemented via a pre-trained autoregressive language model with a hierarchical scoring head that supports both fine-grained and coarse-grained evaluation. Statements and their associated expert scores were obtained from authentic debate competitions, and a constrained data augmentation strategy was employed to address the elite bias inherent in the original data. DEFINED adopts a mixed-granularity training strategy enabling robust learning from limited fine-grained supervision annotated by trained graduate experts. To rigorously validate ecological validity beyond synthetic benchmarks, we incorporate an empirical study with debate-naive participants, utilizing these authentic data to serve as a qualitative case study for mid-to-low proficiency populations. Across our evaluation protocol, our scoring model achieves accurate and stable scoring, outperforming prompt-based large language model evaluators and existing debate scoring methods.

AI.tldr: DEFINED is a data-efficient hierarchical framework for fine-grained creativity assessment in debate scenarios, using an eight-dimensional metric system, constrained data augmentation, and mixed-granularity training to outperform prompt-based LLM evaluators and existing debate scoring methods.
AI.motivation: Current creativity assessment methods are limited to simple standardized tasks and lack fine-grained expert data, making them unsuitable for complex, ecologically valid settings like debate. Debate serves as an ideal assessment context—reflecting divergent and convergent thinking—yet still relies on costly human evaluation due to the inadequacy of existing automated scoring methods.
AI.method: The paper proposes DEFINED, which operationalizes debate creativity via a hierarchical eight-dimensional metric system. It uses a pre-trained autoregressive language model with a hierarchical scoring head for both fine- and coarse-grained evaluation. Data comes from authentic debate competitions with expert scores. A constrained data augmentation strategy addresses elite bias, and a mixed-granularity training strategy enables robust learning from limited expert annotations. An empirical study with debate-naive participants provides ecological validation.
AI.result: DEFINED achieves accurate and stable scoring, outperforming both prompt-based LLM evaluators and existing debate scoring methods across the evaluation protocol.
AI.conclusion: DEFINED provides a scalable, data-efficient solution for fine-grained creativity assessment in open-ended, complex domains like debate, bridging the gap between automated scoring and ecologically valid human evaluation.

title: Generative Molecular Morphing for Flexible-Size Design via Unbalanced Optimal Transport
summary: The success of generative molecular design hinges on a model's steerability toward high-reward samples. Because many molecular properties are intrinsically linked to molecular size, accurately capturing the joint distribution of properties and the number of atoms is essential. However, current diffusion and flow-based models fix the number of atoms, which ultimately limits their ability to navigate this complex relationship. To address this, we introduce Morph, a flexible-size generative model for conditional and unconditional 3D molecular design based on geometric graphs. By dynamically adapting size, Morph can seamlessly integrate existing structural priors, like scaffolds, and significantly enhances property steering. We show that Morph matches current fixed-size state-of-the-art models while offering the benefit of unparalleled sampling flexibility. We demonstrate out-of-distribution generation in regimes where previous models fail, paving the way for enhanced generative modeling for molecular design.

AI.tldr: Morph is a flexible-size generative model for 3D molecular design that dynamically adapts the number of atoms, overcoming the fixed-size limitation of current diffusion/flow models, enabling better property steering and out-of-distribution generation.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion and flow-based molecular generative models fix the number of atoms, which limits their ability to capture the intrinsic joint distribution between molecular properties and molecular size. This fundamentally restricts steerability toward high-reward samples.
AI.method: The authors introduce Morph, a flexible-size generative model for conditional and unconditional 3D molecular design based on geometric graphs. It dynamically adapts molecular size during generation and can integrate structural priors such as scaffolds.
AI.result: Morph matches the performance of fixed-size state-of-the-art models while offering unparalleled sampling flexibility. It succeeds in out-of-distribution generation regimes where previous models fail.
AI.conclusion: Flexible-size generation is a critical advancement for molecular design, enabling better property steering and paving the way for enhanced generative modeling in the domain.

title: A Held-Out Transition-Pair Falsifier for Long-Horizon Non-Abelian State Tracking
summary: State tracking exposes a sharp limitation of sequence models: the relevant signal is often not a summary of observed tokens, but an ordered latent state that evolves through non-commutative transformations. We introduce a held-out transition-pair falsifier for finite non-Abelian group tracking. The protocol forbids selected ordered generator pairs during training and requires the same local patterns during evaluation, blocking one direct local-transition memorization pathway. In a controlled $S_3 \times S_3$ benchmark, a projected recurrent state model trained only on length-8 sequences produces error-free final-state predictions (perfect 250/250 per horizon) through evaluation horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens across five seeds. Matched native-readout baselines, including bag, GRU, and a single-configuration structured state-space model, remain near floor under the same protocol. Projection-matched GRU, structured SSM, and bag baselines equipped with analogous finite-group prototype readouts also remain near chance under the same split. Mechanism diagnostics show that hard projection coincides with low homomorphism error, low state-consistency drift, and non-trivial commutator separation, while softened projection collapses final-state accuracy. Clean-split audits verify zero verbatim reduced-word overlap and zero structural-template overlap between training and evaluation partitions. The evidence is scoped to this controlled finite-group falsifier rather than to a general architecture ranking. Within that regime, explicit projected non-commutative state composition acts as a useful inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking.

AI.tldr: Sequence models struggle with tracking latent states that evolve through non-commutative transformations. The authors introduce a held-out transition-pair falsifier for finite non-Abelian group tracking, showing that a projected recurrent state model achieves perfect final-state prediction over horizons up to 1M tokens, while standard baselines fail. Hard projection is shown to be critical for maintaining low homomorphism error and state consistency.
AI.motivation: State tracking in sequence models is fundamentally limited because the relevant signal is not a summary of observed tokens but an ordered latent state evolving through non-commutative (order-dependent) transformations. Standard sequence models tend to rely on summary statistics or local-transition memorization rather than faithfully composing transformations, creating a need to diagnose and address this capability gap.
AI.method: The authors introduce a held-out transition-pair falsifier protocol: during training on a controlled S₃ × S₃ finite non-Abelian group, selected ordered generator pairs are forbidden, and these same local patterns are required during evaluation. This blocks direct local-transition memorization. They then test a projected recurrent state model (with hard projection to valid group elements) against baselines (bag, GRU, structured SSM, and projection-matched variants with finite-group prototype readouts), evaluating on horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens. Mechanism diagnostics examine homomorphism error, state-consistency drift, and commutator separation.
AI.result: The projected recurrent state model trained only on length-8 sequences achieves perfect 250/250 final-state accuracy per horizon through evaluation horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens (five seeds). Matched native-readout baselines remain near floor, and projection-matched GRU/SSM/bag baselines with finite-group prototype readouts remain near chance. Hard projection coincides with low homomorphism error, low state-consistency drift, and non-trivial commutator separation, while softened projection collapses accuracy. Clean-split audits confirm zero verbatim reduced-word overlap and zero structural-template overlap between training and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit projected non-commutative state composition serves as a useful inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking in the finite-group regime. The evidence is scoped specifically to this controlled finite-group falsifier and does not constitute a general architecture ranking.

title: Where Rectified Flows Leak: Characterising Membership Signals Along the Interpolation Path
comment: ICML 2026 article, 9 main pages and 25 with annexes, 11 figures
summary: Understanding what generative models retain from training data remains challenging, with implications for copyright and privacy. Beyond verbatim reproduction, models can encode subtler traces of their training data that never surface in their outputs yet remain exploitable. We study this regime for Rectified Flows, which are increasingly used in deployed generative systems.
