We analyse the interpolation path $X_λ= (1-λ)X_0 + λX_1$ that defines the Rectified Flow training. We show that a gap exists between the reconstruction of train and test data that follows a bell-shaped curve over $λ$, wich accumulates during training, while the validation metrics remain stable. The signal has a maximum whose location we derive in closed form under Gaussian assumptions. We validate these predictions on both audio and images and show that the bell-shaped structure is universal, while the peak prediction holds when our assumptions are satisfied. As a proof of concept, we exploit this specific $λ$-resolved structure to perform a Membership Inference Attack, distinguishing members of the training set from non-members.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how Rectified Flow generative models retain subtle, non-verbatim traces of training data that can be exploited. It identifies a bell-shaped reconstruction gap between train and test data along the interpolation path, derives the peak location analytically under Gaussian assumptions, and validates predictions on audio and image domains, culminating in a proof-of-concept Membership Inference Attack.
AI.motivation: Generative models may encode subtle training data traces beyond verbatim reproduction, raising copyright and privacy concerns. These traces never surface in outputs but remain exploitable, yet this regime is poorly understood for Rectified Flows—a model class increasingly deployed in real-world generative systems.
AI.method: The authors analyze the interpolation path X_λ = (1-λ)X_0 + λX_1 central to Rectified Flow training. They examine the reconstruction error gap between training and test data as a function of λ, revealing a bell-shaped curve that accumulates over training while validation metrics remain stable. Under Gaussian assumptions, they derive the peak location of this signal in closed form. Validation spans audio and image modalities, testing both the universality of the bell-shaped structure and the accuracy of the peak prediction.
AI.result: A bell-shaped reconstruction gap between train and test data exists along λ, with a maximum whose location matches the derived closed-form expression under Gaussian assumptions. The bell-shaped structure is universal across audio and image domains. The peak prediction holds when Gaussian assumptions are satisfied. The λ-resolved signal enables a proof-of-concept Membership Inference Attack that distinguishes training set members from non-members.
AI.conclusion: Rectified Flows leak non-verbatim, exploitable traces of training data through a characteristic bell-shaped reconstruction signal along the interpolation path. This signal is universal across modalities and analytically predictable under Gaussian conditions, representing a previously unidentified privacy vulnerability that can be operationalized for membership inference.

title: Closed-Form Spectral Regularization for Multi-Task Model Merging
summary: Model merging combines several independently fine-tuned experts into a single multi-task model without any training data, reducing the storage, serving, and decentralized-development costs of large foundation models. State-of-the-art merging methods formulate merging as a layer-wise quadratic interference minimization problem. Although this problem admits an exact closed-form pseudoinverse solution, that solution underperforms hundreds of iterations of gradient descent in practice. The iterative loop dominates the cost of the pipeline, yet its effectiveness has remained unexplained. We revisit this regime and show that the iterative solver does not primarily act as an optimizer; rather, it serves as an implicit spectral regularizer for an ill-posed normal equation, where small-eigenvalue directions of the per-layer interference operator amplify proxy noise. Building on this finding, we formalize multi-task model merging as a noisy linear inverse problem and propose a spectral filtering estimator parameterized by a per-direction filter. We instantiate this estimator with SWUDI, a closed-form method that combines a soft exponential filter, which matches the gradient-flow trajectory of iterative descent, with a hard top-K truncation that suppresses noise-amplifying small-eigenvalue directions. Furthermore, we propose SWUDI-A, an adaptive variant that replaces the global rank hyperparameter with per-layer rank rules, further improving robustness across architectures. Both variants share a single symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer and require no training data or optimizer state. Across four general benchmarks and a multimodal merging benchmark spanning VQA, Geometry, Chart, OCR, Grounding, and modality merging, our proposed spectral solvers match or outperform state-of-the-art merging methods. Crucially, they reduce wall-clock time by 28-72x and peak GPU memory by up to 50%.

AI.tldr: Model merging combines fine-tuned models without training data but is dominated by costly iterative gradient descent. The authors show this iterative solver acts as an implicit spectral regularizer for an ill-posed problem, then propose SWUDI — a closed-form spectral filtering estimator that matches or outperforms SOTA methods while reducing wall-clock time by 28-72× and GPU memory by up to 50%.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art model merging formulates the task as layer-wise quadratic interference minimization, solved via hundreds of gradient descent iterations. While a closed-form pseudoinverse solution exists, it underperforms the iterative approach in practice. The effectiveness of the iterative loop has remained unexplained, and its computational cost dominates the merging pipeline. This motivates understanding why the iterative solver works and developing a principled, efficient alternative.
AI.method: The authors first reframe iterative descent as an implicit spectral regularizer: the gradient-flow trajectory corresponds to applying a soft exponential filter that attenuates small-eigenvalue directions of the per-layer interference operator, which otherwise amplify proxy noise in the ill-posed normal equation. Building on this insight, they formalize multi-task merging as a noisy linear inverse problem and propose a spectral filtering estimator. SWUDI combines (1) a soft exponential filter matching the iterative gradient-flow trajectory with (2) a hard top-K truncation to suppress noise-amplifying small-eigenvalue directions. SWUDI-A further replaces the global rank K with per-layer adaptive rank rules. Both require only a single symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer, with no training data or optimizer state.
AI.result: Across four general benchmarks and a multimodal merging benchmark (spanning VQA, Geometry, Chart, OCR, Grounding, and modality merging), SWUDI and SWUDI-A match or outperform state-of-the-art merging methods. Crucially, they reduce wall-clock time by 28–72× and peak GPU memory by up to 50% compared to iterative approaches.
AI.conclusion: Iterative gradient descent in model merging functions primarily as an implicit spectral regularizer rather than an optimizer. The proposed closed-form spectral filtering approach (SWUDI/SWUDI-A) provides a principled, data-free, and optimizer-free solution that dramatically reduces computational cost while maintaining or improving merging quality, with per-layer adaptive rank rules further enhancing robustness across architectures.

title: Trio: Learning Time-Series Forecasting with Temporal-Spatial-Sample Attention and Structural Causal Priors
summary: Multivariate time-series forecasting requires models to reason over temporal dynamics, cross-variable dependencies, and historical input-output correspondences. Recent Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) suggest that synthetic tasks can be useful for learning transferable inference behavior. However, directly transferring this paradigm to time-series forecasting remains difficult, since temporal order, dynamic lags, and recurring historical patterns are not naturally captured by ordinary tabular priors. Motivated by this observation, we propose Trio, a sample-aware time-series forecasting architecture based on Temporal-Spatial-Sample attention. Temporal attention captures within-window dynamics, spatial attention models inter-variable dependencies, and sample attention retrieves relevant historical lookback-future pairs to guide the current prediction. Rather than claiming a fully general PFN-style forecaster, our goal is to study how historical input-output examples can be explicitly organized and reused within a forecasting model. We further introduce a Time-Series Structural Causal Model (TS-SCM) generator to create structured synthetic forecasting tasks with dynamic lags, cross-variable interactions, noise, feedback, and distributional drift. Experiments on synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks show that the proposed architecture improves forecasting performance. Exploratory zero-shot experiments further suggest that TS-SCM-generated tasks may provide useful structural priors, while fully general PFN-style time-series forecasting remains an open problem.

AI.tldr: Trio is a time-series forecasting architecture using Temporal-Spatial-Sample attention that explicitly retrieves and reuses historical input-output pairs, trained with a TS-SCM synthetic data generator. It improves forecasting performance on synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks, but fully general PFN-style time-series forecasting remains unsolved.
AI.motivation: PFNs demonstrate that synthetic tasks can teach transferable inference, but directly applying this to time-series forecasting is challenging because tabular priors fail to capture temporal order, dynamic lags, and recurring historical patterns. The authors aim to study how historical input-output examples can be explicitly organized and reused within a forecasting model rather than claiming a fully general PFN-style solution.
AI.method: The Trio architecture employs a triple-attention mechanism: (1) Temporal attention for within-window dynamics, (2) Spatial attention for inter-variable dependencies, and (3) Sample attention to retrieve relevant historical lookback-future pairs to guide current predictions. A TS-SCM (Time-Series Structural Causal Model) generator creates structured synthetic forecasting tasks featuring dynamic lags, cross-variable interactions, noise, feedback, and distributional drift to serve as training priors.
AI.result: Trio improves forecasting performance across synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks. Exploratory zero-shot experiments indicate that TS-SCM-generated tasks provide useful structural priors for transfer learning.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly organizing and reusing historical input-output examples via sample-aware attention is an effective strategy for time-series forecasting. TS-SCM-generated synthetic data offers promising structural priors, though fully general PFN-style time-series forecasting remains an open problem requiring further research.

title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
summary: A growing failure mode in agent evaluation and training is that models can achieve high evaluation scores by exploiting shortcuts instead of solving the intended task, producing deceptive performance. This makes evaluation scores unreliable as measures of true task-solving ability. We propose CapCode, a framework for constructing coding datasets with randomized tests whose best achievable non-cheating performance is deliberately capped below one. This capped-performance design gives evaluation scores a clearer interpretation: scores substantially above the cap are implausible and therefore provide evidence of cheating. To prevent cheating, we propose CapReward, a reward design based on the CapCode principle to discourage optimization beyond the cap. Experiments across multiple datasets show that CapCode detects cheating while preserving performance ranking of models, and CapReward reduces cheating behavior, yielding models that better follow the intended task specification.

AI.tldr: CapCode introduces capped-performance coding datasets where maximum non-cheating scores are deliberately limited, making scores above this cap evidence of shortcut exploitation. The companion CapReward mechanism discourages optimization beyond the cap during training.
AI.motivation: Agent evaluation suffers from deceptive performance where models exploit shortcuts to achieve high scores without solving the intended task. This renders evaluation scores unreliable as measures of genuine task-solving ability, creating a need for methods that can both detect and deter such cheating behavior.
AI.method: The framework has two components: (1) CapCode — constructing coding datasets with randomized tests where the best achievable non-cheating performance is deliberately capped below 1.0, so scores substantially above this cap provide evidence of cheating; (2) CapReward — a reward design that disincentivizes optimization beyond the capped threshold during model training.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that CapCode effectively detects cheating while preserving the performance ranking among models. CapReward successfully reduces cheating behavior, resulting in models that adhere more faithfully to the intended task specification.
AI.conclusion: The capped-performance paradigm offers a principled approach to both detecting and preventing shortcut exploitation in agent evaluation and training, leading to more trustworthy assessments of model capabilities and better-aligned model behavior.

title: Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence: Explanatory Insufficiency as a Driver of Representation Learning and World Models
comment: 24 pages, 25 references. Theoretical framework relating representation learning, representational emergence, and world models
summary: Representation learning is central to modern machine learning, enabling transitions from handcrafted features to learned embeddings, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, and digital twins. Yet most research examines how representations are optimized after a representational framework has been selected, while less attention is given to when a new level of representation becomes necessary. We introduce the Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER), a framework describing how new representations arise when existing ones become explanatorily insufficient. In this view, representational innovation is not only driven by more data, larger models, or greater computational power, but also by persistent explanatory gaps: situations in which a representation can still describe observations but can no longer make their organization or transformations intelligible. TBER identifies explanatory insufficiency as a positive signal for representational transition. A representation becomes insufficient not because it is necessarily false, but because its explanatory domain has been exceeded. The bootstrap dynamic follows a recursive sequence: observations reveal anomalies; anomalies expose insufficiencies; insufficiencies motivate new representations; and these new representations generate further observations and possible new insufficiencies.We formalize this process through five stages: stabilized observation, anomaly detection, recognition of explanatory insufficiency, representational emergence, and provisional stabilization. We discuss applications to representation learning, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, digital twins, adaptive biological systems, and scientific discovery. TBER suggests that future AI systems may benefit from mechanisms for detecting the explanatory limits of their own internal representations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER), which argues that new representations arise when existing ones become "explanatorily insufficient" — able to describe observations but unable to make their organization intelligible. This recursive, five-stage bootstrap process reframes representational innovation as driven by explanatory gaps rather than merely more data or compute.
AI.motivation: Most representation learning research focuses on optimizing representations after a framework is selected, neglecting the question of when and why a new level of representation becomes necessary. The authors aim to fill this gap by formalizing the conditions under which representational transitions occur.
AI.method: The authors propose a theoretical framework (TBER) that identifies "explanatory insufficiency" as the key trigger for representational emergence. They formalize the process as a recursive bootstrap sequence of five stages: stabilized observation, anomaly detection, recognition of explanatory insufficiency, representational emergence, and provisional stabilization. The framework is developed conceptually and applied across multiple domains.
AI.result: TBER provides a unified account of representational emergence applicable to representation learning, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, digital twins, adaptive biological systems, and scientific discovery. It suggests explanatory insufficiency is a positive, informative signal rather than merely a failure condition.
AI.conclusion: Future AI systems could benefit from mechanisms that detect the explanatory limits of their own internal representations. TBER reframes representational progress as driven by persistent explanatory gaps rather than scale alone.

title: TabSwift: An Efficient Tabular Foundation Model with Row-Wise Attention
summary: Tabular foundation models, exemplified by TabPFN, perform prediction via in-context learning, inferring test labels directly from labeled training examples. They have demonstrated competitive performance, particularly on small-to-medium datasets. However, recent tabular foundation models often improve accuracy with increasingly complex architectures, incurring higher inference cost and limiting practical deployment. In this work, we revisit the original TabPFN design and show that a lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone can remain highly competitive with two simple enhancements: a gated attention stabilization mechanism and a small set of learnable register tokens that provide global context and improve pretraining quality. The resulting model, TabSwift, supports both classification and regression, and is competitive with stronger tabular foundation models (e.g., TabPFN v2 and TabICL) while being more efficient at inference. For latency-sensitive serving, we further introduce an adaptive layer-wise early-exit mechanism that dynamically adjusts inference depth per sample. Overall, TabSwift enables efficient and anytime tabular in-context learning for practical deployments.

AI.tldr: TabSwift revisits TabPFN's design, using a lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone enhanced with gated attention stabilization and learnable register tokens to match stronger models while being more efficient. An adaptive early-exit mechanism further reduces inference latency.
AI.motivation: Recent tabular foundation models improve accuracy via increasingly complex architectures, but this incurs higher inference cost and limits practical deployment. The authors aim to achieve competitive performance with a simpler, more efficient architecture.
AI.method: TabSwift uses a lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone with two enhancements: (1) a gated attention stabilization mechanism and (2) learnable register tokens for global context. For latency-sensitive scenarios, an adaptive layer-wise early-exit mechanism dynamically adjusts inference depth per sample. The model supports both classification and regression.
AI.result: TabSwift is competitive with stronger tabular foundation models (TabPFN v2, TabICL) while being more inference-efficient. The early-exit mechanism further improves serving latency.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight architectures with targeted enhancements can match or approach the performance of complex tabular foundation models. TabSwift enables efficient, anytime tabular in-context learning suitable for practical deployment.

title: SleepExplain: Explainable Non-Rapid Eye Movement and Rapid Eye Movement Sleep Stage Classification from EEG Signal
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 2022 25th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
summary: Classification of sleep stages is one of the most important diagnostic approaches for a variety of sleep-related disorders. Electroencephalography (EEG) is regarded as a powerful tool for examining the association between neurological effects and sleep phases since it correctly identifies sleep-related neurological alterations. During Non-Rapid Eye Movement (NREM) and Rapid Eye Movement (REM) sleep phases, a number of nerve and bodily functions are affected and therefore hold an important role both in their functionalities. This work aims to classify NREM and REM sleep stages from sleep EEG data and present a noble SleepExplain model, an explainable NREM and REM sleep stage classification to explain its predictions. In this work, sleep stages were classified using Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting ensemble classification models. Overall, we obtained an accuracy of 92.54% (Random Forest), 94.25% (Gradient Boosting), and 94.30% (XGBoost). For explainable classification model, we utilized a game theoretic approach, SHAP (SHapley Addictive exPlanations) to offer a convincing explanation for the prediction.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SleepExplain, an explainable model for classifying NREM vs. REM sleep stages from EEG data using ensemble classifiers (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost), achieving up to 94.30% accuracy with XGBoost, and employs SHAP for interpretability.
AI.motivation: Sleep stage classification is critical for diagnosing sleep disorders. While EEG effectively captures neurological changes during NREM and REM sleep, existing classification models often lack transparency. The authors aim to build not only accurate classifiers but also explainable ones that can justify their predictions to clinicians.
AI.method: The authors employed three ensemble classification models — Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting — to classify NREM and REM sleep stages from sleep EEG data. For model interpretability, they applied SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), a game-theoretic approach that attributes prediction importance to individual input features.
AI.result: The three models achieved the following accuracies: Random Forest (92.54%), Gradient Boosting (94.25%), and XGBoost (94.30%). SHAP provided post-hoc explanations for the model predictions, offering insight into which EEG features drove classification decisions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SleepExplain framework demonstrates that ensemble methods, particularly XGBoost, can effectively classify NREM vs. REM sleep stages from EEG data, and that SHAP-based explanations can enhance clinical trust by making the model's decision-making process transparent and interpretable.

title: Breaking the Ice: Analyzing Cold Start Latency in vLLM
summary: As scalable inference services become popular, the cold start latency of an inference engine becomes important. Today, vLLM has evolved into the de facto inference engine of choice for many inference workloads. Although popular, due to its complexity and rapid evolution, there has not been a systematic study of its startup latency. With major architectural innovations such as the V1 API and the introduction of torch.compile, this paper presents the first detailed performance characterization of vLLM startup latency. We break down the startup process into six foundational steps and demonstrate that it is predominantly CPU bound. Each step exhibits consistent and interpretable scaling trends with respect to model-level and system-level parameters, enabling fine-grained attribution of latency sources. Building on these insights, we develop a lightweight analytical model that accurately predicts vLLM startup latency for a given hardware configuration, providing actionable guidance for resource planning in large-scale inference environments. All benchmarking datasets, analysis tools, and prediction scripts are open sourced at https://github.com/upb-cn/vllm-startup-profiler.

AI.tldr: First systematic study of vLLM's startup latency, breaking it into six CPU-bound steps with consistent scaling trends. Authors develop an analytical prediction model and open-source all tooling.
AI.motivation: As scalable inference services proliferate, cold start latency becomes critical, yet vLLM—now the de facto inference engine—has never been systematically profiled for startup performance despite its complexity and rapid evolution (e.g., V1 API, torch.compile).
AI.method: The authors decompose the vLLM startup process into six foundational steps, perform detailed performance characterization across model-level and system-level parameters to identify scaling trends and latency attribution, and then construct a lightweight analytical model to predict startup latency for given hardware configurations.
AI.result: Startup latency is predominantly CPU-bound. Each of the six steps exhibits consistent, interpretable scaling trends with respect to model and system parameters, enabling fine-grained attribution of latency sources. The analytical model accurately predicts startup latency.
AI.conclusion: The characterization and predictive model provide actionable guidance for resource planning in large-scale inference environments, with all artifacts open-sourced for reproducibility and practical adoption.

title: A robust PPG foundation model using multimodal physiological supervision
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG), a non-invasive measure of changes in blood volume, is widely used in both wearable devices and clinical settings. Recent PPG foundation models either use open-source ICU datasets with pretraining paradigms that require curated data and thus complicate generalization to field-like data, or use closed-source field-like PPG data. In contrast, we propose a PPG foundation model that does not require high-quality or field-like pretraining data, and instead leverages accompanying electrocardiogram and respiratory signals in ICU datasets to select contrastive samples during pretraining. Our approach allows the model to retain and learn from noisy PPG segments, improving robustness at inference. Our model, pretrained on 3x fewer subjects than existing state-of-the-art approaches, achieves performance improvements on 14 out of 15 diverse downstream tasks, including field-like daily activity and heart rate prediction. Our results demonstrate that multimodal supervision can integrate complementary physiological information to improve the robustness of PPG foundation models and enhance their generalization to consumer-grade data.

AI.tldr: A PPG foundation model pretrained on multimodal ICU data (PPG + ECG + respiratory signals) using contrastive learning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on 14/15 downstream tasks with 3× fewer pretraining subjects, while generalizing robustly to noisy consumer-grade data.
AI.motivation: Existing PPG foundation models face a critical limitation: those using open-source ICU datasets require curated, high-quality data and struggle to generalize to noisy field conditions, while those trained on field-like data rely on closed-source datasets. The authors aim to bridge this gap by developing a model that works with readily available ICU data yet generalizes well to consumer-grade, noisy PPG signals.
AI.method: The proposed model uses a contrastive pretraining paradigm on ICU datasets that leverages multimodal signals — specifically ECG and respiratory waveforms — to intelligently select contrastive samples from PPG segments. Rather than discarding noisy segments, the multimodal supervision guides the model to retain and learn meaningful representations from them. The model is pretrained on a dataset 3× smaller than prior state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.result: The model achieves superior performance on 14 out of 15 diverse downstream tasks, including field-like daily activity monitoring and heart rate prediction, despite using only one-third the number of pretraining subjects compared to existing SOTA methods.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal supervision (ECG + respiratory signals) provides complementary physiological information that significantly enhances the robustness and generalization of PPG foundation models to consumer-grade, real-world data, without requiring expensive curated or proprietary field-like pretraining datasets.

title: Self-evolving LLM agents with in-distribution Optimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently emerged as powerful controllers for interactive agents in complex environments, yet training them to perform reliable long-horizon decision making remains a fundamental challenge. A key difficulty lies in credit assignment: agents often receive delayed rewards only at the end of episodes. In this paper, we propose Q-Evolve, a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that unifies automatic process-reward labeling and policy learning within a principled in-distribution reinforcement learning paradigm. In each evolving iteration, our method learns an in-distribution critic from a hybrid off-policy dataset that combines expert demonstrations with agent-generated trajectories, stabilizing Bellman backups in sparse-reward settings via a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective. The learned value function is then used to derive step-wise process rewards through advantage estimation, enabling dense and reliable supervision without environment backtracking or human annotation. Leveraging these signals, we perform behavior-proximal policy optimization that evolves the agent over the data used for process reward labeling, allowing iterative self-improvement without exacerbating distribution shift. We evaluate our method on AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld, showing Q-Evolve outperforms strong baselines in sample efficiency, robustness, and overall task performance. Our results demonstrate that stable agent self-evolution is achievable through the co-evolution of process-level supervision and policy, both grounded within a shared in-distribution learning loop.

AI.tldr: Q-Evolve is a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that jointly learns process-reward labeling and policy optimization within an in-distribution RL paradigm, using a weighted Implicit Q-Learning critic to derive dense step-wise rewards from sparse episode-level signals, enabling stable iterative self-improvement.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with long-horizon decision making due to the credit assignment problem: rewards are sparse and delayed (only at episode end), making it hard to determine which actions contributed to success. Existing methods rely on environment backtracking, human annotation, or suffer from distribution shift during iterative training.
AI.method: Q-Evolve operates iteratively in three stages: (1) trains an in-distribution critic via weighted Implicit Q-Learning on a hybrid dataset of expert demonstrations and agent trajectories; (2) derives step-wise process rewards using advantage estimation from the learned value function; (3) performs behavior-proximal policy optimization using these dense signals, updating the agent on the same data distribution to prevent distribution shift. The critic and policy co-evolve within a shared in-distribution loop.
AI.result: Q-Evolve outperforms strong baselines on AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld across sample efficiency, robustness, and overall task performance, demonstrating that stable self-evolution is achievable through the co-evolution of process-level supervision and policy.
AI.conclusion: Stable agent self-improvement can be realized by grounding both process-reward generation and policy learning within a shared in-distribution loop, avoiding distribution shift while converting sparse episode rewards into dense, reliable step-wise supervision signals.

title: Covariance Shrinkage via Stochastic Interpolation
summary: We recast classical shrinkage of high-dimensional covariance estimators as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant between a source and a target distribution. This formalism recovers known shrinkage estimators as special cases and reveals three distinct mechanisms for reducing statistical risk: (i) Scheduling: the interpolant schedule determines the class of admissible covariances, and hence the achievable risk. (ii) Flow maps and couplings: whereas naive constructions amount to assuming independence between the distributions, specific coupling structures (e.g., solutions of optimal transport problems) can lower the empirical risk. Moreover, non-linear flow maps realizing such couplings free the interpolant covariance from the eigenbasis of the empirical estimate, enabling eigenvector regularization. (iii) Early stopping: estimators defined by integrating a regressed vector field afford an additional bias-variance trade-off through approximation of the true interpolant distribution. We then propose a neural estimator of the interpolant, together with an upper bound on its quadratic risk in terms of the interpolant approximation error, and validate both on synthetic experiments. Finally, we apply the estimator to real neuroimaging data, demonstrating the additional regularization power this approach offers in practice.

AI.tldr: Reinterprets high-dimensional covariance shrinkage as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant between source and target distributions. Identifies three risk-reduction mechanisms—scheduling, coupling/flow maps, and early stopping—and proposes a neural estimator validated on synthetic and neuroimaging data.
AI.motivation: Classical shrinkage estimators for high-dimensional covariance matrices (e.g., Ledoit-Wolf) are effective but offer limited flexibility. The authors aim to uncover the underlying statistical mechanisms that govern shrinkage performance and to generalize beyond linear shrinkage through a distributional interpolant framework, enabling richer regularization strategies.
AI.method: The paper recasts shrinkage as empirical risk minimization over a stochastic interpolant that continuously transforms a source distribution (e.g., empirical covariance) into a target (e.g., a well-conditioned prior). Three mechanisms are analyzed: (1) the interpolant schedule controlling the admissible covariance class; (2) optimal transport couplings and non-linear flow maps that decouple the interpolant from the empirical eigenbasis, enabling eigenvector regularization; (3) early stopping of a regressed vector field integration providing bias-variance trade-off. A neural network estimator of the interpolant is proposed, with an upper bound on its quadratic risk derived.
AI.result: The neural interpolant estimator is validated on synthetic experiments, demonstrating the benefits of coupling structure and early stopping. Application to real neuroimaging data shows additional regularization power beyond classical shrinkage in practice.
AI.conclusion: The stochastic interpolant perspective unifies and extends classical covariance shrinkage, with coupling design, non-linear flows, and early stopping each providing distinct axes of risk reduction. Neural estimation makes the framework practical, offering meaningful gains on real-world high-dimensional data.

title: Making the Most of Limited Data: Score-Aware Training for Text-to-Music Generation
summary: State-of-the-art text-to-music generation systems rely on massive proprietary datasets and industrial-scale compute, making it impossible to disentangle architectural contributions from resource advantages. We propose \textit{score-aware training}, which treats audio-caption alignment score as a direct supervision signal throughout the pipeline. Rather than discarding low-scoring segments, we repurpose them via a CLAP-conditioned Beta noise timestep schedule that routes them to high-noise training regimes, acting as an effective implicit regularizer. Complementarily, segment-level filtering removes the most misaligned examples, and a two-stage caption procedure bridges the distribution gap between verbose training captions and concise inference prompts. A REPA auxiliary loss further transfers structured semantic knowledge from pretrained CLAP and MuQ encoders without additional data. Our 450M-parameter FluxAudio-based system, submitted to the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge Efficiency Track, ranked 2nd across both tracks in the objective evaluation and 3rd in the Efficiency Track in the final MOS evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes score-aware training for text-to-music generation, repurposing low-scoring audio-caption pairs via a CLAP-conditioned Beta noise schedule as implicit regularization, combined with segment filtering and a two-stage captioning strategy. Their 450M-parameter FluxAudio-based system achieved 2nd in objective evaluation and 3rd in MOS evaluation at the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge Efficiency Track.
AI.motivation: Current SOTA text-to-music systems rely on massive proprietary datasets and industrial-scale compute, making it impossible to isolate architectural innovations from resource advantages. The authors aim to improve data efficiency and model quality without requiring additional data or compute, disentangling training methodology from resource scale.
AI.method: (1) Score-aware training: uses CLAP audio-caption alignment scores as direct supervision. (2) CLAP-conditioned Beta noise timestep schedule: routes low-scoring segments to high-noise regimes as implicit regularization rather than discarding them. (3) Segment-level filtering: removes the most misaligned examples. (4) Two-stage caption procedure: bridges the distribution gap between verbose training captions and concise inference prompts. (5) REPA auxiliary loss: transfers structured semantic knowledge from pretrained CLAP and MuQ encoders without additional data. All built on a 450M-parameter FluxAudio backbone.
AI.result: The system ranked 2nd across both tracks in objective evaluation and 3rd in the Efficiency Track in the final MOS evaluation at the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge.
AI.conclusion: Score-aware training effectively leverages alignment signals to improve text-to-music generation without additional data or compute. The combination of noise schedule routing, filtering, caption bridging, and REPA loss provides a resource-efficient path to competitive performance, validated by strong challenge rankings.

title: Generative Modeling of Discrete Latent Structures via Dynamic Policy Gradients
summary: Many scientific problems require inferring unobserved mechanistic latent states from indirect observations. While classical approaches, including expectation maximization, do not scale to combinatorially large spaces, deep learning approaches such as variational autoencoders typically form artificial latent states rather than reconstructing the mechanistic ground-truth states. Here, we introduce GReinSS, a policy learning framework that uses dynamically rescaled rewards to learn latent state distributions that maximize the observed data likelihood. We show that GReinSS accurately reconstructs simulated latent sets and latent graphs, outperforming alternative policy learning and generative modeling baselines. Additionally, GReinSS reconstructs isoforms from real short-read RNA sequencing data that better match isoforms detected by orthogonal long-read sequencing than the standard RSEM algorithm. Overall, GReinSS is a principled and practically effective approach for generative modeling and inference of combinatorial latent states from indirect observations.

AI.tldr: GReinSS is a policy learning framework that uses dynamically rescaled rewards to infer combinatorial latent states from indirect observations, outperforming baselines on simulated latent sets/graphs and reconstructing RNA isoforms from short-read sequencing data that better match long-read sequencing validation than RSEM.
AI.motivation: Classical methods for inferring unobserved mechanistic latent states (e.g., expectation maximization) do not scale to combinatorially large spaces, while deep learning approaches like VAEs typically learn artificial latent representations rather than recovering the true mechanistic ground-truth states. There is a need for a principled method that can scalably infer combinatorial mechanistic latent states from indirect observations.
AI.method: GReinSS (Generative Reinforcement for State Space inference) is a policy learning framework that employs dynamically rescaled rewards to learn latent state distributions. The objective is to maximize the observed data likelihood through reinforcement learning, where the policy outputs distributions over combinatorial latent states and rewards are adaptively rescaled to guide learning.
AI.result: GReinSS accurately reconstructs simulated latent sets and latent graphs, outperforming alternative policy learning and generative modeling baselines. On real short-read RNA sequencing data, GReinSS reconstructs isoforms that better match those detected by orthogonal long-read sequencing compared to the standard RSEM algorithm.
AI.conclusion: GReinSS provides a principled and practically effective approach for generative modeling and inference of combinatorial latent states from indirect observations, bridging the gap between scalability and mechanistic interpretability.

title: Reversible Foundations: Training a 120B Sparse MoE through State-Preserving Scaling
comment: 58 pages, 9 figures, 37 tables. Code: https://github.com/The-School-of-AI/LLM. Released models: huggingface.co/theschoolofai/LightningLM-0.1V-{2B, 5B-MoE, 9B-MoE, 120B-MoE}. Companion work: arXiv:2605.29379 (BrahmicTokenizer-131K), arXiv:2605.29459 (Kronecker Embeddings)
summary:
This paper reports on training a hundred-billion-parameter sparse mixture of experts on a single eight-GPU node, end to end. LightningLM 0.1V is a recurrence-backbone language model family grown in four stages from a small dense seed, through a 5B and a 9B mixture of experts, to a 120B model with 460 routed experts under top-12 routing. Each larger model is grown from the trained weights of the smaller one; active parameters rise monotonically from 1.78B at the dense seed to 5.93B at 120B (about 5% of the 118.67B stored). The full lineage runs on single nodes, the larger stages at 8K context, reaching a released training loss of 1.78 at 120B scale.
  This is a systems and experience report. It is organized around three disciplines. Reversibility: a reversible recurrence stack reconstructs activations in the backward pass instead of storing them, holding activation memory flat as the model grows. State-preserving growth: each expansion (dense to MoE, shallow to deep, few experts to many) is given as a reproducible principle paired with the failure that results from getting it wrong; several failures are silent. Single-node economics: the 120B trains through TQP, a strategy of quantized base expert weights and trained low-rank adapters that carries optimizer state on 2.26B adapter parameters rather than 100B+ resident in routed experts, cutting expert-path optimizer state by a factor of ~45.
  What is new is the integration of known primitives, not any primitive in isolation: one grown lineage running end to end on a single node, documented at practitioner level, with per-domain held-out loss as evidence that targeted capabilities (multilingual Indic competence, code) were learned by construction. Model family, tokenizer, and training code are released.

AI.tldr: A 120B-parameter sparse mixture-of-experts language model (LightningLM 0.1V) was trained end-to-end on a single 8-GPU node by growing from a small dense seed through four stages, using reversible recurrence for memory efficiency, state-preserving growth principles, and a quantization-plus-adapter strategy (TQP) to slash optimizer state by ~45×.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that hundred-billion-parameter sparse MoE language models with targeted capabilities (multilingual Indic, code) can be trained entirely on a single commodity 8-GPU node, democratizing large-scale training by integrating reversibility, principled model growth, and extreme optimizer-state compression.
AI.method: The model lineage grows through four stages: dense seed → 5B MoE → 9B MoE → 120B MoE (460 routed experts, top-12 routing, 5.93B active parameters). Three integrated techniques: (1) a reversible recurrence stack that reconstructs activations in backward pass instead of storing them, keeping activation memory constant; (2) state-preserving growth — principled, reproducible expansion recipes (dense→MoE, shallow→deep, few→many experts) with documented silent failure modes; (3) TQP (Training with Quantized base experts and low-rank adapters), which quantizes base expert weights and trains only low-rank adapters, reducing optimizer state from 100B+ to 2.26B adapter parameters (~45× reduction).
AI.result: The full 120B model trained on a single 8-GPU node at 8K context length, achieving a training loss of 1.78. Active parameters grew monotonically from 1.78B (dense) to 5.93B (120B), representing ~5% of stored parameters (118.67B). Targeted capabilities in multilingual Indic and code were learned by construction, evidenced by per-domain held-out loss.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale MoE training is feasible on single-node hardware by integrating known primitives (reversibility, principled growth, optimizer-state compression via TQP) into a unified, reproducible, end-to-end lineage. The work emphasizes practitioner-level documentation and releases model, tokenizer, and training code.

title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
summary: The emergence of "Aha moments" in large language models, particularly DeepSeek-R1-0120, has raised the question of whether these systems genuinely reason or merely imitate the appearance of reasoning. We conduct a comprehensive empirical comparison between model and human reasoning across all 30 problems from AIME 2025, exhaustively annotating 10,247 reasoning steps into five functional categories: Analysis, Inference, Branch, Backtrace, and Reflection. We find a clear structural difference. Human solutions maintain a compact alternation between analysis and deduction, whereas DeepSeek-R1 frequently revisits intermediate results, performs shallow and often unnecessary verification, and loops through local checks without meaningful logical progress. We describe this as topological mimicry: reproducing the surface form of reasoning without its functional role. Despite this, we identify two signals of genuine reasoning. First, successful traces exhibit stable use of branching and backtracking, while failed traces either underuse or overuse exploratory actions. Second, reflection is only effective when placed within deductive inference; reflections trapped in analysis loops focus on local numerical details while missing global logical errors. These findings suggest that current long-CoT models may be rewarded more for the appearance of reasoning than for genuine deductive progress. We discuss directions for improving evaluation and training, including measuring cross-trace stability, penalising "spinning-wheel" traces, encouraging deeper logical correction, and reallocating inference-time compute toward deduction and backtracking. Overall, reasoning quality depends not simply on how much reflection occurs, but on whether reflection appears consistently and at the appropriate logical scale.

AI.tldr: A large-scale comparison of human and DeepSeek-R1 reasoning on AIME 2025 problems reveals that the model engages in "topological mimicry" — reproducing surface forms of reasoning (revisiting results, shallow verification, local loops) without genuine deductive progress. However, two signals of authentic reasoning emerge: effective use of branching/backtracking in successful traces, and reflection that is embedded within deductive inference rather than trapped in analysis loops. The paper argues current long-CoT models are rewarded for the appearance, not substance, of reasoning.
AI.motivation: To determine whether large language models like DeepSeek-R1 genuinely reason or merely imitate reasoning by conducting a fine-grained, step-level empirical comparison between model and human problem-solving traces on challenging mathematical problems.
AI.method: The authors annotated 10,247 reasoning steps across all 30 AIME 2025 problems, classifying each step into five functional categories (Analysis, Inference, Branch, Backtrace, Reflection). They then compared structural patterns between human solutions and DeepSeek-R1-0120 traces, analyzing how these patterns correlate with solution success.
AI.result: Human solutions show compact analysis-deduction alternation, while DeepSeek-R1 exhibits frequent revisiting of intermediate results, shallow unnecessary verification, and local checking loops without logical progress — termed "topological mimicry." Two signals of genuine reasoning were identified: (1) successful traces use branching and backtracking stably, while failed traces underuse or overuse exploratory actions; (2) reflection is effective only when situated within deductive inference, not when trapped in analysis loops focused on local numerical details.
AI.conclusion: Current long-CoT models are likely rewarded more for the appearance of reasoning than for genuine deductive progress. Evaluation and training improvements should measure cross-trace stability, penalize "spinning-wheel" traces, encourage deeper logical correction, and reallocate inference-time compute toward deduction and backtracking rather than superficial reflection.

title: Sparsely gated tiny linear experts
summary: Sparsity allows scaling model parameters without proportionally increasing computational cost. While mixture of experts (MoE) models are made increasingly sparse, individual experts typically remain large and dense. Here, we demonstrate that further increasing sparsity by shrinking each expert to consist of a single neuron and selecting a tiny fraction of many available neurons can improve compute efficiency and interpretability. Counterintuitively, the key to achieving both is removing the nonlinearity typically applied to the experts, resulting in a network of sparsely gated linear neurons (sgatlin). In an isoflop comparison, we find that replacing all transformer feedforward layers with sgatlin improves perplexity in language models across different compute budgets. At the same time, the sparsity and linearity of the resulting feedforward circuits present new opportunities for model interpretability. In a small-scale case study, we demonstrate that feedforward circuits in sgatlin can be interpreted without having to train additional replacement models. We find that they form semantically structured clusters and are causally implicated in factual recall. Our findings paint a possible path towards compute-efficient and interpretable transformer feedforward layers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes sgatlin (sparsely gated linear neurons), which replaces standard transformer feedforward layers with extremely sparse, single-neuron experts that use no nonlinearity. This improves both perplexity and interpretability, with feedforward circuits forming semantically structured clusters causally linked to factual recall.
AI.motivation: While MoE models achieve sparsity at the expert-routing level, individual experts remain large and dense, leaving untapped sparsity potential. The authors aim to push sparsity to the extreme — single-neuron experts — and investigate whether removing the standard nonlinearity can simultaneously improve compute efficiency and model interpretability.
AI.method: The authors replace all transformer feedforward layers with sgatlin: a sparsely gated mixture of linear neurons. Each "expert" is a single neuron with no activation nonlinearity; a gating mechanism selects a tiny fraction of the many available neurons. Isoflop comparisons are conducted across different compute budgets for language modeling. Interpretability is assessed via a small-scale case study analyzing the resulting feedforward circuits without training auxiliary replacement models.
AI.result: In isoflop comparisons, sgatlin improves perplexity over standard transformer feedforward layers across all compute budgets tested. The resulting feedforward circuits are directly interpretable: they form semantically structured clusters and are causally implicated in factual recall, without requiring additional replacement models.
AI.conclusion: Extreme sparsity with linear single-neuron experts is a viable and promising direction for building transformer feedforward layers that are both more compute-efficient and more interpretable than dense nonlinear alternatives.

title: Video-Based Prediction of In-Flight Particle Characteristics in Atmospheric Plasma Spraying
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Applied Data Science Track)
summary: Atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) is a widely used coating process in which in-flight particle temperature and velocity strongly influence coating quality. However, these particle characteristics are difficult to monitor continuously during operation, motivating the development of non-invasive data-driven diagnostic methods. In this work, we investigate the predictive potential of high-speed video observations of the plasma plume for estimating in-flight particle characteristics in APS. We introduce three different video-derived feature representations and evaluate them using Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Networks (TabPFN), convolutional neural networks (CNN), and classical regression baselines including Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Support Vector Regression, and XGBoost. Experiments are conducted using grouped leave-one-out cross-validation on 126 labeled pre- and post-spray video recordings from 63 APS spray runs. Across the engineered feature experiments, TabPFN achieves the most consistent performance for temperature prediction, reaching R2 = 0.86 using the combined feature representation. CNN models particularly perform stronger for velocity prediction, achieving R2 of 0.81. In addition, we evaluate models operating directly on raw video frames using pretrained CNNs and find that the highest performance is achieved by a pretrained CNN with a regression head with R2 of 0.90 and 0.82 for temperature and velocity, respectively. The results demonstrate that video-derived plume information provides a promising and scalable foundation for non-invasive APS diagnostics and real-time process monitoring.

AI.tldr: High-speed video of plasma plumes combined with deep learning (TabPFN, CNNs, pretrained CNNs) can non-invasively predict in-flight particle temperature (R² up to 0.90) and velocity (R² up to 0.82) in atmospheric plasma spraying, enabling scalable real-time process monitoring.
AI.motivation: In-flight particle temperature and velocity are critical to APS coating quality but are difficult to monitor continuously. The authors aim to develop non-invasive, data-driven diagnostics using easily acquired high-speed video of the plasma plume as a predictive signal.
AI.method: Three video-derived feature representations (engineered features, combined features, raw frames) are evaluated using TabPFN, CNNs (ResNet, etc.), and classical regressors (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, SVR, XGBoost). Grouped leave-one-out cross-validation is applied across 126 video recordings from 63 spray runs with paired pre- and post-spray labels.
AI.result: For temperature: TabPFN reaches R²=0.86 on combined features; pretrained CNN with regression head achieves R²=0.90. For velocity: CNN achieves R²=0.81 on engineered features; pretrained CNN reaches R²=0.82. TabPFN shows most consistent performance across feature sets for temperature; CNNs excel for velocity.
AI.conclusion: Video-derived plasma plume information provides a promising, scalable, non-invasive foundation for real-time APS diagnostics. Pretrained CNNs operating on raw frames yield the highest overall predictive performance, while TabPFN offers strong and consistent results with engineered features.

title: Discovering Multiscale Deep Formulas in Complex Systems via Neural-Guided Lambda Calculus
comment: 35 pages, 5 figures; Supplementary Information available as an ancillary file (79 pages)
summary: A fundamental problem in science is identifying underlying patterns of complex systems in the form of concise mathematical formulas. Current Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based methods have shown strong performance in single-scale systems, yet remain limited in identifying scale-specific formulas in multiscale complex systems. We present Deflex, an end-to-end AI method to automatically extract multiscale formulas with potentially different forms, including invariants and distributions, from complex systems. Deflex consists of two subsystems named Deflexformer and Deflexpressor. Deflexpressor is a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model for higher-order formulas. Deflexformer is a decomposable deep energy model for learning unified representations across scales. Deflexpressor generates synthetic data to pre-train Deflexformer, which then guides formula discovery by decoupling multiscale latent relationships. Across six representative complex systems with diverse behaviors, Deflex achieves up to 7-fold higher efficiency than the state-of-the-art methods while enabling automated multiscale discovery. Our work could be a useful tool for scientific discovery across disciplines.

AI.tldr: Deflex is an end-to-end AI method combining a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model (Deflexpressor) and a decomposable deep energy model (Deflexformer) to automatically extract multiscale mathematical formulas from complex systems, achieving up to 7× higher efficiency than state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Current AI-based formula discovery methods excel at single-scale systems but cannot effectively identify scale-specific formulas in multiscale complex systems, where different mathematical forms (invariants, distributions) may operate at different scales.
AI.method: Deflex employs two subsystems: (1) Deflexpressor — a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model for higher-order formula generation, which produces synthetic data; (2) Deflexformer — a decomposable deep energy model that learns unified cross-scale representations and decouples multiscale latent relationships. Deflexpressor pre-trains Deflexformer, which then guides formula discovery.
AI.result: Across six representative complex systems with diverse behaviors, Deflex achieves up to 7-fold higher efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods while enabling automated multiscale discovery.
AI.conclusion: Deflex represents a useful tool for automated scientific discovery across disciplines, addressing the key limitation of prior methods in handling multiscale complex systems.

title: Time series Foundation Models based on Physics-Informed Synthetic Histories for Cold-Start Photovoltaic Forecasting
comment: To be published in the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: At commissioning time, Photovoltaic (PV) operators must forecast production before target-site observations are available, limiting the direct use of standard supervised forecasters. This cold-start setting is addressed with a zero-shot pipeline that generates a synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates, enabling time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to forecast through inference-time conditioning. Five TSFMs are benchmarked against classical baselines under strict Cold-Start Baseline, Real Feedback, and Self-Forecast Feedback strategies. The evaluation spans $440$ PV sites across four datasets and diverse climate regimes. Covariate-aware foundation models outperform baselines by approximately $1.7-2\times$: TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE $0.514$, RMSE $0.721$ $kWh$ ${kWp}^{-1}$ ${d}^{-1}$), while Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source, indicating that accuracy is driven more by the availability of plausible temporal context than by the specific generator.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot pipeline using synthetic production history enables time-series foundation models to forecast PV output in cold-start settings (no target-site observations), outperforming classical baselines by 1.7-2× across 440 sites.
AI.motivation: PV operators face a cold-start problem at commissioning: they must forecast production without any historical on-site observations, making supervised learning infeasible. The authors seek a zero-shot solution that leverages plant metadata and meteorological data alone.
AI.method: A zero-shot pipeline generates synthetic production history from plant metadata (location, capacity, orientation) and meteorological covariates. This synthetic history serves as conditioning context for time-series foundation models (TSFMs) at inference time, treating forecasting as a conditional generation task. Five TSFMs (including TabPFN-TS and Chronos-2) are evaluated under three feedback strategies: Cold-Start Baseline (no real data), Real Feedback (ground-truth history available after initial forecasts), and Self-Forecast Feedback (model uses its own predictions as history).
AI.result: Covariate-aware TSFMs outperform classical baselines by ~1.7-2×. TabPFN-TS achieves lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE 0.514, RMSE 0.721 kWh kWp⁻¹ d⁻¹). Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is insensitive to which synthetic-history generator is used, suggesting that the mere availability of plausible temporal context—not its specific origin—drives accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot PV forecasting using TSFMs with synthetic history is viable and significantly outperforms classical methods. Covariate-aware models hold an advantage, and robustness to synthetic history source indicates that approximate temporal context suffices for effective inference-time conditioning.

title: Unsupervised Continual Clustering via Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Research Track). arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2405.19234
summary: Unsupervised Continual Learning (UCL) aims to enable neural networks to learn sequential tasks without labels or access to past data. A major challenge in this setting is Catastrophic Forgetting, where models forget previously learned tasks upon learning new ones. This challenge is amplified in UCL due to the absence of labels to guide learning and memory retention. Existing mitigation strategies, such as knowledge distillation and replay buffers, often raise memory and privacy concerns. Moreover, current UCL methods largely overlook clustering-specific objectives. To fill this gap, we introduce Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) and propose Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation for Continual Clustering (FBCC). FBCC employs a continual teacher network with a clustering projector and lightweight task-specific students. Through a dual-phase forward-backward distillation process, the teacher learns new clusters while preserving previously discovered cluster structure without storing past data. FBCC represents a pioneering approach to UCC, demonstrating improved clustering performance across sequential tasks. Experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that FBCC consistently outperforms existing continual learning baselines in clustering accuracy while significantly reducing catastrophic forgetting.

AI.tldr: FBCC introduces Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) using a dual-phase forward-backward knowledge distillation framework with a continual teacher and lightweight task-specific students, achieving state-of-the-art clustering accuracy and reduced catastrophic forgetting on four benchmarks without storing past data.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised Continual Learning faces amplified catastrophic forgetting due to absent labels. Existing solutions (knowledge distillation, replay buffers) raise memory/privacy concerns, and current UCL methods neglect clustering-specific objectives, creating a gap for a dedicated Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) approach.
AI.method: FBCC employs a continual teacher network with a clustering projector and lightweight task-specific student networks. A dual-phase process: (1) forward distillation where the previous student transfers knowledge to the teacher, and (2) backward distillation where the updated teacher transfers consolidated knowledge back to the current student, enabling new cluster learning while preserving prior cluster structure without storing raw data.
AI.result: Experiments on four benchmark datasets show FBCC consistently outperforms existing continual learning baselines in clustering accuracy while significantly reducing catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: FBCC is a pioneering approach to Unsupervised Continual Clustering that effectively balances learning new clusters and retaining old ones through forward-backward distillation, demonstrating strong empirical performance and addressing key limitations of prior UCL methods.

title: Graph Neural Network leveraging Higher-order Class Label Connectivity for Heterophilous Graphs
summary: Node classification in graph neural networks (GNNs) has been widely applied in various fields of graph analysis. GNNs achieve high-accuracy node classification in homophilous graphs, where nodes with the same class label tend to be connected. However, their performance remains limited in heterophilous graphs, where nodes with different class labels are more likely to be connected. In particular, current GNNs derived from graph convolutional networks cannot capture higher-order class label connectivity, which is frequently observed in real-world heterophilous graphs. To address this issue, we propose a novel classifier, Label Context Classifier (LCC), designed to capture higher-order class label connectivity in directed graphs. LCC estimates the class label of a target node by leveraging label context embeddings that are generated through four distinct types of walks. In addition, our approach allows the integration of LCC and any GNN by adaptively learning their importance. Experimental results demonstrate that GNNs integrated with LCC outperform SOTA methods and the label context embeddings improve the node classification performance in heterophilous directed graphs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Label Context Classifier (LCC), a novel classifier that captures higher-order class label connectivity in directed heterophilous graphs via label context embeddings from four walk types. LCC can be adaptively integrated with any GNN, and experiments show it outperforms state-of-the-art methods on node classification in heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.motivation: Current GNNs derived from graph convolutional networks fail to capture higher-order class label connectivity patterns prevalent in real-world heterophilous graphs (where connected nodes tend to have different labels), limiting their node classification performance.
AI.method: Label Context Classifier (LCC) generates label context embeddings through four distinct types of walks on directed graphs. These embeddings capture higher-order class label connectivity. LCC is then integrated with any existing GNN by adaptively learning the importance weight between the GNN's predictions and LCC's label context-based predictions.
AI.result: GNNs integrated with LCC outperform state-of-the-art methods. The label context embeddings demonstrably improve node classification performance in heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.conclusion: LCC effectively addresses the limitation of standard GNNs in modeling higher-order label dependencies in heterophilous directed graphs. Its adaptive, model-agnostic integration with any GNN provides a practical and performant solution for node classification in such challenging graph settings.

title: Drifting Models for Surrogate Flow Modeling
comment: Accepted to the 2nd International Symposium AI and Fluid Mechanics 2026
summary: While Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) provides high-fidelity flow fields for optimizing indoor environments, its computational cost limits rapid exploration. To solve this problem generative surrogates offer better distribution modeling than deterministic networks, but iterative sampling is slow. To enable high-quality, single-pass generation, we adapt the novel generative drifting framework to fluid mechanics. We introduce a conditional architecture that performs drifting in a learned VAE latent space and uses label-aware masking to align generated samples with their boundary conditions. Our label-conditioned model matches iterative diffusion in accuracy and flow consistency while running two orders of magnitude faster. Additionally, we propose a spatial-conditioning variant that establishes a promising path towards generalization to unseen geometries. Ultimately, conditional drifting serves as a highly efficient alternative to diffusion based approaches, unlocking real-time CFD surrogates where inference speed is critical.

AI.tldr: The paper adapts a generative drifting framework to CFD, introducing a conditional architecture that performs single-pass generation in a VAE latent space with label-aware masking, matching diffusion model accuracy while being 100x faster, and proposes a spatial-conditioning variant for generalization to unseen geometries.
AI.motivation: CFD simulations provide high-fidelity flow fields for indoor environment optimization but are computationally prohibitive for rapid exploration. Existing generative surrogates (e.g., diffusion models) offer better distribution modeling than deterministic networks, yet their iterative sampling process remains too slow for real-time applications.
AI.method: The authors adapt the generative drifting framework to fluid mechanics with two key innovations: (1) a conditional architecture that performs drifting in a learned VAE latent space, enabling single-pass generation instead of iterative sampling; and (2) label-aware masking that aligns generated flow samples with their specified boundary conditions. A spatial-conditioning variant is also proposed to handle unseen geometries.
AI.result: The label-conditioned model matches iterative diffusion models in both accuracy and flow consistency while achieving approximately two orders of magnitude faster inference. The spatial-conditioning variant demonstrates promising generalization capabilities to previously unseen geometries.
AI.conclusion: Conditional drifting serves as a highly efficient alternative to diffusion-based approaches for CFD surrogate modeling, enabling real-time applications where inference speed is critical, without sacrificing generation quality.

title: Network Recovery from Cascade Data: A Debiased Jacobian-Based Machine Learning Approach
summary: Many important outcomes unfold as dynamic cascades, including product adoption, disease spread, financial distress, and information diffusion. A central challenge is to recover the hidden influence network behind these cascades. Existing methods typically assume a specific diffusion model, and their performance degrades substantially when that assumption is misspecified. We propose CascadeNet, a Jacobian-based machine learning framework for network recovery that does not require specifying a diffusion mechanism. The key idea is that the underlying influence structure can be characterized by the Jacobian of the one-step transition function. CascadeNet first constructs a flexible estimator of the transition function, and further applies Neyman-orthogonal debiasing via the Riesz representer, so that the debiased Jacobian is $\sqrt{n}$-consistent and asymptotically normal, enabling formal inference on the network structure. We validate CascadeNet in both a simulation exercise and a real-world empirical application. In simulations, where the data-generating process is known, CascadeNet achieves the highest network recovery accuracy across nine common data-generating processes. In an empirical application to COVID-19 transmission across Spain's 52 provinces, CascadeNet recovers transmission networks that are significantly correlated with the true inter-province mobility network, whereas networks recovered by baseline methods show no significant alignment with the ground truth.

AI.tldr: CascadeNet is a machine learning framework for recovering hidden influence networks from cascade data without specifying a diffusion model. It uses Jacobian estimation of the one-step transition function combined with Neyman-orthogonal debiasing to achieve √n-consistency and asymptotic normality, outperforming nine baseline models in simulations and successfully recovering COVID-19 transmission networks aligned with true mobility data across Spanish provinces.
AI.motivation: Existing network recovery methods from cascade data require specifying a particular diffusion model (e.g., independent cascade, linear threshold), and their performance degrades substantially under model misspecification. There is a need for a robust, model-agnostic method that can recover influence networks without relying on restrictive parametric assumptions about the diffusion process.
AI.method: CascadeNet consists of two stages: (1) constructing a flexible, nonparametric estimator of the one-step transition function (mapping current states to next-step states), and (2) applying Neyman-orthogonal debiasing via the Riesz representer to the Jacobian of this transition function. This debiasing yields √n-consistent, asymptotically normal estimates of the influence structure, enabling formal statistical inference (confidence intervals, hypothesis tests) on network edges.
AI.result: In simulations across nine different data-generating processes, CascadeNet achieved the highest network recovery accuracy compared to existing methods. In a real-world application to COVID-19 transmission across Spain's 52 provinces, the network recovered by CascadeNet was significantly correlated with the true inter-province mobility network, whereas baseline methods showed no significant alignment with ground truth.
AI.conclusion: CascadeNet provides a robust, model-agnostic framework for influence network recovery that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy without requiring specification of a diffusion model. Its debiased Jacobian estimator enables formal inference, making it a practical and theoretically grounded tool for empirical cascade analysis in economics, epidemiology, and information diffusion.

title: CoMetaPNS: Continually Meta-learning Personalized Neural Surrogates for Cardiac Electrophysiology Simulations
summary: Personalized virtual heart simulations face challenges in model personalization and computational cost. While neural surrogates offer state-of-the-art solutions, they typically address either efficient personalization or training generalizable models. Recent work reframes this by learning the process of personalizing a surrogate using limited subject-specific context data, through few-shot generative modeling with set-conditioned surrogates and meta-learned amortized inference. These methods, however, assume a static and diverse training distribution with known task identifiers. When new data becomes available, they require costly retraining with all prior data to avoid catastrophic forgetting - a phenomena where the model forgets earlier tasks when trained on new ones. This is a major limitation in clinical settings where often unlabeled data arrives sequentially and full retraining is infeasible. This paper presents a new continual meta-learning framework to achieve personalized neural surrogates able to not only continually integrate information but also identify whether incoming data stems from a known or unknown dynamics source. By leveraging a continual Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model over a memory buffer, our framework can infer the identifiers and relationships of data over time - required for effective meta-learning. Empirical results on synthetic cardiac data demonstrate superior simulation forecasting, computational scalability, and resilience to catastrophic forgetting compared to existing baselines.

AI.tldr: A continual meta-learning framework for personalized cardiac surrogates that integrates sequential data without catastrophic forgetting and can identify novel dynamics, using a Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model over a memory buffer.
AI.motivation: Existing few-shot personalized neural surrogates for virtual heart simulations assume a static training distribution with known task IDs and require costly full retraining when new data arrives, causing catastrophic forgetting. This is impractical in clinical settings where data arrives sequentially and full retraining is infeasible.
AI.method: The paper proposes a continual meta-learning framework that uses a continual Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) over a memory buffer to infer task identifiers and relationships among sequentially arriving data. This enables continual integration of new information and detection of unknown dynamics sources without retraining on all prior data.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic cardiac data demonstrate superior simulation forecasting accuracy, better computational scalability, and improved resilience to catastrophic forgetting compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: The continual meta-learning framework effectively addresses the limitation of static training distributions in personalized cardiac surrogates, enabling practical deployment in clinical settings with sequential, unlabeled data streams.

title: Second-Order Path Kernel Interpolation Formulas in Machine Learning
summary: Understanding how training data shape neural network predictions is a central problem in modern learning theory. In 2020, Pedro Domingos proposed an interpolation formula valid for every model learned by deterministic gradient descent. It expresses the model's prediction as an integral, along the optimization path, of a data-dependent kernel that aligns the model's gradients at the test and training data. Such a first-order characterization remains valid for models trained with batch-based stochastic optimization. In this paper, we develop second-order forms of these interpolation formulas. We show that the leading path-kernel interpolation is supplemented by a curvature-weighted interpolation term. For stochastic gradient descent, an additional sampling-induced component appears, coupling the curvature of the prediction with the covariance of mini-batch gradient noise. We also extend the representation to stochastic gradient descent with momentum, where the interpolation structure is preserved but with the weights modified by a memory-related factor. Moreover, we establish a concentration estimate for the terminal prediction, identifying the fluctuation scale around the expected second-order representation. Together, these results provide a refinement of the path-kernel interpretation of neural network prediction.
